topics.title: Bioreactors - Function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT526 - BIOREACTOR DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bioreactors - Function

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bioreactor (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> A **bioreactor** is a closed, engineered vessel or system that provides a controlled environment to support a biologically active environment — typically a **microbial, plant, animal, or enzymatic culture** — by regulating physical, chemical, and biological parameters (pH, temperature, dissolved oxygen, substrate concentration, agitation, etc.) to facilitate the synthesis of a desired product via biochemical transformations.

In the KTU Bioreactor Design framework, a bioreactor is *not merely a container* — it is a **unit operation device** whose **function** is to convert a substrate (input) into a biological product (output) under tightly controlled conditions that mimic the optimal physiological state of the host organism.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a Bioreactor as a "Smart Kitchen" for Microbes** 🍲
>
> Imagine you are a chef. To bake a perfect cake, you need:
> - A temperature-controlled oven (temperature control)
> - Continuous mixing of batter (agitation)
> - Controlled humidity (aeration/oxygen)
> - No outside dust or contamination (sterility)
> - Timely addition of sugar/flour (substrate feeding)
> - A glass window to watch progress (sensors/monitoring)
>
> A **bioreactor is exactly that** — but instead of baking a cake, you are "baking" a **vaccine, an antibiotic, an enzyme, or a biofuel**. The chef is the **microorganism**, and the oven is the **reactor vessel**. Without a bioreactor's controlled environment, microbes either die, produce garbage byproducts, or work too slowly to be industrially useful.

## 1.3 Core Functions of a Bioreactor (Syllabus Anchor)

The **primary functions** of a bioreactor, as specified in the KTU 2024 PEPOT526 Module 1 syllabus, are:

| # | Function | Purpose |
|---|----------|---------|
| 1 | **Containment** | Holds the culture medium and the biological catalyst safely |
| 2 | **Sterility Maintenance** | Prevents contamination by unwanted microorganisms |
| 3 | **Mixing & Agitation** | Ensures uniform distribution of nutrients, cells, and gases |
| 4 | **Aeration / Oxygen Transfer** | Supplies $\text{O}_2$ for aerobic cultures; removes $\text{CO}_2$ |
| 5 | **Heat Transfer / Temperature Control** | Removes metabolic heat to maintain optimal temperature (often **37°C** for mesophiles) |
| 6 | **Mass Transfer** | Transfers substrates (sugars, amino acids) to cells and products (metabolites) out of cells |
| 7 | **pH Control** | Maintains optimal acidity for enzyme activity (typically **pH 6.5 – 7.5**) |
| 8 | **Foam Control** | Suppresses excessive foam formed by proteins and surfactants |
| 9 | **Process Monitoring & Control** | Real-time measurement of critical process parameters (CPPs) |
| 10 | **Scale-up Compatibility** | Allows consistent transfer of process performance from lab to industrial scale |

## 1.4 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics in Bioreactor Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Metrics You Must Memorize for KTU Exams:**
> - **Sterilization temperature:** **121.1 °C** (at **15 psi** for **15–20 min**)
> - **Standard mesophilic growth temperature:** **30–37 °C**
> - **Henry's Law Constant (O₂ in water at 25°C):** $\text{K}_{\text{H,O}_2} \approx 769.2 \;\text{atm·L/mol}$
> - **Dissolved Oxygen threshold for aerobic cultures:** typically **> 30% air saturation**
> - **Reynolds Number threshold for turbulent mixing:** $\text{Re} > 10{,}000$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Bioreactor Function

Every bioreactor — whether a **Stirred Tank Reactor (STR)**, **Air-Lift Reactor (ALR)**, **Bubble Column**, **Packed-Bed Reactor (PBR)**, or **Membrane Bioreactor (MBR)** — must perform **three core physiological functions** that mirror the *living requirements* of the host cell:

### Pillar 1: Provision of an Optimal Physicochemical Environment
The bioreactor must maintain:
- A uniform **temperature field** (no hot/cold spots)
- A uniform **pH field** throughout the bulk liquid
- A controlled **redox potential** (for anaerobic vs. aerobic decisions)

### Pillar 2: Efficient Mass Transfer
This includes:
- **Gas–Liquid Mass Transfer:** Oxygen moving from sparged air bubbles → into the liquid → into the cell. This is governed by the **Two-Film Theory** by **Lewis & Whitman (1924)**.
- **Liquid–Solid Mass Transfer:** Substrate moving from bulk liquid → to the cell surface (or onto an immobilized enzyme surface).
- **Inter-Phase Heat Transfer:** Metabolic heat (e.g., ~**10–20 kW/m³** for high-cell-density cultures) being removed via the cooling jacket.

### Pillar 3: Containment & Biosafety
The vessel must:
- Withstand internal pressure (typically **0.2–0.5 bar** gauge)
- Prevent ingress of contaminants
- Prevent egress of genetically modified organisms (GMO containment per **BSL-1 to BSL-4** standards)

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for solving any numerical in PEPOT526 Module 1. Memorize the units, not just the symbols.

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Standard Units |
|---|--------------------|-------------|----------------|
| 1 | $\text{OTR} = k_L a \cdot (C^* - C_L)$ | Oxygen Transfer Rate (Two-Film Theory) | $\text{mol O}_2 / (\text{L·h})$ |
| 2 | $k_L a = K \cdot \left(\dfrac{P_g}{V_L}\right)^{\alpha} \cdot u_s^{\beta}$ | Van't Riet correlation for $k_L a$ | $\text{s}^{-1}$ |
| 3 | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho N D_i^2}{\mu}$ | Reynolds Number (impeller) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\text{Fr} = \dfrac{N^2 D_i}{g}$ | Froude Number (impeller) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\text{Np} = \dfrac{P}{\rho N^3 D_i^5}$ | Power Number (impeller) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $P = \text{Np} \cdot \rho \cdot N^3 \cdot D_i^5$ | Power consumption by impeller | W (watts) |
| 7 | $\text{ST} = k_L a \cdot \dfrac{C^*}{\text{OUR}}$ | Standard aeration efficiency (Shuler & Kargi) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $t_m = \dfrac{\ln N_0 / N}{k_d}$ | Decimal reduction time (sterilization) | minutes |
| 9 | $\nabla = \dfrac{k_d \cdot E_g / (k_d + k_d')$ | Del factor design equation (continuous sterilization) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\theta = e^{k_d \cdot t}$ | Sterilization kill ratio | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\text{HTP} = \dfrac{1}{\text{kWh per m}^3 \text{ gas}}$ | Hydromechanical Time Parameter | $\text{kWh}^{-1}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notation Glossary (used in formulas above):**
> - $k_L a$ = volumetric mass-transfer coefficient $\vert$ $C^*$ = saturation dissolved O₂ concentration $\vert$ $C_L$ = actual dissolved O₂ $\vert$ $P_g$ = gassed power input $\vert$ $V_L$ = liquid volume $\vert$ $u_s$ = superficial gas velocity
> - $\rho$ = fluid density ($\approx \mathbf{1000 \; kg/m^3}$ for water) $\vert$ $\mu$ = viscosity $\vert$ $N$ = impeller speed (rev/s) $\vert$ $D_i$ = impeller diameter
> - $\text{OUR}$ = Oxygen Uptake Rate $\vert$ $\text{OTR}$ = Oxygen Transfer Rate

## 2.3 Functional Classification of Bioreactors

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners love asking *"Classify bioreactors based on their function/mode of operation."* The KTU 2024 syllabus recognizes the following functional categories:

### A. Based on the Culture System
- **Batch Bioreactor** — All nutrients added at $t = 0$; no feed; harvested at end.
- **Fed-Batch Bioreactor** — Nutrients added incrementally; volume increases with time.
- **Continuous Bioreactor (Chemostat / CSTR)** — Continuous inflow of feed and outflow of broth at steady state.
- **Perfusion Bioreactor** — Cells retained inside; spent medium continuously replaced (used in mammalian cell culture for **monoclonal antibodies**).

### B. Based on the Oxygen Supply Mechanism
- **Aerated / Sparged Bioreactor** — Air bubbled through sparger (e.g., STR with **Rushton turbine**).
- **Surface / Tray Bioreactor** — Thin film of liquid exposed to air (low-tech, e.g., vinegar production).
- **Membrane-Aerated Bioreactor** — $\text{O}_2$ supplied via silicone tubing; bubble-free.

### C. Based on the Biological Catalyst Form
- **Suspended-Cell Bioreactor** — Free cells in liquid (most common).
- **Immobilized-Cell Bioreactor** — Cells trapped in gel beads, alginate, or attached to a surface.
- **Enzyme Bioreactor** — Pure enzyme, no living cells.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this matter in industry?**
> - In **insulin production**, *E. coli* is grown in a **fed-batch STR** at **> 50 g/L** cell density — a function that batch reactors simply cannot achieve.
> - In **wastewater treatment**, an **activated-sludge CSTR** must continuously degrade organics — a function that demands steady-state mass-transfer control.
> - In **monoclonal antibody (mAb) manufacturing**, **perfusion bioreactors** with cell densities of **$10^7$ cells/mL** demand extreme sterility and oxygen-transfer functions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Mass Balance Framework Underlying Bioreactor Function

Every bioreactor function can be expressed as a **mass balance** on a generalized component $i$:

$$
\text{Accumulation} = \text{Inflow} - \text{Outflow} + \text{Generation} - \text{Consumption}
$$

Let us derive the steady-state mass balance for a **continuous stirred tank bioreactor (CSTR)** with volume $V$, volumetric feed flow rate $F$, inlet substrate concentration $S_{\text{in}}$, and outlet substrate concentration $S$.

**Step 1: Define the rate of change of substrate mass inside the reactor.**

The mass of substrate in the reactor at any instant is $V \cdot S$. Its rate of change is:

$$
\frac{d(V S)}{dt} = \frac{d(V S)}{dt}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the generalized mass balance.**

$$
\frac{d(V S)}{dt} = F \cdot S_{\text{in}} - F \cdot S + V \cdot r_s
$$

Here $r_s$ is the net rate of substrate generation/consumption per unit volume (negative for consumption).

**Step 3: Assume constant volume $V$ (valid when feed flow rate equals outlet flow rate).**

Since $V = $ constant,

$$
V \cdot \frac{dS}{dt} = F \cdot S_{\text{in}} - F \cdot S + V \cdot r_s
$$

**Step 4: Divide throughout by $V$ and define the dilution rate $D = F/V$.**

$$
\frac{dS}{dt} = D \cdot S_{\text{in}} - D \cdot S + r_s
$$

**Step 5: At steady state, $\dfrac{dS}{dt} = 0$.**

$$
0 = D \cdot S_{\text{in}} - D \cdot S + r_s
$$

**Step 6: Solve for the outlet substrate concentration $S$ (this is the *function* of the bioreactor — what it does to the substrate).**

$$
S = S_{\text{in}} + \frac{r_s}{D}
$$

Since $r_s$ is negative (substrate is consumed), the term $\dfrac{r_s}{D}$ is negative, and therefore $S < S_{\text{in}}$ — confirming that the bioreactor *consumes* substrate.

$$
\boxed{\;S = S_{\text{in}} - \frac{q_s X}{D}\;}
$$

where $q_s$ is the specific substrate consumption rate and $X$ is the biomass concentration. This is the **fundamental functional equation** of a continuous bioreactor.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Oxygen Transfer Rate (OTR) as the Rate-Limiting Function

For aerobic cultures, **oxygen supply is the rate-limiting function**. The Two-Film Theory gives:

**Step 1: Express flux of $\text{O}_2$ across the gas–liquid interface.**

By Fick's First Law applied to a thin film of thickness $y$:

$$
N_{O_2} = k_L \cdot (C^* - C_L)
$$

where $k_L$ is the local mass-transfer coefficient across the liquid film.

**Step 2: Multiply by the interfacial area per unit volume, $a$, to get the volumetric rate.**

$$
\text{OTR} = k_L a \cdot (C^* - C_L)
$$

**Step 3: Note the units.**

$\text{OTR}$ has units of $\text{mol O}_2 / (\text{L} \cdot \text{h})$, $k_L a$ has units of $\text{h}^{-1}$, and $(C^* - C_L)$ has units of $\text{mol/L}$.

**Step 4: At steady state, $\text{OTR} = \text{OUR}$ (Oxygen Uptake Rate of cells).**

$$
\text{OUR} = \mu \cdot X \cdot Y_{X/O_2}
$$

where $\mu$ is the specific growth rate, $X$ is biomass, and $Y_{X/O_2}$ is the yield coefficient (g biomass per g O₂).

**Step 5: Equate and solve for the design-driving variable, $C_L$.**

$$
k_L a \cdot (C^* - C_L) = \mu \cdot X \cdot Y_{X/O_2}
$$

$$
C_L = C^* - \dfrac{\mu \cdot X \cdot Y_{X/O_2}}{k_L a}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** As biomass $X$ rises, $C_L$ falls. If $C_L$ drops below the **critical dissolved oxygen concentration** $C_{\text{crit}}$ (typically **~0.1 mg/L for *E. coli***), cells shift to mixed-acid fermentation — ruining the product. **Thus the function of the bioreactor is to maintain $C_L \geq C_{\text{crit}}$**, and this is achieved by manipulating $k_L a$ via impeller speed, aeration rate, or baffle design.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Functional Design Trade-offs

| Design Decision | Functional Benefit | Functional Cost / Trade-off |
|-----------------|-------------------|------------------------------|
| Increase impeller speed $N$ | Higher $k_L a$ (better O₂ transfer) | Higher power $P$ (cubic in $N$); shear damage to cells |
| Increase aeration rate $Q_g$ | Higher $k_L a$ | Foam formation; antifoam may reduce OTR |
| Increase cell density $X$ | Higher productivity | Higher OUR; risk of O₂ limitation |
| Reduce temperature setpoint | Lower metabolic heat | Slower growth rate; risk of contamination |
| Use fed-batch over batch | Avoids catabolite repression | Complex control; risk of substrate accumulation |
| Use continuous (CSTR) over batch | Steady-state operation; consistent quality | Risk of washout at high $D$; contamination persistence |

## 3.4 Numerical Walkthrough (KTU 2024 Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Sample problem (Worked Example):**
> An STR is operating at steady state. $V = 1000$ L, $F = 200$ L/h, $S_{\text{in}} = 20$ g/L, $S = 2$ g/L, $X = 5$ g/L, $q_s = 0.4$ g substrate/(g cells·h). Find the specific growth rate $\mu$ (assuming $Y_{X/S} = 0.5$).

**Solution:**

Step 1: Compute dilution rate.

$$
D = \frac{F}{V} = \frac{200}{1000} = 0.2 \;\text{h}^{-1}
$$

Step 2: Use the steady-state substrate balance.

$$
D \cdot (S_{\text{in}} - S) = q_s \cdot X
$$

Step 3: Verify.

$$
0.2 \cdot (20 - 2) = 0.2 \cdot 18 = 3.6 \;\text{g/(L·h)}
$$

$$
q_s \cdot X = 0.4 \cdot 5 = 2.0 \;\text{g/(L·h)}
$$

Step 4: Since they are unequal, $\mu$ deviates from $q_s / Y_{X/S}$. At true steady state, $\mu = D$ (in a chemostat), so:

$$
\mu = 0.2 \;\text{h}^{-1}
$$

$$
t_d = \frac{\ln 2}{\mu} = \frac{0.693}{0.2} = 3.47 \;\text{h}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's note:** In a chemostat, **$\mu = D$** at steady state — this is a *direct consequence* of the mass balance. Memorize this.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: Inputs, Functions, and Outputs of a Bioreactor

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram maps the *functional architecture* of a generic bioreactor. It shows the **inputs** (raw materials and energy), the **internal functions** (mass transfer, heat transfer, sterility), and the **outputs** (biomass, product, exhaust gases).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph inputsBlock["INPUT STREAMS"]
        inputA["Sterile Air / O2 Supply"]
        inputB["Liquid Feed Substrate"]
        inputC["pH Adjusters Acid / Base"]
        inputD["Antifoam Agent"]
        inputE["Utility Steam for Sterilization"]
    end

    subgraph reactorCore["BIOREACTOR FUNCTIONAL CORE"]
        funcA["Function 1: Containment Vessel"]
        funcB["Function 2: Sterility Barrier"]
        funcC["Function 3: Agitation and Mixing"]
        funcD["Function 4: Aeration and O2 Transfer"]
        funcE["Function 5: Heat Transfer via Cooling Jacket"]
        funcF["Function 6: pH and Dissolved O2 Control"]
        funcG["Function 7: Foam Suppression"]
        funcH["Function 8: Real Time Sensor Monitoring"]
    end

    subgraph controlBlock["PROCESS CONTROL LAYER"]
        ctrlA["PID Controller for Temperature"]
        ctrlB["PID Controller for pH"]
        ctrlC["PID Controller for DO"]
        ctrlD["PID Controller for Foam"]
    end

    subgraph outputsBlock["OUTPUT STREAMS"]
        outA["Harvested Biomass"]
        outB["Desired Bio Product"]
        outC["Exhaust CO2 and Off Gas"]
        outD["Waste Heat to Cooling Water"]
    end

    inputA --> funcD
    inputB --> funcA
    inputC --> funcF
    inputD --> funcG
    inputE --> funcB

    funcA --> funcC
    funcC --> funcD
    funcD --> funcE
    funcE --> funcF
    funcF --> funcG
    funcG --> funcH

    funcH --> ctrlA
    funcH --> ctrlB
    funcH --> ctrlC
    funcH --> ctrlD

    ctrlA --> funcE
    ctrlB --> funcF
    ctrlC --> funcD
    ctrlD --> funcG

    funcA --> outA
    funcF --> outB
    funcD --> outC
    funcE --> outD
```

## 4.2 Hierarchy of Bioreactor Functions (Mermaid Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    root["BIOREACTOR CORE FUNCTIONS"]

    root --> f1["PRIMARY FUNCTIONS"]
    root --> f2["SECONDARY FUNCTIONS"]
    root --> f3["TERTIARY FUNCTIONS"]

    f1 --> f1a["Containment of Culture"]
    f1 --> f1b["Provision of Optimum Environment"]
    f1 --> f1c["Product Formation and Recovery"]

    f2 --> f2a["Sterility Maintenance"]
    f2 --> f2b["Mixing and Agitation"]
    f2 --> f2c["Aeration and O2 Supply"]
    f2 --> f2d["Heat Removal"]
    f2 --> f2e["pH Control"]

    f3 --> f3a["Foam Control"]
    f3 --> f3b["Sensor Integration"]
    f3 --> f3c["Scale up Compatibility"]
    f3 --> f3d["Cleaning in Place CIP"]
    f3 --> f3e["Sterilization in Place SIP"]
```

## 4.3 Functional Flow: From Substrate Input to Product Output

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Substrate Feed S_in"] --> B["Mixing Impeller"]
    B --> C["Cell Biomass X"]
    C --> D["Metabolic Pathway"]
    D --> E["Product P"]
    D --> F["CO2 Off Gas"]
    D --> G["Heat Generation"]
    E --> H["Harvest Stream"]
    F --> I["Exhaust Vent"]
    G --> J["Cooling Jacket"]
    J --> K["Cooling Water Out"]
```

## 4.4 Diagram Legend / Interpretation Notes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagrams above:**
> - **Input Streams Block** = what *enters* the bioreactor (mass, energy, utilities).
> - **Reactor Functional Core** = the *physical and chemical* functions the reactor performs on those inputs.
> - **Process Control Layer** = the *regulatory* functions (PID control loops) that maintain CPPs within specified limits.
> - **Output Streams** = the *useful products* (biomass, target metabolite) and the *waste streams* (off-gas, heat) that the function of the bioreactor produces.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**"List the primary functions of a bioreactor. Why is oxygen transfer considered the rate-limiting function in most aerobic bioreactors?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **primary functions** of a bioreactor are:
1. **Containment** of the biological culture in a controlled environment.
2. **Provision of optimal physicochemical conditions** — temperature, pH, and dissolved oxygen.
3. **Efficient mass transfer** of substrates into the cells and products out of the cells.
4. **Heat removal** to maintain isothermal conditions.
5. **Sterility maintenance** to prevent contamination.

**Why oxygen transfer is rate-limiting:**
Oxygen has **poor aqueous solubility** — at 25°C in water, $C^* \approx 8.26$ mg/L — and **microbes consume $\text{O}_2$ 10–20 times faster** than it can dissolve. Therefore, $k_L a$ becomes the bottleneck, and a high $k_L a \cdot (C^* - C_L)$ must be sustained — typically requiring **$k_L a > 200 \; \text{h}^{-1}$** in industrial STRs. *(2 marks for listing functions + 1 mark for justifying O₂ rate-limitation.)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"Differentiate between a batch, fed-batch, and continuous bioreactor in terms of their functional characteristics."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Batch | Fed-Batch | Continuous (CSTR) |
|---------|-------|-----------|-------------------|
| **Feed addition** | All at $t = 0$ | Incremental | Continuous |
| **Volume** | Constant | Increases with time | Constant |
| **Operation state** | Unsteady | Unsteady | Steady state |
| **Function** | Produces one batch per cycle | Avoids substrate overflow | Sustains long-term production |
| **Productivity** | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| **Application** | Vaccines, small-scale | Recombinant proteins | Wastewater, ethanol |

*(1 mark each for the three reactors in tabular form.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]

**Q. (a)** With the help of a neat labeled diagram, describe the **various functions of a stirred-tank bioreactor (STR)**. Discuss in detail the **role of each functional component**. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** Derive the **steady-state mass balance** for a continuous stirred-tank bioreactor (CSTR) treating a substrate $S$ at dilution rate $D$. Show that at steady state, $\mu = D$, and comment on the **implication of washout**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### SOLUTION TO Q.A(a): Functions of a STR (7 marks)

A **Stirred Tank Reactor (STR)** is the *workhorse* of industrial biotechnology. It performs the following **interrelated functions**:

1. **Containment Function (Vessel Wall):**
   The cylindrical vessel (typically **L:D ratio ≈ 2:1**, with dished top and bottom) is made of **SS 316L stainless steel** to withstand repeated sterilization cycles at **121°C / 15 psi** and to prevent corrosion by acidic/alkaline media. *(1 mark)*

2. **Agitation Function (Impeller System):**
   Typically uses **Rushton turbines** (low-viscosity broths) or **marine / pitched-blade impellers** (animal cell cultures, where shear is damaging). The impeller provides **bulk mixing** (eliminating concentration gradients) and **micromixing** (at the molecular level near cells). *(1 mark)*

3. **Aeration Function (Sparger + Baffles):**
   Air enters through a **porous sparger** (ring or disc) at the bottom, generating bubbles of **2–5 mm** diameter. **Four baffles** mounted on the vessel wall break the central vortex and improve radial mixing. *(1 mark)*

4. **Heat Transfer Function (Cooling Jacket / Coil):**
   Metabolic heat (up to **15–25 kW/m³** for high-density cultures) is removed via a **limpet coil** or **double-walled jacket** with circulating coolant at **5–10°C**. *(1 mark)*

5. **Sensor and Control Function (Probes):**
   Sensors measure **pH, DO (dissolved oxygen), temperature, foam level, and OD (optical density)** in real time, feeding signals to **PID controllers**. *(1 mark)*

6. **Sterility Function (Mechanical Seals, Sterile Filters):**
   All inlet and outlet pipes are fitted with **0.2 µm hydrophobic sterile filters**; the agitator shaft passes through a **magnetic coupling or double mechanical seal** to prevent ingress of contaminants. *(1 mark)*

7. **Foam Control Function (Mechanical Foam Breaker + Chemical Antifoam):**
   A **mechanical foam breaker** at the top plus **silicone-based antifoam (e.g., polypropylene glycol)** added on demand. *(1 mark)*

---

#### SOLUTION TO Q.A(b): CSTR Steady-State Mass Balance (7 marks)

**Step 1: General mass balance on substrate $S$:**

$$
\frac{d(VS)}{dt} = F S_{\text{in}} - F S + V r_s
$$

**Step 2: Constant volume assumption:** $V = $ constant, so $dV/dt = 0$.

$$
V \frac{dS}{dt} = F (S_{\text{in}} - S) + V r_s
$$

**Step 3: Divide by $V$ and substitute $D = F/V$:**

$$
\frac{dS}{dt} = D (S_{\text{in}} - S) + r_s
$$

**Step 4: Apply Monod kinetics for substrate consumption:**

$$
r_s = -\frac{\mu X}{Y_{X/S}} = -\frac{\mu_{\max} S \cdot X}{Y_{X/S}(K_s + S)}
$$

**Step 5: Apply the steady-state condition $dS/dt = 0$ and $dX/dt = 0$:**

At steady state for biomass:

$$
D X = \mu X \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\mu = D}
$$

*(4 marks for derivation up to this point)*

**Step 6: Solve for $S$ at steady state:**

$$
S = \frac{D \cdot K_s}{\mu_{\max} - D}
$$

**Step 7: Comment on washout:**

The **critical dilution rate** is:

$$
D_c = \mu_{\max} \left( \frac{S_{\text{in}}}{K_s + S_{\text{in}}} \right)
$$

If $D > D_c$, then $\mu_{\max} < D$, which means the cells cannot reproduce fast enough to be retained — the **biomass is washed out** of the reactor. The reactor's function (converting substrate to product) collapses. **Therefore, industrial CSTRs always operate at $D \leq 0.8 D_c$ to ensure safe production.** *(3 marks for washout discussion)*

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the **Two-Film Theory** of oxygen transfer. Derive the equation $\text{OTR} = k_L a (C^* - C_L)$ and discuss the factors that affect $k_L a$ in a stirred-tank bioreactor. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** A **fed-batch bioreactor** is used to produce **recombinant insulin in *E. coli***. The initial volume is $V_0 = 500$ L. A feed of $S_{\text{in}} = 400$ g/L glucose is added at flow rate $F = 50$ L/h. The specific growth rate is maintained at $\mu = 0.3 \; \text{h}^{-1}$ and yield $Y_{X/S} = 0.4$ g/g. Determine the **glucose concentration in the broth** at $t = 4$ h assuming quasi-steady state. **(7 marks)**

---

#### SOLUTION TO Q.B(a): Two-Film Theory (7 marks)

**Step 1: Concept of Two-Film Theory (Lewis & Whitman, 1924):**
The theory postulates that at the gas–liquid interface, two stagnant films exist — a **gas film** and a **liquid film** — across which mass transfer occurs by molecular diffusion. **The liquid film is the rate-controlling resistance** because $\text{O}_2$ is poorly soluble in water. *(2 marks)*

**Step 2: Fick's First Law applied to the liquid film:**

$$
N_{O_2} = -D_L \frac{dC}{dy}
$$

For a thin film of thickness $y_L$, this integrates to:

$$
N_{O_2} = \frac{D_L}{y_L} (C^* - C_L) = k_L (C^* - C_L)
$$

where $k_L = D_L / y_L$ is the local mass-transfer coefficient (m/s). *(2 marks)*

**Step 3: Multiply by interfacial area per unit volume, $a$:**

$$
\text{OTR} = k_L a (C^* - C_L)
$$

**Step 4: Factors affecting $k_L a$:**
- **Agitation speed $N$** (increases turbulence → higher $k_L a$)
- **Aeration rate $Q_g$** (more bubbles → more interfacial area)
- **Bubble size** (smaller bubbles → higher $a$)
- **Liquid viscosity $\mu$** (higher viscosity → lower $k_L$)
- **Presence of antifoam** (reduces $k_L a$ by 20–50%)
- **Power input per unit volume $P_g/V$** *(3 marks)*

---

#### SOLUTION TO Q.B(b): Fed-Batch Numerical (7 marks)

**Step 1: Write the differential volume balance.**

$$
\frac{dV}{dt} = F = 50 \;\text{L/h}
$$

Integrating from $t = 0$ to $t = 4$ h:

$$
V(t) = V_0 + F \cdot t = 500 + 50 \cdot 4 = 700 \;\text{L}
$$

**Step 2: Apply quasi-steady state assumption (QSSA):**
At QSSA, the substrate concentration $S$ is approximately constant, so:

$$
\frac{dS}{dt} \approx 0
$$

**Step 3: Substrate mass balance:**

$$
\frac{d(VS)}{dt} = F S_{\text{in}} - V r_s
$$

Expanding:

$$
V \frac{dS}{dt} + S \frac{dV}{dt} = F S_{\text{in}} - V r_s
$$

With QSSA ($dS/dt = 0$) and $r_s = \mu X / Y_{X/S}$:

$$
S \cdot F = F S_{\text{in}} - V \cdot \frac{\mu X}{Y_{X/S}}
$$

**Step 4: Solve for $S$:**

$$
S = S_{\text{in}} - \frac{V \mu X}{F \cdot Y_{X/S}}
$$

**Step 5: At $t = 4$ h, $V = 700$ L. Assume $X = 10$ g/L (typical for fed-batch *E. coli*).**

$$
S = 400 - \frac{700 \cdot 0.3 \cdot 10}{50 \cdot 0.4}
$$

$$
S = 400 - \frac{2100}{20} = 400 - 105 = 295 \;\text{g/L}
$$

**Step 6: Interpretation:**
The residual glucose concentration is **295 g/L**, which is **unphysiologically high** — the assumption $X = 10$ g/L may be too low. In practice, $X$ at $t = 4$ h would be **~20 g/L**, giving:

$$
S = 400 - \frac{700 \cdot 0.3 \cdot 20}{50 \cdot 0.4} = 400 - 210 = 190 \;\text{g/L}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to update $V(t)$** — students often use $V = 500$ L instead of the time-dependent volume. **−2 marks.**
> 2. **Using $r_s = -\mu X$** instead of $-\mu X / Y_{X/S}$. **−1 mark.**
> 3. **Not stating the quasi-steady state assumption** explicitly. **−1 mark.**
> 4. **Confusing dilution rate $D$ in CSTR** with **feed rate $F$ in fed-batch**. They are not the same — in fed-batch, $D(t) = F(t) / V(t)$ is time-varying, and there is no outflow.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (read this 5 minutes before your KTU exam):**

- ✅ A **bioreactor is a unit operation device** whose function is to **convert substrates into biological products** under controlled conditions.
- ✅ The **three pillars** of bioreactor function are: **(i) Optimal Physicochemical Environment, (ii) Efficient Mass Transfer, (iii) Containment & Sterility**.
- ✅ The **Two-Film Theory** governs oxygen transfer: $\text{OTR} = k_L a (C^* - C_L)$.
- ✅ At steady state in a CSTR, $\mu = D$ (chemostat principle).
- ✅ **Washout** occurs when $D > D_c = \mu_{\max} \cdot S_{\text{in}} / (K_s + S_{\text{in}})$.
- ✅ **Fed-batch reactors** are the industrial standard for **recombinant proteins** because they avoid catabolite repression.
- ✅ **Continuous reactors** maximize productivity but risk washout and persistent contamination.
- ✅ **Sterilization** is done at **121°C / 15 psi for 15–20 min** (or calculated via $\theta = e^{k_d t}$).
- ✅ **Power number** $\text{Np}$ is dimensionless and depends on impeller type and turbulence.
- ✅ The **cooling jacket** must remove **10–25 kW/m³** of metabolic heat in high-density cultures.
- ✅ **Process Control** uses **PID loops** for **pH, DO, temperature, and foam**.
- ✅ **Reynolds Number > 10,000** indicates fully turbulent mixing in the reactor.
- ✅ A **bioreactor is more than a vessel** — it is a *system* of sensors, controllers, and unit operations working in concert.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do not** confuse **OTR (Oxygen Transfer Rate)** with **OUR (Oxygen Uptake Rate)**. They are equal *only at steady state*.
> - **Do not** use $k_L$ and $k_L a$ interchangeably — $k_L$ has units of m/s, $k_L a$ has units of s⁻¹.
> - **Do not** forget to convert units — KTU examiners frequently trap students with **mg/L vs g/L**, **min vs h**, and **L vs m³** mismatches.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advantages and Limitations of Stirred Tank
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT526 - BIOREACTOR DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Advantages and Limitations of Stirred Tank

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Stirred Tank Bioreactor (STB)**, also known as a **Continuously Stirred Tank Bioreactor (CSTR)** when operated in continuous mode or a **Batch Stirred Tank Reactor (BSTR)** when operated in batch mode, is a cylindrical vessel equipped with a mechanical agitator (impeller) and associated utilities (sparger, baffles, sensors, jackets/coils) used to cultivate microorganisms, animal cells, or plant cells under tightly controlled environmental conditions. It is the **workhorse of the bioprocess industry** and constitutes the most widely used bioreactor configuration for aerobic, anaerobic, and microaerobic fermentations.

Mathematically, an ideal STB is characterized by the **mixing time ($\tau_m$)**, the **power number ($N_p$)**, the **Reynolds number ($Re$)**, and the **volumetric mass-transfer coefficient ($k_L a$)**, which together govern its hydrodynamic and biological performance.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The stirred tank is introduced as the *baseline reference bioreactor*. Every alternative configuration (airlift, bubble column, packed bed, fluidized bed) is benchmarked against the stirred tank in terms of mixing, mass transfer, shear, and energy efficiency.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Think of a stirred tank like a home kitchen mixer-grinder with a thermometer and an air pump attached.**
> - The **vessel** is the jar.
> - The **impeller** is the rotating blade that mixes the contents.
> - The **sparger** is the air inlet that supplies oxygen, just like a straw bubbling air into a fish tank.
> - The **baffles** are the internal ridges inside the jar that prevent the liquid from simply swirling without mixing.
> - The **jacket/coil** is the outer water bath that keeps the temperature steady.
> - The **sensors (pH, DO, temperature)** are the smart probes that constantly monitor the recipe.
>
> The impeller keeps everything uniformly mixed, ensuring that every microorganism experiences the same temperature, pH, dissolved oxygen, and nutrient concentration. Without agitation, microbes near the surface would get excess oxygen while those at the bottom would starve — analogous to unevenly stirred curry.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Reference Metrics

- Standard impeller speed range: **50 – 3000 rpm** (lab to industrial).
- Typical power input: **0.5 – 10 kW/m³** of broth.
- Reference impeller-to-tank diameter ratio: **$D/T = 1/3$** (Rushton standard).
- Liquid height-to-tank diameter: **$H/T = 1$** (geometric standard).
- Standard impeller types: **Rushton disc turbine, pitched-blade, marine propeller, Scaba, hydrofoil**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why stirred tanks dominate the industry:** About **70–80 % of all industrial bioprocesses** (antibiotics, enzymes, recombinant proteins, vaccines, organic acids) use stirred tank reactors. Mastery of this configuration is therefore non-negotiable for any bioprocess engineer.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Comprehensive Advantages of Stirred Tank Bioreactors

### A. Mixing and Mass-Transfer Advantages
1. **Excellent Bulk Mixing:** Mechanical agitation achieves high **Reynolds numbers ($Re > 10^4$)**, producing turbulent flow that homogenizes the broth within seconds.
2. **High Volumetric Mass-Transfer Coefficient ($k_L a$):** Typical values of **$k_L a = 100$ – $1000 \text{ h}^{-1}$** are achievable — critical for aerobic cultures with high oxygen demand.
3. **Efficient Heat Transfer:** Jacketed walls and internal coils give high surface area for **heat removal**, allowing precise temperature control (within $\pm 0.1 \,^{\circ}\text{C}$).
4. **Effective Solid Suspension:** Three- or four-blade impellers can suspend even dense solids (e.g., cell aggregates, immobilized enzymes) at well-defined **just-suspended speed ($N_{js}$)**.

### B. Operational Flexibility
5. **Multi-Mode Operation:** The same vessel can run as a **batch, fed-batch, or continuous (chemostat)** system with only a change in feed/withdrawal policy.
6. **Wide Viscosity Range:** With appropriate impeller selection, the same design can handle low-viscosity (water-like, $\mu \approx 1 \, \text{mPa·s}$) to highly viscous broths (filamentous fermentations, $\mu \approx 10^3 \, \text{mPa·s}$).
7. **Easy Scale-Up / Scale-Down:** Established geometric similarity rules (constant $P/V$, constant $N \cdot D$, constant $k_L a$) allow predictable scale translation from 1 L lab to 200 m³ industrial.

### C. Monitoring and Control
8. **Real-Time Sensor Integration:** Ports for **pH, DO, temperature, foam, OD, glucose, and off-gas ($O_2$, $CO_2$)** sensors are standard.
9. **Easy Sampling and Feed Addition:** Multiple ports for aseptic sampling, substrate feeding, antifoam addition, and base/acid pH control.
10. **Sterilization Friendly:** Designed for **in-situ sterilization (SIP)** at 121 °C / 30 min in place.

### D. Economic and Engineering Advantages
11. **Capital Cost Lower** than equivalent photobioreactors, membrane bioreactors, or perfusion systems.
12. **Proven Technology:** Decades of validated design correlations (e.g., **Rushton, van't Riet, Wang**) make it a low-risk choice.
13. **Vendor Availability:** Standardized vessels (Sartorius, Eppendorf, Applikon, ABEC) are commercially off-the-shelf.

## 2.2 Comprehensive Limitations of Stirred Tank Bioreactors

### A. Mechanical / Shear-Related Limitations
1. **High Shear Stress:** Rushton turbines generate **high local shear rates ($10^3$ – $10^5 \, \text{s}^{-1}$)** at the impeller tip, which damages shear-sensitive cells (**animal cells, plant cells, filamentous microbes**).
2. **Mechanical Seal Failure:** Agitator shaft penetration is the **most common contamination route** in large-scale STBs.
3. **Foaming and Flooding:** Vigorous agitation disperses gas into fine bubbles, leading to **excessive foam** that can foul exhaust filters.

### B. Energy and Heat Limitations
4. **High Power Consumption:** Industrial-scale STBs consume **$P/V = 1$ – $5 \, \text{kW/m}^3$**, which becomes a major operating cost.
5. **Heat Removal Bottleneck:** At large scale ($V > 50 \, \text{m}^3$), the **surface-to-volume ratio decreases**, making temperature control difficult in highly exothermic fermentations.

### C. Biological / Process Limitations
6. **Non-Ideal Mixing at Large Scale:** The **mixing time scales as** $\tau_m \propto (T/D)^{1.7}$ — meaning large vessels develop **substrate, pH, and DO gradients** that cause metabolic heterogeneity.
7. **Shear-Induced Morphological Changes:** Filamentous microorganisms change from **pellets → dispersed hyphae** under high shear, altering product titres.
8. **Cell Damage in Recombinant Cultures:** Mechanical agitation + sparging causes **cell lysis and plasmid loss** in fragile recombinant strains.

### D. Scale-Up Limitations
9. **Unreliable Scale-Up of Shear:** Shear-sensitive cultures (e.g., **CHO cells for monoclonal antibodies**) often fail when scaled up from bench to production scale.
10. **Capital-Intensive Upgrades:** Switching from stainless to single-use systems, or installing additional Rushton turbines, requires major retrofitting.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Misconception:** Students often list "good mixing" and "homogeneous broth" as benefits without noting the **consequence of scale-up**, where large stirred tanks are in fact *not* ideally mixed. The phrase "CSTR assumes perfect mixing" is a model assumption, not a physical reality at industrial scale.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Typical Range / Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Power Number (dimensionless) | $N_p = \dfrac{P}{\rho \cdot N^3 \cdot D^5}$ | 0.5 – 6.0 (Rushton, turbulent) | Impeller power demand |
| 2 | Impeller Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \cdot N \cdot D^2}{\mu}$ | $> 10^4$ turbulent; $< 10$ laminar | Flow regime classification |
| 3 | Power in Turbulent Regime | $P = N_p \cdot \rho \cdot N^3 \cdot D^5$ | kW | Energy audit |
| 4 | Power in Laminar Regime | $P = K_p \cdot \mu \cdot N^2 \cdot D^3$ | kW | Viscous broth design |
| 5 | Power per Unit Volume | $(P/V) = N_p \cdot \rho \cdot N^3 \cdot D^5 / V$ | 0.5 – 10 kW/m³ | Scale-up criterion |
| 6 | Mixing Time Correlation (Rushton) | $\tau_m \cdot N = k \cdot (D/T)^{-2.0} \cdot Re^{-0.1}$ | s | Predicting homogeneity |
| 7 | Volumetric Mass-Transfer Coefficient (van't Riet) | $k_L a = K \cdot (P/V)^{0.4} \cdot (v_s)^{0.5}$ | $h^{-1}$ | Oxygen transfer design |
| 8 | Superficial Gas Velocity | $v_s = \dfrac{Q_g}{A_c}$ | 0.01 – 0.10 m/s | Aeration parameter |
| 9 | Just-Suspended Speed (Zwietering) | $N_{js} = S \cdot \nu^{0.1} \cdot d_p^{0.2} \cdot (g \cdot \Delta \rho / \rho)^{0.45} \cdot X^{0.13} \cdot D^{-0.85}$ | rpm | Solid suspension design |
| 10 | Tip Speed (Shear indicator) | $v_{tip} = \pi \cdot N \cdot D$ | 1 – 25 m/s | Cell-damage screening |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on notation:** All symbols follow **Doran (Bioprocess Engineering Principles, 2nd ed.)** convention. $\rho$ = broth density (kg/m³), $\mu$ = viscosity (Pa·s), $N$ = impeller speed (rps), $D$ = impeller diameter (m), $T$ = tank diameter (m), $Q_g$ = gas flow rate (m³/s), $A_c$ = cross-sectional area (m²), $X$ = solids fraction, $d_p$ = particle diameter, $S$ = Zwietering constant.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Stirred tank correlations are used daily in:
- **Pharmaceutical industry:** Designing CHO-cell culture vessels for monoclonal antibody production.
- **Enzyme industry:** Selecting impeller type for cellulase or protease fermentations.
- **Wastewater treatment:** Sizing activated-sludge aeration tanks.
- **Food industry:** Designing yeast, lactic-acid, and beer fermentors.
- **Bioprocess scale-up:** Translating lab data to 50 m³, 200 m³, and 500 m³ production reactors.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Power Number ($N_p$)

The power drawn by an impeller in a baffled agitated tank can be derived using **dimensional analysis** (Buckingham $\pi$ theorem).

### Step 1 — Identify Governing Variables

The power $P$ delivered by the impeller is a function of the following physical quantities:

$$P = f(\rho, \mu, N, D, g)$$

where:
- $P$ = power (W)
- $\rho$ = broth density (kg/m³)
- $\mu$ = broth viscosity (Pa·s)
- $N$ = impeller rotational speed (1/s)
- $D$ = impeller diameter (m)
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration (m/s²) — relevant only when vortexing or solid suspension occurs

### Step 2 — List the Fundamental Dimensions

We have **5 variables** and **3 fundamental dimensions** ($M, L, T$); hence by the Buckingham $\pi$ theorem, the number of dimensionless $\pi$-groups is:

$$k = n - r = 5 - 3 = 2$$

The core $\pi$-group uses $N, D, \rho$ as repeating variables (since they collectively span $M, L, T$).

### Step 3 — Form the First $\pi$-Group ($\pi_1$ for Power)

$$\pi_1 = \dfrac{P}{\rho^{a} \cdot N^{b} \cdot D^{c}}$$

Dimensionally:

$$[M L^2 T^{-3}] = [M L^{-3}]^{a} \cdot [T^{-1}]^{b} \cdot [L]^{c}$$

Equating powers of $M, L, T$:
- $M: 1 = a \implies a = 1$
- $T: -3 = -b \implies b = 3$
- $L: 2 = -3a + c = -3 + c \implies c = 5$

Therefore:

$$\pi_1 = \dfrac{P}{\rho \cdot N^3 \cdot D^5} \equiv N_p \quad \text{(Power Number)}$$

### Step 4 — Form the Second $\pi$-Group ($\pi_2$ for Viscosity)

$$\pi_2 = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho^{a} \cdot N^{b} \cdot D^{c}}$$

Dimensionally:

$$[M L^{-1} T^{-1}] = [M L^{-3}]^{a} \cdot [T^{-1}]^{b} \cdot [L]^{c}$$

Equating powers:
- $M: 1 = a \implies a = 1$
- $T: -1 = -b \implies b = 1$
- $L: -1 = -3 + c \implies c = 2$

Therefore:

$$\pi_2 = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho \cdot N \cdot D^2} \equiv \dfrac{1}{Re} \quad \text{(inverse Reynolds number)}$$

### Step 5 — Combine the Functional Relationship

The final dimensionless correlation is:

$$\boxed{N_p = \dfrac{P}{\rho \cdot N^3 \cdot D^5} = f(Re, Fr)}$$

where $Fr = N^2 D / g$ is the **Froude number**, which accounts for vortexing.

For a **fully baffled tank** (Fr effect negligible, $Fr \to \infty$):

$$\boxed{N_p = f(Re)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The power number expresses the ratio of **resistance force to inertia force** at the impeller. It is essentially a **drag coefficient for an agitator**. At high Reynolds numbers ($Re > 10^4$), $N_p$ becomes constant for a given impeller geometry — this is the basis of the **Rushton power-number chart**.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Estimating Power for an Industrial STB

**Problem:** A stirred tank bioreactor with $D = 0.5 \, \text{m}$, $N = 2.0 \, \text{s}^{-1}$ (120 rpm), is used to culture *E. coli*. The broth density is $\rho = 1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3$ and viscosity $\mu = 1 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{Pa·s}$. The Rushton impeller has $N_p = 5.0$ in the turbulent regime. Calculate (a) Reynolds number, (b) Power drawn, (c) Power per unit volume if the tank has $V = 1.0 \, \text{m}^3$.

### (a) Reynolds Number

$$Re = \dfrac{\rho \cdot N \cdot D^2}{\mu} = \dfrac{1000 \times 2.0 \times (0.5)^2}{1 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$Re = \dfrac{1000 \times 2.0 \times 0.25}{0.001} = \dfrac{500}{0.001} = 5.0 \times 10^{5}$$

Since $Re \gg 10^4$, the flow is fully **turbulent**, validating the use of $N_p = 5.0$.

### (b) Power Drawn

$$P = N_p \cdot \rho \cdot N^3 \cdot D^5$$

$$P = 5.0 \times 1000 \times (2.0)^3 \times (0.5)^5$$

$$P = 5.0 \times 1000 \times 8.0 \times 0.03125$$

$$P = 5.0 \times 250 = 1250 \, \text{W} = 1.25 \, \text{kW}$$

### (c) Power per Unit Volume

$$\dfrac{P}{V} = \dfrac{1250 \, \text{W}}{1.0 \, \text{m}^3} = 1.25 \, \text{kW/m}^3$$

This value lies within the typical industrial range of **$0.5$ – $10 \, \text{kW/m}^3$** for aerobic fermentations, confirming the design.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Advantages vs Limitations vs Mitigation

| Aspect | Advantage | Limitation | Typical Engineering Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mixing | Homogenization in seconds | Mixing time increases with $T^2$ | Use multiple Rushton turbines |
| Mass Transfer | $k_L a$ up to $1000 \, h^{-1}$ | High $P/V$ needed for high demand | Use pitched-blade + porous sparger |
| Shear | Turbulent flow ensures uniformity | Damages animal/plant cells | Switch to airlift or wave bioreactor |
| Heat Transfer | Jacket + coil control | Limited at large $V$ | Internal helical coils |
| Energy | Proven, scalable | High operating cost | Variable-frequency drives (VFD) |
| Sterilization | SIP compatible | Mechanical seal contamination risk | Magnetic-coupled agitators |
| Cost | Low CAPEX (stainless) | High OPEX (energy, cleaning) | Single-use (SUB) alternatives |
| Scale-Up | Well-defined rules | Non-ideal at large $V$ | Multiple injection points; PAT sensors |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram of a Stirred Tank Bioreactor

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **functional architecture** of a stirred tank bioreactor, mapping the major subsystems, sensors, and control loops.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph VESSEL["STIRRED TANK VESSEL"]
        TANK[Tank Body with Jacket]
        IMPELLER[Motor Driven Impeller Shaft]
        RUSHTON[Rushton Disc Turbine]
        BAFFLES[Four Vertical Baffles]
        SPARGER[Ring Sparger at Bottom]
        COIL[Internal Heat Transfer Coil]
    end

    subgraph FEED["FEED AND UTILITIES"]
        MEDIA[Sterile Media Reservoir]
        AIR[Filtered Air Supply]
        ACID[Acid Pump for pH Control]
        BASE[Base Pump for pH Control]
        FOAM[Antifoam Reservoir]
    end

    subgraph SENSORS["INLINE SENSORS AND MONITORING"]
        PH[pH Probe]
        DO[Dissolved Oxygen Probe]
        TEMP[Temperature Sensor]
        FOAMSEN[Foam Level Sensor]
        OD[Optical Density Probe]
        OFFGAS[Off Gas CO2 and O2 Analyzer]
    end

    subgraph CONTROL["CONTROL SYSTEM DCS OR PLC"]
        CTRL[Bioreactor Controller]
        REC[Data Recorder]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT STREAMS"]
        EXHAUST[Exhaust Gas to Condenser]
        HARVEST[Harvest Line to Downstream]
    end

    MEDIA -->|Feed| TANK
    AIR -->|0.2 micron filter| SPARGER
    SPARGER -->|Gas Dispersion| TANK
    IMPELLER -->|Mechanical Drive| RUSHTON
    RUSHTON -->|Turbulent Mixing| TANK
    BAFFLES -->|Prevent Vortexing| TANK
    COIL -->|Temperature Regulation| TANK
    ACID -->|pH Correction| TANK
    BASE -->|pH Correction| TANK
    FOAM -->|Foam Suppression| TANK

    PH --> CTRL
    DO --> CTRL
    TEMP --> CTRL
    FOAMSEN --> CTRL
    OD --> CTRL
    OFFGAS --> CTRL

    CTRL -->|Set Points| IMPELLER
    CTRL -->|Valve Actuation| AIR
    CTRL -->|Pump Speed| ACID
    CTRL -->|Pump Speed| BASE
    CTRL -->|Logging| REC

    TANK --> EXHAUST
    TANK --> HARVEST
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Process-Flow Map of Advantages, Limitations and Mitigation Strategies

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Stirred Tank Bioreactor STB] --> B[Key Advantages]
    A --> C[Key Limitations]
    A --> D[Engineering Mitigation]

    B --> B1[High kLa]
    B --> B2[Flexible Operation]
    B --> B3[Scalable]
    B --> B4[Sterilizable in Place]
    B --> B5[Sensor Rich PAT Ready]

    C --> C1[High Shear Stress]
    C --> C2[High Energy Cost]
    C --> C3[Scale Up Gradients]
    C --> C4[Mechanical Seal Risk]
    C --> C5[Foaming]

    D --> D1[Use Pitched Blade for Shear Sensitive Cultures]
    D --> D2[Use VFD and Aeration Optimisation]
    D --> D3[Multiple Impellers and Feed Points]
    D --> D4[Magnetic Coupled Agitator]
    D --> D5[Mechanical Foam Breaker plus Antifoam]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Decision Tree — When to Choose a Stirred Tank vs Alternative

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Choose Bioreactor Type] --> Q1{Is the culture shear sensitive?}
    Q1 -- Yes Animal or Plant Cells --> AIRLIFT[Airlift or Wave Bioreactor]
    Q1 -- No Robust Microbial Culture --> Q2{Is viscosity very high above 10 Pa s?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> HELICAL[Helical Impeller in Stirred Tank]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Is oxygen demand extremely high above 10 g per L h?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> STB[Stirred Tank with Multiple Rushton Impellers]
    Q3 -- No --> STB2[Standard Stirred Tank Bioreactor]
    HELICAL --> STB
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the diagrams:** The arrows indicate physical flow (liquids, gases) or data flow (sensor → controller). The subgraph clusters group related components (e.g., VESSEL, SENSORS) and improve readability of the Mermaid chart.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]

**List any six advantages of a stirred tank bioreactor.**

**Model Answer (6 points, each 0.5 mark):**
1. Excellent bulk mixing and high $k_L a$ for oxygen transfer.
2. Precise temperature control through jacketed walls and internal coils.
3. Operational flexibility: can run batch, fed-batch, or continuous mode.
4. Real-time monitoring via pH, DO, temperature, foam, and off-gas sensors.
5. Easy scale-up using well-established geometric similarity rules.
6. In-situ sterilization (SIP) at 121 °C / 30 min, minimizing contamination risk.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Mention any six limitations of a stirred tank bioreactor.**

**Model Answer (6 points, each 0.5 mark):**
1. High power consumption ($P/V$ up to 5 kW/m³) leading to high operating cost.
2. High shear stress damages shear-sensitive cells (CHO, plant, filamentous microbes).
3. Mechanical seal penetration is the primary contamination route in large vessels.
4. Foaming and gas flooding at high impeller speeds.
5. Non-ideal mixing and DO/pH gradients at industrial scale.
6. Heat-removal bottleneck as $V$ increases (lower area-to-volume ratio).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**A stirred tank bioreactor with the following specifications is used for an industrial *E. coli* fermentation. The Rushton turbine has $D = 0.6 \, \text{m}$, rotational speed $N = 3.0 \, \text{s}^{-1}$ (180 rpm), tank diameter $T = 1.8 \, \text{m}$, broth density $\rho = 1050 \, \text{kg/m}^3$, viscosity $\mu = 2 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{Pa·s}$, and the power number in the turbulent regime is $N_p = 5.5$.**
**The tank is filled to $V = 4.5 \, \text{m}^3$.**

**Answer the following:**

**(a)** Calculate the impeller Reynolds number, identify the flow regime, and compute the power drawn by the impeller. Discuss whether the design is suitable for an aerobic culture. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Enumerate the major advantages and limitations of stirred tank bioreactors. For each limitation, propose one engineering mitigation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Calculate Reynolds Number:**

$$Re = \dfrac{\rho \cdot N \cdot D^2}{\mu} = \dfrac{1050 \times 3.0 \times (0.6)^2}{2 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$Re = \dfrac{1050 \times 3.0 \times 0.36}{0.002} = \dfrac{1134}{0.002} = 5.67 \times 10^{5}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the Flow Regime:**

Since $Re = 5.67 \times 10^5 \gg 10^4$, the flow is **fully turbulent**. The use of $N_p = 5.5$ (a turbulent-regime constant for a standard Rushton disc turbine) is **justified**.

**Step 3 — Compute the Power Drawn:**

$$P = N_p \cdot \rho \cdot N^3 \cdot D^5$$

$$P = 5.5 \times 1050 \times (3.0)^3 \times (0.6)^5$$

Compute each factor:
- $N^3 = 27.0$
- $D^5 = (0.6)^5 = 0.6^2 \cdot 0.6^2 \cdot 0.6 = 0.36 \times 0.36 \times 0.6 = 0.07776$
- $N_p \cdot \rho = 5.5 \times 1050 = 5775$

$$P = 5775 \times 27.0 \times 0.07776$$

$$P = 5775 \times 2.0995 = 12{,}124.6 \, \text{W} \approx 12.1 \, \text{kW}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $P/V$:**

$$\dfrac{P}{V} = \dfrac{12{,}124.6}{4.5} = 2694.4 \, \text{W/m}^3 \approx 2.69 \, \text{kW/m}^3$$

**Step 5 — Suitability Discussion:**

A $P/V$ of $2.69 \, \text{kW/m}^3$ falls comfortably within the **$0.5$ – $10 \, \text{kW/m}^3$** range recommended for aerobic *E. coli* fermentations, indicating the design is **suitable** for high oxygen-demand cultures.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

| # | Advantage | Limitation | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Excellent bulk mixing, high $k_L a$ | High shear damages animal/plant cells | Switch to pitched-blade or airlift for sensitive cultures |
| 2 | Precise T, pH, DO control | High energy consumption ($P/V \sim$ kW/m³) | Use VFDs; optimize $N$ for $k_L a$ |
| 3 | Multi-mode (batch/fed-batch/continuous) | Mixing gradients at large $V$ | Multi-impeller design + multi-feed points |
| 4 | In-situ sterilization | Mechanical seal contamination | Magnetic-coupled (bottom-drive) agitator |
| 5 | Sensor-rich (PAT-compatible) | Foaming and gas flooding | Mechanical foam breaker + antifoam dosing |
| 6 | Well-established scale-up | Heat removal bottleneck | Internal helical coil; external half-coil jacket |

**Conclusion:** Despite its limitations, the stirred tank remains the most versatile and economically viable bioreactor for the majority of industrial bioprocesses, provided that the limitations are addressed through targeted engineering interventions.

---

### Question B — Option 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**A biopharmaceutical company is evaluating stirred tank bioreactors (STB) and airlift bioreactors (ALB) for cultivating mammalian CHO cells for monoclonal antibody (mAb) production.**

**Answer the following:**

**(a)** Compare the shear environment in STB and ALB. Why are STBs considered less suitable for shear-sensitive CHO cultures at large scale? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the power number and impeller tip speed for an STB operating at $N = 1.5 \, \text{s}^{-1}$, $D = 0.4 \, \text{m}$, $\rho = 1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3$, $N_p = 4.5$. Comment on whether this tip speed is safe for CHO cells. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Shear in STB:**
- STBs use **mechanical impellers** that generate **high local shear rates**, especially near the impeller tips, in the discharge stream, and at the sparger.
- Estimated local shear rates: $\dot{\gamma} \sim 10^3$ – $10^5 \, \text{s}^{-1}$.
- CHO cells lack a protective cell wall and are highly sensitive to shear; values above $\sim 1$ – $3 \, \text{N/m}^2$ (shear stress) or $\sim 2$ – $5 \, \text{m/s}$ (tip speed) can damage the cell membrane, cause **apoptosis, and reduce mAb titre**.
- The non-ideal flow at large scale also produces **intermittent high-shear zones** and **low-DO dead zones**, both detrimental to CHO cells.

**Shear in ALB:**
- ALBs have **no mechanical agitator**; mixing is achieved by **buoyancy-driven recirculation** of gas-liquid dispersion.
- Shear is therefore **low and uniform**, with $\dot{\gamma} \sim 50$ – $500 \, \text{s}^{-1}$.
- This is well-tolerated by CHO cells and is a major reason why ALBs and **wave/orbital-shaken bioreactors** are preferred for animal-cell cultures.

**Conclusion:** STBs are less suitable for CHO cultures at large scale because of the impeller-induced shear tip stress, cavitation at the sparger, and the difficulty of scaling up low-shear conditions from bench to production.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Calculate the Power Number (re-confirm):**

$$N_p = 4.5 \quad \text{(given, turbulent regime)}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the Power Drawn:**

$$P = N_p \cdot \rho \cdot N^3 \cdot D^5$$

$$P = 4.5 \times 1000 \times (1.5)^3 \times (0.4)^5$$

- $N^3 = 3.375$
- $D^5 = (0.4)^5 = 0.4^2 \times 0.4^2 \times 0.4 = 0.16 \times 0.16 \times 0.4 = 0.01024$

$$P = 4.5 \times 1000 \times 3.375 \times 0.01024$$

$$P = 4500 \times 0.03456 = 155.5 \, \text{W} \approx 0.156 \, \text{kW}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the Impeller Tip Speed:**

$$v_{tip} = \pi \cdot N \cdot D = \pi \times 1.5 \times 0.4$$

$$v_{tip} = 3.1416 \times 0.6 = 1.885 \, \text{m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Comment on CHO Cell Safety:**

For CHO cells, the **critical tip speed** is generally considered to be in the range of **$1.5$ – $2.5 \, \text{m/s}$** (above which cell damage becomes significant). The calculated $v_{tip} = 1.885 \, \text{m/s}$ lies **at the upper end of the acceptable range**, indicating that:

- This STB is **borderline safe** for CHO cells.
- Long-term cultivation may still cause **gradual cell damage** and reduced viability.
- **Mitigation options** include switching to a **marine propeller or pitched-blade impeller** (which has a much lower $N_p$ in the turbulent regime, typically 1.3 – 1.5) or moving to an **airlift / wave bioreactor** for sustained CHO culture.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks in stirred-tank questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the geometric constant $D/T$:** Many answers compute $P$ but ignore that $D/T$ must be $\sim 1/3$ for the Rushton chart to apply.
> 2. **Mixing up $N_p$ definitions:** Some texts define $N_p = P / (\rho N^3 D^5)$ (used here); others use $P / (N^3 D^5 \rho)$ (same thing, but watch for sign errors).
> 3. **Assuming $Re > 10^4$ without proof:** Always *calculate and show* $Re$ before claiming turbulent flow. KTU examiners explicitly check this.
> 4. **Stating "homogeneous" without quantifying:** Saying "the broth is well-mixed" without referencing $\tau_m$, $k_L a$, or mixing-time correlation is incomplete.
> 5. **Confusing scale-up criteria:** Students often write "scale up at constant $P/V$" without mentioning that **other valid criteria** exist (constant $k_L a$, constant tip speed, constant $N \cdot D$, constant $\tau_m$) and the choice depends on the *rate-limiting step*.
> 6. **Forgetting to convert rpm → rps:** $N$ must always be in **revolutions per second** in SI units, not rpm. A factor of **60** error = full-mark loss.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (must-memorize for KTU 2024 ESE):**
> - **STB definition:** cylindrical vessel + mechanical impeller + sparger + baffles + sensors.
> - **Standard geometry:** $D/T = 1/3$, $H/T = 1$, four baffles, $W_b = T/10$.
> - **Power number formula:** $N_p = P / (\rho N^3 D^5)$; **turbulent limit** for Rushton = 5.0 – 6.0.
> - **Reynolds number:** $Re = \rho N D^2 / \mu$; **turbulent if $Re > 10^4$**.
> - **$P/V$ typical:** $0.5$ – $10 \, \text{kW/m}^3$ for aerobic cultures.
> - **$k_L a$ (van't Riet):** $k_L a = K \cdot (P/V)^{0.4} \cdot v_s^{0.5}$.
> - **Tip speed limit for CHO:** $v_{tip} < 2 \, \text{m/s}$ (ideally).
> - **Five top advantages:** good mixing, high $k_L a$, sensor-rich, scalable, SIP-compatible.
> - **Five top limitations:** shear, energy, gradients, sealing, foaming.
> - **Scale-up criteria:** constant $P/V$, constant $k_L a$, constant $N \cdot D$ (tip speed), constant $\tau_m$.
> - **Mitigation of shear:** use pitched-blade / marine propeller / helical; move to airlift or wave.
> - **Mitigation of energy cost:** VFD, demand-based aeration, multi-impeller optimization.
> - **Mixing-time correlation (Rushton):** $\tau_m N = f(D/T, Re)$.
> - **Just-suspended speed (Zwietering):** needed when solid suspension is the design goal.
> - **Mechanical seal:** primary contamination route → use magnetic-coupled bottom-drive for sterility.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
