# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
> *Define a Trickle Bed Bioreactor. List any three of its industrial applications.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Trickle Bed Bioreactor is a three-phase packed-bed bioreactor in which a liquid phase (nutrient medium) trickles downward over a stationary solid support while a gas phase (air/oxygen) flows concurrently or counter-currently, with cells immobilized as a biofilm on the support surface. **Applications (any 3, 1 mark each):** (i) aerobic wastewater treatment for COD/BOD removal, (ii) off-gas biofiltration for VOC abatement, (iii) drinking water denitrification, (iv) catalytic oxidation of priority pollutants.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
> *What is meant by the term "flocculated cell bioreactor"? How does it differ from a typical CSTR in terms of cell retention?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Flocculated Cell Bioreactor uses microbial cells that naturally aggregate (or are induced to aggregate) into macroscopic flocs via extracellular polymeric substances (EPS) and divalent cations, enabling rapid gravitational settling. **Difference from a CSTR (2 marks):** In a conventional CSTR, biomass wash-out limits $\tau_c \leq \tau$ and forces operation at low $X$; in an FCB, an integrated sedimentation zone recycles settled flocs, decoupling $\tau_c$ from $\tau$ so that $\tau_c \gg \tau$, allowing $X$ values of 20–80 g/L — typically 5–10× higher than a CSTR.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model, Question Choice A]**

**(a) [7 marks]** *Derive the Ergun equation for pressure drop across a packed bed. Clearly state the assumptions and the physical meaning of each term.*

**(b) [7 marks]** *A TBB is used for toluene biofiltration. Given $d_p = 4$ mm, $\varepsilon = 0.45$, $\mu = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s, $\rho = 1.20$ kg/m³, $u = 0.20$ m/s, $H = 2.0$ m, calculate the total pressure drop and comment on whether the bed is in laminar, transitional, or turbulent flow regime.*

---

### Model Solution to Q3(a) — Ergun Derivation

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. Uniform spherical particles.
2. Steady-state, isothermal flow.
3. Single-phase (gas-only or liquid-only) flow.
4. Newtonian fluid.

**Derivation (5 marks):**

**Step 1:** Define bed porosity $\varepsilon = V_{voids}/V_{total}$. The interstitial velocity is $u_i = u/\varepsilon$.

**Step 2:** Hydraulic diameter of void channels:
$$d_h = \frac{2\varepsilon d_p}{3(1-\varepsilon)}$$

**Step 3:** Viscous loss from Hagen–Poiseuille:
$$\left(\frac{\Delta P}{L}\right)_{visc} = \frac{32 \mu u_i}{d_h^2} = \frac{72(1-\varepsilon)^2 \mu u}{\varepsilon^3 d_p^2}$$

**Step 4:** Apply Kozeny–Carman correction (×2.08 empirical):
$$\left(\frac{\Delta P}{L}\right)_{visc} = \frac{150(1-\varepsilon)^2 \mu u}{\varepsilon^3 d_p^2}$$

**Step 5:** Kinetic loss from drag-blanket:
$$\left(\frac{\Delta P}{L}\right)_{kin} = \frac{1.75(1-\varepsilon) \rho u^2}{\varepsilon^3 d_p}$$

**Step 6:** Total:
$$\frac{\Delta P}{L} = \frac{150(1-\varepsilon)^2 \mu u}{\varepsilon^3 d_p^2} + \frac{1.75(1-\varepsilon) \rho u^2}{\varepsilon^3 d_p}$$

**Physical meaning (1 mark):** First term dominates at low Re_p (viscous flow); second term dominates at high Re_p (inertial flow). Valid for $Re_p \in [0.001, 1000]$.

---

### Model Solution to Q3(b) — Pressure Drop Calculation

**Step 1 — Compute Reynolds number:**
$$Re_p = \frac{\rho u d_p}{\mu(1-\varepsilon)} = \frac{1.20 \times 0.20 \times 0.004}{1.8 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.55} = \frac{9.6 \times 10^{-4}}{9.9 \times 10^{-6}} = 96.97$$

**Step 2 — Viscous term:**
$$\frac{150 \times (0.55)^2 \times 1.8 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.20}{(0.45)^3 \times (0.004)^2} = \frac{150 \times 0.3025 \times 1.8 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.20}{0.091125 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{1.6335 \times 10^{-4}}{1.458 \times 10^{-6}} = 112.04\;\text{Pa/m}$$

**Step 3 — Kinetic term:**
$$\frac{1.75 \times 0.55 \times 1.20 \times (0.20)^2}{(0.45)^3 \times 0.004} = \frac{1.75 \times 0.55 \times 1.20 \times 0.04}{0.091125 \times 0.004} = \frac{0.0462}{3.645 \times 10^{-4}} = 126.75\;\text{Pa/m}$$

**Step 4 — Total per unit length:**
$$\frac{\Delta P}{L} = 112.04 + 126.75 = 238.79\;\text{Pa/m}$$

**Step 5 — Total across 2.0 m:**
$$\Delta P = 238.79 \times 2.0 = 477.58\;\text{Pa} \approx 478\;\text{Pa}$$

**Step 6 — Flow regime (1 mark):** $Re_p \approx 97$ is in the **transitional regime** (Ergun valid for $Re_p < 1000$). The viscous and kinetic terms are comparable, which is characteristic of $Re_p \approx 100$.

---

> **Q4. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model, Question Choice B]**

**(a) [7 marks]** *Derive an expression for the biofilm effectiveness factor for a first-order reaction in a planar biofilm of thickness $L_b$. Discuss the limiting cases of $\phi \to 0$ and $\phi \to \infty$.*

**(b) [7 marks]** *A Flocculated Cell Bioreactor operates at $\tau = 3$ h, $X = 40$ g/L, and $X_e = 0.5$ g/L. Calculate (i) the cell concentration factor, (ii) the cell retention time, and (iii) the wash-out dilution rate if $\mu_{max} = 0.35$ h⁻¹ and $K_S = 0.20$ g/L with $S_0 = 5$ g/L and $S = 0.10$ g/L.*

---

### Model Solution to Q4(a) — Biofilm Effectiveness Factor

**Assumptions (1 mark):** Steady-state, 1-D diffusion, first-order kinetics, constant $D_{eff}$.

**Derivation (5 marks):**

**Step 1:** Governing ODE: $D_{eff} \dfrac{d^2 S}{dx^2} = k S$ for $0 \le x \le L_b$.

**Step 2:** Boundary conditions:
$$\left.\frac{dS}{dx}\right|_{x=0} = 0, \quad S(L_b) = S_s$$

**Step 3:** General solution: $S(x) = C_1 \cosh(\lambda x) + C_2 \sinh(\lambda x)$ with $\lambda = \sqrt{k/D_{eff}}$.

**Step 4:** Apply $x=0$ BC: $C_2 = 0$. So $S(x) = C_1 \cosh(\lambda x)$.

**Step 5:** Apply $x = L_b$ BC: $C_1 = S_s / \cosh(\phi)$ where $\phi = \lambda L_b = L_b\sqrt{k/D_{eff}}$.

**Step 6:** Flux at interface:
$$J_s = -D_{eff}\left.\frac{dS}{dx}\right|_{L_b} = -S_s \sqrt{k D_{eff}} \tanh(\phi)$$

**Step 7:** Effectiveness factor:
$$\eta_{eff} = \frac{J_s}{k L_b S_s} = \frac{\tanh(\phi)}{\phi}$$

**Limiting cases (1 mark):**
- $\phi \to 0$ (thin biofilm): $\tanh(\phi)/\phi \to 1$ → **reaction-controlled**, no diffusion limitation.
- $\phi \to \infty$ (thick biofilm): $\eta_{eff} \to 1/\phi \to 0$ → **diffusion-controlled**, only a thin outer layer is active.

---

### Model Solution to Q4(b) — Flocculated Cell Reactor Calculations

**Step 1 — Cell concentration factor:**
$$CF = \frac{X}{X_e} = \frac{40}{0.5} = 80$$

**Step 2 — Cell retention time:**
$$\tau_c = CF \times \tau = 80 \times 3 = 240\;\text{h}$$

**Step 3 — Specific growth rate from Monod at $S = 0.10$ g/L:**
$$\mu = \mu_{max} \frac{S}{K_S + S} = 0.35 \times \frac{0.10}{0.20 + 0.10} = 0.35 \times \frac{1}{3} = 0.1167\;\text{h}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Wash-out dilution rate** (the critical $D$ at which biomass cannot sustain itself, $D_{washout} = \mu$ at $S_0$):
$$\mu(S_0) = 0.35 \times \frac{5.0}{0.20 + 5.0} = 0.35 \times \frac{5}{5.20} = 0.3365\;\text{h}^{-1}$$
$$D_{washout} \approx 0.337\;\text{h}^{-1}$$

**Step 5 — Actual operating dilution rate** (for completeness):
$$D = 1/\tau = 1/3 = 0.333\;\text{h}^{-1}$$

**Comment (1 mark):** Since $D = 0.333 < D_{washout} = 0.337$, the FCB operates **just below wash-out** — confirming that floc recycle allows operation at very high $D$ while maintaining stable biomass.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting units in the Ergun equation** — Mixing cgs/SI gives wrong answers; stick to **Pa, m, kg, s**.
> 2. **Misidentifying BCs in biofilm ODE** — $dS/dx = 0$ at the **wall** (no flux), NOT at the surface.
> 3. **Confusing HRT and CRT** — $\tau$ is hydraulic, $\tau_c$ is biomass; they are equal only in CSTR without recycle.
> 4. **Ignoring the zero-order assumption** in Thiele modulus for flocs — full Monod kinetics requires numerical solution.
> 5. **Sign error in $J_s$** — the negative sign in Fick's law is conventional; magnitude is what matters.
> 6. **Not stating assumptions** — KTU evaluators deduct up to 2 marks for missing "Newtonian fluid," "isothermal," "spherical particles," etc.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY BULLET SUMMARY — Pin this in your revision notebook**

### Trickle Bed Bioreactor (TBB)
- Three-phase (G–L–S), fixed-film, packed-bed system.
- Liquid trickles as a thin film; gas flows co- or counter-current.
- Biofilm on packing is the biocatalyst — **complete cell retention**.
- **Wetting efficiency $\eta_w$** is the key design lever — controls mass transfer.
- **Ergun equation** gives pressure drop (viscous + kinetic terms).
- Operates in **low–moderate interaction** regime for wastewater applications.
- Typical applications: VOC biofiltration, COD/BOD removal, denitrification.
- Biofilm effectiveness factor: $\eta_{eff} = \tanh(\phi)/\phi$ where $\phi = L_b\sqrt{k/D_{eff}}$.
- $k_L a \approx 0.017 L^{0.59}$ for typical packings.

### Flocculated Cell Bioreactor (FCB)
- Two- or three-phase suspended culture with self-aggregating cells.
- **EPS + divalent cations (Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺)** glue cells into flocs.
- **Richardson–Zaki settling:** $v_s = v_t (1-\phi)^n$ with $n \approx 4.6$ for low Re_p.
- **Decoupled HRT and CRT:** $\tau_c = CF \cdot \tau$ where $CF = X/X_e$.
- Allows $X = 20$ – $80$ g/L — much higher than plain CSTR.
- **Thiele modulus** $\phi_s = R_f\sqrt{k_0/(D_{eff} S_s)}$ characterizes intra-floc diffusion.
- Typical applications: brewing, ethanol, SCP, high-cell-density fermentation.
- Stable operation just below $D_{washout} = \mu_{max} S_0/(K_S + S_0)$.

### Cross-Cutting Equations to Memorize
- Ergun: $\Delta P/L = 150(1-\varepsilon)^2 \mu u / (\varepsilon^3 d_p^2) + 1.75(1-\varepsilon)\rho u^2/(\varepsilon^3 d_p)$.
- Biofilm η: $\tanh(\phi)/\phi$.
- Stokes settling: $v_t = g(\rho_f - \rho_L)d_f^2/(18\mu_L)$.
- Richardson–Zaki: $v_s = v_t(1-\phi)^n$.
- Monod: $\mu = \mu_{max} S/(K_S + S)$.

### Common KTU Question Triggers
- "Derive" → use full step-by-step, never skip algebra.
- "Compare" → use a table.
- "Calculate pressure drop" → use Ergun; verify $Re_p$ range.
- "Calculate CF or CRT" → identify recycle ratio first.
- "Discuss limitations" → mention diffusion, flooding, channeling, floc breakup.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Novel Bioreactors - Inverse Fluid Flow Units
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT526 - BIOREACTOR DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Novel Bioreactors - Inverse Fluid Flow Units

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Inverse Fluid Flow Bioreactor (IFFB)** — also termed an **Inverse Fluidized Bed Bioreactor (IFBB)** or **Inverse Three-Phase Fluidized Bed** — is a novel multiphase bioreactor configuration in which the **continuous liquid phase flows downward** (co-currently or counter-currently) while the **gas phase is sparged upward** from the bottom of the column. The net result is that the *effective* fluidizing velocity acting on the solid support particles (carriers for biofilm, immobilized enzymes, or cell aggregates) is the **vector difference between the upward gas velocity and the downward liquid velocity**:

$$U_{eff} = U_g - U_l$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** "Inverse fluidized bed bioreactors are three-phase systems in which the liquid is introduced from the top and flows downward, while air is sparged from the bottom and flows upward. The relative motion of the two fluids suspends low-density bioparticles, enabling efficient biofilm growth with low shear stress."

Because the bioparticles (typically made of low-density polymers such as **polystyrene, polyethylene, polypropylene, polyvinyl chloride (PVC), or reticulated polyurethane foam** with densities between **0.85–1.05 g/cm³**) are *lighter* than the surrounding liquid medium, a downward liquid drag combined with an upward buoyancy-like gas drag produces a **quasi-stable, expanded fluidized bed**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Champagne Glass Suspension"**
> Imagine pouring a clear fizzy drink into a tall, narrow glass. The bubbles (**gas**) race upward, while the liquid is pushed slightly downward to make room. Now, if you dropped tiny pieces of cork (lower density than the drink) into the glass, you would see them **hover in the middle** — neither sinking to the bottom nor floating to the top. The upward rush of bubbles keeps them aloft against gravity, while the downward liquid current prevents them from rising all the way out. **That suspended "cork zone" is exactly the fluidized bed in an Inverse Fluidized Bed Bioreactor.** The "corks" carry living cells or enzymes on their surface, gently bathed by nutrients from the descending liquid, while oxygen is delivered by the rising bubbles.

This is fundamentally the **opposite** of a conventional fluidized bed, where the liquid flows **upward** and carries denser particles with it. In the inverse configuration, the flow directions are deliberately swapped to:
- **Decouple** the particle motion from the liquid drag (which now *reduces* rather than *enhances* fluidization),
- Exploit the low density of biocarriers, and
- Operate at much **lower shear rates**, which is critical for fragile mammalian, plant, or insect cell cultures and for shear-sensitive biofilms.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Key Physical Constants

| Parameter | Typical Range (Industrial / Lab) | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Liquid superficial velocity, $U_l$ | 0.5 – 8.0 | $\text{cm/s}$ |
| Gas superficial velocity, $U_g$ | 0.5 – 10.0 | $\text{cm/s}$ |
| Bioparticle density, $\rho_p$ | 0.85 – 1.05 | $\text{g/cm}^3$ |
| Liquid density (aqueous medium), $\rho_l$ | 0.99 – 1.05 | $\text{g/cm}^3$ |
| Particle diameter, $d_p$ | 0.5 – 5.0 | $\text{mm}$ |
| Bed porosity at minimum fluidization, $\varepsilon_{mf}$ | 0.40 – 0.55 | dimensionless |
| Volumetric mass transfer coefficient, $k_L a$ | 50 – 600 | $\text{h}^{-1}$ |
| Gas holdup, $\varepsilon_g$ | 0.05 – 0.35 | dimensionless |
| Bed expansion ratio, $H/H_0$ | 1.2 – 3.5 | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Conceptual Rule:** For the inverse bed to fluidize, the **gas velocity must exceed the terminal settling velocity of the bioparticle** (or, equivalently, the net upward drag from gas must overcome both gravity *and* the downward liquid drag).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The hydrodynamic behavior of an Inverse Fluidized Bed Bioreactor can be broken into the following logical steps:

1. **Particle Selection:** Light, near-buoyant biocarriers (density < 1.05 g/cm³) are chosen so that the **net gravitational force** is small.
2. **Gas Sparging:** Air (or oxygen-enriched gas) is introduced through a sintered distributor at the column base at superficial velocity $U_g$.
3. **Downward Liquid Flow:** Nutrient medium is pumped from the top at superficial velocity $U_l$.
4. **Vector Force Balance:** On each particle, the **upward gas drag** and the **buoyancy force** are counteracted by **gravity** and the **downward liquid drag**. Equilibrium yields the fluidized state.
5. **Bed Expansion:** As $U_g$ increases, the bed expands smoothly from its packed height $H_0$ to a fluidized height $H$.
6. **Biofilm Growth:** Microorganisms colonize the surface of the carriers, forming a stable biofilm; substrate diffuses from the descending liquid; oxygen transfers from the rising bubbles.
7. **Steady-State Operation:** At constant $U_l$ and $U_g$, the system reaches steady hydrodynamic, mass-transfer, and biological equilibrium.

## 2.2 Why "Inverse"?

> [!NOTE]
> The "inverse" terminology is owed to two simultaneous reversals relative to the classical fluidized bed:
> - **Direction of liquid flow** is reversed (top → bottom instead of bottom → top).
> - **Density relationship** is reversed (particles *lighter* than fluid instead of *heavier*).

This unique geometry offers three KTU-highlighted engineering advantages:

- **Low Shear:** The downward liquid cancels part of the gas-induced agitation, producing gentle mixing — ideal for **shear-sensitive cells** (mammalian, insect, plant).
- **High Mass Transfer:** Continuous gas–liquid contact along the entire column height yields a high $k_L a$.
- **Counter-current Operation:** Fresh substrate meets fully-grown biofilm near the top; spent medium meets young biofilm near the bottom — a built-in **axial concentration gradient** that enhances overall conversion (analogous to a counter-current heat exchanger).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning / When to Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $U_{eff} = U_g - U_l$ | Effective fluidizing velocity | $\text{m/s}$ |
| 2 | $\Delta P = H (1-\varepsilon)(\rho_p - \rho_l) g$ | Static pressure drop (Archimedes balance) | $\text{Pa}$ |
| 3 | $\dfrac{U_{eff}}{U_t} = \varepsilon^n$ | Modified **Richardson–Zaki** correlation for inverse bed expansion | dimensionless |
| 4 | $U_t = \dfrac{d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_l) g}{18 \mu_l}$ | Stokes' terminal settling velocity (creeping flow) | $\text{m/s}$ |
| 5 | $n = 2.4 + 0.075\,\text{Re}_t^{0.87}$ | Richardson–Zaki expansion index | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\text{Re}_t = \dfrac{d_p \rho_l U_t}{\mu_l}$ | Particle **Reynolds number** at terminal velocity | dimensionless |
| 7 | $k_L a = k_1 \, U_g^{k_2} \, U_l^{k_3}$ | Empirical mass transfer correlation; $k_1, k_2, k_3$ are system-specific | $\text{s}^{-1}$ |
| 8 | $\varepsilon_g = a \, U_g^{b}$ | Gas holdup correlation (Bubbly → Churn-turbulent flow) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\dfrac{H}{H_0} = \dfrac{1 - \varepsilon_0}{1 - \varepsilon}$ | Bed expansion ratio | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\tau = \dfrac{V}{Q_l} = \dfrac{A \cdot H}{Q_l}$ | Liquid residence time (HRT) | $\text{s}$ |
| 11 | $X = \dfrac{S_0 - S}{S_0}$ | Substrate conversion (single-pass) | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units reminder for KTU Board Exam:** Always express $U_g$ and $U_l$ in **consistent SI units (m/s)** when computing Reynolds numbers or terminal velocities. Board examiners specifically look for unit conversion steps in Part B answers.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

Inverse fluidized bed bioreactors are used in:

- **Municipal & Industrial Wastewater Treatment** — for high-strength effluents, ammonia nitrification, and phenol degradation.
- **Bioethanol & Biofuel Production** — immobilized *Zymomonas mobilis* or *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* on low-density carriers.
- **Recombinant Protein Production** — shear-sensitive mammalian (CHO, HEK293) and insect cell cultures.
- **Heavy Metal Biosorption** — biofilm-coated carriers for $\text{Cu}^{2+}$, $\text{Pb}^{2+}$, $\text{Cd}^{2+}$ removal.
- **Aquaculture & Algal Cultivation** — counter-current nutrient/CO₂ delivery in photobioreactors.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Modified Richardson–Zaki Correlation for Inverse Fluidization

The classical Richardson–Zaki equation relates the superficial velocity of a fluid passing upward through a bed of settling particles to the resulting bed porosity. In an **inverse** bed, the *effective* fluidizing velocity is $U_{eff} = U_g - U_l$, and the particle terminal velocity is replaced by a **modified terminal velocity** that accounts for the counter-current liquid drag.

### Step 1: Start with the classical Richardson–Zaki equation

$$
\frac{U}{U_t} = \varepsilon^n
$$

where $U$ is the superficial fluidizing velocity, $U_t$ is the particle terminal settling velocity, $\varepsilon$ is the bed porosity, and $n$ is the expansion index.

### Step 2: Substitute the inverse-bed effective velocity

In the inverse configuration, the gas phase plays the role of the fluidizing fluid (acting upward), while the descending liquid opposes motion. The net fluidizing velocity is therefore:

$$
U_{eff} = U_g - U_l
$$

Substituting:

$$
\frac{U_g - U_l}{U_t} = \varepsilon^n
$$

### Step 3: Solve for the bed porosity

$$
\varepsilon = \left( \frac{U_g - U_l}{U_t} \right)^{1/n}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constraint:** For the bed to fluidize, $U_{eff} > U_{mf,\,inverse}$, where $U_{mf,\,inverse}$ is the minimum gas velocity at which bioparticles first become suspended. This requires $U_g > U_l + U_{mf,\,inverse}$.

### Step 4: Bed expansion ratio

Using $\varepsilon_0$ (porosity of the packed bed, typically ≈ 0.40):

$$
\frac{H}{H_0} = \frac{1 - \varepsilon_0}{1 - \varepsilon} = \frac{1 - \varepsilon_0}{1 - \left( \dfrac{U_g - U_l}{U_t} \right)^{1/n}}
$$

### Step 5: Richardson–Zaki expansion index

For spherical particles in the inertial regime:

$$
n = 2.4 + 0.075 \, \text{Re}_t^{0.87}
$$

For biocarriers of density ≈ 0.95 g/cm³ in water, $d_p \approx 2$ mm, $\text{Re}_t \approx 40$, and so $n \approx 2.4 + 0.075 \times 40^{0.87} \approx 4.1$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Minimum Fluidization Velocity in Inverse Bed

At incipient fluidization, the upward gas drag exactly balances the **net weight of particles in liquid** *plus* the **downward liquid drag**.

### Step 1: Force balance on a single particle at minimum fluidization

$$
F_{drag,\,gas} = W_{net} + F_{drag,\,liquid}
$$

The net submerged weight per unit bed cross-section is:

$$
W_{net} = H_{mf} \, (1 - \varepsilon_{mf}) \, (\rho_p - \rho_l) \, g
$$

The pressure drop across the bed at minimum fluidization equals the net submerged weight:

$$
\Delta P_{mf} = H_{mf} \, (1 - \varepsilon_{mf}) \, (\rho_p - \rho_l) \, g
$$

### Step 2: Relate $\Delta P_{mf}$ to the gas flow through a pressure-drop / velocity relation (modified Ergun equation)

$$
\frac{\Delta P_{mf}}{H_{mf}} = 150 \frac{\mu_g (1 - \varepsilon_{mf})^2}{\varepsilon_{mf}^3 d_p^2} U_{g,\,mf} + 1.75 \frac{\rho_g (1 - \varepsilon_{mf})}{\varepsilon_{mf}^3 d_p} U_{g,\,mf}^{\,2} - C_{liq} \cdot U_l
$$

where $C_{liq}$ is an empirical coefficient representing the **magnitude of the downward liquid-drag contribution** that *opposes* fluidization. This makes the required minimum gas velocity **higher** than in a conventional upflow bed.

### Step 3: Solve the quadratic for $U_{g,\,mf}$

$$
A \cdot U_{g,\,mf}^{\,2} + B \cdot U_{g,\,mf} - C = 0
$$

giving:

$$
U_{g,\,mf} = \frac{-B + \sqrt{B^2 + 4AC}}{2A}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** Because the descending liquid pulls particles *downward*, the gas must work harder to lift them. Hence $U_{g,\,mf,\,inverse} > U_{mf,\,conventional}$ for the same carrier.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Empirical $k_L a$ Correlation for Inverse Beds

The volumetric mass transfer coefficient is most often reported in the **dimensionless** form:

$$
\frac{k_L a \, d_p^2}{D_L} = \alpha \, \text{Re}_g^{\beta} \, \text{Re}_l^{\gamma} \, \text{Sc}^{\delta}
$$

where $\text{Re}_g = d_p U_g \rho_g / \mu_g$, $\text{Re}_l = d_p U_l \rho_l / \mu_l$, $\text{Sc} = \mu_l / (\rho_l D_L)$, and $D_L$ is the oxygen diffusivity in the liquid.

**Simplified engineering form (commonly accepted in IFBB literature):**

$$
k_L a = k_1 \, U_g^{0.7} \, U_l^{0.3}
$$

with $k_1$ determined experimentally (typical range $k_1 \approx 0.02 - 0.05 \,\text{s}^{-1}$ in water at 25 °C).

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Problem:** An inverse fluidized bed uses bioparticles of $d_p = 2$ mm, $\rho_p = 0.95 \,\text{g/cm}^3$ in water at 25 °C. Superficial gas velocity $U_g = 4.0 \,\text{cm/s}$, downward liquid velocity $U_l = 1.5 \,\text{cm/s}$. Packed-bed porosity $\varepsilon_0 = 0.42$, expansion index $n = 4.0$. Calculate (a) the effective fluidizing velocity, (b) the bed expansion ratio, and (c) the bed porosity at operating conditions. Use $U_t = 6.5 \,\text{cm/s}$ (computed from Stokes' law).

#### Solution

**(a) Effective fluidizing velocity:**

$$
U_{eff} = U_g - U_l = 4.0 - 1.5 = 2.5 \,\text{cm/s}
$$

**(b) Bed porosity:**

$$
\varepsilon = \left( \frac{U_{eff}}{U_t} \right)^{1/n} = \left( \frac{2.5}{6.5} \right)^{1/4.0} = (0.3846)^{0.25}
$$

$$
\varepsilon = 0.786
$$

**(c) Bed expansion ratio:**

$$
\frac{H}{H_0} = \frac{1 - \varepsilon_0}{1 - \varepsilon} = \frac{1 - 0.42}{1 - 0.786} = \frac{0.58}{0.214} = 2.71
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** A 2.0 mm particle bed expanded to **2.71 times** its packed height — a typical operating range for IFBBs. The expansion must remain below the column freeboard to prevent particle elutriation.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Performance Matrix

| Criterion | Conventional Fluidized Bed (Upflow) | Inverse Fluidized Bed (Downflow) |
|---|---|---|
| Liquid flow direction | Bottom → Top | Top → Bottom |
| Gas flow direction | Bottom → Top | Bottom → Top |
| Carrier density | $\rho_p > \rho_l$ | $\rho_p < \rho_l$ |
| Shear stress on cells | High | **Low** ✓ |
| Particle elutriation risk | Moderate–High | **Low** ✓ |
| Mass transfer ($k_L a$) | High | **High** ✓ |
| Energy for liquid pumping | Higher (against gravity) | **Lower** (gravity-assisted) ✓ |
| Scale-up complexity | Moderate | Moderate–High |
| Suitable for biofilm cultures | Limited | **Excellent** ✓ |
| Suitable for free-cell cultures | Moderate | **Excellent** (low shear) ✓ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of an Inverse Fluidized Bed Bioreactor (Block-Level Flow Diagram)

The diagram below maps the **process flow, control loops, and material streams** of a typical IFBB. It is rendered as a Mermaid block-level functional architecture (a *process & instrumentation* style schematic) because the physical column itself is best represented as an annotated flow graph.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Feed["FEED PREPARATION UNIT"]
        A1[Nutrient Medium Tank]
        A2[Sterile Reservoir]
    end

    subgraph Gas["GAS SUPPLY UNIT"]
        B1[Air Compressor]
        B2[Filter Sterile 0.2 micron]
        B3[Rotameter Gas Flowmeter]
        B4[Humidifier CO2 Control]
    end

    subgraph Reactor["INVERSE FLUIDIZED BED COLUMN"]
        C1[Top Liquid Distributor Perforated Plate]
        C2[Freeboard Region]
        C3[Fluidized Bed Zone Biofilm Carriers]
        C4[Gas Distributor Sintered Plate]
        C5[Bottom Settling Zone]
    end

    subgraph Control["INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL"]
        D1[DO Probe Dissolved Oxygen]
        D2[pH Probe]
        D3[Temperature Sensor PT100]
        D4[PLC SCADA Controller]
    end

    subgraph Effluent["PRODUCT AND EFFLUENT HANDLING"]
        E1[Effluent Collection Tank]
        E2[Biomass Harvest Filter]
        E3[Sample Port for Analysis]
    end

    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> C5
    C5 --> E1

    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B4
    B4 --> B3
    B3 --> C4
    C4 --> C3

    C3 --> D1
    C3 --> D2
    C3 --> D3
    D1 --> D4
    D2 --> D4
    D3 --> D4
    D4 --> B3
    D4 --> A1

    E1 --> E2
    C3 --> E3
```

## 4.2 Hydrodynamic Force-Balance Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Particle["BIOCARRIER PARTICLE Density less than liquid"]
        P[Particle]
    end

    F_grav[Weight W = mp times g] -->|downward| P
    F_drag_l[Liquid Drag FD liquid proportional to Ul] -->|downward| P
    F_buoy[Buoyancy FB equal to rhoL times V times g] -->|upward| P
    F_drag_g[Gas Drag FD gas proportional to Ug] -->|upward| P
    P --> NetForce[Net Force at Fluidization equals zero]
    F_grav --> NetForce
    F_drag_l --> NetForce
    F_buoy --> NetForce
    F_drag_g --> NetForce
```

## 4.3 Reading the Diagram

> [!NOTE]
> **Process Interpretation (for KTU Board exam):**
> 1. Sterile nutrient medium enters the column from the **top** (downflow).
> 2. Filtered, humidified air is sparged from the **bottom** (upflow).
> 3. The two streams meet in the **fluidized bed zone**, where biofilm-coated carriers are suspended.
> 4. **DO, pH, and temperature probes** continuously feed signals to the PLC, which adjusts gas flow, nutrient feed rate, and coolant flow.
> 5. Spent medium exits at the bottom, while biomass can be harvested periodically from a side port.
> 6. The downward liquid aids gravity, **reducing pumping energy** by approximately 15–25 % compared to a conventional upflow system.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define an Inverse Fluidized Bed Bioreactor. What is the key density criterion for the bioparticles used?** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- An Inverse Fluidized Bed Bioreactor (IFBB) is a three-phase (gas–liquid–solid) bioreactor in which the **liquid phase flows downward** from the top while **gas is sparged upward** from the bottom, suspending low-density bioparticles in an expanded bed. **(2 marks)**
- The **key density criterion** is that the bioparticles must be **lighter than the liquid medium**, i.e., $\rho_p < \rho_l$ (typically $0.85 - 1.05 \,\text{g/cm}^3$ versus water at ≈ 1.0 g/cm³). This allows the combined upward gas drag and buoyancy to support the particles against gravity and the downward liquid drag. **(1 mark)**

### **Q2. State the modified Richardson–Zaki correlation for an inverse fluidized bed and explain the meaning of each term.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Q]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The correlation is:
  $\dfrac{U_g - U_l}{U_t} = \varepsilon^{n}$ **(1 mark)**
- **$U_g$** — superficial gas velocity (m/s); **$U_l$** — superficial downward liquid velocity (m/s); their difference is the **effective fluidizing velocity**. **(1 mark)**
- **$U_t$** — terminal settling velocity of the bioparticle (m/s); **$\varepsilon$** — operating bed porosity; **$n$** — Richardson–Zaki expansion index (function of $\text{Re}_t$). **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**, and offers an internal choice (either OR). Below are **two independent 14-mark questions** to cover both choices.

### **Part B — Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**Q A. (a)** With a neat schematic, describe the construction and working principle of an Inverse Fluidized Bed Bioreactor (IFBB). Highlight the role of the two opposing fluid streams. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pilot-scale IFBB operates with biocarriers of $d_p = 1.5$ mm, $\rho_p = 0.92 \,\text{g/cm}^3$ in an aqueous medium of $\rho_l = 1.00 \,\text{g/cm}^3$ and $\mu_l = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s. The superficial gas velocity is $U_g = 3.5 \,\text{cm/s}$ and the downward liquid velocity is $U_l = 1.0 \,\text{cm/s}$. The packed-bed porosity is $\varepsilon_0 = 0.40$ and the Richardson–Zaki index is $n = 3.8$. Given $U_t = 4.8 \,\text{cm/s}$ (from Stokes' law), calculate:
- (i) the **effective fluidizing velocity**, **(1 mark)**
- (ii) the **operating bed porosity**, **(2 marks)**
- (iii) the **bed expansion ratio** $H/H_0$, **(2 marks)**
- (iv) the **bed height** if $H_0 = 1.0$ m. **(2 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q A**

**(a) Construction and Working (7 marks):**

1. **Column body:** A vertical cylindrical column (typically glass or SS-316 for visibility and sterility), aspect ratio 5:1 to 10:1. **(1 mark)**
2. **Gas distributor:** A sintered stainless-steel or porous ceramic plate at the bottom ensures uniform bubble formation. **(1 mark)**
3. **Liquid distributor:** A perforated plate or spray nozzle at the top ensures uniform downflow of nutrient medium. **(1 mark)**
4. **Bioparticles:** Low-density carriers (PVC, polystyrene, polyethylene, reticulated foam), $\rho_p < \rho_l$, typically 0.5–5 mm diameter, often pre-colonized with biofilm. **(1 mark)**
5. **Working principle:** Filtered, humidified air is sparged upward; nutrient medium is pumped downward. The two streams create an effective fluidizing velocity $U_{eff} = U_g - U_l$ that suspends the carriers in an expanded bed. Biofilm-coated particles consume substrate from the descending liquid and oxygen from the rising bubbles. **(2 marks)**
6. **Role of opposing streams:** Gas provides upward drag and oxygen transfer; liquid provides substrate, removes products, and *opposes* fluidization — so the gas must work harder. This simultaneously yields **high $k_L a$** and **low shear** — the central engineering trade-off that defines IFBB operation. **(1 mark)**

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Effective fluidizing velocity:**

$$
U_{eff} = U_g - U_l = 3.5 - 1.0 = 2.5 \,\text{cm/s} \quad \text{(1 mark)}
$$

**(ii) Operating bed porosity:**

$$
\varepsilon = \left( \frac{U_{eff}}{U_t} \right)^{1/n} = \left( \frac{2.5}{4.8} \right)^{1/3.8} = (0.5208)^{0.2632}
$$

$$
\varepsilon \approx 0.836 \quad \text{(2 marks)}
$$

**(iii) Bed expansion ratio:**

$$
\frac{H}{H_0} = \frac{1 - \varepsilon_0}{1 - \varepsilon} = \frac{1 - 0.40}{1 - 0.836} = \frac{0.60}{0.164} = 3.66 \quad \text{(2 marks)}
$$

**(iv) Bed height at operating conditions:**

$$
H = 3.66 \times H_0 = 3.66 \times 1.0 = 3.66 \,\text{m} \quad \text{(2 marks)}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #1 — Direction of Liquid Velocity:** Many students mistakenly add $U_l$ to $U_g$ instead of subtracting. **Always remember: in an inverse bed, liquid flows DOWN and OPPOSES fluidization.** Subtraction is mandatory.

---

### **Part B — Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**Q B. (a)** Compare the **Inverse Fluidized Bed Bioreactor (IFBB)** with a **Conventional (Upflow) Fluidized Bed Bioreactor** under the following heads: principle, particle density, shear, mass transfer, energy, and suitable applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive, starting from a force balance on a single bioparticle, the expression for the **minimum gas fluidization velocity** in an inverse bed. Clearly state the assumptions. Explain physically why $U_{g,mf,\,inverse} > U_{mf,\,conventional}$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q B**

**(a) Comparative Analysis (7 marks — 1 mark per head + 1 mark for synthesis):**

| Head | Conventional FBB (Upflow) | Inverse FBB (Downflow) |
|---|---|---|
| **Principle** | Liquid flows upward and supports denser particles. | Liquid flows downward; gas flows upward; both support *lighter* particles. |
| **Particle density** | $\rho_p > \rho_l$ (sand, glass, etc.) | $\rho_p < \rho_l$ (PVC, polystyrene, foam). |
| **Shear** | High — vigorous particle–particle and particle–wall collisions. | **Low** — gentle suspension; ideal for fragile cells. |
| **Mass transfer** | High $k_L a$ from intense mixing. | High $k_L a$ from counter-current gas–liquid contact. |
| **Energy** | Liquid pumping against gravity — high energy. | **Lower** — gravity assists liquid flow. |
| **Applications** | Catalytic cracking, wastewater (high shear tolerance). | Biofilm reactors, mammalian cell culture, biosorption, biofuel. |

**Synthesis (1 mark):** The IFBB trades off a slightly more complex gas-distribution requirement for a uniquely low-shear, high-mass-transfer, counter-current environment — making it the **bioreactor of choice for shear-sensitive bioprocesses**.

**(b) Derivation of Minimum Gas Fluidization Velocity (7 marks):**

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. Spherical, mono-disperse bioparticles.
2. Isothermal, constant physical properties.
3. Uniform gas and liquid distribution across the column cross-section.
4. The bed at incipient fluidization is at the verge of expansion: $\Delta P_{mf}$ equals the net submerged weight minus the downward liquid drag.

**Force balance per unit bed cross-section (3 marks):**

$$
\Delta P_{mf} = H_{mf} (1 - \varepsilon_{mf}) (\rho_p - \rho_l) g - \Delta P_{liq}
$$

where $\Delta P_{liq} = C_{liq} \cdot U_l$ is the additional pressure drop caused by the descending liquid.

Equating to the gas-phase Ergun pressure drop:

$$
\frac{150 \mu_g (1-\varepsilon_{mf})^2}{\varepsilon_{mf}^3 d_p^2} U_{g,mf} + \frac{1.75 \rho_g (1-\varepsilon_{mf})}{\varepsilon_{mf}^3 d_p} U_{g,mf}^{\,2} = H_{mf} (1 - \varepsilon_{mf}) (\rho_p - \rho_l) g - C_{liq} U_l
$$

Solving the quadratic in $U_{g,mf}$:

$$
U_{g,mf} = \frac{-B + \sqrt{B^2 + 4 A (C_{net})}}{2 A}
$$

where $C_{net} = H_{mf} (1-\varepsilon_{mf})(\rho_p - \rho_l)g - C_{liq} U_l$. **(2 marks for explicit form)**

**Physical reason $U_{g,mf,\,inverse} > U_{mf,\,conventional}$ (extra synthesis, may be credited 1 mark):**
The descending liquid applies a **downward drag** on the carriers, which adds to the gravitational force that the gas must overcome. Therefore the **effective weight of the bed is increased**, and a **larger $U_g$** is required to reach the fluidization threshold.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #2 — Wrong Sign in the Force Balance:** Students frequently write the liquid-drag term with a **positive** sign on the right-hand side, implying that the liquid *helps* fluidize. This is the single most common error and costs 2–3 marks. The liquid in an inverse bed always **opposes** fluidization, so its contribution is **subtracted** from the right-hand side or added to the bed weight on the left.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 1: Inverse Fluid Flow Bioreactors**

- **Definition:** Three-phase bioreactor; liquid flows **down**, gas flows **up**, suspending *low-density* bioparticles.
- **Key velocity relation:** $U_{eff} = U_g - U_l$ (subtract, not add).
- **Particle density:** $\rho_p < \rho_l$ (typically 0.85–1.05 g/cm³).
- **Modified Richardson–Zaki:** $\dfrac{U_g - U_l}{U_t} = \varepsilon^{n}$.
- **Bed expansion ratio:** $\dfrac{H}{H_0} = \dfrac{1 - \varepsilon_0}{1 - \varepsilon}$.
- **Minimum fluidization:** $U_{g,mf,\,inverse} > U_{mf,\,conventional}$ because liquid drag opposes fluidization.
- **Mass transfer:** $k_L a$ typically $50 - 600 \,\text{h}^{-1}$; correlation of the form $k_L a \propto U_g^{0.7} U_l^{0.3}$.
- **Gas holdup:** $\varepsilon_g = 0.05 - 0.35$; increases with $U_g$, weakly with $U_l$.
- **Counter-current advantage:** Fresh substrate (top) meets mature biofilm; spent medium (bottom) meets young biofilm — gives a built-in axial gradient.
- **Low-shear advantage:** Ideal for mammalian, insect, plant cells; biofilm cultures; immobilized enzymes.
- **Energy benefit:** Downflow liquid is gravity-assisted → ≈ 15–25 % pumping energy savings.
- **Common exam pitfalls:**
  1. Adding $U_l$ to $U_g$ instead of subtracting.
  2. Forgetting to convert cm/s to m/s in Reynolds number calculations.
  3. Writing the liquid-drag term with the wrong sign in the force balance.
  4. Confusing inverse bed (light particles) with conventional bed (heavy particles).
  5. Not stating the density criterion $\rho_p < \rho_l$ explicitly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hollow Fiber Reactors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT526 - BIOREACTOR DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hollow Fiber Reactors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Hollow Fiber Bioreactor (HFBR)** is a high-performance **membrane-based immobilized-cell/enzyme reactor** in which biological catalysts (cells, enzymes, or tissues) are retained within or around a bundle of semi-permeable, capillary-like polymeric hollow fibers housed inside a cylindrical shell. Nutrients, gases (especially oxygen), and substrates are delivered by convective flow through either the fiber **lumen** (inner bore) or the **shell space** (extra-capillary space, ECS), while metabolic products and waste diffuse out through the fiber wall. The fibers mimic the *in vivo* capillary network, providing a large surface-area-to-volume ratio (typically **>30 cm²/cm³**), efficient cell retention, and a gentle, low-shear hydrodynamic environment, making them ideal for anchorage-dependent mammalian cells, hybridoma cultures, and enzyme-catalyzed biotransformations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Straw-in-a-Cup" Analogy**
> Imagine a sealed plastic drinking cup (the **shell**) packed with thousands of thin drinking straws (the **hollow fibers**), arranged parallel to one another. Now pour a thick slurry of cells into the cup so that cells get trapped in the spaces *between* the straws. If you pump a nutrient-rich medium *through the straws* (lumen flow), the nutrients seep sideways through the straw walls (membrane diffusion) and feed every cell in the surrounding matrix. Waste products travel the opposite way. The straws therefore act like an artificial **lung**, bringing oxygen and food to every "neighborhood" of cells without ever letting them wash away. This is exactly how a Hollow Fiber Bioreactor works — *capillaries, not beaters, deliver the life support.*

### 1.3 Salient Features at a Glance

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Metrics of a Standard HFBR**
> - **Fiber inner diameter:** 100 – 500 µm
> - **Fiber wall thickness:** 50 – 100 µm
> - **Molecular Weight Cut-Off (MWCO):** 1 – 100 kDa (ultrafiltration range)
> - **Packing density (φ):** 0.3 – 0.6 (fraction of shell volume occupied by fibers)
> - **Surface area per unit volume:** 30 – 50 cm²/cm³ (vs. ~2 cm²/cm³ for stirred tank)
> - **Shear stress on cells:** < 0.5 dyn/cm² (extremely gentle)
> - **Typical cell density achieved:** 10⁷ – 10⁸ cells/mL of ECS (10–100× higher than stirred tank)

### 1.4 Classification of HFBR Configurations

| Configuration | Cell Location | Perfusion Path | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Type A** | Shell (ECS) | Lumen | Mammalian cell / hybridoma culture |
| **Type B** | Lumen | Shell | Enzyme immobilization |
| **Type C** | In matrix surrounding fibers | Both sides | Tissue engineering / 3-D scaffolds |
| **Type D** | Stagnant ECS with lumen flow | Lumen | Monoclonal antibody production |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step by Step

1. **Cell Immobilization:** Cells (anchorage-dependent or suspension-adapted) are inoculated into the shell side and allowed to attach to the outer surface of the fibers or to entangle within the porous matrix.
2. **Dual Perfusion:** Fresh, oxygenated medium is pumped through the **lumen** of the fibers in a single pass or recirculating mode.
3. **Radial Diffusion (Trans-membrane Transport):** Dissolved oxygen (DO), glucose, glutamine, and other low-MW substrates diffuse *radially outward* through the fiber wall into the ECS to reach immobilized cells.
4. **Convective-Diffusive Shell Transport:** Inside the ECS, further transport of nutrients to distant cells occurs by a combination of molecular diffusion and **Starling flow** (pressure-driven convective leakage through the porous wall).
5. **Metabolic Response:** Cells consume substrates and produce metabolites (lactate, ammonia, monoclonal antibody / recombinant protein).
6. **Counter-Diffusion of Products:** Metabolites and secreted products diffuse back into the lumen stream (for low-MW wastes) or accumulate in the ECS (for high-MW products like mAbs, which are too large to cross the fiber wall and are *harvested periodically*).
7. **Harvesting Strategy:** For high-MW products, the ECS is periodically flushed to recover product; for low-MW products, the lumen effluent is collected continuously.

### 2.2 The Krogh Cylinder Model (Why It Matters)

> [!NOTE]
> Each hollow fiber with its surrounding cell-laden region is modeled as a **Krogh cylinder** — a control volume of radius $R_K$ (the Krogh radius) centered on the fiber axis. The model assumes:
> - Steady-state, axisymmetric transport.
> - Homogeneous cell distribution in the ECS.
> - Constant effective diffusivity $D_e$ in the ECS.

The Krogh radius is defined as the half-distance between two adjacent fiber centers:

$$R_K = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi \cdot n_L}}$$

where $n_L$ is the number of fibers per unit cross-sectional area of the shell. Typically $R_K \approx 100 - 300 \,\mu m$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU numerical and Part B question on HFRs.**

| # | Quantity / Parameter | Governing Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Krogh radius** | $R_K = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\pi \, n_L}}$ | $n_L$ = fibers per m² of shell cross-section |
| 2 | **Volumetric oxygen consumption rate** | $q_{O_2} = \dfrac{V_{max,O_2} \, C_{O_2}}{K_{O_2} + C_{O_2}}$ | Monod-type; mol O₂ / (m³·h) |
| 3 | **Oxygen flux at fiber wall** | $N_{O_2} = \dfrac{D_{O_2}}{\tau} \left( C_{O_2,L}^* - C_{O_2,S} \right)$ | Steady-state radial diffusion; $D_{O_2}$ is the O₂ diffusivity, $\tau$ is wall thickness |
| 4 | **Critical radius for O₂ depletion** | $R_{crit} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \, D_e \, C_{O_2,S}}{q_{O_2}}}$ | Beyond $R_{crit}$, cells become O₂-starved and necrotic |
| 5 | **Damköhler number** (reaction vs. diffusion) | $Da = \dfrac{q_{O_2} \, R_K^2}{D_e \, C_{O_2}^*}$ | $Da \gg 1$ → diffusion-limited; $Da \ll 1$ → kinetics-limited |
| 6 | **Thiele modulus** (effectiveness) | $\phi = R_K \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{D_e}}$ | $k$ = first-order rate constant (s⁻¹) |
| 7 | **Effectiveness factor** | $\eta = \dfrac{3}{\phi^2} \left( \phi \coth \phi - 1 \right)$ | For spherical/cylindrical geometries; $0 < \eta \le 1$ |
| 8 | **Membrane Péclet number** | $Pe_m = \dfrac{v_m \, d_f}{D_m}$ | $v_m$ = lumen velocity, $d_f$ = fiber ID |
| 9 | **Sherwood number (lumen side)** | $Sh = 1.62 \left( Re \, Sc \, d_f / L \right)^{1/3}$ | Leveque approximation for developing flow |
| 10 | **Overall mass-transfer coefficient** | $\dfrac{1}{K_o} = \dfrac{1}{k_L} + \dfrac{d_{ln}}{P_m \, D_m}$ | Resistances-in-series; $P_m$ = membrane permeability |
| 11 | **Cell productivity rate** | $r_P = Y_{P/X} \, q_S \, X$ | $Y_{P/X}$ = product yield on biomass; $X$ = cell density |
| 12 | **Starling flow velocity** | $v_S = \dfrac{L_p \, (\Delta P_{wall})}{r}$ | $L_p$ = hydraulic permeability; ultrafiltration-driven crossflow |
| 13 | **Fiber packing density** | $\varphi = N \, \dfrac{d_{out}^2}{D_s^2}$ | $D_s$ = shell ID; $N$ = number of fibers |
| 14 | **Reactor residence time** | $\tau_R = V_{shell} / Q_{lumen}$ | For ECS volume calculations |
| 15 | **Shear stress (lumen side)** | $\tau_w = \dfrac{\Delta P \, d_f}{4 L}$ | Hagen-Poiseuille estimate |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Biopharmaceutical Industry:** Commercial production of **monoclonal antibodies (mAbs)**, **erythropoietin (EPO)**, and **Factor VIII** using hybridoma or CHO cell lines in HFRs (e.g., the Aastrom / Unisyn Maxi-React systems).
> - **Artificial Organs:** The HFBR concept directly inspired the design of **hollow-fiber dialyzers** for kidney patients, **oxygenators** for cardio-pulmonary bypass, and bioartificial liver devices.
> - **Tissue Engineering:** Used as scaffolds for building vascularized tissue constructs; the fiber network acts as a template for angiogenesis.
> - **Enzyme Biocatalysis:** Multi-enzyme cascade reactions co-immobilized in separate shell compartments for continuous-flow synthesis of fine chemicals and pharmaceuticals.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Radial Oxygen Concentration Profile in the Krogh Cylinder

**Starting Point:** Consider a single hollow fiber of outer radius $r_o$ surrounded by a cylindrical cell-laden region extending to the Krogh radius $R_K$. We assume steady-state, axisymmetric, constant effective diffusivity $D_e$ and uniform zero-order oxygen consumption $q_{O_2}$ (mol m⁻³ s⁻¹).

**Step 1 — Mass Balance on a Differential Shell**

Take a cylindrical shell of radius $r$, thickness $dr$, and unit length. At steady state, the diffusive flux entering at $r$ must equal the flux leaving at $r + dr$ plus the consumption within the shell.

$$-D_e \frac{dC}{dr}\bigg|_r \cdot 2\pi r \;=\; -D_e \frac{dC}{dr}\bigg|_{r+dr} \cdot 2\pi (r+dr) \;+\; q_{O_2} \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$$

**Step 2 — Linearization and Division**

Divide both sides by $2\pi \, dr$ and rearrange the flux terms using the first principles of differential calculus:

$$\frac{d}{dr}\left( r \frac{dC}{dr} \right) \;=\; -\frac{q_{O_2}}{D_e}\, r$$

**Step 3 — First Integration**

Integrate with respect to $r$:

$$r \frac{dC}{dr} \;=\; -\frac{q_{O_2}}{2 D_e} r^2 + C_1$$

Dividing by $r$:

$$\frac{dC}{dr} \;=\; -\frac{q_{O_2}}{2 D_e} r + \frac{C_1}{r}$$

**Step 4 — Second Integration**

Integrate again with respect to $r$:

$$C(r) \;=\; -\frac{q_{O_2}}{4 D_e} r^2 + C_1 \ln r + C_2$$

**Step 5 — Apply Boundary Conditions**

> **BC-1 (No-flux symmetry at outer edge):** At $r = R_K$, there is no cell region beyond; hence the gradient must be zero (no flux leaves the Krogh cylinder).

$$\frac{dC}{dr}\bigg|_{r=R_K} \;=\; 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; -\frac{q_{O_2}}{2 D_e} R_K + \frac{C_1}{R_K} \;=\; 0$$

Solving for $C_1$:

$$C_1 \;=\; \frac{q_{O_2}}{2 D_e} R_K^2$$

> **BC-2 (Known wall concentration):** At the outer fiber wall $r = r_o$, the dissolved oxygen concentration equals $C_{w}$ (determined by lumen-side transport).

Substituting $C_1$ into the integrated expression and applying BC-2:

$$C_{w} \;=\; -\frac{q_{O_2}}{4 D_e} r_o^2 + \frac{q_{O_2} R_K^2}{2 D_e} \ln r_o + C_2$$

Solving for $C_2$:

$$C_2 \;=\; C_{w} + \frac{q_{O_2}}{4 D_e} r_o^2 - \frac{q_{O_2} R_K^2}{2 D_e} \ln r_o$$

**Step 6 — Final Closed-Form Solution**

Combining the constants, the **oxygen concentration profile** in the ECS is:

$$\boxed{\;C(r) \;=\; C_{w} + \frac{q_{O_2}}{4 D_e}\left[ r_o^2 - r^2 \right] + \frac{q_{O_2} R_K^2}{2 D_e} \ln\!\left( \frac{r}{r_o} \right)\;}$$

**Step 7 — Critical Radius Calculation**

The critical radius is the location where $C(r_{crit}) = 0$ (oxygen just reaches zero). For a special case where $r_o \ll R_K$ and the logarithmic term dominates, the approximate critical radius becomes:

$$r_{crit} \;\approx\; R_K \exp\!\left( -\tfrac{1}{2} \right) \sqrt{\,1 - \frac{4 D_e C_{w}}{q_{O_2} R_K^{2}}\,}$$

A commonly tabulated engineering approximation is:

$$R_{crit} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{4 D_e \, C_{w}}{q_{O_2}}}$$

This sets an **upper design limit** on the inter-fiber spacing: if the cell density is so high that $R_K > R_{crit}$, the cells near the periphery of each Krogh cylinder will become **necrotic (oxygen-starved)**.

### 3.2 Derivation II — Effectiveness Factor for a Cylindrical Catalyst (Krogh Geometry)

**Starting Point:** Consider first-order consumption of substrate $S$ at rate $r = k C_S$, where $k$ has units of s⁻¹, in the same Krogh cylinder geometry.

**Step 1 — Governing ODE**

At steady state, with no convection in the ECS:

$$\frac{D_e}{r} \frac{d}{dr}\left( r \frac{dC_S}{dr} \right) - k C_S \;=\; 0$$

**Step 2 — Define the Thiele Modulus**

Introduce dimensionless radial coordinate $\xi = r / R_K$ and the Thiele modulus $\phi = R_K \sqrt{k / D_e}$:

$$\frac{1}{\xi}\frac{d}{d\xi}\!\left(\xi \frac{dC_S}{d\xi}\right) - \phi^2 C_S \;=\; 0$$

**Step 3 — Recognize as a Modified Bessel Equation**

The general solution is:

$$C_S(\xi) \;=\; A \, I_0(\phi \xi) + B \, K_0(\phi \xi)$$

where $I_0$ and $K_0$ are modified Bessel functions of the first and second kind, order zero.

**Step 4 — Apply Boundary Conditions**

> BC-1: Finite concentration at $\xi = 1$ (the Krogh boundary): $C_S(1) = C_{S,b}$
> BC-2: Symmetry gradient at $\xi = 0$ (or, in our case, at $r = r_o$): the gradient matches the wall flux.

For simplicity, when the wall is thin ($r_o \to 0$), $B = 0$ (Bessel function $K_0$ diverges at origin) and we obtain:

$$C_S(\xi) \;=\; C_{S,b} \frac{I_0(\phi \xi)}{I_0(\phi)}$$

**Step 5 — Effectiveness Factor**

The effectiveness factor is the ratio of actual uptake to uptake if all cells were exposed to $C_{S,b}$:

$$\eta \;=\; \frac{\int_{0}^{1} k C_S(\xi) \, 2\pi \xi \, d\xi}{k C_{S,b} \cdot \pi (1^2)} \;=\; \frac{2 \int_0^1 I_0(\phi \xi)\, \xi \, d\xi}{I_0(\phi)}$$

Using the integral identity $\int_0^1 \xi I_0(\phi \xi) d\xi = \dfrac{I_1(\phi)}{\phi}$:

$$\eta \;=\; \frac{2 I_1(\phi)}{\phi \, I_0(\phi)}$$

This is the classical **cylindrical effectiveness factor expression**. For $\phi \to 0$, $\eta \to 1$ (kinetics-limited, no diffusion resistance). For $\phi \to \infty$, $\eta \to 2/\phi$ (strong diffusion limitation, cell viability collapse).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Quick-Reference Derivations

| Derivation | Governing ODE | Key Boundary Conditions | Final Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| **O₂ profile (zero-order)** | $\dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{d}{dr}\!\left(r\dfrac{dC}{dr}\right) = -\dfrac{q_{O_2}}{D_e}$ | $\dfrac{dC}{dr}\big\vert_{R_K}=0$, $C(r_o)=C_w$ | $C(r) = C_w + \dfrac{q_{O_2}}{4 D_e}(r_o^2 - r^2) + \dfrac{q_{O_2} R_K^2}{2 D_e}\ln(r/r_o)$ |
| **O₂ profile (first-order)** | $\dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{d}{dr}\!\left(r\dfrac{dC}{dr}\right) = \dfrac{k}{D_e} C$ | Symmetry at $r=0$, $C(R_K)=C_b$ | $C(r) = C_b \dfrac{I_0(\phi \, r / R_K)}{I_0(\phi)}$ |
| **Effectiveness factor (cylinder)** | As above | — | $\eta = \dfrac{2 I_1(\phi)}{\phi \, I_0(\phi)}$ |
| **Effectiveness factor (sphere, comparison)** | $\dfrac{1}{r^2}\dfrac{d}{dr}\!\left(r^2\dfrac{dC}{dr}\right) = \dfrac{k}{D_e}C$ | Symmetry at $r=0$ | $\eta = \dfrac{3}{\phi^2}\left(\phi\coth\phi - 1\right)$ |
| **Lumen Sherwood (Leveque)** | Convective-diffusive in tube | Constant wall concentration | $Sh = 1.62 \left(Re\, Sc\, d_f / L\right)^{1/3}$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of a Hollow Fiber Bioreactor

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ShellAssembly["Shell / Cartridge Assembly"]
        direction TB
        InletL["Lumen Inlet\nOxygenated Medium"] --> FiberBundle
        FiberBundle["Hollow Fiber Bundle\nn = 500 to 10000 fibers\nInner Diameter 200 to 500 micron\nMWCO 10 to 50 kDa"] --> OutletL["Lumen Outlet\nDeoxygenated Medium\nplus low MW waste"]
        ShellSpace["Extra Capillary Space ECS\nCell Immobilization Zone\nCell density 1e7 to 1e8 cells per mL\nKrogh Radius 100 to 300 micron"] --- FiberBundle
        ShellInlet["Shell Inlet\nInoculum or Matrix\nPeriodic ECS Flush"] --> ShellSpace
        ShellOutlet["Shell Outlet\nHarvested Product\nmAb or Recombinant Protein"] --> ShellSpace
    end

    subgraph TransportModes["Dominant Transport Modes"]
        direction LR
        RadialDiff["Radial Diffusion\nTrans Membrane\nDriving Force = dC dr"]
        Starling["Starling Flow\nPressure Driven\nDriven by Delta P wall"]
        ConvecL["Lumen Convection\nAxial Flow\nPeclet Number approx 10 to 100"]
    end

    subgraph ControlLoop["Process Monitoring and Control"]
        direction TB
        DO["Dissolved O2 Sensor\nLumen Outlet"] --> CtrlPLC["PLC Controller"]
        pHSensor["pH Sensor\nECS Loop"] --> CtrlPLC
        GlucoseSensor["Glucose / Lactate Analyzer\nAt line"] --> CtrlPLC
        CtrlPLC --> RecircPump["Recirculation Pump\nVariable Speed 0 to 500 mL per min"]
        RecircPump --> InletL
    end

    FiberBundle --> RadialDiff
    ShellSpace --> Starling
    InletL --> ConvecL
    DO --> RadialDiff
    pHSensor --> Starling
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Block Diagram of a Complete HFBR Process Train

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Media Reservoir\nGlucose, Gln, Salts, pH 7.2] --> B[Oxygenator\nHollow Fiber Gas Exchanger]
    B --> C[Media Preheater\n37 deg C]
    C --> D[HFBR Cartridge\nCells in Shell]
    D --> E[O2 and pH Sensor Array]
    D --> F[Cell Bleed / Waste Port]
    G[Peristaltic Recirculation Pump] --> B
    G --> D
    D --> H[Product Collection Vessel\nECS Harvest Tank]
    E --> I[Supervisory Control PC]
    I --> G
```

### 4.3 Functional Map of Governing Phenomena

> [!NOTE]
> **Mapping of the three simultaneous transport processes in an HFR:**
> - **Convection** dominates in the lumen (high Péclet).
> - **Diffusion** dominates across the fiber wall and within the ECS.
> - **Reaction** (cellular metabolism) occurs in the ECS.
> - **Starling flow** is a small secondary cross-flow driven by lumen hydrostatic pressure when the fiber wall is ultrafiltration-grade (MWCO < 30 kDa).

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working principle of a hollow fiber bioreactor. List any three industrial applications.*

**Model Answer:**
A **hollow fiber bioreactor (HFBR)** consists of a cylindrical shell housing several thousand parallel, semi-permeable polymeric fibers. Cells are immobilized in the **shell side (extra-capillary space, ECS)**. Oxygenated medium flows through the **lumen** of the fibers; oxygen, glucose, and other low-MW nutrients diffuse radially outward through the fiber wall to feed the cells. High-MW products (e.g., mAbs) cannot pass the membrane and accumulate in the ECS for periodic harvest, while low-MW wastes diffuse back into the lumen stream.

> **Three industrial applications:**
> 1. Production of **monoclonal antibodies** from hybridoma cells.
> 2. Bioartificial **liver-assist devices** using hepatocyte-laden fibers.
> 3. Continuous-flow **enzyme biocatalysis** for pharmaceutical intermediates.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the Krogh cylinder model. What is the significance of the Krogh radius in the design of hollow fiber bioreactors?*

**Model Answer:**
The **Krogh cylinder model** idealizes the ECS surrounding each hollow fiber as a cylinder of radius $R_K$ (Krogh radius) co-axial with the fiber. It allows analytical treatment of radial substrate diffusion with simultaneous cellular consumption.
The **Krogh radius** is significant because:
- It determines the **maximum inter-fiber spacing** beyond which central cells become oxygen-starved ($R_K \le R_{crit}$).
- It governs the **effectiveness factor** $\eta$ and hence the volumetric productivity.
- It is a **design lever**: choosing $n_L$ (fiber density) directly fixes $R_K = ( \pi n_L)^{-1/2}$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Confusing Krogh radius with fiber outer radius** — $R_K$ is the *half-distance to the neighboring fiber*, not the fiber's geometric radius. Many students lose 2–3 marks by mixing them.
> 2. **Forgetting boundary conditions** in radial profile derivations — KTU awards 2 marks specifically for stating BCs. Always write: (i) flux/gradient condition at $r = R_K$, (ii) known concentration at $r = r_o$.
> 3. **Mixing the effectiveness-factor formulas** for slab, cylinder, and sphere. For *Krogh (cylindrical) geometry* use $\eta = 2 I_1(\phi) / [\phi I_0(\phi)]$, not the spherical one.
> 4. **Ignoring Monod kinetics** for mammalian cells — use $q_{O_2} = q_{max} C_{O_2} / (K_{O_2} + C_{O_2})$; do not assume zero-order unless stated.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Style]**
**(a)** Derive the steady-state radial concentration profile of dissolved oxygen in the extra-capillary space (ECS) of a hollow fiber bioreactor, assuming **zero-order oxygen consumption** by immobilized cells. Clearly state the assumptions and boundary conditions used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A hollow fiber bioreactor is used to culture **Chinese Hamster Ovary (CHO) cells** for monoclonal antibody production. The fiber has an outer diameter of $300 \,\mu m$ and a packing density $\varphi = 0.4$. The shell has an ID of $2.5 \, cm$. The cell density in the ECS is $X = 5 \times 10^7$ cells/mL and the specific oxygen consumption rate is $q_{O_2,sp} = 5 \times 10^{-17}$ mol O₂/(cell·s). The effective diffusivity of O₂ in the ECS is $D_e = 2 \times 10^{-5}$ cm²/s and the oxygen concentration at the fiber wall is $C_w = 0.20$ mM. Calculate:
(i) the **Krogh radius** $R_K$ of the cartridge, and
(ii) the **critical radius** $R_{crit}$ at which oxygen just reaches zero at the periphery of the Krogh cylinder. Comment on whether the design is oxygen-sufficient. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

The radial O₂ balance in a Krogh cylinder at steady state is given by:

$$\frac{D_e}{r} \frac{d}{dr}\!\left( r \frac{dC}{dr} \right) - q_{O_2} = 0$$

where $q_{O_2}$ is the volumetric consumption rate (mol m⁻³ s⁻¹). With the boundary conditions:

- **BC-1:** $\dfrac{dC}{dr}\Big|_{r = R_K} = 0$ (no flux at the Krogh boundary — symmetry).
- **BC-2:** $C(r_o) = C_w$ (concentration fixed at the fiber outer surface).

Integrating twice (as shown in Section 3.1) gives:

$$\boxed{\;C(r) = C_w + \frac{q_{O_2}}{4 D_e}\bigl(r_o^2 - r^2\bigr) + \frac{q_{O_2} R_K^{2}}{2 D_e}\ln\!\left(\frac{r}{r_o}\right)\;}$$

The assumptions are: (1) steady-state, (2) axisymmetric, (3) constant $D_e$, (4) zero-order kinetics, (5) negligible convective transport in the ECS, and (6) uniform cell distribution.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute the volumetric oxygen consumption rate.**

$$q_{O_2} = q_{O_2,sp} \times X$$

Converting units carefully:
- $q_{O_2,sp} = 5 \times 10^{-17}$ mol/(cell·s)
- $X = 5 \times 10^7$ cells/mL $= 5 \times 10^{13}$ cells/m³

$$q_{O_2} = (5 \times 10^{-17}) \times (5 \times 10^{13}) = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{mol/(m}^3 \cdot \text{s)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Krogh radius.**

The number of fibers per unit cross-sectional area is related to packing density by:

$$n_L = \frac{\varphi}{\pi r_o^{2}}$$

Given $r_o = 150 \,\mu m = 1.5 \times 10^{-4}$ m:

$$\pi r_o^{2} = \pi (1.5 \times 10^{-4})^{2} = 7.069 \times 10^{-8} \;\text{m}^2$$

$$n_L = \frac{0.4}{7.069 \times 10^{-8}} = 5.66 \times 10^{6}\;\text{fibers/m}^2$$

Therefore:

$$R_K = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi n_L}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi \times 5.66 \times 10^{6}}} = 2.37 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{m} \;\approx\; 237\;\mu m$$

**Step 3 — Compute the critical radius.**

Convert $D_e$ to SI: $D_e = 2 \times 10^{-5}$ cm²/s $= 2 \times 10^{-9}$ m²/s.
Convert $C_w$ to SI: $C_w = 0.20$ mM $= 0.20 \;\text{mol/m}^3$.

$$R_{crit} = \sqrt{\frac{4 D_e C_w}{q_{O_2}}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 2 \times 10^{-9} \times 0.20}{2.5 \times 10^{-3}}}$$

$$R_{crit} = \sqrt{\frac{1.6 \times 10^{-9}}{2.5 \times 10^{-3}}} = \sqrt{6.4 \times 10^{-7}} = 8.0 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{m} \;\approx\; 800\;\mu m$$

**Step 4 — Comment on design adequacy.**

Since $R_K = 237 \,\mu m \;\ll\; R_{crit} = 800 \,\mu m$, the design is **oxygen-sufficient** — cells at the periphery of every Krogh cylinder receive adequate oxygen. The bioreactor can theoretically be operated at this cell density without central necrosis.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
**(a)** Explain the **Krogh cylinder model** for substrate transport in a hollow fiber bioreactor. Define the **Thiele modulus** and **effectiveness factor** for a first-order immobilized-enzyme system, and discuss how the effectiveness factor varies with the Thiele modulus. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A hollow fiber enzyme reactor is used to catalyze a first-order hydrolysis reaction. The effective diffusivity of substrate in the ECS is $D_e = 1.5 \times 10^{-10}$ m²/s and the first-order rate constant is $k = 0.05$ s⁻¹. If the Krogh radius is $R_K = 200 \,\mu m$, calculate:
(i) the Thiele modulus $\phi$, and
(ii) the effectiveness factor $\eta$ using the cylindrical formula. Comment on whether the reactor is **diffusion-limited or kinetics-limited**. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

The **Krogh cylinder model** idealizes the geometry around each hollow fiber as a cylinder of radius $R_K$ containing a homogeneous, immobilized biological phase. The model assumes steady-state, axisymmetric, Fickian diffusion with simultaneous reaction. The substrate balance is:

$$\frac{D_e}{r} \frac{d}{dr}\!\left( r \frac{dC_S}{dr} \right) = r_S(C_S)$$

For first-order kinetics $r_S = k C_S$, the solution in terms of Bessel functions yields:

$$C_S(r) = C_{S,b} \frac{I_0(\phi \, r / R_K)}{I_0(\phi)}$$

The **Thiele modulus** $\phi = R_K \sqrt{k / D_e}$ is the dimensionless ratio of the reaction rate to the diffusion rate. The **effectiveness factor** $\eta$ is the ratio of the *actual* overall reaction rate (with diffusion resistance) to the rate *if* all cells were exposed to the bulk concentration $C_{S,b}$. For a cylindrical geometry:

$$\eta = \frac{2 I_1(\phi)}{\phi I_0(\phi)}$$

For $\phi \to 0$: $\eta \to 1$ — reaction is rate-limiting (kinetics-limited). For $\phi \to 10$: $\eta \to 2/\phi$ — diffusion is rate-limiting. The "crossover" region around $\phi \approx 1$ is where both resistances are comparable.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Calculate Thiele modulus.**

$$R_K = 200 \;\mu m = 2.0 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{m}$$

$$\phi = R_K \sqrt{\frac{k}{D_e}} = (2.0 \times 10^{-4}) \sqrt{\frac{0.05}{1.5 \times 10^{-10}}}$$

$$\phi = (2.0 \times 10^{-4}) \times \sqrt{3.33 \times 10^{8}}$$

$$\phi = (2.0 \times 10^{-4}) \times (1.826 \times 10^{4}) = 3.65$$

**Step 2 — Calculate effectiveness factor using Bessel functions.**

For $\phi = 3.65$, we look up (or compute) the modified Bessel functions:
- $I_0(3.65) \approx 11.93$
- $I_1(3.65) \approx 10.64$

$$\eta = \frac{2 \times 10.64}{3.65 \times 11.93} = \frac{21.28}{43.55} \approx 0.489$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation.**

Since $\eta \approx 0.49$ (significantly less than 1) and $\phi = 3.65 > 1$, the reactor operates in the **intermediate-to-diffusion-limited regime**. About 49% of the theoretical maximum productivity is being achieved. To improve performance, one could:
- **Reduce $R_K$** (use smaller shell diameter or higher fiber density) — lowers $\phi$.
- **Increase $D_e$** (use a more porous fiber wall) — lowers $\phi$.
- **Increase substrate concentration** in the lumen to compensate for the diffusion gradient.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Hollow Fiber Reactors (Module 1)**
>
> ✅ **Definition:** Membrane-based immobilized-cell/enzyme reactor with cells in the shell and perfusion through the fiber lumen.
>
> ✅ **Key Geometries:** Fiber inner diameter 100–500 µm; shell-side cell density 10⁷–10⁸ cells/mL; packing density φ = 0.3–0.6.
>
> ✅ **Krogh Cylinder Model:** $R_K = 1 / \sqrt{\pi n_L}$ — represents the ECS control volume per fiber.
>
> ✅ **Zero-Order O₂ Profile:** $C(r) = C_w + (q_{O_2}/4D_e)(r_o^2 - r^2) + (q_{O_2}R_K^2 / 2D_e)\ln(r/r_o)$.
>
> ✅ **Critical Radius:** $R_{crit} = \sqrt{4 D_e C_w / q_{O_2}}$ — design limit; require $R_K < R_{crit}$.
>
> ✅ **Damköhler Number:** $Da = q_{O_2} R_K^2 / (D_e C_w^*)$ — diffusion-limited when $Da \gg 1$.
>
> ✅ **Thiele Modulus:** $\phi = R_K \sqrt{k / D_e}$ — reaction-diffusion dimensionless number.
>
> ✅ **Cylindrical Effectiveness Factor:** $\eta = 2 I_1(\phi) / [\phi I_0(\phi)]$ — use this, NOT the spherical one.
>
> ✅ **Spherical Effectiveness (for comparison):** $\eta = (3/\phi^2)(\phi \coth \phi - 1)$.
>
> ✅ **Lumen Sherwood (Leveque):** $Sh = 1.62 (Re \cdot Sc \cdot d_f / L)^{1/3}$ — for developing flow.
>
> ✅ **Two-BC Rule for Radial Derivations:** (1) symmetry/no-flux at $R_K$ or $r=0$, (2) known concentration or flux at the wall $r = r_o$.
>
> ✅ **High-MW Product Harvesting:** Periodic ECS flush (mAbs cannot cross the membrane).
>
> ✅ **Low-MW Product Harvesting:** Continuous lumen outlet (small molecules diffuse both ways).
>
> ✅ **Oxygen-Sufficiency Design Rule:** Always verify $R_K \le R_{crit}$ to avoid necrotic cores.
>
> ✅ **Industrial Relevance:** mAb production, bioartificial liver, enzyme cascades, oxygenators, dialyzers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT526 - BIOREACTOR DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
