<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Centrifugal Field Reactors — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Centrifugal Field Bioreactor** (also termed a *Centrifugal Contact Bioreactor* or *High-G Bioreactor*) is a class of multiphase biochemical reactor in which the reaction medium is subjected to an artificially amplified gravitational field generated by controlled rotation of either the vessel itself, an internal rotor, or the working fluid. The induced centrifugal acceleration $(\omega^2 r)$ replaces (or supplements) the natural gravitational vector to drive phase separation, intensify fluid–fluid and fluid–solid contact, and substantially enhance volumetric mass-transfer rates $\left(k_L a\right)$ and heat-removal capacity per unit volume.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Lock:** A *centrifugal field reactor* in bioprocess engineering is any reactor whose primary driving force for phase contacting, residence-time distribution, or separation is the *centrifugal pseudo-force* $\vec{F}_c = m\vec{\omega}\times(\vec{\omega}\times\vec{r})$ acting on a rotating mass element.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are making a milkshake in a blender. The blades spin so fast that the milk, sugar, and cocoa powder are **violently thrown outward** against the wall of the jar. Now imagine a *giant* version of that blender where, instead of a culinary mix, the "ingredients" are microbial cells, a nutrient broth, and a continuous stream of **sterile air** (oxygen). The spinning creates an enormously stronger version of gravity — often **hundreds to thousands of times Earth-gravity ($1\,g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$)** — and this artificial "hyper-gravity" does three engineering jobs simultaneously:

1. **It separates** the liquid culture from any gas or solid waste almost instantly (think of a spin dryer for clothes).
2. **It mixes** the gas–liquid interface so violently that oxygen dissolves into the broth far faster than in a normal stirred-tank fermenter.
3. **It compresses** the biomass against the rotor wall, creating a thin, well-controlled *biological film* that behaves almost like an immobilized-cell layer.

In essence, a centrifugal field reactor "cheats" gravity to give a microbe the best possible supply of oxygen, nutrients, and rapid removal of inhibitory by-products — all inside a single rotating device.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key contrast with a standard Stirred-Tank Reactor (STR):** In a STR, mass transfer is limited by the impeller tip speed (typically 2–5 m/s) and by the size of gas bubbles. In a centrifugal field reactor, the *entire fluid mass* can be accelerated to 20–100 m/s, so $k_L a$ values can exceed $0.5\ \text{s}^{-1}$ — an order of magnitude higher than classical STRs.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used in This Module

- Standard gravity: $\mathbf{g = 9.81\ m/s^2}$
- Density of water at 25 °C: $\mathbf{\rho_{H_2O} = 997\ kg/m^3}$
- Dynamic viscosity of water at 25 °C: $\mathbf{\mu_{H_2O} = 8.9 \times 10^{-4}\ Pa\cdot s}$
- Typical operating $G$-factor range: $\mathbf{G = 100\ \text{to}\ 10{,}000}$ (dimensionless multiple of $g$)
- Typical rotational speeds: $\mathbf{N = 500\ \text{to}\ 10{,}000\ rpm}$
- Typical rotor radius range: $\mathbf{r = 0.05\ \text{to}\ 0.5\ m}$

## 1.4 Classification of Centrifugal Field Bioreactors (KTU Module 1 Coverage)

| Family | Representative Device | Primary Function |
|---|---|---|
| Centrifugal Contactor | Podbielniak extractor, Alfa-Laval contactor | Liquid–liquid extraction with simultaneous reaction |
| Rotating Biological Contactor (RBC) variant | Centrifugal-disc biofilm reactor | Biofilm cultivation under high shear |
| Centrifugal Packed-Bed Reactor (CPBR) | Spinning packed rotor | Immobilized-enzyme/cell catalysis |
| Centrifugal Film Reactor | Spinning-disk evaporator type | Shear-thinned broths, viscous fermentations |
| Magnetically/Centrifugally Stabilized Fluidized Bed | Centrifugal fluidized bed | High-G solid-suspension biocatalysis |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Physics of the Centrifugal Field

When a fluid element of mass $m$ rotates at angular velocity $\vec{\omega}$ at radial position $\vec{r}$ from the axis, the body (frame) experiences a fictitious *centrifugal* force directed **radially outward**, of magnitude:

$$
F_c = m\,\omega^2 r
$$

The corresponding *centrifugal acceleration* is:

$$
a_c = \omega^2 r = \frac{v_\theta^2}{r}
$$

where $v_\theta = \omega r$ is the tangential velocity. The *dimensionless G-factor* compares this to Earth's gravity:

$$
G \equiv \frac{a_c}{g} = \frac{\omega^2 r}{g}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering significance of $G$:** A device spinning at $N = 3000\ \text{rpm}$ with rotor radius $r = 0.15\ \text{m}$ produces $G \approx 1500$. This means the effective "weight" of a 1-µm gas bubble is increased ~1500-fold, which is why coalescence and phase disengagement occur in **milliseconds** instead of seconds.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Theoretical Framework

1. **Generation of the field.** A motor drives a rotor at controlled $\omega$ (often via variable-frequency drive, VFD). For very high $G$, mag-lev or air-bearing supports are used to eliminate mechanical friction.

2. **Radial pressure gradient.** The rotating fluid establishes a hydrostatic-like pressure that rises with $r$:

$$
\frac{dP}{dr} = \rho\,\omega^2 r
$$

   Integrating from hub ($r_i$) to rim ($r_o$):

$$
P(r_o) - P(r_i) = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,\omega^2 \left(r_o^2 - r_i^2\right)
$$

   This is the *centrifugal pressure head* and directly determines the maximum permissible gas–liquid dispersion.

3. **Phase contacting.** Gas is introduced near the hub; centrifugal force throws the denser liquid outward while gas migrates inward. Counter-current contact is naturally established.

4. **Mass transfer intensification.** The high relative velocity between phases raises the *Sherwood number* via the Higbie penetration model:

$$
k_L = 2\,\sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi\,t_e}}
$$

   where $t_e$ (exposure time) is drastically reduced in the centrifugal field, thereby **increasing $k_L$**.

5. **Biomass retention.** Cells/enzymes can be retained as a thin film on the rotor wall, eliminating washout and enabling very high cell densities ($\sim$ 100–200 g dry-cell-weight/L).

6. **Heat removal.** The thin liquid film on a high-conductivity metal rotor enables very high $U$ (overall heat-transfer coefficient), often $> 5\ \text{kW/(m}^2\text{·K)}$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All variables below must be memorized for the ESE. Use **SI units** unless explicitly noted.

| Symbol | Formula / Definition | Typical Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| $F_c$ | $F_c = m\,\omega^2 r$ | N | Centrifugal force on mass $m$ |
| $a_c$ | $a_c = \omega^2 r$ | m/s² | Centrifugal acceleration |
| $G$ | $G = \omega^2 r / g$ | dimensionless | Multiplier over Earth gravity |
| $v_\theta$ | $v_\theta = \omega r = 2\pi N r / 60$ | m/s | Tangential velocity at radius $r$ |
| $\Delta P_\text{cent}$ | $\Delta P = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho\omega^2(r_o^2 - r_i^2)$ | Pa | Radial pressure rise across rotor |
| $P_\text{shaft}$ | $P = T\,\omega = 2\pi N T / 60$ | W | Mechanical power required |
| $N_p$ | $N_p = P / (\rho N^3 D^5)$ | dimensionless | Power number (impeller analogy) |
| $k_L a$ correlation | $k_L a = K\,(\epsilon / V)^{0.4} u_s^{0.5}$ | s⁻¹ | Mass-transfer coefficient (van't Riet) |
| Reynolds no. (rotor) | $Re = \rho \omega r^2 / \mu$ | dimensionless | Flow regime in rotating annulus |
| Taylor no. | $Ta = \frac{4\,\Omega^2 R^4 (R_o - R_i)^3}{\nu^2}$ | dimensionless | Stability of Couette flow between concentric cylinders |
| Centrifugal settling velocity | $v_s = \frac{d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f)\,\omega^2 r}{18\mu}$ | m/s | Stokes-law settling in radial field |
| Gas retention | $\varepsilon_g = f(\omega,\, Q_g,\, \rho)$ | dimensionless | Hold-up fraction (empirical) |

> [!NOTE]
> In markdown tables, the absolute-value bars are written as `\vert` so the table parser does not break. In handwritten work, you may write $|x|$ freely.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Bio-Engineering

- **Antibiotic production** (e.g., penicillin, erythromycin) at very high cell densities in centrifugal packed-bed reactors.
- **Whole-cell biocatalysis** for chiral intermediates — cells retained in the rotating annulus, fed with substrate once-through.
- **High-cell-density E. coli / Pichia pastoris cultures** for recombinant protein production.
- **Wastewater bio-oxidation** in centrifugal RBCs (rotating biological contactors) handling toxic or recalcitrant loads.
- **Photosynthetic cell culture** where the thin film provides uniform light penetration.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Radial Pressure Distribution in a Rotating Liquid

**Starting point:** For a fluid element in steady rigid-body rotation, the net force in the radial direction must provide the centripetal acceleration. In the rotating frame, the pressure gradient balances the centrifugal body force:

$$
\frac{dP}{dr} = \rho\,\omega^2 r
$$

**Step 1 — Separation of variables.** Multiply both sides by $dr$:

$$
dP = \rho\,\omega^2 r\,dr
$$

**Step 2 — Integrate from inner radius $r_i$ to outer radius $r_o$:**

$$
\int_{P_i}^{P_o} dP = \rho\,\omega^2 \int_{r_i}^{r_o} r\,dr
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate both integrals:**

$$
P_o - P_i = \rho\,\omega^2 \left[\frac{r^2}{2}\right]_{r_i}^{r_o}
$$

$$
P_o - P_i = \frac{1}{2}\,\rho\,\omega^2 \left(r_o^2 - r_i^2\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Express in terms of $G$-factor at the rim.** Using $G_o = \omega^2 r_o / g$:

$$
\Delta P = \frac{1}{2}\,\rho\,g\,G_o\,r_o - \frac{1}{2}\,\rho\,\omega^2 r_i^2
$$

> If the hub is essentially at atmospheric pressure (open to gas feed), then $P_i \approx P_{atm}$ and the *gauge* pressure at the rim is:

$$
P_o - P_{atm} = \frac{1}{2}\,\rho\,\omega^2 \left(r_o^2 - r_i^2\right)
$$

**Numerical check.** Take $\rho = 1000\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $N = 3000\ \text{rpm}\ (\omega = 2\pi\times 50 = 314.16\ \text{rad/s})$, $r_i = 0.05\ \text{m}$, $r_o = 0.15\ \text{m}$:

$$
\Delta P = \tfrac{1}{2}(1000)(314.16)^2(0.15^2 - 0.05^2)
$$

$$
\Delta P = 500 \times 98{,}696 \times (0.0225 - 0.0025)
$$

$$
\Delta P = 500 \times 98{,}696 \times 0.02 = 9.87 \times 10^{5}\ \text{Pa} \approx 9.87\ \text{bar}
$$

This ≈10 bar rim pressure explains why **pressure-rated vessels and rotors are mandatory** for centrifugal bioreactors.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Centrifugal Settling Velocity (Stokes Regime)

For a particle of diameter $d_p$, density $\rho_p$, suspended in fluid of density $\rho_f$ and viscosity $\mu$, the radial settling velocity in a centrifugal field is found by equating drag force to net centrifugal body force:

$$
F_\text{drag} = 3\pi\mu d_p\,v_s
$$

$$
F_\text{body} = \tfrac{\pi}{6}d_p^3 (\rho_p - \rho_f)\,\omega^2 r
$$

Equate:

$$
3\pi\mu d_p\,v_s = \tfrac{\pi}{6}d_p^3 (\rho_p - \rho_f)\,\omega^2 r
$$

Solve for $v_s$:

$$
v_s = \frac{d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f)\,\omega^2 r}{18\,\mu}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** Compare with gravitational settling $v_{s,g} = d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f)\,g / 18\mu$. The ratio is exactly $G = \omega^2 r / g$. So a $G = 1000$ field settles a cell 1000× faster — explaining why residence times in centrifugal bioreactors are in **seconds to minutes** instead of hours.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Power Number and Aeration Power Requirement

For a spinning rotor in a baffled housing, the dimensionless Power Number is:

$$
N_p = \frac{P}{\rho\,N^3 D^5}
$$

**Step 1 — Impeller analogy.** The rotor of diameter $D = 2 r_o$ rotating at $N$ (rev/s) imparts a tangential velocity $v_\theta = \pi N D$.

**Step 2 — Kinetic-energy flux.** The power imparted equals mass flow × kinetic energy per unit mass:

$$
P = \dot{m}\cdot\tfrac{1}{2} v_\theta^2 = \rho\,Q\,\tfrac{1}{2}(\pi N D)^2
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and simplify:**

$$
P = \tfrac{1}{2}\pi^2 \rho\,Q\,N^2 D^2
$$

**Step 4 — Express in Power-Number form:**

$$
N_p = \frac{\tfrac{1}{2}\pi^2 \rho\,Q\,N^2 D^2}{\rho\,N^3 D^5} = \frac{\pi^2 Q}{2\,N\,D^3}
$$

For typical centrifugal bioreactors, $N_p$ ranges from **1 to 5** in turbulent regime, slightly higher than Rushton impellers due to high wall shear.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Centrifugal Reactor

| Criterion | Centrifugal Contactor | Centrifugal Packed Bed | Rotating Biofilm Disc | Centrifugal Film |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Preferred scale | Pilot → Production | Lab → Pilot | Lab → Pilot | Lab |
| Suitable broth viscosity | Low–Medium | Medium | Low–Medium | Low |
| Cell density achievable (gDCW/L) | 30–80 | 80–200 (immobilized) | 50–150 (biofilm) | 20–60 |
| Typical $k_L a$ (s⁻¹) | 0.1–0.5 | 0.05–0.2 | 0.02–0.1 | 0.05–0.3 |
| Main advantage | Very fast phase separation | Very high catalyst loading | Long cell retention | Handles viscous/shear-thinned broths |
| Main limitation | High capital cost, sealing | Heat removal from bed | Diffusional biofilm resistance | Foaming at high $G$ |
| Typical NEP 2020 / KTU application | Two-phase biocatalysis | Recombinant protein | Wastewater bio-oxidation | Filamentous-fungus fermentation |

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow — Centrifugal Packed-Bed Bioreactor (CPBR)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Nutrient Feed Reservoir] --> B[Peristaltic Feed Pump]
    B --> C[Hub Inlet Manifold]
    C --> D[Rotating Packed Rotor]
    D --> E[Immobilized-Cell / Enzyme Packing]
    E --> F[Liquid Film on Packing Surface]
    F --> G[Rim Collector]
    G --> H[Heat Exchanger / Cooler]
    H --> I[Product Outlet Tank]
    D -.-> J[Sterile Air / O2 Inlet at Hub]
    J --> K[Radial Gas Channel]
    K --> L[Counter-current Gas Outflow at Rim]
    L --> M[Off-gas Condenser / Analyzer]
    M --> N[CO2 and O2 Mass Spectrometer]
    D --> O[Drive Motor and VFD Controller]
    O --> P[Tachometer and G-factor Display]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FORCE[Force Generation Block]
        F1[Electric Motor] --> F2[Variable Frequency Drive]
        F2 --> F3[Shaft and Bearings]
        F3 --> F4[Rotor / Impeller]
    end

    subgraph TRANSPORT[Fluid Transport Block]
        T1[Liquid Inlet] --> T2[Hub Distributor]
        T2 --> T3[Rotating Annulus / Packing]
        T3 --> T4[Rim Collector]
        T4 --> T5[Liquid Outlet]
        G1[Gas Inlet] --> T2
        T3 --> G2[Gas Outlet]
    end

    subgraph REACT[Reaction and Mass-Transfer Block]
        R1[Gas-Liquid Interface] --> R2[Liquid-Solid Biofilm]
        R2 --> R3[Intracellular Metabolism]
        R3 --> R4[Biomass and Product]
    end

    subgraph CONTROL[Instrumentation Block]
        C1[Temperature Sensor] --> C2[PID Controller]
        C3[pH and DO Sensor] --> C2
        C2 --> F2
        C2 --> H1[Coolant Flow Valve]
    end

    F4 --> T3
    T3 --> R1
    R4 --> T4
    C1 --> T3
    H1 --> T3
```

## 4.3 Centrifugal Force vs Radius — Concept Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S0[Input: N rpm and rotor radius r] --> S1[Compute angular velocity omega = 2 pi N / 60]
    S1 --> S2[Compute tangential velocity v_theta = omega r]
    S1 --> S3[Compute centrifugal acceleration a_c = omega squared r]
    S3 --> S4[Compute G factor G = a_c / g]
    S2 --> S5[Compute Reynolds number Re = rho v_theta r / mu]
    S3 --> S6[Compute radial pressure rise Delta P = half rho omega squared r_o squared minus r_i squared]
    S4 --> S7[Check if G between 100 and 10000]
    S5 --> S8[Identify flow regime: laminar Re less than 2300, transitional, turbulent]
    S6 --> S9[Compare with rotor pressure rating]
    S7 -->|Yes| S10[Design acceptable]
    S7 -->|No| S11[Redesign: change N or r]
    S8 --> S10
    S9 -->|Below rating| S10
    S9 -->|Above rating| S11
```

## 4.4 Operating-Regime Map (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Low G less than 100] -->|Mostly gravitational settling| R2[Soft separation - similar to STR]
    R3[Medium G 100 to 1000] -->|Rapid gas-liquid contact| R4[High kLa fermentation]
    R5[High G 1000 to 10000] -->|Film formation, biofilm| R6[Immobilized-cell biocatalysis]
    R7[Very High G above 10000] -->|Rotor stress, sealing issues| R8[Generally not used in biology]
```

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a centrifugal field bioreactor. What is the significance of the G-factor?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A centrifugal field bioreactor is a multiphase biochemical reactor in which the reaction medium is rotated at high angular velocity $\omega$, generating a centrifugal pseudo-force that intensifies phase contact, mass transfer, and biomass retention beyond what is achievable under normal gravity. (2 marks)

The **G-factor**, $G = \omega^2 r / g$, is the dimensionless ratio of centrifugal acceleration to Earth's gravity. It is significant because it directly scales up (a) gas–liquid mass-transfer coefficient $k_L a$, (b) particle settling velocity, and (c) the radial pressure head. A typical $G$ range of 100–10 000 enables second-scale phase separation and $k_L a$ up to $0.5\ \text{s}^{-1}$. (1 mark)

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any three advantages of centrifugal field reactors over conventional stirred-tank reactors.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Higher $k_L a$** — order-of-magnitude improvement due to high relative velocity between phases. (1 mark)
2. **Cell retention / no wash-out** — biomass held in the rotating zone enables very high cell densities. (1 mark)
3. **Faster phase separation** — counter-current gas–liquid disengagement in milliseconds reduces downstream cost. (1 mark)

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the working principle of a **Centrifugal Packed-Bed Bioreactor (CPBR)**. Discuss its key components and operating zones. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A centrifugal bioreactor operates at $N = 4000\ \text{rpm}$ with inner radius $r_i = 0.04\ \text{m}$ and outer radius $r_o = 0.20\ \text{m}$. The broth has $\rho = 1020\ \text{kg/m}^3$. Compute:
  (i) The angular velocity $\omega$ in rad/s.  
  (ii) The G-factor at the rim.  
  (iii) The radial pressure rise $\Delta P$ between hub and rim.  
  (iv) The centrifugal settling velocity of a 5-µm cell cluster ($\rho_p = 1080\ \text{kg/m}^3$) in broth of $\mu = 1.2 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{Pa·s}$ at $r = 0.10\ \text{m}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

- **Working principle (3 marks):** A CPBR uses a rotating annular basket packed with immobilized biocatalyst (cells or enzymes on a porous support). The broth is fed at the hub and flung outward through the packing by centrifugal force, while sterile air is introduced counter-currently. The high $G$ ensures a thin, well-mixed liquid film on the support, minimizing external mass-transfer resistance.
- **Key components (2 marks):** Drive motor + VFD, shaft + bearings (often air-bearing for high $G$), rotor basket with packing, hub inlet manifold, rim liquid collector, gas inlet/outlet, temperature and DO probes, cooling jacket.
- **Operating zones (2 marks):** (i) Hub zone — fresh feed + gas mixing; (ii) Bulk-packing zone — main reaction; (iii) Rim zone — liquid collection, gas disengagement, heat-exchange.

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Angular velocity (1 mark):**

$$
\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 4000}{60} = 418.88\ \text{rad/s}
$$

**(ii) G-factor at the rim (1 mark):**

$$
G_o = \frac{\omega^2 r_o}{g} = \frac{(418.88)^2 \times 0.20}{9.81}
$$

$$
G_o = \frac{175{,}460 \times 0.20}{9.81} = \frac{35{,}092}{9.81} \approx 3577
$$

**(iii) Radial pressure rise (2 marks):**

$$
\Delta P = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho\omega^2\left(r_o^2 - r_i^2\right)
$$

$$
\Delta P = \tfrac{1}{2}(1020)(418.88)^2\left(0.20^2 - 0.04^2\right)
$$

$$
\Delta P = 510 \times 175{,}460 \times (0.04 - 0.0016) = 510 \times 175{,}460 \times 0.0384
$$

$$
\Delta P \approx 3.44 \times 10^{6}\ \text{Pa} \approx 34.4\ \text{bar}
$$

**(iv) Centrifugal settling velocity (3 marks):**

$$
v_s = \frac{d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f)\,\omega^2 r}{18\,\mu}
$$

$$
d_p = 5\ \mu\text{m} = 5\times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}
$$

$$
\Delta\rho = 1080 - 1020 = 60\ \text{kg/m}^3
$$

$$
v_s = \frac{(5\times 10^{-6})^2 \times 60 \times (418.88)^2 \times 0.10}{18 \times 1.2\times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
v_s = \frac{2.5\times 10^{-11} \times 60 \times 175{,}460 \times 0.10}{2.16 \times 10^{-2}}
$$

$$
v_s = \frac{2.6317 \times 10^{-5}}{2.16\times 10^{-2}} \approx 1.22 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m/s} = 1.22\ \text{mm/s}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently (a) confuse $N$ in rpm with $N$ in rev/s when computing $\omega$, and (b) forget to square the *outer* radius, $r_o$, in the $\Delta P$ formula. Always write $r_o^2 - r_i^2$, **not** $(r_o - r_i)^2$. This single error costs ~1 mark.

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** Compare **centrifugal contactor bioreactors** and **centrifugal packed-bed bioreactors** with respect to (i) phase contact mechanism, (ii) typical $k_L a$, (iii) achievable cell density, (iv) major application, and (v) primary engineering limitation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the relationship between the **G-factor** and the **volumetric mass-transfer coefficient $k_L a$** using the Higbie penetration model. Show clearly how a ten-fold increase in $G$ affects $k_L a$ in a centrifugal bioreactor where the bubble residence time scales as $t_e \propto G^{-0.5}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

| S.No. | Criterion | Centrifugal Contactor | Centrifugal Packed Bed |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) | Phase contact | Continuous counter-current, droplet–droplet or bubble–liquid | Liquid film over immobilized biocatalyst |
| (ii) | Typical $k_L a$ | 0.1 – 0.5 s⁻¹ | 0.05 – 0.2 s⁻¹ |
| (iii) | Cell density | 30 – 80 gDCW/L (suspended) | 80 – 200 gDCW/L (immobilized) |
| (iv) | Major application | Two-phase enzymatic synthesis, antibiotic extraction | Recombinant protein, chiral-API biocatalysis |
| (v) | Limitation | High seal/seal-leak risk at high $G$ | Heat removal from packed bed, pressure drop |

(1.4 marks per row × 5 = 7 marks; award proportional marks for any 5 correctly filled rows.)

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Higbie penetration model (2 marks):**

$$
k_L = 2\,\sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi\,t_e}}
$$

The *volumetric* mass-transfer coefficient is:

$$
k_L a = 2\,a\,\sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi\,t_e}}
$$

where $a$ is the interfacial area per unit volume $\left(\text{m}^2/\text{m}^3\right)$ and $t_e$ is the gas–liquid contact (exposure) time.

**Step 1 — Express $t_e$ in terms of $G$ (2 marks):** The bubble radial transit time is the rim-to-hub distance divided by the bubble's terminal velocity in the centrifugal field. From the Stokes derivation above, the bubble velocity scales linearly with $G$:

$$
v_b \propto G
$$

Hence exposure time $t_e \propto 1/G$. The given scaling $t_e \propto G^{-0.5}$ accounts for partial turbulent enhancement:

$$
t_e = t_0 \, G^{-0.5}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into $k_L a$ (2 marks):**

$$
k_L a = 2\,a\,\sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi\,t_0\,G^{-0.5}}}
      = 2\,a\,\sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi\,t_0}}\;G^{0.25}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,k_L a \;\propto\; G^{0.25}\,}
$$

**Step 3 — Quantitative effect (1 mark):** A ten-fold increase in $G$ gives:

$$
\frac{(k_L a)_\text{new}}{(k_L a)_\text{old}} = 10^{0.25} \approx 1.78
$$

So $k_L a$ increases by **~78 %** for a 10× increase in $G$ — a strong but sub-linear improvement, consistent with empirical data from high-G fermenters.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students assume $k_L a$ scales linearly with $G$ or with $G^2$ — both wrong. The Higbie model rigorously gives the **quarter-power** dependence. Marks are deducted for stating any other exponent without derivation.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-revision checklist (memorize before walking into the ESE hall):**

- Centrifugal bioreactor = a reactor whose **driving force** is the centrifugal pseudo-force $F_c = m\omega^2 r$.
- G-factor definition: $G = \omega^2 r / g$. Typical biological range: **100 ≤ G ≤ 10 000**.
- Radial pressure rise: $\Delta P = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho\omega^2(r_o^2 - r_i^2)$. Always use $r_o^2 - r_i^2$, **not** $(r_o - r_i)^2$.
- Centrifugal settling velocity: $v_s = d_p^2(\rho_p - \rho_f)\omega^2 r / 18\mu$. Scales linearly with $G$.
- Tangential velocity: $v_\theta = 2\pi N r / 60$ when $N$ is in rpm.
- Power number: $N_p = P / (\rho N^3 D^5)$ — for centrifugal bioreactors typically **1–5**.
- Higbie model gives $k_L a \propto G^{0.25}$ when $t_e \propto G^{-0.5}$; a 10× $G$ boost → ~78 % $k_L a$ boost.
- Main **engineering merits:** high $k_L a$, cell retention, fast phase separation, good heat removal.
- Main **engineering limits:** high capital cost, sealing at high $G$, rotor pressure rating (often 10–35 bar), heat removal in packed variants.
- Always keep **SI units** in the final answer; KTU examiners deduct ½ mark for mixed-unit errors.
- Diagram questions expect: feed inlet (hub) → rotating bed/annulus → rim collector → product; counter-current gas lines; instrumentation loop.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT526 - BIOREACTOR DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Rotating Drum Bioreactor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Rotating Drum Bioreactor (RDB)** is a horizontally-oriented, cylindrical vessel bioreactor in which biological reactions occur while the drum is rotated about its longitudinal axis at controlled speeds (typically **1 – 50 rpm**, most commonly **5 – 15 rpm**). The gentle tumbling action imparted by rotation provides **periodic mixing, aeration, and heat/mass transfer** between the substrate bed and the gas headspace, making the RDB especially suited for **Solid-State Fermentation (SSF)**, **composting**, **tissue engineering scaffolds**, and **biofilm cultivation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** RDB is classified under *mechanically-agitated solid-bed bioreactors* and is contrasted with stirred-tank, airlift, bubble-column, and packed-bed reactors covered elsewhere in Module 1. The defining geometric feature is the **aspect ratio (L/D)**, generally held between **0.5 and 4.0**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tumble-Dryer of Biology"

Imagine a **front-loading clothes dryer** running in slow motion. Instead of wet clothes, the drum holds a moist, nutrient-rich solid substrate (sawdust, wheat bran, agricultural waste) inoculated with a microorganism. As the drum turns, gravity pulls the substrate bed upwards along the wall; beyond a critical angle, the bed **avalanches** back down. This continuous "lift-and-fall" cycle:

- **Mixes** the substrate without mechanical shear blades,
- **Exposes** fresh surface area to the inlet air, and
- **Evacuates** CO₂ / metabolic heat through the headspace.

The same principle is used in **cement mixers, rotary kilns, and grain dryers** — only the "reaction" inside is biological rather than chemical or thermal.

## 1.3 Key Operating Metrics (Standard Range)

| Metric | Typical Range | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Rotation speed, **N** | 1 – 50 rpm | Controls mixing intensity & shear |
| Drum diameter, **D** | 0.1 – 3.0 m | Determines throughput |
| Aspect ratio, **L/D** | 0.5 – 4.0 | Influences plug-flow character |
| Loading fraction, **φ** | 0.20 – 0.50 of V | Empty volume required for tumbling |
| Aeration rate | 0.1 – 2.0 vvm | Oxygen/heat removal |
| Power density | 10 – 1000 W/m³ | Energy per unit volume |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biomass density of substrate** typically lies in **ρ_s = 500 – 1200 kg/m³** (moist solids), and **bed moisture content** is held between **40 – 80 % w/w** for most SSF processes. These two physical constants govern nearly every subsequent design calculation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Regimes of a Rotating Drum

The motion of the granular bed inside a rotating drum is governed primarily by the **dimensionless Froude number**, which compares centrifugal to gravitational forces:

$$Fr = \frac{N^{2} D}{g}$$

where **N** is the rotational speed in **rev/s**, **D** is the drum diameter in **m**, and **g = 9.81 m/s²** is gravitational acceleration.

As **Fr** increases, the bed transitions through five distinct regimes. Each regime has a characteristic "look" inside the drum and a sharply different mixing/heat-transfer efficiency.

### Regime I — Slipping (Fr < 10⁻³)
- The drum wall slides under the stationary bed.
- **No relative motion**, no mixing.
- **Inadmissible** for a bioreactor.

### Regime II — Slumping (10⁻³ < Fr < 10⁻²)
- The bed is dragged part-way up, then slides back as a coherent plug.
- **Avalanching** at low frequency; poor aeration.

### Regime III — Cascading / Rolling (10⁻² < Fr < 10⁻¹) ⭐ IDEAL
- The upper surface of the bed continuously cascades down.
- Excellent mixing, gentle shear, good gas–solid contact.
- **This is the KTU-specified design target for an RDB.**

### Regime IV — Cataracting (10⁻¹ < Fr < 1)
- Particles are **thrown across the drum** as free-falling streams.
- Aggressive mixing but **high mechanical shear** that may damage hyphae or mammalian cells.

### Regime V — Centrifuging (Fr > 1)
- Centrifugal force pins the bed to the drum wall.
- **Mixing ceases**; the bioreactor is effectively stalled.

> [!TIP]
> **Board-exam strategy:** When a problem gives you N and D, *first* compute Fr, *then* identify the regime, *then* select the appropriate power-number correlation. Examiners award marks for regime identification, not just the final number.

## 2.2 Power Consumption — The Buckingham-Pi Framework

The power required to rotate a filled drum depends on six variables. By dimensional analysis (Buckingham's Π-theorem) the power is expressed as a **Power number (N_p)** that is a function of two dimensionless groups — Froude and Reynolds:

$$N_{p} = \frac{P}{\rho_{b} \, N^{3} \, D^{5}} = f\!\left( Fr,\; Re \right)$$

where the **bed Reynolds number** is:

$$Re = \frac{\rho_{b} \, N \, D^{2}}{\mu_{b}}$$

and ρ_b, μ_b are the *effective bulk density and viscosity* of the substrate bed.

### 2.2.1 The Froude-Corrected Power Number

For tumbling beds (Re < 10³), gravity cannot be neglected, so we write:

$$N_{p}^{*} = N_{p} \cdot \left(Fr\right)^{-m}$$

Empirically, for unbaffled drums the exponent **m ≈ 0.18 – 0.25** (Armenante & Li, 1993-style correlations). The KTU-acceptable simplified form is:

$$P = N_{p}^{*} \, \rho_{b} \, N^{3} \, D^{5} \cdot \left(\frac{Fr}{Fr_{ref}}\right)^{m}$$

with **Fr_ref = 1.0** (dimensionless reference).

## 2.3 Mass Transfer in an RDB

Oxygen transfer in SSF-RDBs is typically the **rate-limiting step**. A widely-used empirical correlation is:

$$k_{L} a = K \cdot N^{a_{1}} \cdot Q^{a_{2}} \cdot \phi^{a_{3}}$$

where **Q** is the volumetric aeration rate, **φ** is the loading fraction, and **K, a₁, a₂, a₃** are substrate-specific empirical constants (for *Aspergillus niger* on wheat bran, K ≈ 12, a₁ ≈ 0.45, a₂ ≈ 0.62, a₃ ≈ -0.30 — values cited in standard SSF literature).

## 2.4 Heat Transfer

Heat removal is critical because SSF is highly exothermic. The overall heat-transfer coefficient for a jacketed RDB is:

$$\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_{i}} + \frac{\delta}{k_{w}} + \frac{1}{h_{o}}$$

where **h_i** (inside, drum wall) is correlated to N and φ, **δ/k_w** is the wall resistance, and **h_o** is the jacket-side coefficient.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is your **revision cheat-sheet**. Every quantity, its symbol, its defining equation, and its SI unit is listed. Use **\vert** instead of the pipe character anywhere in expressions so the markdown table renders correctly.

| # | Quantity / Symbol | Equation | SI Unit | Notes / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Froude number, Fr | $Fr = N^{2} D \,/\, g$ | dimensionless | Cascading: 10⁻² \vert 10⁻¹ |
| 2 | Reynolds number, Re | $Re = \rho_{b} N D^{2} \,/\, \mu_{b}$ | dimensionless | RDB beds: Re \vert 10² |
| 3 | Power number, N_p | $N_{p} = P \,/\, \left(\rho_{b} N^{3} D^{5}\right)$ | dimensionless | Empty drum: \vert 1 ; filled: 0.3 – 3 |
| 4 | Froude-corrected N_p | $N_{p}^{*} = N_{p} \left(Fr\right)^{-m}$ | dimensionless | m ≈ 0.18 – 0.25 |
| 5 | Power input, P | $P = N_{p}^{*} \, \rho_{b} \, N^{3} \, D^{5}$ | W (J/s) | Multiply by η_motor for shaft power |
| 6 | Bulk bed volume, V_b | $V_{b} = \phi \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^{2} L$ | m³ | φ = 0.20 – 0.50 |
| 7 | Tumbling frequency, f_t | $f_{t} = N \,/\, 60$ | s⁻¹ | Revolutions per second |
| 8 | Shear rate (approx.), γ̇ | $\dot{\gamma} \approx \pi N$ | s⁻¹ | Valid for rotating surface |
| 9 | k_La (oxygen transfer) | $k_{L} a = K N^{a_{1}} Q^{a_{2}} \phi^{a_{3}}$ | s⁻¹ | Empirical; KTU permits generic form |
| 10 | Damköhler number, Da | $Da = r_{max} \,/\, \left(k_{L} a \cdot C^{*}\right)$ | dimensionless | Da \vert 0.1 means transport-limited |
| 11 | Aeration number, N_a | $N_{a} = Q \,/\, \left(N D^{3}\right)$ | dimensionless | Compares gas vs. rotation |
| 12 | Power density | $P \,/\, V_{b}$ | W/m³ | Typical: 10 – 1000 W/m³ |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world engineering use:** These formulae are used in scaling RDBs from **lab (1 L)** → **pilot (100 L)** → **industrial (50 m³)** for enzyme, biopesticide, and bioethanol plants. The Froude-criterion is the single most important design constraint — *all* scale-up rules preserve **Fr** constant.

## 2.6 Design Philosophy — Why Tumbling and Not Stirring

The RDB was developed as an alternative to helical-impeller or paddle-agitated SSF reactors for three engineering reasons:

1. **Shear-sensitive cultures** (filamentous fungi, plant cells, mammalian cells on microcarriers) are damaged by Rushton turbines but tolerate the *gentle bulk tumbling* of an RDB.
2. **Self-cleaning action** — the cascading bed naturally dislodges biomass from the wall, preventing fouling.
3. **Energy efficiency** — power per unit mass transferred is lower in tumbling than in impeller-driven SSF reactors because most of the energy is dissipated as lift against gravity, not as turbulent shear.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — The Froude Number from First Principles

We begin with **Newton's second law** for a particle of mass **m_p** stuck to the inner wall of a drum of diameter **D** rotating at angular velocity **ω = 2πN**:

The centripetal force required to keep the particle on the circular path of radius **R = D/2** is:

$$F_{c} = m_{p} \, \omega^{2} \, R = m_{p} \left(2\pi N\right)^{2} \left(\frac{D}{2}\right)$$

The opposing gravitational force holding the particle down is:

$$F_{g} = m_{p} \, g$$

The Froude number is defined as the ratio of these two competing forces:

$$Fr = \frac{F_{c}}{F_{g}} = \frac{m_{p} \left(2\pi N\right)^{2} \left(D/2\right)}{m_{p} \, g}$$

The particle mass **m_p** cancels. Simplify the angular-velocity term:

$$Fr = \frac{\left(2\pi\right)^{2} N^{2} \, (D/2)}{g} = \frac{4\pi^{2}}{2} \cdot \frac{N^{2} D}{g}$$

$$Fr = 2\pi^{2} \cdot \frac{N^{2} D}{g}$$

Since the factor **2π² ≈ 19.74** is a pure numerical constant absorbed into the *definition* of Froude number (we define Fr with the squared speed rather than the angular speed), the **engineering form** used throughout the literature is:

$$\boxed{\,Fr \;=\; \dfrac{N^{2} D}{g}\,}$$

**Each line explained:**

- **Line 1:** Newton's second law on the wall particle — centrifugal force depends on ω²R.
- **Line 2:** Weight is m_p·g; this is the only force resisting "lift-off" of the particle.
- **Line 3:** Forming the dimensionless ratio gives a pure number.
- **Line 4:** Algebraic simplification of (2π)²·(D/2) = 2π²·D.
- **Line 5:** The conventional engineering definition drops the numerical coefficient to give the cleaner form N²D/g.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why drop the 2π²?** Because in dimensionless analysis Froude is *defined* by the squared translational speed (N²D/g), not the angular one. The factor 2π² is folded into the empirical *critical Fr* values used to demarcate the regimes.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Power Number by Dimensional Analysis

The power **P** drawn by a rotating, filled drum depends on the following physically-relevant variables:

$$P = f\!\left( \rho_{b},\; \mu_{b},\; N,\; D,\; g,\; L \right)$$

- **ρ_b** — bulk density of the bed [kg/m³]
- **μ_b** — effective viscosity of the bed [Pa·s]
- **N** — rotational speed [s⁻¹]
- **D** — drum diameter [m]
- **g** — gravitational acceleration [m/s²]
- **L** — drum length [m]

Total variables: **n = 7**.  Fundamental dimensions involved: **M, L, T** (k = 3).  By Buckingham's Π-theorem, the number of dimensionless groups is **n − k = 4**.

We choose **ρ_b, N, D** as the repeating set (they span M, L, T independently). The four Π-groups are:

### Π₁ — Power number

$$\Pi_{1} = P \cdot \rho_{b}^{a} N^{b} D^{c}$$

For dimensional homogeneity:

- **M:** 0 = 1 + a → a = −1
- **L:** 0 = 2 + 2a − 2b + 2c → substitute a = −1 → 0 = 2 − 2 − 2b + 2c → c = b
- **T:** 0 = −3 − b → b = −3 → c = −3

Therefore:

$$\Pi_{1} = \frac{P}{\rho_{b} \, N^{3} \, D^{5}} \;\equiv\; N_{p}$$

### Π₂ — Reynolds number

$$\Pi_{2} = \mu_{b} \cdot \rho_{b}^{a} N^{b} D^{c}$$

Setting exponents of M, L, T to zero simultaneously gives **a = 1, b = 1, c = 2**:

$$\Pi_{2} = \frac{\rho_{b} N D^{2}}{\mu_{b}} \;\equiv\; Re$$

### Π₃ — Froude number

$$\Pi_{3} = g \cdot \rho_{b}^{a} N^{b} D^{c}$$

The exponents a, b, c must satisfy mass-, length-, and time-dimensional balance. Solving yields **a = 0, b = −2, c = −1**:

$$\Pi_{3} = \frac{N^{2} D}{g} \;\equiv\; Fr$$

### Π₄ — Aspect ratio

$$\Pi_{4} = L \,/\, D$$

### Functional Relationship

$$\boxed{\,N_{p} \;=\; f\!\left( Re,\; Fr,\; L/D \right)\,}$$

For geometrically similar drums (L/D = constant), this collapses to **two-parameter** behaviour:

$$N_{p} = \phi\!\left(Re,\; Fr\right)$$

## 3.3 Derivation III — Aeration Number and Mass-Transfer Scaling

The aeration number compares the *volumetric gas flow* to the *volumetric displacement* caused by drum rotation:

$$N_{a} = \frac{Q}{N D^{3}}$$

The **gas–solid mass-transfer coefficient** in a tumbling bed is empirically correlated as:

$$k_{L} a = K \cdot N^{a_{1}} \cdot Q^{a_{2}} \cdot \phi^{a_{3}}$$

**Derivation outline (each step):**

- **Step 1:** Experimental data are fitted to a power-law form because mass transfer is dominated by surface renewal, which is itself a power-law function of agitation intensity.
- **Step 2:** Dimensionless analysis gives the exponents a₁, a₂, a₃; typical reported values are 0.4 – 0.6 for a₁, 0.5 – 0.7 for a₂, and −0.4 to −0.2 for a₃.
- **Step 3:** K is the lumped proportionality constant capturing particle-size, moisture, and porosity effects.

The **critical oxygen demand** must satisfy:

$$O_{2,\text{demand}} = \mu_{\max} X \,/\, Y_{X/O} \;\leq\; k_{L} a \, C_{O_{2}}^{*}$$

If **O₂_demand > k_La · C\*_O₂**, the reactor is **oxygen-limited** and the RDB must be run at higher N or Q, or the loading must be reduced.

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — Power Requirement for an SSF-RDB

**Given:**

- Drum diameter **D = 0.40 m**, length **L = 0.80 m**
- Rotation speed **N = 20 rpm = 1/3 s⁻¹ = 0.333 s⁻¹**
- Loading fraction **φ = 0.30**
- Substrate bulk density **ρ_b = 850 kg/m³**
- Effective bed viscosity **μ_b = 0.10 Pa·s**
- Operating regime: **Cascading** → use N_p\* = 1.2 (from empirical chart)
- Froude correction exponent **m = 0.20**

**Step 1 — Froude number:**

$$Fr = \frac{N^{2} D}{g} = \frac{\left(0.333\right)^{2} \left(0.40\right)}{9.81} = \frac{0.1111 \times 0.40}{9.81} = \frac{0.0444}{9.81} = 4.53 \times 10^{-3}$$

Regime identification: 10⁻³ < Fr < 10⁻² → **Slumping** (sub-optimal; we will proceed for illustration).

**Step 2 — Froude-corrected N_p:**

$$N_{p}^{*} = N_{p,\text{uncorrected}} \cdot \left(Fr\right)^{-m} = 1.2 \times \left(4.53 \times 10^{-3}\right)^{-0.20}$$

Compute the correction factor:

$$\left(4.53 \times 10^{-3}\right)^{-0.20} = e^{-0.20 \, \ln\!\left(4.53 \times 10^{-3}\right)}$$

$$\ln(4.53 \times 10^{-3}) = \ln(4.53) + \ln(10^{-3}) = 1.511 - 6.908 = -5.397$$

$$e^{-0.20 \times (-5.397)} = e^{1.0794} = 2.943$$

Therefore:

$$N_{p}^{*} = 1.2 \times 2.943 = 3.53$$

**Step 3 — Power input:**

$$P = N_{p}^{*} \, \rho_{b} \, N^{3} \, D^{5} = 3.53 \times 850 \times \left(0.333\right)^{3} \times \left(0.40\right)^{5}$$

Compute each term separately:

- N³ = (0.333)³ = 0.0370
- D⁵ = (0.40)⁵ = 0.01024
- ρ_b · N³ · D⁵ = 850 × 0.0370 × 0.01024 = 0.3220 W
- P = 3.53 × 0.3220 = **1.14 W**

**Step 4 — Power density:**

$$P/V_{b} = \frac{1.14}{0.0301} = 37.9 \; \text{W/m}^{3}$$

where **V_b = φ · (π/4) D² L = 0.30 × 0.7854 × 0.16 × 0.80 = 0.0301 m³**.

**Result:** P ≈ **1.14 W** and power density ≈ **38 W/m³** — squarely in the typical 10 – 1000 W/m³ range for an RDB.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key takeaway from the calculation:** Because P ∝ N³D⁵, doubling N increases power by **8×**, and doubling D increases power by **32×**. This is why **scale-up** is almost always done by increasing **D**, not **N**, to avoid shear damage.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Pros / Cons / Design Choices

| Design Aspect | Option A | Option B | KTU-Recommended |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rotation mode | Continuous | Intermittent (5 min on / 30 min off) | Intermittent — preserves viability |
| Direction | Unidirectional | Alternating (bidirectional) | Alternating — avoids bed mounding |
| Baffles | None | Longitudinal / angled | Baffles at 30° lift cascading efficiency |
| Aspect ratio L/D | 0.5 | 3.0 | 1.5 – 2.0 (compromise between plug-flow & tumbling) |
| Loading φ | 0.20 | 0.50 | 0.30 (best mixing-to-throughput trade-off) |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an RDB

The diagram below maps the *physical components* and the *information / material flows* between them. Pure block-level architecture is used (rather than an isometric drawing) so that every Mermaid node has an alphanumeric identifier and clean uppercase text labels.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[DRIVE MOTOR] --> A2[GEAR REDUCER]
    A2 --> A3[DRUM SHAFT]
    A3 --> A4[ROTATING DRUM VESSEL]
    A4 --> A5[INTERNAL BAFFLES]
    A4 --> A6[SUBSTRATE BED]
    A4 --> A7[GAS HEADSPACE]
    B1[FRESH AIR INLET] --> B2[STERILE FILTER 0.2 MICRON]
    B2 --> B3[FLOWMETER AND HUMIDIFIER]
    B3 --> A7
    A7 --> B4[OFF GAS OUTLET]
    B4 --> B5[CO2 AND O2 ANALYSER]
    B5 --> B6[CONDENSATE TRAP]
    C1[TEMPERATURE SENSOR PT100] --> A4
    C2[HEATING COOLING JACKET] --> A4
    A4 --> C3[DATA ACQUISITION SYSTEM]
    D1[STERILE SAMPLING PORT] --> A6
    D2[INOCULATION PORT] --> A6
    A6 --> D3[HARVEST DISCHARGE CHUTE]
    E1[CONTROL PLC] -. CONTROL SIGNAL .-> A1
    E1 -. CONTROL SIGNAL .-> B3
    E1 -. CONTROL SIGNAL .-> C2
    C3 -. FEEDBACK .-> E1
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- **Solid arrows** = material flow (substrate, gas, condensate).
- **Dotted arrows** = control / signal flow.
- The **drum vessel (A4)** is the *central* element receiving mechanical, thermal, and chemical inputs.
- The **PLC (E1)** closes the feedback loop using temperature, gas-composition, and motor-torque sensors.

## 4.2 Operating-Regime Decision Flow (Fr-Based Selection)

The next diagram shows the *decision tree* an engineer follows once the rotation speed and drum diameter are known. It is the single most-tested concept in KTU viva and Part-A questions on this topic.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[START: GIVEN N rpm AND D m] --> S1[CONVERT N TO rev per s]
    S1 --> S2[COMPUTE Fr = N SQUARED D OVER g]
    S2 --> S3{IS Fr LESS THAN 10 TO THE MINUS 3}
    S3 -- YES --> R1[REGIME 1 SLIPPING NO MIXING UNACCEPTABLE]
    S3 -- NO --> S4{IS Fr LESS THAN 10 TO THE MINUS 2}
    S4 -- YES --> R2[REGIME 2 SLUMPING AVALANCHING POOR MIXING]
    S4 -- NO --> S5{IS Fr LESS THAN 10 TO THE MINUS 1}
    S5 -- YES --> R3[REGIME 3 CASCADING IDEAL RDB TARGET]
    S5 -- NO --> S6{IS Fr LESS THAN 1}
    S6 -- YES --> R4[REGIME 4 CATARACTING HIGH SHEAR DAMAGE LIKELY]
    S6 -- NO --> R5[REGIME 5 CENTRIFUGING MIXING STOPS]
    R1 --> S7[INCREASE N OR DECREASE D]
    R2 --> S8[INCREASE N SLIGHTLY]
    R3 --> S9[PROCEED WITH DESIGN]
    R4 --> S10[REDUCE N OR CHANGE GEOMETRY]
    R5 --> S11[REDUCE N DRAMATICALLY]
    S7 --> S2
    S8 --> S2
    S10 --> S2
    S11 --> S2
```

## 4.3 Material & Energy Flow in Steady-State Operation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN1[STERILE MOIST AIR IN] --> MIX[GAS HEADSPACE]
    O2[OXYGEN TRANSFER kLa] --> MIX
    MIX --> BED[SUBSTRATE BED MICROBIAL REACTION]
    BED --> O2
    BED --> H2O[EVAPORATED WATER]
    BED --> CO2[CARBON DIOXIDE RELEASE]
    BED --> QH[HEAT GENERATION]
    H2O --> OUT1[OFF GAS HUMIDIFIED]
    CO2 --> OUT1
    QH --> JACK[HEATING COOLING JACKET]
    JACK --> COOL[COOLANT OUT]
    IN1 --> JACK
    BED --> OUT2[HARVESTED BIOMASS AND SPENT SOLIDS]
```

The diagrams are *block-level functional architectures* rather than isometric drawings — a deliberate choice to avoid the rendering errors that Mermaid produces for complex pictorial content.
