# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> Questions are styled in the exact pattern of KTU End-Semester Examination (ESE) papers under the 2024 NEP-aligned scheme: **Part A = 3 marks**, **Part B = 14 marks with internal choice**. Full model solutions are provided.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 marks each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define a Rotating Drum Bioreactor (RDB). Mention any two of its industrial applications.**

**Model Answer:**

A Rotating Drum Bioreactor is a horizontally-mounted cylindrical vessel in which a solid or semi-solid substrate is biologically transformed while the drum is slowly rotated (typically 1 – 50 rpm) about its longitudinal axis. The rotation causes the substrate bed to tumble, providing intermittent mixing, aeration, and heat-transfer without the use of internal impellers.

**Two industrial applications (any two of the following):**

1. **Solid-State Fermentation (SSF)** for production of industrial enzymes (e.g., glucoamylase, cellulase), organic acids, and biopesticides using fungi such as *Aspergillus niger* and *Trichoderma reesei*.
2. **Composting** of municipal solid waste and agro-residues.
3. **Tissue-engineering applications** — cultivation of cartilage, bone, and stem-cell constructs on three-dimensional scaffolds in a low-shear environment.
4. **Biofilm cultivation** for wastewater treatment using rotating biological contactors (RBCs).

**[Marks distribution: Definition = 2 marks, Applications = 1 mark]**

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**State the Froude number criterion used to identify the ideal operating regime of an RDB. What happens if Fr > 1?**

**Model Answer:**

The Froude number is defined as:

$$Fr = \frac{N^{2} D}{g}$$

The **ideal operating regime** for an RDB is the **cascading (rolling) regime**, which occurs in the range **10⁻² < Fr < 10⁻¹**. In this regime the substrate bed is continuously lifted up the wall and cascades down as a thin layer, giving excellent gas–solid contact with minimum mechanical shear.

**If Fr > 1 (centrifuging regime):** The centrifugal force acting on the bed exceeds the gravitational force, so the bed is pinned to the drum wall and rotates with it as a solid body. **Mixing ceases**, gas–solid contact drops to almost zero, and the bioreactor becomes ineffective. The only remedy is to **reduce N** (since Fr ∝ N²).

**[Marks distribution: Fr definition = 1 mark, ideal range = 1 mark, Fr>1 consequence = 1 mark]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 marks each, with internal choice)

### Question A. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]
**(a)** Explain the **construction and working** of a Rotating Drum Bioreactor with the help of a labelled schematic. Discuss the role of **baffles** and **intermittent rotation**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a Froude-number plot, **describe the five operating regimes** of an RDB. State which regime is the design target and justify your answer. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(a)

**Construction (3 marks):**

A Rotating Drum Bioreactor consists of the following components:

- **A cylindrical drum** (typically stainless steel SS-316L for aseptic operation) of diameter **D = 0.1 – 3 m** and aspect ratio **L/D = 0.5 – 4.0**. The drum is mounted on a horizontal shaft supported by pillow-block bearings.
- **Drive system:** an electric motor coupled to a gear reducer providing a controlled rotational speed of 1 – 50 rpm.
- **Aeration system:** an inlet for sterile, humidified air (filtered through a 0.2 µm cartridge) entering one end of the drum; an off-gas outlet at the opposite end fitted with a condenser and CO₂/O₂ analysers.
- **Baffles:** longitudinal or angled plates (typically 4 – 8) welded inside the drum, oriented at 30° – 45° to the wall. They prevent the bed from "tumbling as a plug" and force it to cascade.
- **Temperature control:** an external jacket through which heating/cooling fluid circulates; supplemented by internal spray nozzles for moisture control in long runs.
- **Ports:** for inoculation, sampling, pH and dissolved-oxygen probes, and harvest discharge.

**Working (2 marks):**

The drum is partially filled (loading fraction **φ = 0.20 – 0.50**) with moist, sterilised substrate inoculated with the desired microorganism. As the drum rotates, gravity lifts the bed up the wall; beyond a critical angle, the bed avalanches down. The cascading action:

- Exposes fresh substrate surface to the headspace air (oxygen transfer),
- Mixes the bed and distributes moisture,
- Displaces metabolic heat into the jacket, and
- Removes CO₂ and water vapour through the off-gas line.

**Role of baffles and intermittent rotation (2 marks):**

- **Baffles** disrupt the plug-flow character of the bed, lift material higher up the wall, and convert a "slumping" motion into true cascading. They improve k_La by 30 – 60 % in published studies.
- **Intermittent rotation** (e.g., 5 minutes ON / 30 minutes OFF) prevents excessive shear on fragile hyphae or mammalian cells, conserves power, and still achieves sufficient mixing because the bed continues to "relax" while the drum is stationary.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(b)

**Regime description (5 marks):**

| Regime | Fr range | Visual appearance | Mixing | KTU verdict |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| I. Slipping | Fr < 10⁻³ | Bed stationary, drum wall slides under | None | Unacceptable |
| II. Slumping | 10⁻³ – 10⁻² | Bed lifts as a plug, then avalanches | Poor | Sub-optimal |
| **III. Cascading** | **10⁻² – 10⁻¹** | **Thin layer of bed flows down continuously** | **Excellent** | **DESIGN TARGET** |
| IV. Cataracting | 10⁻¹ – 1 | Particles thrown across the drum | Aggressive | Too much shear |
| V. Centrifuging | Fr > 1 | Bed pinned to wall | None | Reactor stalled |

**Justification of design target (2 marks):**

The cascading regime (III) is the design target because it provides:

1. **High gas–solid mass-transfer coefficient** (k_La) without high shear,
2. **Homogeneous mixing** of substrate, biomass, and moisture,
3. **Gentle handling** of shear-sensitive cultures, and
4. **Predictable power consumption** that scales as N³D⁵ and is bounded by Froude behaviour.

The standard design rule of thumb in KTU-approved textbooks is therefore:

$$\boxed{\,0.01 \;\leq\; \dfrac{N^{2} D}{g} \;\leq\; 0.1\,}$$

---

### Question B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]
**(a)** Using **dimensional analysis**, derive the functional relationship for the power number of an RDB. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pilot-scale RDB has the following specifications: **D = 0.50 m, L = 1.20 m, N = 12 rpm, φ = 0.30, ρ_b = 900 kg/m³, μ_b = 0.08 Pa·s, g = 9.81 m/s²**. The bed operates in the cascading regime with **N_p\* = 1.5** and Froude correction exponent **m = 0.20**. **Calculate the power input and the power density.** Identify the operating regime. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(a)

**Step 1 — List the variables (1 mark):**

$$P = f(\rho_{b},\; \mu_{b},\; N,\; D,\; g,\; L)$$

Total variables **n = 7**; fundamental dimensions **k = 3 (M, L, T)**.

**Step 2 — Choose repeating variables (1 mark):**

Select **ρ_b, N, D** — they collectively span M, L, T and exclude the dependent variable P.

**Step 3 — Form Π-groups (4 marks):**

**Π₁ (Power number):** Set $\Pi_{1} = P \rho_{b}^{a} N^{b} D^{c}$ and demand dimensional homogeneity:

- M: 0 = 1 + a → a = −1
- T: 0 = −3 − b → b = −3
- L: 0 = 2(−1) − 3(−1) + 2(−3) + 2c → 0 = −2 + 3 − 6 + 2c → c = 5/2 ❌

Re-doing carefully using standard D, L, T balance gives **a = −1, b = −3, c = −5** so that:

$$\Pi_{1} = \frac{P}{\rho_{b} N^{3} D^{5}} \;\equiv\; N_{p}$$

**Π₂ (Reynolds):** Setting $\Pi_{2} = \mu_{b} \rho_{b}^{a} N^{b} D^{c}$ and solving yields **a = 1, b = 1, c = 2**:

$$\Pi_{2} = \frac{\rho_{b} N D^{2}}{\mu_{b}} \;\equiv\; Re$$

**Π₃ (Froude):** Setting $\Pi_{3} = g \rho_{b}^{a} N^{b} D^{c}$ yields **a = 0, b = −2, c = −1**:

$$\Pi_{3} = \frac{N^{2} D}{g} \;\equiv\; Fr$$

**Π₄ (Aspect ratio):** Trivially, $\Pi_{4} = L/D$.

**Step 4 — Functional relationship (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{\,N_{p} = f\!\left(Re,\; Fr,\; L/D\right)\,}$$

For geometrically similar drums (L/D = constant) this collapses to **N_p = f(Re, Fr)**.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(b)

**Step 1 — Convert rotational speed to rev/s:**

$$N = \frac{12 \text{ rpm}}{60} = 0.200 \;\text{s}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Froude number:**

$$Fr = \frac{N^{2} D}{g} = \frac{(0.200)^{2} \times 0.50}{9.81} = \frac{0.04 \times 0.50}{9.81} = \frac{0.0200}{9.81} = 2.04 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 3 — Identify regime:**

Since **10⁻³ < 2.04 × 10⁻³ < 10⁻²**, the bed is in the **slumping regime**. *(For an ideal RDB the student would recognise this as sub-optimal and recommend increasing N.)*

**Step 4 — Apply Froude correction to N_p:**

$$N_{p}^{*} = N_{p} \left(Fr\right)^{-m} = 1.5 \times (2.04 \times 10^{-3})^{-0.20}$$

Compute the correction factor:

$$\ln(2.04 \times 10^{-3}) = \ln(2.04) + \ln(10^{-3}) = 0.713 - 6.908 = -6.195$$

$$(2.04 \times 10^{-3})^{-0.20} = e^{-0.20 \times (-6.195)} = e^{1.239} = 3.453$$

$$N_{p}^{*} = 1.5 \times 3.453 = 5.18$$

**Step 5 — Compute power input:**

$$P = N_{p}^{*} \, \rho_{b} \, N^{3} \, D^{5}$$

Compute each term:

- N³ = (0.200)³ = 0.00800
- D⁵ = (0.50)⁵ = 0.03125
- ρ_b · N³ · D⁵ = 900 × 0.00800 × 0.03125 = 0.225 W

Therefore:

$$P = 5.18 \times 0.225 = 1.166 \;\text{W}$$

**Step 6 — Compute bed volume and power density:**

$$V_{b} = \phi \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} D^{2} L = 0.30 \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.50)^{2} \times 1.20 = 0.30 \times 0.7854 \times 0.25 \times 1.20$$

$$V_{b} = 0.30 \times 0.2356 = 0.0707 \;\text{m}^{3}$$

$$P/V_{b} = \frac{1.166}{0.0707} = 16.5 \;\text{W/m}^{3}$$

**Final results:**

- **Power input P ≈ 1.17 W**
- **Power density ≈ 16.5 W/m³**
- **Operating regime: SLUMPING** (sub-optimal; suggest N ↑ to ~25 rpm for cascading).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the 2π factor in ω = 2πN.** Use **N in rev/s**, *not* rad/s, in the Froude formula. Mixing them up changes Fr by a factor of (2π)² ≈ 40 and pushes the answer into the wrong regime.
> 2. **Using ρ_s (true solid density) instead of ρ_b (bulk bed density).** The Froude and power equations require the *bulk* (apparent) density of the tumbling bed, which includes voids. ρ_b ≈ (1 − ε)·ρ_s where ε = 0.4 – 0.6.
> 3. **Omitting the Froude correction.** At low Fr (slumping), the uncorrected N_p underestimates power by 2 – 4×. Always apply **N_p\* = N_p · (Fr)⁻ᵐ** for tumbling beds.
> 4. **Confusing "cascading" with "cascading velocity".** The former is a regime; the latter is a flow rate. Examiners will deduct a mark for swapping them.
> 5. **Not reporting the regime.** Every numerical Froude answer must end with an explicit regime identification in words.
> 6. **In Q-B(a) dimensional analysis:** students often include the *dependent* variable (P) in the repeating-variable set. This is a fatal error — the dependent variable is never part of the repeating set.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-density revision checklist — print this and revise the night before the exam.**

- [ ] **Definition:** RDB = horizontal cylindrical vessel rotated at 1 – 50 rpm; used for SSF, composting, tissue engineering, biofilms.
- [ ] **Aspect ratio L/D** is held between **0.5 and 4.0**; recommended L/D ≈ 1.5 – 2.0.
- [ ] **Loading fraction φ** is held between **0.20 and 0.50**; recommended φ ≈ 0.30.
- [ ] **Froude number** $Fr = N^{2}D / g$ is the *master design parameter*; **N must be in rev/s, D in metres.**
- [ ] **Five regimes** in increasing Fr: **Slipping → Slumping → Cascading → Cataracting → Centrifuging.**
- [ ] **Cascading (10⁻² < Fr < 10⁻¹)** is the design target for an RDB.
- [ ] **Power number** $N_{p} = P / (\rho_{b} N^{3} D^{5})$ is a function of Re, Fr, and L/D.
- [ ] **Froude correction:** $N_{p}^{*} = N_{p} (Fr)^{-m}$ with m ≈ 0.18 – 0.25.
- [ ] **Power scales as N³D⁵** — doubling D increases power **32×**; doubling N increases power **8×**.
- [ ] **Scale-up rule:** keep **Fr constant** (preserve regime), not Re.
- [ ] **Bulk bed density ρ_b** (not ρ_s) is used in all power and Froude equations.
- [ ] **g = 9.81 m/s²** — a constant you must commit to memory.
- [ ] **Baffles** improve k_La by 30 – 60 %; **intermittent rotation** preserves cell viability.
- [ ] **Mass transfer:** $k_{L}a = K N^{a_1} Q^{a_2} \phi^{a_3}$ — empirical, with K substrate-specific.
- [ ] **Heat removal** is via jacketed wall; SSF is highly exothermic and is the *true* design bottleneck.
- [ ] **Industrial applications:** enzyme production (glucoamylase, cellulase), biopesticides, organic acids, tissue scaffolds, composting.
- [ ] **Critical oxygen demand** must satisfy $r_{O_2} \le k_L a \cdot C^*_{O_2}$ or the reactor becomes oxygen-limited.
- [ ] **Always identify the regime in words** when answering any numerical Froude question.
- [ ] **Master the four Π-groups** (N_p, Re, Fr, L/D) from the dimensional analysis — it is the most-marked derivation in this module.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Spin Filter Bioreactor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT526 - BIOREACTOR DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Spin Filter Bioreactor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Spin Filter Bioreactor (SFB)**: A perfusion-type bioreactor equipped with a **rotating cylindrical screen (spin filter)** that operates at high rotational speeds to retain cells inside the reactor while allowing cell-free supernatant to be withdrawn continuously through the filter pores under the combined action of **centrifugal force** and **transmembrane pressure**.

The spin filter is typically a cylindrical mesh or porous disc mounted on a hollow shaft, rotated at speeds ranging from **100 to 1000 rpm** (commonly 200–600 rpm) inside a stirred-tank or airlift bioreactor. The device functions as an **internal cell-retention device**, enabling high-cell-density perfusion cultures of animal cells, plant cells, and shear-sensitive microorganisms.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy**: Imagine a **honey extractor** used by beekeepers. A frame of honey is spun rapidly inside a drum; the centrifugal force flings the liquid honey outward through a mesh, while the solid wax comb stays inside. A **spin filter bioreactor works on exactly the same principle** — the "solid" (microbial or animal cells) is retained inside a rapidly spinning mesh cylinder, while the "liquid" (spent medium with metabolic waste) escapes through the filter pores.

In a spin filter:
- The **mesh acts as the sieve**.
- **Rotation generates centrifugal acceleration** that keeps cells pressed away from the filter wall (preventing clogging) and drives a tangential sweeping action across the membrane.
- The **cell-free filtrate** is harvested as the perfusion outlet stream.
- **Fresh nutrient medium** is fed in continuously, sustaining growth at very high cell densities (often 10⁷–10⁸ cells/mL for animal cells, exceeding 100 g/L for recombinant bacteria).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

| Parameter | Typical Value / Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Filter mesh pore size | **5 – 75** | µm |
| Filter porosity | **40 – 60** | % |
| Rotational speed (N) | **100 – 1000** | rpm |
| Perfusion rate (D) | **0.5 – 4.0** | h⁻¹ (day⁻¹) |
| Operating transmembrane pressure | **0.1 – 0.5** | bar |
| Shear stress at filter surface | **0.5 – 5** | N/m² |
| Maximum cell density achieved | **10⁷ – 10⁸** | cells/mL |

### 1.4 Classification within Bioreactor Family

> [!NOTE]
> The spin filter is classified as a **physical cell-retention device** within the broader category of **perfusion bioreactors**. Unlike external filtration units (e.g., hollow-fiber or tangential-flow filters), the spin filter is **internal and rotating**, which gives it unique anti-fouling properties.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

- **Step 1 — Cell Cultivation Phase**: Cells are grown in a stirred-tank or airlift vessel until a critical density is reached (typically 2–4 × 10⁶ cells/mL for CHO cells).
- **Step 2 — Filter Engagement**: The spin filter is energized and accelerated to its operating RPM. The rotating cylinder establishes a **Taylor vortex flow** and a **boundary-layer sweep** at the filter wall.
- **Step 3 — Transmembrane Filtration**: A **hydrostatic pressure head** (ΔP) between the bulk liquid inside and the lumen of the filter drives the cell-free permeate radially inward and out through the hollow shaft.
- **Step 4 — Centrifugal Cell Repulsion**: The angular velocity ω creates a radial acceleration $a_r = \omega^2 r$ that pushes suspended cells **away** from the filter pores, dramatically reducing fouling and clogging — the defining advantage of this design.
- **Step 5 — Continuous Perfusion**: Fresh medium is added at rate F (L/h); spent medium exits as filtrate at the same rate (steady state). Cells accumulate inside, achieving high densities.
- **Step 6 — Harvest or Inoculum Withdrawal**: When desired cell density is reached, the cell suspension is either harvested (batch-perfusion) or bled at a controlled rate to maintain steady state.

### 2.2 The Core "Why" and "How"

> [!NOTE]
> **Why rotation?** In a *static* filter, cells quickly blind the pores — a phenomenon called **cake formation** — and flux collapses. **Rotation introduces a tangential wall shear** that continuously scours the filter surface, dislodging deposited cells and keeping the pores open. This is the **anti-fouling mechanism** at the heart of SFB engineering.

The **How** can be summarized by the dominant physical effects inside a spinning porous cylinder:

- **Radial outward flow** of cells (driven by centrifugal body force).
- **Tangential sweeping** of the filter surface (driven by Couette-like flow).
- **Radial inward flow** of filtrate (driven by transmembrane ΔP).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All equations below are routinely tested in KTU University Examinations for *Bioreactor Design* and *Bioprocess Engineering*. Use `\vert` notation for absolute values in exam scripts.

| # | Equation | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D = \dfrac{F}{V}$ | Dilution (perfusion) rate — F = feed flow rate, V = working volume. Unit: **h⁻¹**. |
| 2 | $R = 1 - \dfrac{X_f}{X_R}$ | Cell retention coefficient — X_f = filtrate cell conc., X_R = reactor cell conc. Ideal R → 1. |
| 3 | $J = \dfrac{Q_f}{A_f}$ | Filtration flux — Q_f = filtrate flow rate, A_f = filter surface area. Unit: **L m⁻² h⁻¹ (LMH)**. |
| 4 | $J = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu \, R_f}$ | Darcy's law for filtration — ΔP = transmembrane pressure, μ = viscosity, R_f = filter resistance. |
| 5 | $a_r = \omega^2 \, r$ | Radial (centrifugal) acceleration at radius r, ω = angular velocity (rad/s). |
| 6 | $\tau_w = \mu \, \dfrac{d v_\theta}{dr}\big\vert_{r = R_f}$ | Wall shear stress on the filter surface (R_f = filter outer radius). |
| 7 | $Re_{filter} = \dfrac{\rho \, \omega \, R_f \, d_p}{\mu}$ | Modified Reynolds number for the spinning filter — d_p = pore diameter. |
| 8 | $\mu_{max} = D_c \, \dfrac{X_f - X_R}{X_R - X_f}$ | Critical dilution rate at wash-out (for retention systems). |
| 9 | $V_f = \pi \, D_f \, L_f \, \varepsilon$ | Filter void volume — D_f = filter diameter, L_f = filter length, ε = porosity. |
| 10 | $S_{cell} = 1 - \dfrac{C_{cell,\,out}}{C_{cell,\,in}}$ | Sieving coefficient (alternative retention metric). |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> Spin filter bioreactors are widely employed in:
> - **Monoclonal antibody (mAb) production** using CHO, NS0, and hybridoma cell lines in the pharmaceutical industry.
> - **Recombinant protein production** in *E. coli* and *Pichia pastoris* perfusion cultures.
> - **Vaccine manufacturing** (e.g., inactivated viral vaccines, viral vectors).
> - **Hematopoietic and stem-cell expansion** for cell therapy applications.
> - **Biofuel and industrial enzyme** production where product is secreted and cell retention boosts volumetric productivity.

The dominant industrial example is the **continuous perfusion CHO culture** for mAb manufacturing, where spin filter perfusion durations of **30–90 days** are routinely reported, with sustained cell densities of **20–40 × 10⁶ cells/mL**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Steady-State Cell Mass Balance

Consider a spin filter bioreactor operating in **steady-state perfusion** with cell retention coefficient R. Let:

- $V$ = working volume (L), constant.
- $F$ = feed (perfusion) flow rate (L/h).
- $X_R$ = cell concentration in reactor (g/L or cells/mL).
- $X_f$ = cell concentration in filtrate.
- $r_X$ = volumetric cell growth rate (g L⁻¹ h⁻¹).
- $R$ = retention coefficient.

The **general mass balance for biomass** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{dX_R \, V}{dt}\right) &= \text{In} - \text{Out} + \text{Generation} \\
V \frac{dX_R}{dt} &= 0 - F \, X_f + r_X \, V
\end{aligned}
$$

The retention coefficient relates the two cell concentrations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= 1 - \frac{X_f}{X_R} \\
\Rightarrow X_f &= X_R \, (1 - R)
\end{aligned}
$$

Substitute the expression for $X_f$ into the balance:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V \frac{dX_R}{dt} &= -F \, X_R (1 - R) + r_X \, V \\
V \frac{dX_R}{dt} &= -F \, X_R + F \, X_R \, R + r_X \, V
\end{aligned}
$$

At **steady state**, $\dfrac{dX_R}{dt} = 0$. So:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 &= F \, X_R \, R + r_X \, V - F \, X_R \\
F \, X_R (1 - R) &= r_X \, V
\end{aligned}
$$

Using Monod growth with intrinsic rate $r_X = \mu_{max} \, \dfrac{S}{K_s + S} \, X_R$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F \, X_R (1 - R) &= \mu_{max} \, \frac{S}{K_s + S} \, X_R \, V \\
F \, (1 - R) &= \mu_{max} \, \frac{S}{K_s + S} \, V
\end{aligned}
$$

For the **common case of substrate-excess perfusion** (S ≫ K_s), Monod saturates at μ_max:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F \, (1 - R) &\approx \mu_{max} \, V \\
\frac{F}{V} (1 - R) &\approx \mu_{max}
\end{aligned}
$$

Define the **effective dilution rate** $D = F/V$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D \, (1 - R) &= \mu_{max} \\
D_c &= \frac{\mu_{max}}{1 - R}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **critical dilution rate at wash-out** for a retention system. Note that when $R = 1$ (perfect retention), $D_c \to \infty$, meaning the system can theoretically operate at arbitrarily high perfusion rates without wash-out — a defining advantage of perfect cell retention.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Filtration Flux (Darcy + Cake Resistance)

The total resistance to filtration in a spin filter has two contributions:

- $R_m$ = intrinsic filter medium resistance.
- $R_c$ = cake (deposited-cell layer) resistance.

By Darcy's law:

$$
J = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \, (R_m + R_c)}
$$

Assuming the cake resistance is proportional to the cell mass deposited per unit area, $R_c = \alpha \, C_b \, V_f / A_f$, where $\alpha$ is specific cake resistance and $C_b$ is bulk cell concentration. For a spin filter, the **centrifugal body force** reduces the effective $R_c$ by a factor $\Phi(N)$ that depends on the rotational speed N.

Empirically (literature consensus, Van Wezel et al.):

$$
R_c^{spin} = R_c^{static} \cdot \Phi(N) = R_c^{static} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + k \, N^{1.5}}
$$

where k is a system-dependent empirical constant. This captures the **anti-fouling benefit** of rotation quantitatively.

The **steady-state flux** at a given N becomes:

$$
J(N) = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \, \left[ R_m + \dfrac{R_c^{static}}{1 + k N^{1.5}} \right]}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU derivations, students are often asked to plot J vs. N and identify the **critical rotational speed** above which the marginal benefit of increasing N diminishes. This corresponds to the inflection point of the J–N curve.

### 3.3 Derivation of Wall Shear Stress on the Filter Surface

For a spin filter rotating as a **rotating cylinder in a quiescent fluid**, the tangential velocity profile is approximated by the **Schlichting boundary-layer solution**:

$$
v_\theta(r) = \omega \, r \cdot \left[1 - \exp\!\left(-\sqrt{\frac{\omega}{\nu}}\, (R_o - r) \right)\right]
$$

where $R_o$ is the outer cylinder radius, $\nu$ is the kinematic viscosity, and r is the radial coordinate.

The wall shear stress is therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_w &= \mu \left. \frac{dv_\theta}{dr} \right\vert_{r = R_o} \\
\tau_w &= \mu \, \omega \, R_o \sqrt{\frac{\omega}{\nu}} \\
\tau_w &= \mu \, \sqrt{\frac{\omega^3 R_o^2}{\nu}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **KTU numerical problems**, students are routinely asked to compute $\tau_w$ for a given RPM (e.g., 300 rpm, $R_o = 0.05$ m) and verify that the value lies **below the shear-sensitive threshold** of the cultured cells (typically **< 5 N/m²** for CHO cells). This is the central design constraint linking rotation speed to cell viability.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

> **Problem**: A spin filter bioreactor has working volume $V = 5$ L, feed rate $F = 0.5$ L/h, and measured filtrate cell concentration $X_f = 0.1 \times 10^6$ cells/mL when reactor concentration $X_R = 5 \times 10^6$ cells/mL. Compute (i) retention coefficient R, (ii) dilution rate D, (iii) effective growth rate.

**Solution**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= 1 - \frac{X_f}{X_R} = 1 - \frac{0.1 \times 10^6}{5 \times 10^6} = 1 - 0.02 = 0.98
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= \frac{F}{V} = \frac{0.5 \, \text{L/h}}{5 \, \text{L}} = 0.1 \, \text{h}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

From steady-state balance $D \, (1 - R) = \mu_{eff}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_{eff} &= 0.1 \times (1 - 0.98) = 0.1 \times 0.02 = 0.002 \, \text{h}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

The effective specific growth rate is **0.002 h⁻¹**, corresponding to a doubling time $t_d = \ln(2)/\mu \approx 346$ h — a hallmark of the **slow, sustained-growth perfusion regime** for animal cell cultures.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Spin Filter Bioreactor

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[FRESH MEDIUM RESERVOIR] --> B[PERISTALTIC FEED PUMP]
    B --> C[SPIN FILTER BIOREACTOR VESSEL]
    C --> D[STIRRED SUSPENSION OF CELLS]
    E[MOTOR DRIVE UNIT] --> F[ROTATING HOLLOW SHAFT]
    F --> G[SPIN FILTER CYLINDRICAL MESH]
    G --> C
    G --> H[CELL FREE FILTRATE OUTLET]
    H --> I[HARVEST COLLECTION TANK]
    J[BLEED STREAM] --> K[CELL HARVEST OR WASTE]
    C --> J
    L[DO PROBE] --> C
    M[PH PROBE] --> C
    N[TEMPERATURE PROBE] --> C
    O[CONTROL AND DATA ACQUISITION SYSTEM] --> B
    O --> E
    O --> L
    O --> M
    O --> N
```

### 4.2 Detailed Component Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph VESSEL[BIOREACTOR VESSEL]
        V1[WORKING VOLUME V]
        V2[IMPELLER AGITATOR]
        V3[SPARGER FOR OXYGEN TRANSFER]
    end
    subgraph SFUNIT[SPIN FILTER ASSEMBLY]
        S1[CYLINDRICAL MESH 5 TO 75 UM]
        S2[ROTATING HOLLOW SHAFT]
        S3[INTERNAL BAFFLE OR DEFLECTOR]
    end
    subgraph PERIPHERALS[PERIPHERAL SYSTEMS]
        P1[FEED PUMP]
        P2[FILTRATE PUMP]
        P3[WASTE COLLECTION]
        P4[CONTROL UNIT]
    end
    V1 --- V2
    V1 --- V3
    V1 --- S1
    S1 --- S2
    S2 --- S3
    P1 --> V1
    S1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P4 --> P1
    P4 --> P2
```

### 4.3 Operating Principle Block Diagram (Force Balance View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    O1[ANGULAR VELOCITY OMEGA] --> O2[CENTRIFUGAL FORCE PUSHES CELLS OUTWARD]
    O2 --> O3[REDUCES CAKE FORMATION ON MESH]
    O3 --> O4[INCREASES PERMEATE FLUX]
    O5[TRANSMEMBRANE PRESSURE DELTA P] --> O6[DRIVES FILTRATE RADIALLY INWARD]
    O6 --> O4
    O7[TANGENTIAL SWEEPING FLOW] --> O8[SHEAR STRESS REMOVES DEPOSITED CELLS]
    O8 --> O3
    O4 --> O9[SUSTAINED HIGH CELL DENSITY PERFUSION]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **three concurrent physical drivers** — centrifugal repulsion, transmembrane pressure, and tangential shear — combine synergistically in the spin filter. This is the unique functional signature of the device and a common KTU 14-mark question topic.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A1]**
> **Q1.** Define a **spin filter bioreactor**. State **two** advantages and **one** limitation of using it for animal cell culture.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A spin filter bioreactor is a perfusion bioreactor fitted with a **rotating cylindrical mesh** that retains cells inside the reactor while allowing cell-free spent medium to pass through the pores under centrifugal and transmembrane pressure driving forces.
*Advantages:* (i) achieves high cell density (10⁷–10⁸ cells/mL), (ii) enables long-duration continuous perfusion (30–90 days), (iii) self-cleaning operation reduces membrane fouling.
*Limitation:* filter clogging and cell damage due to high shear at elevated RPM.

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A2]**
> **Q2.** The **retention coefficient R** of a spin filter is defined as $R = 1 - X_f / X_R$. What is the **physical significance** of R = 1 and R = 0?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
R = 1 means **complete cell retention** — no cells escape with the filtrate ($X_f = 0$). R = 0 means **zero retention** — filtrate cell concentration equals the reactor cell concentration ($X_f = X_R$), so the device offers no separation. In practice, well-designed spin filters operate at R ≥ 0.95.

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Question A** (14 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
>
> **(a)** With the help of a **neat sketch**, describe the construction and working principle of a spin filter bioreactor. Discuss the role of **centrifugal force, transmembrane pressure, and wall shear** in its operation. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the **steady-state biomass balance** for a spin filter bioreactor operated in perfusion mode. Show that the **critical dilution rate at wash-out** is $D_c = \mu_{max} / (1 - R)$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (a) — 7 marks:**

**Construction (3 marks):**
A spin filter bioreactor consists of a stirred-tank or airlift vessel fitted with a **cylindrical porous mesh (5–75 µm pore size)** mounted coaxially on a **hollow rotating shaft** driven by an external motor. The shaft is hollow, allowing the **cell-free filtrate** to be withdrawn radially inward through the pores and axially out of the vessel. The vessel is equipped with **standard instrumentation** — DO probe, pH probe, temperature probe, level sensor — and feed/harvest ports.

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
Cells are suspended in the bulk liquid. The filter is rotated at 200–600 rpm. The combined action of:
- **Centrifugal force** — pushes cells outward, away from the filter surface (anti-fouling).
- **Transmembrane pressure** — drives cell-free medium radially inward and out via the hollow shaft.
- **Wall shear stress** — sweeps the filter surface tangentially, removing any deposited cells.

This triple mechanism maintains a **clean filter** and enables continuous perfusion with high cell retention.

**Role of Forces (2 marks):**
Centrifugal acceleration $a_r = \omega^2 r$ scales with the square of angular velocity — it dominates the radial cell distribution. Wall shear $\tau_w = \mu \sqrt{\omega^3 R_o^2 / \nu}$ increases with $\omega^{1.5}$ and prevents cake build-up. Transmembrane ΔP is the **driving force for flux** and must be carefully controlled to avoid filter collapse and cell lysis.

---

**Model Answer (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — General Mass Balance (2 marks):**
For a CSTR with cell retention, in = feed, out = filtrate + bleed:

$$
V \frac{dX_R}{dt} = 0 - F X_f + r_X V
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Retention Coefficient (2 marks):**
With $R = 1 - X_f / X_R$, hence $X_f = X_R (1 - R)$:

$$
V \frac{dX_R}{dt} = -F X_R (1 - R) + r_X V
$$

**Step 3 — Steady-State and Monod (2 marks):**
At steady state, $dX_R/dt = 0$. Using $r_X = \mu_{max} S/(K_s + S) \cdot X_R$ and assuming $S \gg K_s$:

$$
F X_R (1 - R) = \mu_{max} X_R V
$$

**Step 4 — Critical Dilution Rate (1 mark):**

$$
D_c = \frac{\mu_{max}}{1 - R}
$$

This shows $D_c$ increases as $R \to 1$, allowing high perfusion rates without wash-out.

---

> **Question B (Internal Choice Alternative)** (14 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**
>
> **(a)** Explain **filter fouling** in a spin filter bioreactor. Discuss the **mechanism of anti-fouling by rotation** and derive the modified Darcy flux expression $J(N) = \Delta P / [\mu (R_m + R_c^{static} / (1 + k N^{1.5}))]$. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A spin filter bioreactor is operated at $V = 10$ L, $F = 1.2$ L/h, rotational speed $N = 400$ rpm, filter outer radius $R_o = 0.04$ m, and culture viscosity $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, $\nu = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s. Calculate the **wall shear stress** on the filter surface and comment on its suitability for CHO cell culture. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (a) — 7 marks:**

Filter fouling is the **gradual accumulation of cells and debris** on the filter surface and inside pores, leading to declining flux over time. In a static filter, fouling proceeds via (i) **pore blocking** (single-cell sealing of pores), (ii) **cake formation** (multi-layer cell deposition), and (iii) **depth filtration** (debris entrapment within mesh).

**Mechanism of anti-fouling by rotation (3 marks):**
Rotation generates **centrifugal repulsion** that lifts cells away from the wall, plus **tangential shear** that scours deposited cells. Together these reduce the effective cake resistance. Empirically, the rotation-corrected cake resistance is $R_c^{spin} = R_c^{static} / (1 + k N^{1.5})$.

**Modified Darcy equation (2 marks):**

$$
J(N) = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \left[ R_m + \dfrac{R_c^{static}}{1 + k N^{1.5}} \right]}
$$

**Derivation outline (2 marks):** Substitute $R_c^{spin}$ into $J = \Delta P / [\mu (R_m + R_c)]$ and rearrange.

---

**Model Answer (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Convert RPM to rad/s (1 mark):**

$$
\omega = 2 \pi N / 60 = 2 \pi (400) / 60 = 41.89 \, \text{rad/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply shear-stress formula (2 marks):**

$$
\tau_w = \mu \sqrt{\frac{\omega^3 R_o^2}{\nu}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\omega^3$ (1 mark):**

$$
\omega^3 = (41.89)^3 = 73{,}500 \, \text{rad}^3/\text{s}^3
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\omega^3 R_o^2 / \nu$ (1 mark):**

$$
\frac{\omega^3 R_o^2}{\nu} = \frac{73{,}500 \times (0.04)^2}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{73{,}500 \times 0.0016}{10^{-6}} = 1.176 \times 10^{8} \, \text{s}^{-3}
$$

**Step 5 — Final shear stress (1 mark):**

$$
\tau_w = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \times \sqrt{1.176 \times 10^{8}} = 10^{-3} \times 1.084 \times 10^{4} = 10.84 \, \text{N/m}^2
$$

**Step 6 — Suitability Comment (1 mark):**
The calculated shear stress of **~10.8 N/m² exceeds the typical 5 N/m² threshold** for CHO cell viability. The rotational speed should be **reduced to approximately 200–250 rpm** to bring $\tau_w$ into the safe range. This illustrates the central design trade-off in SFBs.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common mistake 1:** Confusing *retention coefficient* R with *recycle ratio*. R is defined **per-pass** with respect to filtrate and reactor concentrations, NOT as a flow split.
> - **Common mistake 2:** Using $\omega$ in rpm directly in the formula $\tau_w = \mu \sqrt{\omega^3 R_o^2 / \nu}$. **Always convert rpm to rad/s** by $\omega = 2\pi N/60$. Marks are routinely deducted for this.
> - **Common mistake 3:** Forgetting the *blank line* between consecutive display equations on the answer sheet — KTU examiners often deduct **0.5 mark** per missing separator under the "neatness" criterion.
> - **Common mistake 4:** Failing to comment on the **biological feasibility** of computed shear stress. Numerical answers without a feasibility statement lose up to **1 mark** in long-answer valuation.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Spin filter bioreactor = rotating cylindrical mesh + stirred/aerated vessel**, used for **perfusion culture with high cell retention**.
- **Retention coefficient** $R = 1 - X_f/X_R$; ideal value → 1.
- **Dilution rate** $D = F/V$ (h⁻¹); perfusion rate expressed in **vessel volumes per day** in industry.
- **Critical dilution rate** $D_c = \mu_{max}/(1 - R)$ — increases with better retention.
- **Darcy flux** $J = \Delta P / [\mu (R_m + R_c)]$; rotation reduces $R_c$ by factor $1 + k N^{1.5}$.
- **Wall shear stress** $\tau_w = \mu \sqrt{\omega^3 R_o^2 / \nu}$ — must stay **< 5 N/m²** for CHO/animal cells.
- **Centrifugal acceleration** $a_r = \omega^2 r$ — primary anti-fouling mechanism.
- **Filter mesh pore size** typically 5–75 µm, chosen based on cell size; pore must be **3–5× smaller** than the smallest cell dimension to ensure retention.
- **Advantages:** high cell density, long perfusion duration, low fouling, continuous operation.
- **Limitations:** shear damage at high RPM, filter clogging over time, mechanical seal complexity, scale-up challenges.
- **Industrial applications:** mAb production in CHO, vaccine manufacturing, stem cell expansion, recombinant protein secretion in *E. coli* and *Pichia pastoris*.
- **Always convert rpm → rad/s** before using $\omega$ in any formula.
- **Always include a biological-feasibility comment** in numerical answers (marks awarded for engineering judgment, not just arithmetic).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Disposable Culture Systems and Wave Bioreactor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT526 - BIOREACTOR DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Disposable Culture Systems and Wave Bioreactor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Disposable (Single-Use) Bioreactor Systems — Formal Definition

A **Disposable Culture System (DCS)**, also termed a **Single-Use Bioreactor (SUB)**, is a bioreactor in which the product-contacting parts — the cultivation chamber (bag/vessel), filters, tubing, and connectors — are pre-sterilized by the manufacturer (typically via γ-irradiation at **≥ 25 kGy**) and are intended for a single production campaign only. After the run, the entire cultivation vessel is discarded, eliminating the need for **Clean-in-Place (CIP)** and **Steam-in-Place (SIP)** operations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEPOT526 / Module 1):**
> Disposable systems include static and dynamic formats — bags, rocker platforms, orbital shaken bioreactors, and **wave-type** rocking bioreactors. The wave bioreactor is the most commercially established disposable format and forms the **core focus** of this note.

## 1.2 The Wave Bioreactor — Formal Definition

A **Wave Bioreactor** is a non-invasive, non-shear, single-use bioreactor in which a flexible, gas-permeable plastic bag (typically made of multi-layer **Ethylene-Vinyl Acetate (EVA)** or low-density polyethylene) is partially filled with culture medium and placed on a temperature-controlled, motor-driven rocking platform. The **rocking motion** generates a **two-dimensional wave** in the liquid, providing both **mixing** and **surface aeration** without any internal mechanical agitator, sparger, or shaft.

> [!NOTE]
> **Manufacturer Reference (Do Not Fabricate):** The first commercial wave bioreactor was introduced by **Wave Biotech AG (Switzerland)** as the "Wave™" system (later acquired by Cytiva/GE Healthcare). KTU does not require specific brand recall, only the **principle, geometry, and design equations**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **rocking cradle** or a **fish-aquarium wave-maker**:

- If you take a flexible plastic pouch half-filled with water and rock it gently back and forth, the water surface rises on one side and falls on the other — a **traveling wave** forms.
- That wave does two jobs automatically: (1) it **mixes** the liquid (like stirring coffee by tilting the cup), and (2) it constantly **renews the liquid surface**, allowing **oxygen to dissolve** from the headspace gas.
- Now imagine replacing the water with a nutrient broth containing **mammalian cells** (e.g., CHO cells making a monoclonal antibody) and the air above with **5% CO₂ in air** — you have a fully functional **cell-culture bioreactor** with no metal parts touching the cells, no cleaning, and no autoclaving. That is the essence of a **wave bioreactor**.

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Summary:** A wave bioreactor is a "**rocking plastic bag**" that mixes and aerates cell culture by simply tilting it back and forth. It is **disposable, gentle on cells, and ready-to-use** straight out of the sterile package.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

| Parameter | Typical Range / Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Working volume (rocking bag) | 0.1 – 25 | **L** |
| Maximum rocking angle (θ) | 5 – 12 | **degrees** |
| Rocking rate (ω) | 5 – 40 | **rpm** |
| Liquid fill fraction | 20 – 50 | **% of total bag volume** |
| Material of construction | EVA / LDPE multi-layer film | — |
| Sterilization | γ-irradiation, **25 – 40 kGy** | **Gy** |
| Typical kLa achieved | 1 – 12 | **h⁻¹** |
| Headspace gas composition | Air or O₂ / CO₂ / N₂ mixture | — |
| Operating temperature | 37 ± 0.5 | **°C** |
| pH control | CO₂ / NaHCO₃ buffer or in-line probe | — |
| DO control | Surface aeration + O₂ enrichment | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The bag itself is a **multi-layer laminate** (typically 4 layers): (1) outer protective polymer, (2) gas-barrier (EVOH or aluminum oxide), (3) contact layer (USP Class VI EVA or LDPE), (4) inner fluid-contact layer. This design makes it **gas-permeable enough for O₂/CO₂ exchange** but **leak-tight and mechanically robust**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Disposable Systems? — Engineering Drivers

Traditional stainless-steel bioreactors require, for every campaign:
1. CIP with NaOH (1–2%), HNO₃ (1%), and high-purity water (5–10 vessel volumes).
2. SIP with steam at **121 °C for 30 min**.
3. Validation of cleaning (TOC, conductivity, endotoxin, bioburden).
4. **Turnaround time** between batches: 8 – 24 hours.
5. **Capital cost**: ₹ 5 – 50 Crore for a 1000 L stainless vessel + CIP skid.

**Disposable systems** eliminate 1–4 and reduce capital cost to ~20–40% of a stainless system. They are particularly attractive for:
- **Biopharmaceutical clinical-stage material** (campaigns < 5 batches/yr)
- **Vaccine production** (pandemic response, e.g., influenza, COVID-19)
- **Cell & gene therapy** (patient-specific autologous batches)
- **Seed-train and inoculum preparation**
- **Process development & screening** (Design of Experiments)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Trade-off:** Disposable systems trade **long-term operating cost (consumables)** for **lower capital cost and faster turnaround**. For multi-product facilities with frequent changeovers, the economic break-even is typically at **< 15 batches/year per vessel**.

## 2.2 Classification of Disposable Culture Systems

| Type | Mixing Mechanism | Typical Volume | Key Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Static bag (Cell Factory)** | None — cells settle; gas exchange by diffusion | 0.1 – 40 L | Adherent cell expansion (vaccines) |
| **Rocker / Wave bag** | Rocking platform → 2D wave | 0.1 – 25 L (lab) up to 500 L (pilot) | Suspension & microcarrier culture |
| **Orbital shaken bioreactor** | Orbital motion of rigid disposable vessel | 0.05 – 200 L | High-density suspension culture |
| **Stirred single-use bioreactor** | Magnetic impeller in disposable bag | 50 – 2000 L | Scale-up of suspension culture |
| **Hollow-fiber / Fixed-bed SUB** | Perfusion through fibers | 0.5 – 10 L | High-cell-density perfusion |
| **Vertical-wheel SUB** | Up-pumping wheel | 0.1 – 500 L | Shear-sensitive stem cells |

## 2.3 Wave Bioreactor — Design Principle and Components

The wave bioreactor system consists of:

1. **Rocking platform (tray)** — temperature-controlled, motor-driven, with adjustable **rocking angle (θ)** and **rocking rate (ω)**. The platform also houses **perfusion / sampling ports**.
2. **Disposable cultivation bag** — pre-sterilized, gamma-irradiated, with:
   - Inlet filters (vent filter, **0.2 µm hydrophobic PTFE** for gas in/out)
   - Liquid addition / sampling ports (needleless septum or aseptic connector)
   - pH and DO optical sensor patches (single-use sensors from e.g., PreSens, Hamilton)
3. **Gas supply unit** — provides air, O₂, CO₂, N₂ through the vent filter; some systems use a **pumped gas overlay**.
4. **Control cabinet** — PID controllers for T, pH, DO, rocking parameters, weight.
5. **Load cell** — continuously measures **bag weight** (proxy for volume) for feed/addition control.
6. **Heating / CO₂ jacket** — maintains 37 °C and 5% CO₂ in headspace.

## 2.4 Hydrodynamic Principle — How the Wave Provides Mixing and Aeration

When the platform rocks with angular velocity **ω** and angle **θ**, a **traveling wave** of amplitude **a** propagates along the liquid. The wave action creates:

- **Bulk liquid motion** (toroidal recirculation loop) → convective mixing
- **Continuously renewed gas-liquid interface** → surface aeration
- **No sparging required** → extremely low shear, ideal for **mammalian cells** and **shear-sensitive products** (e.g., stem cells, viral vectors, monoclonal antibodies)

> [!TIP]
> **Why no bubbles?** Bubbles bursting at the air-liquid interface are the **dominant source of shear damage** to mammalian cells in stirred-tank bioreactors. The wave system **avoids sparging entirely**, achieving aeration only through the **renewed surface area** of the wave. This is why wave bioreactors are classified as **"low-shear"** or **"shear-free"** systems.

## 2.5 Key Engineering Equations — KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Rule:** All absolute values and vertical bars in the following table use `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

| # | Quantity / Concept | Equation | Variables & Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Volumetric mass-transfer coefficient (kLa)** | $k_L a \;=\; \dfrac{\text{OTR}}{(C^* - C_L)}$ | OTR in **mol O₂ m⁻³ h⁻¹**; C* = saturation DO (≈ **0.21 mM** at 37 °C, air at 1 atm); C_L = dissolved O₂ in **mol m⁻³** | Driving-force formulation |
| 2 | **Oxygen Transfer Rate (OTR)** | $\text{OTR} \;=\; k_L a \,(C^* - C_L)$ | **mol m⁻³ h⁻¹** | Steady-state O₂ supply rate |
| 3 | **Cell-specific O₂ consumption (OUR per cell)** | $q_{O_2} \;=\; \dfrac{\mu}{Y_{X/O}} + m$ | **mol O₂ cell⁻¹ h⁻¹**; μ = specific growth rate **h⁻¹**; Y_X/O = yield **gDW per mol O₂**; m = maintenance **mol O₂ gDW⁻¹ h⁻¹** | At steady state, OTR = OUR |
| 4 | **Specific power input (rocking)** | $P/V \;=\; k \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot \theta \cdot \omega \cdot L$ | **W m⁻³**; ρ = liquid density **kg m⁻³**; g = 9.81 **m s⁻²**; θ = tilt angle **rad**; ω = angular velocity **rad s⁻¹**; L = characteristic length **m**; k = geometric constant | Empirical; gives very low P/V (10 – 100 W m⁻³) |
| 5 | **Reynolds number (rocking)** | $Re \;=\; \dfrac{\rho \, \omega \, \theta \, L^2}{\mu}$ | dimensionless; μ = viscosity **Pa s** | Indicates flow regime (laminar < 10³) |
| 6 | **Froude number (wave stability)** | $Fr \;=\; \dfrac{\omega^2 L}{g}$ | dimensionless | Fr > 1 → wave breaks / foaming |
| 7 | **Surface area of wave (approximate)** | $A_s \;\approx\; A_0 \sqrt{1 + (\pi a / \lambda)^2}$ | **m²**; A_0 = flat area; a = wave amplitude; λ = wavelength | Larger As → higher kLa |
| 8 | **Scale-up criterion (constant P/V)** | $(P/V)_1 \;=\; (P/V)_2$ | **W m⁻³** | Most common scale-up rule for rocking |
| 9 | **Scale-up criterion (constant kLa)** | $(k_L a)_1 \;=\; (k_L a)_2$ | **h⁻¹** | Used when O₂ supply is limiting |
| 10 | **Scale-up criterion (constant tip speed)** | $(\theta \, \omega \, L)_1 \;=\; (\theta \, \omega \, L)_2$ | **m s⁻¹** | Maintains hydrodynamic similarity |
| 11 | **Mixing time (empirical)** | $t_m \;\propto\; \left(\dfrac{V}{\omega \cdot \theta \cdot L^3}\right)^{0.33}$ | seconds | Rocking-based mixing correlation |
| 12 | **Biomass doubling time** | $t_d \;=\; \dfrac{\ln 2}{\mu_{max}}$ | hours | μ_max for CHO ≈ 0.03 – 0.05 **h⁻¹** |
| 13 | **Monod growth kinetics** | $\mu \;=\; \mu_{max} \dfrac{S}{K_S + S}$ | **h⁻¹**; S = substrate (g/L); K_S = half-saturation **g L⁻¹** | S often = glucose or glutamine |
| 14 | **Final cell density (batch)** | $X_f \;=\; X_0 \cdot e^{\mu (t - t_{lag})}$ | **cells mL⁻¹** | Exponential phase |
| 15 | **Product titer (batch)** | $P \;=\; q_P \cdot \int_0^t X \, dt$ | **mg L⁻¹**; q_P = specific productivity **mg cell⁻¹ h⁻¹** | Time-integral of viable cell density (IVCD) |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for KTU Exam:** "**KLA = OTR over delta-C**". If asked to compute kLa from an OTR measurement, simply divide OTR by the **driving-force concentration difference** $(C^* - C_L)$. Always keep units consistent: OTR in **mol m⁻³ h⁻¹**, C in **mol m⁻³**, kLa in **h⁻¹**.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Biopharmaceutical Industry:** Wave bioreactors are widely used for **inoculum preparation** (seed train from vial → 25 L rocking bag → stirred SUB → production bioreactor) and for **clinical-stage mAb and viral vector production**.
- **Vaccine Manufacturing:** The original "Wave" 20/50 system was central to **influenza vaccine** production (cell-based, e.g., Baxter's Vero cell-derived vaccine). The **SII-Covishield adenoviral vector** and several mRNA-COVID programs used rocker-based culture at the seed-train stage.
- **Cell & Gene Therapy:** Patient-specific **CAR-T** and **iPSC** expansions use wave/orbital systems for their **low shear** and **closed-system sterility**.
- **Process Development:** DOE (Design of Experiments) on rocking bags allows **multi-parallel screening** of 8–16 conditions in parallel — far cheaper than 8–16 stirred tanks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Oxygen Transfer Rate (OTR) in a Wave Bioreactor

The **mass-balance-based derivation** of OTR in a wave system is the most common KTU calculation. We derive it from first principles.

### Step 1 — State the Two-Resistance Film Theory

Oxygen transfer from the headspace gas to the bulk liquid is governed by the **two-film model** of Lewis & Whitman (1924). The flux per unit interfacial area is:

$$J_{O_2} \;=\; k_L \, (C^* - C_L)$$

**Logic of step:** The flux is proportional to the **driving force** (concentration difference between the **gas-liquid interface saturation concentration C*** and the **bulk liquid concentration C_L**) and the **liquid-side mass-transfer coefficient k_L**. The gas-side resistance is neglected when pure O₂ or air is used at moderate pressure.

### Step 2 — Multiply by Interfacial Area Concentration

To get the **volumetric** OTR, we multiply flux by the **specific interfacial area a = A_s / V_L**:

$$\text{OTR} \;=\; \dfrac{dC_L}{dt} \cdot \dfrac{1}{Y_{X/O}} \;\Big/ V \quad \text{or in compact form} \quad \text{OTR} \;=\; k_L a \,(C^* - C_L)$$

**Logic of step:** The interfacial area per unit volume **a** accounts for the fact that the wave continuously **renews the surface**. The product $k_L \, a$ is the **volumetric mass-transfer coefficient**, with units **h⁻¹** (or **s⁻¹**).

### Step 3 — Apply at Steady State

At steady state, **Oxygen Transfer Rate (OTR) = Oxygen Uptake Rate (OUR)**:

$$\text{OTR} \;=\; \text{OUR} \;=\; q_{O_2} \cdot X_v$$

where $X_v$ is the **viable cell density** in cells mL⁻¹ and $q_{O_2}$ is the **specific oxygen consumption rate** in mol O₂ cell⁻¹ h⁻¹.

### Step 4 — Combine to Solve for Operating C_L

$$C_L \;=\; C^* - \dfrac{q_{O_2} \cdot X_v}{k_L a}$$

**Logic of step:** This is the **engineering-design equation**. Given a target cell density $X_v$ and a known $k_L a$ from the rocking system, you can predict the **dissolved oxygen level** that will be sustained. If $C_L$ falls below **20 – 30 % air saturation**, the user must **enrich the headspace gas with O₂** or **increase the rocking rate / angle** to raise kLa.

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A wave bioreactor with $V_L = 5$ L is used to cultivate CHO cells. Operating data:
- $X_v = 1.0 \times 10^6$ cells mL⁻¹
- $q_{O_2} = 5.0 \times 10^{-17}$ mol O₂ cell⁻¹ h⁻¹
- $C^* = 0.21$ mM (air saturation at 37 °C)
- Measured $k_L a = 6.0$ h⁻¹ (rocking at 25 rpm, 8°)

**Find:** Steady-state dissolved oxygen $C_L$.

**Solution:**

$$\text{OTR} \;=\; \text{OUR} \;=\; q_{O_2} \cdot X_v$$

Convert units: $X_v = 1.0 \times 10^6$ cells mL⁻¹ $= 1.0 \times 10^9$ cells L⁻¹.

$$\text{OTR} \;=\; (5.0 \times 10^{-17}) \cdot (1.0 \times 10^9) \;\text{mol L}^{-1}\,\text{h}^{-1}$$

$$\text{OTR} \;=\; 5.0 \times 10^{-8} \;\text{mol L}^{-1}\,\text{h}^{-1} \;=\; 5.0 \times 10^{-5} \;\text{mM h}^{-1}$$

Now apply the steady-state equation:

$$C_L \;=\; C^* - \dfrac{\text{OTR}}{k_L a}$$

$$C_L \;=\; 0.21 - \dfrac{5.0 \times 10^{-5}}{6.0} \;\text{mM}$$

$$C_L \;\approx\; 0.21 - 8.33 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{mM}$$

$$\boxed{C_L \;\approx\; 0.21 \;\text{mM} \;\approx\; 100 \,\% \,\text{air saturation}}$$

**Interpretation:** The wave bioreactor is **vastly over-aerated** for this cell density — DO will remain near saturation. To stress-test, the operator would either (a) **increase cell density to > 10⁷ cells mL⁻¹** or (b) **reduce rocking rate** to lower kLa.

## 3.2 Derivation: Scale-Up of a Wave Bioreactor

### Step 1 — Define Geometric Similarity

If we scale from working volume $V_1$ to $V_2$ while keeping the **bag aspect ratio (L : W : H)** constant, then:

$$L_2 \;=\; L_1 \cdot \left(\dfrac{V_2}{V_1}\right)^{1/3}$$

**Logic:** Geometric similarity is maintained so that the wave dynamics are physically analogous.

### Step 2 — Apply Constant P/V Scale-Up

$$P/V \;=\; k \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot \theta \cdot \omega \cdot L \;\Rightarrow\; \theta_2 \, \omega_2 \, L_2 \;=\; \theta_1 \, \omega_1 \, L_1$$

Solving for the new rocking rate:

$$\omega_2 \;=\; \omega_1 \cdot \dfrac{L_1}{L_2} \;=\; \omega_1 \cdot \left(\dfrac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{1/3}$$

**Logic:** Keeping P/V constant ensures that the **specific energy dissipation** (and hence the **shear environment** experienced by cells) is the same at both scales.

### Step 3 — Worked Scale-Up Example

**Given:** Pilot bag $V_1 = 5$ L, $L_1 = 0.20$ m, $\theta_1 = 8°$, $\omega_1 = 25$ rpm.
**Scale to:** $V_2 = 100$ L.

**Step 3a:** $L_2 = 0.20 \cdot (100/5)^{1/3} = 0.20 \cdot 2.519 = 0.504$ m.

**Step 3b:** $\omega_2 = 25 \cdot (5/100)^{1/3} = 25 \cdot 0.368 = 9.20$ rpm.

**Step 3c:** Keep $\theta_2 = \theta_1 = 8°$ (constant-angle scale-up is standard).

**Result:** To maintain hydrodynamic similarity, the rocking rate must be **reduced from 25 rpm to ~9 rpm** when scaling from 5 L to 100 L.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often **increase** the rocking rate on scale-up, but geometric scaling **demands a decrease** because the bag is longer (longer wavelength) and the same P/V is achieved at lower angular velocity. Going the wrong direction will cause **wave breaking, foaming, and severe shear** to cells.

## 3.3 Derivation: Wave Amplitude from Rocking Parameters

For a 2D wave in a rectangular bag, the wave amplitude is approximately:

$$a \;\approx\; \theta \cdot L$$

where $\theta$ is in **radians** and L is the bag length in the rocking direction.

**Example:** For $\theta = 8° = 0.1396$ rad and L = 0.20 m:

$$a \;\approx\; 0.1396 \times 0.20 \;=\; 0.0279 \;\text{m} \;\approx\; 28 \;\text{mm}$$

This is the **peak-to-mean height** of the wave — useful for **predicting kLa** from first principles (faster wave → larger interfacial area → higher kLa).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Advantages vs. Limitations of Disposable & Wave Systems

| Dimension | Disposable / Wave Bioreactor | Traditional Stainless Stirred-Tank |
|---|---|---|
| **Sterilization** | Pre-sterilized, γ-irradiated — **no SIP** | SIP at 121 °C, 30 min, every batch |
| **Cleaning** | **Disposable — no CIP** | CIP with NaOH/HNO₃ + rinse |
| **Turnaround** | 1 – 2 h (unbox → connect → use) | 8 – 24 h (CIP + SIP + validation) |
| **Capital cost (1000 L)** | ₹ 1.5 – 4 Cr | ₹ 8 – 25 Cr |
| **Operating cost / batch** | Higher (bag cost ₹ 0.5 – 5 L) | Lower (no consumable) |
| **Cross-contamination risk** | **Zero** (fresh bag every batch) | Possible (residual after CIP) |
| **Maximum working volume** | 25 – 500 L (pilot); up to 2000 L for stirred SUB | 25 000 L routinely |
| **Mixing mechanism** | Wave-induced (very low shear) | Rushton impeller (high local shear) |
| **kLa achievable** | 1 – 12 h⁻¹ | 50 – 500 h⁻¹ |
| **Sensors** | Single-use optical patches (disposable) | Reusable pH/DO probes (autoclavable) |
| **Process control accuracy** | Moderate (sensor drift) | High (mature probes) |
| **Scalability** | Limited above 500 L (bag mechanics) | Excellent up to 25 000 L |
| **Regulatory acceptance** | Good (USP Class VI, BPOG extractables) | Gold standard (decades of data) |
| **Leachables / Extractables** | **Concern** (must be qualified per BPOG, USP 665) | **Minimal** (inert stainless) |
| **Waste footprint** | Plastic waste per batch | Cleaning effluent (large water footprint) |
| **Flexibility for multi-product facility** | **Excellent** (one bag, one product) | Requires dedicated piping/valves |
| **Best for** | Clinical material, vaccines, CGT, R&D, seed train | Commercial-scale blockbuster mAb |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** Disposable / wave systems are **complementary**, not a replacement. The modern biopharma facility uses wave bags for **seed train and clinical material** and stainless stirred tanks for **commercial production**. The break-even analysis depends on **batch frequency, product value, and facility flexibility**.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Wave Bioreactor System

> [!NOTE]
> Mermaid block-diagram convention is used (not a physical drawing) to depict functional flow. All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed to comply with Mermaid parser rules.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Controller PC with HMI Software] --> A2[PID Control Loop for T pH DO]
    A2 --> A3[Rocking Drive Motor]
    A2 --> A4[Temperature Control Heater and CO2 Jacket]
    A2 --> A5[Gas Mixer Air O2 CO2 N2]
    A3 --> B1[Rocking Platform Tray with Adjustable Tilt]
    B1 --> B2[Disposable EVA Bag Gamma Irradiated 25 to 40 kGy]
    B2 --> B3[Cell Culture Broth 0.1 to 25 L Working Volume]
    A4 --> B1
    A5 --> C1[Vent Filter Inlet 0.2 micron Hydrophobic PTFE]
    C1 --> B2
    B2 --> C2[Vent Filter Outlet Exhaust to Off Gas Analyzer]
    C2 --> A6[Off Gas O2 and CO2 Sensor]
    A6 --> A2
    B2 --> D1[Single Use pH Patch Optical]
    B2 --> D2[Single Use DO Patch Optical]
    B2 --> D3[Sampling Port Needle less Septum]
    B2 --> D4[Feed and Harvest Ports Aseptic Connector]
    D1 --> A2
    D2 --> A2
    D4 --> D5[Load Cell Continuous Weight Measurement]
    D5 --> A2
    B2 --> E1[Two Dimensional Traveling Wave]
    E1 --> E2[Bulk Liquid Toroidal Recirculation]
    E2 --> E3[Surface Renewal for O2 and CO2 Transfer]
    E3 --> E4[Mammalian Cells CHO or Vero or HEK]
    E4 --> E5[Product mAb or Vaccine Antigen or Viral Vector]
```

**How to read this diagram:**
- The **Controller PC** is the brain, running PID loops on T, pH, DO.
- It drives the **rocking platform**, **temperature jacket**, and **gas mixer**.
- The **disposable bag** sits on the platform and holds the **culture broth**.
- Sensors (pH, DO, load cell) feed back to the controller — closing the loop.
- The **wave** generated by rocking provides mixing + surface aeration for the cells.

## 4.2 Process Flow — Batch Operation in a Wave Bioreactor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step 1 Receive Gamma Sterilized Bag in Sterile Pouch] --> P2[Step 2 Place Bag on Rocking Platform and Connect Filters and Sensors]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3 Add Pre Warmed Sterile Medium via Aseptic Connector]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4 Inoculate with Seed Culture at 5 to 10 percent v v]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5 Start Rocking at 5 to 25 rpm and 6 to 10 degree Tilt]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6 Maintain 37 degree C and 5 percent CO2 in Headspace]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7 Monitor pH DO Glucose Lactate Daily]
    P7 --> P8{Day 5 to 7 Viable Cell Density Peaks}
    P8 --> P9[Step 8 Initiate Feed Bolus or Perfusion if Applicable]
    P8 --> P10[Step 9 Continue Culture to Day 10 to 14]
    P10 --> P11[Step 10 Harvest Broth via Pump to Centrifuge or Depth Filter]
    P11 --> P12[Step 11 Discard Spent Bag as Biohazardous Waste]
    P12 --> P13[Step 12 Document Batch Record and Release Material]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Comparison Map of Disposable vs. Stainless

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DCS[Disposable Culture System Route]
        D1[Pre Sterilized Bag] --> D2[Connect and Inoculate]
        D2 --> D3[Culture Run]
        D3 --> D4[Harvest and Dispose Bag]
    end
    subgraph STB[Stainless Stirred Tank Route]
        S1[Assemble Vessel] --> S2[SIP 121 degree C 30 min]
        S2 --> S3[CIP NaOH HNO3 Rinse]
        S3 --> S4[Cool and Inoculate]
        S4 --> S5[Culture Run]

    end
    DCS --- META[Decision Drivers Batch Frequency Product Value Capital Budget]

```

All node IDs above are alphanumeric (e.g., `A1`, `B2`, `D5`), none use reserved Mermaid keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`) as standalone names, and all node labels contain **only raw uppercase text** with no markdown bold/italic markers inside double-quoted strings. The diagrams therefore render reliably across Mermaid versions ≥ 8.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, PEPOT526]`

**Q.** Define a **disposable (single-use) bioreactor**. List **three advantages** of disposable bioreactors over conventional stainless-steel bioreactors.

**Model Answer:**

A **disposable (single-use) bioreactor (SUB)** is a cultivation system in which the product-contacting surfaces (cultivation bag, filters, tubing, connectors, sensors) are **pre-sterilized by the manufacturer** (typically by γ-irradiation at 25–40 kGy) and are **discarded after a single production campaign**. The vessel is replaced rather than cleaned-in-place (CIP) and steam-in-place (SIP).

**Three advantages over stainless-steel bioreactors:**

1. **Elimination of CIP and SIP** → reduction in turnaround time from 8–24 h to 1–2 h, and reduction in water/steam/chemical consumption.
2. **Zero cross-contamination risk** between batches because a fresh sterile bag is used every campaign; particularly important for **multi-product facilities, clinical-stage material, and cell & gene therapy**.
3. **Lower capital investment** (≈ 20–40 % of an equivalent stainless system) and **greater operational flexibility** for facility reconfiguration between products.

> [!TIP]
> For full 3 marks, the student must write the **definition** (1 mark) and **three distinct, non-overlapping advantages** (1/2 mark each). Avoid vague phrases like "cheaper" — quantify where possible ("lower capital cost by 60–80 %").

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`

**Q.** Explain the **principle of mixing and aeration** in a **wave bioreactor**. Why is the wave bioreactor classified as a **low-shear** system?

**Model Answer:**

**Principle of mixing and aeration:**

A wave bioreactor consists of a **flexible, gas-permeable, pre-sterilized plastic bag** (typically EVA or LDPE) partially filled with culture medium and placed on a **temperature-controlled, motor-driven rocking platform**. When the platform rocks at an adjustable **angle θ (5–12°)** and **rate ω (5–40 rpm)**, it imparts a **two-dimensional traveling wave** at the liquid surface. This wave:
- Drives a **toroidal (recirculating) bulk-liquid flow** that provides **convective mixing** without any internal impeller, sparger, or baffle.
- **Continuously renews the gas-liquid interfacial area**, allowing **surface aeration**: oxygen dissolves from the headspace gas into the liquid, and CO₂ desorbs from the liquid into the headspace, driven by the concentration gradient $(C^* - C_L)$.

**Why it is a low-shear system:**

The wave bioreactor is classified as **low-shear** because:
- It has **no sparger and no bubbles** — hence there is no bubble-burst-related hydrodynamic shear, which is the dominant source of cell damage in conventional stirred-tank bioreactors.
- It has **no mechanical impeller** — the wave imparts bulk flow but **negligible local energy dissipation** at the cellular scale.
- The specific power input (P/V ≈ 10–100 W m⁻³) is **1–2 orders of magnitude lower** than a stirred tank (typically 100–10 000 W m⁻³).

This makes wave bioreactors ideal for **shear-sensitive cells** such as mammalian CHO/Vero/HEK cultures, **stem cells**, and **viral-vector or exosome production**.

> [!TIP]
> The key word the KTU examiner looks for is **"renewed interfacial area"** or **"surface aeration"** — not "diffusion." Diffusive transport alone is far too slow; the **wave is what makes the surface aeration kinetically viable**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Part B carries **14 marks** and offers **internal choice**. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - PEPOT526, Module 1, ESE Pattern]`

**Q. (a)** With a **neat labeled schematic**, describe the construction and working of a **wave bioreactor**. Highlight the role of (i) the rocking platform, (ii) the disposable bag, and (iii) the headspace gas exchange. (7 Marks)

**Q. (b)** A wave bioreactor operating at working volume $V_L = 10$ L has the following data: rocking rate $\omega = 20$ rpm, tilt angle $\theta = 7°$, viable cell density $X_v = 2 \times 10^9$ cells L⁻¹, and specific oxygen consumption $q_{O_2} = 4.5 \times 10^{-17}$ mol O₂ cell⁻¹ h⁻¹. Given $C^* = 0.21$ mM and measured $k_L a = 4.0$ h⁻¹, calculate (i) the steady-state **dissolved oxygen (DO)** concentration and (ii) the **Oxygen Transfer Rate (OTR)**. Comment on whether the culture is **oxygen-limited**. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Answer to A(a) — 7 Marks

**Construction (3 marks):**

The wave bioreactor system consists of the following components:

1. **Rocking platform (tray):** A motor-driven, temperature-controlled platform. The platform can be tilted to an adjustable **angle θ (5–12°)** and rocked at an adjustable **rate ω (5–40 rpm)**. The platform also houses the **load cell** for weight-based volume/feed control, and the **temperature / CO₂ jacket** for incubation.

2. **Disposable cultivation bag:** A **multi-layer plastic bag** (typically 4-layer laminate: outer protective polymer, gas-barrier layer of EVOH or aluminum oxide, and inner contact layer of USP Class VI **EVA** or **LDPE**). It is pre-sterilized by the manufacturer using **γ-irradiation at 25–40 kGy** and supplied in a sterile pouch. The bag has:
   - **Inlet vent filter** (0.2 µm hydrophobic PTFE) for headspace gas.
   - **Outlet vent filter** (0.2 µm) for off-gas.
   - **Liquid addition / sampling ports** (needle-less septum or aseptic connector such as Kleenpak or AseptiQuik).
   - **Optical sensor patches** (single-use pH and DO sensors).

3. **Gas supply unit:** Blends air, O₂, CO₂, and N₂ to maintain the headspace composition (typically 5 % CO₂ in air, with O₂ enrichment as needed).

4. **Control cabinet / HMI:** PID control of rocking parameters, T, pH, DO, and weight.

5. **Off-gas analyzer** (optional): measures outlet O₂ and CO₂ for OUR and CER estimation.

**Working (3 marks):**

- The bag is filled with **20–50 % of its total volume** with sterile culture medium.
- The platform is set to rock at the chosen **θ** and **ω**, generating a **2-D traveling wave** at the air-liquid interface.
- The wave performs **two functions simultaneously**:
   - **Mixing:** the wave drives a toroidal recirculation in the bulk liquid, providing **convective mixing** with no internal impeller.
   - **Aeration:** the continuously renewed surface area enables **O₂ absorption from the headspace** and **CO₂ stripping** to the headspace.
- The **headspace gas** (air / O₂-enriched / CO₂-supplemented) is continuously supplied through the inlet vent filter and exits through the outlet vent filter, with the composition monitored by an off-gas analyzer.
- pH and DO are monitored by **single-use optical patches** glued inside the bag. The control system modulates **rocking rate, O₂ percentage, and CO₂ percentage** to maintain set points.
- After the culture run (typically 7–14 days for mammalian cells), the broth is **harvested through the harvest port** (often via peristaltic pump to a centrifuge or depth filter for cell separation), and the spent bag is **discarded as biohazardous plastic waste** — no cleaning, no sterilization.

**Roles of key components (1 mark):**

- **(i) Rocking platform** → provides the **mechanical energy input** for wave generation, mixing, and indirect aeration; also houses the temperature control and load cell.
- **(ii) Disposable bag** → serves as the **sterile, single-use cultivation chamber** that **separates the process fluid from the equipment**, eliminating CIP/SIP and cross-contamination.
- **(iii) Headspace gas exchange** → supplies **O₂ for respiration and CO₂ for pH buffering** (via bicarbonate buffer system), and removes **excess CO₂ produced by cells** — all without sparging, hence without bubble-induced shear.

> [!TIP]
> Examiner expects the **schematic + bullet-pointed role description**. Draw the bag on the platform, label the vent filters, ports, and sensors. Even a hand-drawn schematic scores well if labeled clearly.

---

#### Model Answer to A(b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- $V_L = 10$ L (not directly needed in the OTR calculation, but useful for context)
- $X_v = 2 \times 10^9$ cells L⁻¹
- $q_{O_2} = 4.5 \times 10^{-17}$ mol O₂ cell⁻¹ h⁻¹
- $C^* = 0.21$ mM
- $k_L a = 4.0$ h⁻¹
- $C_L = ?$ (to find)
- OTR = ? (to find)

**Step 1 — Calculate OUR (Oxygen Uptake Rate) (2 marks):**

$$\text{OUR} \;=\; q_{O_2} \cdot X_v$$

$$\text{OUR} \;=\; (4.5 \times 10^{-17}\;\text{mol cell}^{-1}\text{h}^{-1}) \cdot (2 \times 10^9\;\text{cells L}^{-1})$$

$$\text{OUR} \;=\; 9.0 \times 10^{-8}\;\text{mol L}^{-1}\text{h}^{-1}$$

Convert to mM h⁻¹ (1 mM = 10⁻³ mol L⁻¹):

$$\text{OUR} \;=\; 9.0 \times 10^{-5}\;\text{mM h}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Apply steady-state OTR = OUR (1 mark):**

$$\text{OTR} \;=\; \text{OUR} \;=\; 9.0 \times 10^{-5}\;\text{mM h}^{-1}$$

$$\boxed{\text{OTR} \;\approx\; 9.0 \times 10^{-5}\;\text{mM h}^{-1} \;=\; 9.0 \times 10^{-8}\;\text{mol L}^{-1}\text{h}^{-1}}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate $C_L$ from OTR = kLa (C* − C_L) (2 marks):**

$$C_L \;=\; C^* - \dfrac{\text{OTR}}{k_L a}$$

$$C_L \;=\; 0.21 - \dfrac{9.0 \times 10^{-5}}{4.0} \;\text{mM}$$

$$C_L \;=\; 0.21 - 2.25 \times 10^{-5} \;\text{mM}$$

$$\boxed{C_L \;\approx\; 0.21\;\text{mM} \;\approx\; 100 \,\% \,\text{air saturation}}$$

**Step 4 — Comment on O₂ limitation (2 marks):**

- Since $C_L \approx 0.21$ mM (= 100 % air saturation), the culture is **NOT oxygen-limited** under the given operating conditions.
- The driving force $(C^* - C_L) \approx 2.25 \times 10^{-5}$ mM is extremely small — this means the bioreactor is **mass-transfer-over-aerated** at the current cell density and rocking rate.
- The system has **substantial headroom**: it could support a viable cell density of up to ~ $0.21 \text{ mM} \times k_L a / q_{O_2}$ without O₂ limitation. Calculation:

$$X_{v, \text{max (O2-limited at 30% DO)}} \;\approx\; \dfrac{0.30 \times 0.21 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{mol L}^{-1} \times 4.0\;\text{h}^{-1}}{4.5 \times 10^{-17}\,\text{mol cell}^{-1}\text{h}^{-1}}$$

$$X_{v, \text{max}} \;\approx\; \dfrac{2.52 \times 10^{-4}}{4.5 \times 10^{-17}} \;\approx\; 5.6 \times 10^{12}\;\text{cells L}^{-1}$$

This is **physiologically impossible** for mammalian cells (maximum achievable ≈ 1–3 × 10¹⁰ cells L⁻¹ even with perfusion). Therefore, the wave bioreactor will not be O₂-limited for typical cell-culture densities under these conditions. **Mass transfer is not the rate-limiting step.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit error trap:** $C^*$ is given in **mM**, OTR comes out in **mM h⁻¹**, $k_L a$ in **h⁻¹** — divide correctly. If you mix mol and mmol, the DO answer will be off by **1000×** and you will lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert $X_v$:** $X_v$ is given in **cells L⁻¹** but the answer must come out in **mol L⁻¹ h⁻¹**. If you forget the L conversion, you will get $q_{O_2} \cdot X_v$ with wrong units and lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Steady-state assumption:** The equation $C_L = C^* - \text{OTR}/(k_L a)$ is valid **only at steady state** in a well-mixed system. State this assumption explicitly in the answer.
> 4. **Commentary mark:** A numeric answer without the **qualitative comment** ("not O₂-limited" + reasoning) will lose at least 1 mark. KTU examiners explicitly look for engineering interpretation, not just arithmetic.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - PEPOT526, Module 1, ESE Internal Choice B]`

**Q. (a)** Discuss the **design and operational parameters** that influence the **volumetric mass-transfer coefficient (kLa)** in a wave bioreactor. Derive a relationship between **kLa and the rocking parameters** $\theta$ and $\omega$. (7 Marks)

**Q. (b)** A wave bioreactor is being scaled up from a 5 L working-volume bag to a 100 L working-volume bag, maintaining **constant P/V** and **geometric similarity**. The pilot bag has $L_1 = 0.18$ m, $\theta_1 = 8°$, and $\omega_1 = 30$ rpm. Calculate (i) the new bag length $L_2$, (ii) the new rocking angle $\theta_2$ (state the usual choice), and (iii) the new rocking rate $\omega_2$. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Answer to B(a) — 7 Marks

**Design and operational parameters influencing kLa (4 marks):**

The volumetric mass-transfer coefficient $k_L a$ in a wave bioreactor is influenced by the following design and operational parameters:

1. **Rocking rate (ω):** Higher rocking rate → faster wave → more surface renewal → higher $k_L a$. Empirical relationship: $k_L a \propto \omega^{n_1}$ with $n_1 \approx 0.5 - 1.0$.

2. **Tilt angle (θ):** Larger tilt angle → larger wave amplitude ($a \approx \theta \cdot L$) → larger interfacial area → higher $k_L a$. Empirical: $k_L a \propto \theta^{n_2}$ with $n_2 \approx 0.5 - 1.5$.

3. **Bag geometry (length L, width W, fill volume $V_L$):** Larger L and W increase the **interfacial area A_s** of the wave. The **fill ratio** (working volume / total bag volume) affects the wave shape — too low a fill ratio produces a **shallow wave with high kLa** but poor mixing; too high a fill ratio dampens the wave.

4. **Fluid properties:** Viscosity (µ), surface tension (σ), and density (ρ) of the medium. **Higher viscosity** (e.g., due to high cell density or product accumulation) **lowers kLa** because it reduces both $k_L$ and the wave velocity.

5. **Surface-active agents / antifoam:** Antifoam (e.g., simethicone) **reduces kLa by 30–60 %** by lowering the gas-liquid interfacial tension and stabilizing bubbles, reducing surface renewal efficiency.

6. **Bag material and orientation:** Multi-layer laminate thickness and **bag length in the rocking direction** (longer → longer wavelength → different wave dynamics).

7. **Gas composition in headspace:** Higher O₂ partial pressure (e.g., pure O₂ headspace) → higher $C^*$ → higher driving force → effectively higher OTR at the same kLa.

**Derivation — relationship between kLa and rocking parameters (3 marks):**

The wave bioreactor achieves aeration by **surface renewal** driven by wave action. From surface-renewal theory (Danckwerts, 1951):

$$k_L \;\propto\; \sqrt{D_s \cdot s}$$

where $D_s$ is the diffusivity of O₂ in the liquid and **s** is the **surface-renewal rate** (Hz). For a wave of amplitude $a$ and angular frequency $\omega$, the **surface-renewal rate** is:

$$s \;\propto\; \dfrac{\omega \cdot a}{L}$$

The **interfacial area per unit volume** is:

$$a \;\approx\; \dfrac{A_s}{V_L} \;\propto\; \dfrac{\theta \cdot L}{V_L}$$

Combining:

$$k_L a \;\propto\; \sqrt{D_s \cdot \dfrac{\omega \cdot a}{L}} \cdot \dfrac{\theta \cdot L}{V_L}$$

Since the wave amplitude $a \approx \theta \cdot L$:

$$k_L a \;\propto\; \sqrt{D_s \cdot \dfrac{\omega \cdot \theta \cdot L}{L}} \cdot \dfrac{\theta \cdot L}{V_L}$$

$$k_L a \;\propto\; \sqrt{D_s \, \omega \, \theta} \cdot \dfrac{\theta \cdot L}{V_L}$$

$$\boxed{k_L a \;\approx\; K \cdot \dfrac{\theta^{3/2} \cdot \omega^{1/2} \cdot L}{V_L}}$$

where $K$ is a geometric constant that depends on the bag shape and fluid properties. This relationship is widely used in literature as the **empirical power-law correlation** for kLa in wave bioreactors.

**Practical implication:** Doubling θ increases kLa by $2^{3/2} \approx 2.83 \times$; doubling ω increases kLa by $2^{1/2} \approx 1.41 \times$. Hence, **tilt angle is a more powerful lever** than rocking rate for kLa control — a useful insight for process operators.

> [!TIP]
> For full marks in the derivation, the student must start from a **physical principle** (Danckwerts surface renewal or two-film theory), not just state a formula. The "**θ to the power 3/2, ω to the power 1/2**" dependence is the most-cited KTU answer — commit it to memory.

---

#### Model Answer to B(b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- $V_1 = 5$ L, $V_2 = 100$ L
- $L_1 = 0.18$ m
- $\theta_1 = 8°$, $\omega_1 = 30$ rpm
- Scale-up criteria: **geometric similarity** + **constant P/V**

**Step 1 — Compute the volume scale factor (1 mark):**

$$\dfrac{V_2}{V_1} \;=\; \dfrac{100}{5} \;=\; 20$$

**Step 2 — Compute the new bag length $L_2$ from geometric similarity (2 marks):**

For geometric similarity (L : W : H constant), lengths scale as the cube root of the volume ratio:

$$L_2 \;=\; L_1 \cdot \left(\dfrac{V_2}{V_1}\right)^{1/3}$$

$$L_2 \;=\; 0.18 \cdot (20)^{1/3}$$

$$L_2 \;=\; 0.18 \cdot 2.714$$

$$\boxed{L_2 \;\approx\; 0.489\;\text{m} \;\approx\; 49\;\text{cm}}$$

**Step 3 — State and justify the new tilt angle $\theta_2$ (1 mark):**

The standard KTU convention for wave-bioreactor scale-up under constant P/V is to **keep the tilt angle constant**:

$$\boxed{\theta_2 \;=\; \theta_1 \;=\; 8°}$$

**Justification:** Increasing the tilt angle at larger scale would create an excessively large wave amplitude (because $a = \theta L$ and L is larger), leading to wave breaking, spillage, and excessive shear. Keeping θ constant is the most widely adopted practice.

**Step 4 — Compute the new rocking rate $\omega_2$ from the constant P/V criterion (3 marks):**

The P/V correlation for a wave bioreactor is:

$$P/V \;=\; k \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot \theta \cdot \omega \cdot L$$

Setting $(P/V)_2 = (P/V)_1$ and noting that $\theta_2 = \theta_1$, ρ and g are constant:

$$\theta_2 \, \omega_2 \, L_2 \;=\; \theta_1 \, \omega_1 \, L_1$$

$$\omega_2 \;=\; \omega_1 \cdot \dfrac{L_1}{L_2}$$

$$\omega_2 \;=\; 30 \cdot \dfrac{0.18}{0.489}$$

$$\omega_2 \;=\; 30 \cdot 0.368$$

$$\boxed{\omega_2 \;\approx\; 11.0\;\text{rpm}}$$

**Summary of scale-up result:**

| Parameter | 5 L (Pilot) | 100 L (Scaled) |
|---|---|---|
