- *Washout:* $X^{\ast} = 0,\ S^{\ast} = S_0$ (always exists regardless of $D$).
- *Non-trivial:* $X^{\ast} = Y_{X/S}\,(S_0 - S^{\ast}) > 0$ and $S^{\ast} = \dfrac{K_s\,D}{\mu_m - D}$ — **this exists only if $D < \mu_m$**.

**Step 3 — Linearise the dynamics.** For a small perturbation $(x, s)$ around the steady state, expand the RHS to first order using the Taylor series. The linearised system is $\dot{\mathbf{z}} = \mathbf{J}\,\mathbf{z}$ where $\mathbf{J}$ is the **2×2 Jacobian matrix**.

**Step 4 — Examine eigenvalues.** Stability is determined by the real parts of the eigenvalues of $\mathbf{J}$:

- Both Re$(\lambda) < 0$ → **locally asymptotically stable**.
- At least one Re$(\lambda) > 0$ → **unstable**.

**Step 5 — The "Why" behind stability.** The biological self-regulating feedback is the cause: if biomass momentarily drops, substrate rises (since less is consumed), which raises $\mu$, which raises the biomass growth term — pushing the system back.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity / Result | Formula | Condition / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Monod specific growth rate | $\mu(S) = \dfrac{\mu_m\,S}{K_s + S}$ | h⁻¹ |
| 2 | Mass balance — biomass | $\dfrac{dX}{dt} = \mu(S)\,X - D\,X$ | g·L⁻¹·h⁻¹ |
| 3 | Mass balance — substrate | $\dfrac{dS}{dt} = -\dfrac{1}{Y_{X/S}}\,\mu(S)\,X + D\,(S_0-S)$ | g·L⁻¹·h⁻¹ |
| 4 | Washout steady state | $X^{\ast} = 0,\ S^{\ast} = S_0$ | Always exists |
| 5 | Non-trivial steady-state substrate | $S^{\ast} = \dfrac{K_s\,D}{\mu_m - D}$ | Requires $D < \mu_m$ |
| 6 | Non-trivial steady-state biomass | $X^{\ast} = Y_{X/S}\,(S_0 - S^{\ast}) = Y_{X/S}\left(S_0 - \dfrac{K_s D}{\mu_m - D}\right)$ | g·L⁻¹ |
| 7 | Critical dilution rate (washout limit) | $D_c = \mu_m \cdot \dfrac{S_0}{K_s + S_0}$ | h⁻¹ |
| 8 | Derivative $\partial \mu/\partial S$ at $S^{\ast}$ | $\dfrac{\partial \mu}{\partial S}\bigg\vert_{S^{\ast}} = \dfrac{\mu_m\,K_s}{(K_s+S^{\ast})^2}$ | h⁻¹·L·g⁻¹ |
| 9 | Trace of Jacobian (non-trivial) | $\text{tr}(\mathbf{J}) = -\dfrac{X^{\ast}}{Y_{X/S}}\,\dfrac{\partial \mu}{\partial S}\bigg\vert_{S^{\ast}} - D < 0$ | Always negative |
| 10 | Determinant of Jacobian (non-trivial) | $\det(\mathbf{J}) = D \cdot \dfrac{X^{\ast}}{Y_{X/S}}\,\dfrac{\partial \mu}{\partial S}\bigg\vert_{S^{\ast}} > 0$ | Always positive |
| 11 | Washout eigenvalue (X-direction) | $\lambda_1 = \mu(S_0) - D = \dfrac{\mu_m S_0}{K_s+S_0} - D$ | Sign determines local stability of washout |
| 12 | Washout eigenvalue (S-direction) | $\lambda_2 = -D < 0$ | Always negative |
| 13 | Volumetric productivity | $P = D \cdot X^{\ast} = D \cdot Y_{X/S}\left(S_0 - \dfrac{K_s D}{\mu_m-D}\right)$ | g·L⁻¹·h⁻¹ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Avoidance Tip:** In KTU 2024 valuation, **never** use the symbol "$D$" for both the dilution rate and the determinant; if needed, write $\det(\mathbf{J})$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The stability result $\det(\mathbf{J}) > 0,\ \text{tr}(\mathbf{J}) < 0$ for the non-trivial steady state means a chemostat is **inherently self-correcting** against small upsets (e.g., feed surges, temperature drifts, pump fluctuations). This is why chemostats dominate industrial **continuous bioprocesses**: antibiotic production, single-cell protein, recombinant protein, and **wastewater treatment** (where the chemostat is called an "activated-sludge tank" and *washout* corresponds to process failure). The stability analysis also gives a quantitative **critical dilution rate** $D_c$ — the maximum safe throughput — which is a primary design specification.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Non-Trivial Steady State

Start from $\dfrac{dX}{dt} = 0$ and $\dfrac{dS}{dt} = 0$:

**Step (a)** — From biomass balance, factor out $X$:

$$X\,(\mu(S) - D) = 0$$

This yields two possibilities: either $X^{\ast} = 0$ (washout) **or** $\mu(S^{\ast}) = D$.

**Step (b)** — Apply $\mu(S^{\ast}) = D$ to the Monod expression:

$$\frac{\mu_m\,S^{\ast}}{K_s + S^{\ast}} = D$$

**Step (c)** — Cross-multiply:

$$\mu_m\,S^{\ast} = D\,(K_s + S^{\ast})$$

**Step (d)** — Collect $S^{\ast}$ terms on one side:

$$\mu_m\,S^{\ast} - D\,S^{\ast} = D\,K_s$$

**Step (e)** — Factor and solve:

$$S^{\ast}\,(\mu_m - D) = D\,K_s \quad\Longrightarrow\quad S^{\ast} = \frac{K_s\,D}{\mu_m - D}$$

**Step (f)** — Substitute into the substrate balance (set $dS/dt = 0$):

$$0 = -\frac{1}{Y_{X/S}}\,\mu(S^{\ast})\,X^{\ast} + D\,(S_0 - S^{\ast})$$

Since $\mu(S^{\ast}) = D$:

$$0 = -\frac{D}{Y_{X/S}}\,X^{\ast} + D\,(S_0 - S^{\ast})$$

**Step (g)** — Divide by $D$ (which is non-zero) and solve:

$$X^{\ast} = Y_{X/S}\,(S_0 - S^{\ast}) = Y_{X/S}\!\left(S_0 - \frac{K_s D}{\mu_m - D}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Existence condition:** For $X^{\ast} > 0$, we require $S^{\ast} < S_0$, which mathematically reduces to $D < \mu_m \cdot \dfrac{S_0}{K_s+S_0} \equiv D_c$. Above $D_c$, **only washout exists**.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Jacobian Matrix

Define state vector $\mathbf{z} = (X,\ S)^T$. The dynamics are:

$$f_1(X,S) = \mu(S)\,X - D\,X, \qquad f_2(X,S) = -\frac{1}{Y_{X/S}}\,\mu(S)\,X + D\,(S_0 - S)$$

**Step (a)** — Partial derivatives of $f_1$:

$$\frac{\partial f_1}{\partial X} = \mu(S) - D, \qquad \frac{\partial f_1}{\partial S} = X\,\frac{\partial \mu}{\partial S} = X\,\frac{\mu_m\,K_s}{(K_s+S)^2}$$

**Step (b)** — Partial derivatives of $f_2$:

$$\frac{\partial f_2}{\partial X} = -\frac{1}{Y_{X/S}}\,\mu(S)$$

$$\frac{\partial f_2}{\partial S} = -\frac{1}{Y_{X/S}}\,X\,\frac{\partial \mu}{\partial S} - D$$

**Step (c)** — Assemble the Jacobian:

$$\mathbf{J}(X,S) = \begin{pmatrix} \mu(S) - D & X\,\dfrac{\mu_m K_s}{(K_s+S)^2} \\[8pt] -\dfrac{\mu(S)}{Y_{X/S}} & -\dfrac{X}{Y_{X/S}}\,\dfrac{\mu_m K_s}{(K_s+S)^2} - D \end{pmatrix}$$

## 3.3 Eigenvalue Analysis at the Non-Trivial Steady State

**Step (a)** — Evaluate $J_{11}$ at the non-trivial steady state. Because $\mu(S^{\ast}) = D$:

$$J_{11} = D - D = 0$$

**Step (b)** — Define shorthand for clarity:

$$\mu'_S \;\equiv\; \left.\frac{\partial \mu}{\partial S}\right\vert_{S^{\ast}} = \frac{\mu_m\,K_s}{(K_s+S^{\ast})^2} \quad (> 0 \text{ always})$$

So $J_{12} = X^{\ast}\,\mu'_S$ and $J_{22} = -\dfrac{X^{\ast}\,\mu'_S}{Y_{X/S}} - D$.

**Step (c)** — Write the Jacobian matrix at the non-trivial steady state:

$$\mathbf{J}^{\ast} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & X^{\ast}\,\mu'_S \\[6pt] -\dfrac{D}{Y_{X/S}} & -\dfrac{X^{\ast}\,\mu'_S}{Y_{X/S}} - D \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step (d)** — Compute the trace:

$$\text{tr}(\mathbf{J}^{\ast}) = 0 + \left(-\frac{X^{\ast}\,\mu'_S}{Y_{X/S}} - D\right) = -\frac{X^{\ast}\,\mu'_S}{Y_{X/S}} - D$$

Since $X^{\ast} > 0,\ \mu'_S > 0,\ Y_{X/S} > 0,\ D > 0$:

$$\boxed{\;\text{tr}(\mathbf{J}^{\ast}) < 0\;}$$

**Step (e)** — Compute the determinant:

$$\det(\mathbf{J}^{\ast}) = (0)\!\left(-\frac{X^{\ast}\,\mu'_S}{Y_{X/S}} - D\right) - \left(X^{\ast}\,\mu'_S\right)\!\left(-\frac{D}{Y_{X/S}}\right)$$

$$\det(\mathbf{J}^{\ast}) = 0 + \frac{D\,X^{\ast}\,\mu'_S}{Y_{X/S}}$$

Since $D > 0,\ X^{\ast} > 0,\ \mu'_S > 0,\ Y_{X/S} > 0$:

$$\boxed{\;\det(\mathbf{J}^{\ast}) > 0\;}$$

**Step (f)** — Interpret via the Routh–Hurwitz criterion for 2×2 systems. A real 2×2 matrix is **asymptotically stable** iff both trace $< 0$ and determinant $> 0$. Both conditions are satisfied, therefore:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion (Non-Trivial Steady State):** The non-trivial steady state of a Monod chemostat is **always locally asymptotically stable** for any $0 < D < D_c$. It is a **stable node** (real eigenvalues) when discriminant is positive, or a **stable spiral** (complex eigenvalues) when discriminant is negative.

**Step (g)** — Eigenvalues (roots of $\lambda^2 - \text{tr}\,\lambda + \det = 0$):

$$\lambda_{\pm} = \frac{\text{tr}(\mathbf{J}^{\ast}) \pm \sqrt{\text{tr}(\mathbf{J}^{\ast})^2 - 4\,\det(\mathbf{J}^{\ast})}}{2}$$

The discriminant may be written explicitly as:

$$\Delta = \left(\frac{X^{\ast}\,\mu'_S}{Y_{X/S}} + D\right)^{\!2} - \frac{4\,D\,X^{\ast}\,\mu'_S}{Y_{X/S}}$$

Whether the eigenvalues are real or complex, their real parts are both equal to $\dfrac{\text{tr}}{2} < 0$, confirming stability.

## 3.4 Eigenvalue Analysis at the Washout Steady State

**Step (a)** — Substitute $X^{\ast} = 0,\ S^{\ast} = S_0$ into the Jacobian. The $J_{12}$ entry vanishes because $X^{\ast} = 0$:

$$\mathbf{J}_{\text{wo}} = \begin{pmatrix} \mu(S_0) - D & 0 \\[4pt] -\dfrac{\mu(S_0)}{Y_{X/S}} & -D \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step (b)** — Eigenvalues are read directly from the triangular matrix:

$$\lambda_1 = \mu(S_0) - D = \frac{\mu_m S_0}{K_s + S_0} - D, \qquad \lambda_2 = -D$$

**Step (c)** — Interpret:

- $\lambda_2 = -D < 0$ always.
- $\lambda_1 > 0 \iff D < \dfrac{\mu_m S_0}{K_s+S_0} = D_c$.
- $\lambda_1 < 0 \iff D > D_c$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion (Washout Steady State):**
> - For $0 < D < D_c$: one eigenvalue positive, one negative → **washout is a saddle point (unstable)**. Perturbations away from washout are *repelled*, pushing the system toward the stable non-trivial steady state.
> - For $D > D_c$: both eigenvalues negative → **washout is locally asymptotically stable** (the only remaining steady state).

## 3.5 Phase-Plane Interpretation

The stability analysis is summarised by the **operating diagram**: on the $S$–$X$ phase plane, trajectories starting from any initial condition with $X_0 > 0$ and $S_0 \leq S(0) \leq S_0$ are attracted to the non-trivial steady state, never to washout, as long as $0 < D < D_c$. A single stable manifold (the line $X = 0$ for the washout state) defines the unstable direction; everything else flows into the viable steady state.

## 3.6 Critical Dilution Rate & Maximum Productivity

Set $dP/dD = 0$ on $P(D) = D\,X^{\ast}(D)$. The result is the classic optimal dilution rate for a chemostat:

$$D_{\text{opt}} = \mu_m \left(1 - \sqrt{\frac{K_s}{K_s + S_0}}\right)$$

This lies strictly below $D_c$ and is the value of $D$ that maximises biomass productivity in a stable operating region.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Chemostat Stability Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: System at Steady State] --> B{Is Xstar greater than 0?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Non-Trivial Steady State]
    B -- No --> D[Washout Steady State]

    C --> E{Check D range}
    E --> F[D is less than Dc]
    E --> G[D is greater than Dc]
    F --> H[Stable: Asymptotically Stable Node or Spiral]
    G --> I[Unphysical: Non-trivial does not exist when D greater than Dc]

    D --> J{Check D range}
    J --> K[D is less than Dc]
    J --> L[D is greater than Dc]
    K --> M[Unstable: Saddle Point - one positive eigenvalue]
    L --> N[Stable: Node - both eigenvalues negative]

    H --> O[Operating Point: Viable Continuous Bioprocess]
    M --> P[Repeller: Any tiny biomass contamination grows back]
    N --> Q[Operating Point: Washout - no production]

    style A fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#1A5490,color:#FFFFFF
    style C fill:#2ECC71,stroke:#1A7A3F,color:#FFFFFF
    style D fill:#E67E22,stroke:#A04000,color:#FFFFFF
    style H fill:#27AE60,stroke:#145A32,color:#FFFFFF

    style N fill:#7F8C8D,stroke:#34495E,color:#FFFFFF
    style O fill:#F1C40F,stroke:#7D6608,color:#000000
    style P fill:#F39C12,stroke:#7E5109,color:#FFFFFF
    style Q fill:#95A5A6,stroke:#424949,color:#FFFFFF
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of Chemostat Stability Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT BLOCK]
        S0[Feed Substrate S0]
        Dval[Dilution Rate D]
        Ksval[Half Saturation Ks]
        umval[Max Growth Rate um]
        Yval[Yield YXS]
    end

    subgraph EQUATIONS[GOVERNING EQUATIONS]
        MonodEq[Monod Kinetics: mu equals um S over Ks plus S]
        MassBalX[Biomass Balance: dX by dt equals mu X minus D X]
        MassBalS[Substrate Balance: dS by dt equals minus mu X over YXS plus D times S0 minus S]
    end

    subgraph STEADYSTATE[STEADY STATE SOLVER]
        SS1[Solve for dX by dt equals 0]
        SS2[Solve for dS by dt equals 0]
        SS3[Compute Sstar and Xstar]
    end

    subgraph STABILITY[LINEAR STABILITY MODULE]
        Jac[Build 2x2 Jacobian Matrix J]
        Trace[Compute trace of J]
        Det[Compute det of J]
        Eigen[Solve Characteristic Polynomial]
    end

    subgraph DECISION[STABILITY DECISION OUTPUT]
        StableViable[Non-trivial steady state is STABLE]
        UnstableWashout[Washout is UNSTABLE saddle]
        WashoutOnly[Washout is the only stable state]
    end

    S0 --> MonodEq
    Ksval --> MonodEq
    umval --> MonodEq
    Dval --> MassBalX
    Dval --> MassBalS
    MonodEq --> MassBalX
    MonodEq --> MassBalS
    MassBalX --> SS1
    MassBalS --> SS2
    SS1 --> SS3
    SS2 --> SS3
    SS3 --> Jac
    Jac --> Trace
    Jac --> Det
    Jac --> Eigen
    Trace --> DECISION
    Det --> DECISION
    Eigen --> DECISION
    Trace --> StableViable
    Det --> StableViable
    Eigen --> UnstableWashout
    Eigen --> WashoutOnly

    style INPUT fill:#D6EAF8,stroke:#1B4F72,color:#000000
    style EQUATIONS fill:#FCF3CF,stroke:#7D6608,color:#000000
    style STEADYSTATE fill:#D5F5E3,stroke:#196F3D,color:#000000
    style STABILITY fill:#FADBD8,stroke:#78281F,color:#000000
    style DECISION fill:#E8DAEF,stroke:#512E5F,color:#000000
```

## 4.3 ASCII Phase-Plane Schematic (Qualitative)

```
   S (substrate)
   ^
   |  S0 -------> (WASHOUT: X* = 0, S* = S0)   UNSTABLE
   |     \                                 /
   |      \                               /
   |       \                             /
   |        \      Stable               /
   |         \    Manifold             /
   |          \   (curves into      /
   |           \  stable point)    /
   |            \                 /
   |             \               /
   |              \  S* < S0   /
   |               *  (STABLE NON-TRIVIAL SS: X* > 0, S* < S0)
   |              /
   |             /
   |            /
   |           /
   |          /
   |         /
   +---------+-------------------------> X (biomass)
            X*
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The unstable washout point at $(0, S_0)$ has one stable direction (along $X = 0$) and one unstable direction (into the interior of the positive quadrant). All interior trajectories are attracted to the stable non-trivial point $(X^{\ast}, S^{\ast})$.
