## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram – PFTR Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **block-level functional architecture** of a Plug Flow Tubular Reactor, illustrating the flow of fluid, the differential control volume, the integral accumulators, and the boundary inputs/outputs. This representation is preferred over a physical sketch, which cannot be rendered directly in this medium.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inlet1["Inlet Stream\nSubstrate A\nCA0 nu0 TA0 PA0"] --> valveA["Feed Control\nDistributor"]
    valveA --> reactor1["Reactor Tube Volume V\nAxial Coordinate z\nL A cross"]
    reactor1 --> outlet1["Outlet Stream\nProduct plus unreacted A\nCAf XAf TAf PAf"]
    reactor1 --> monitor1["Concentration Profile Probe\nC_A vs z Curve"]
    reactor1 --> monitor2["Temperature Profile Probe\nT vs z Curve"]
    reactor1 --> monitor3["Conversion Profile Probe\nX_A vs z Curve"]
    inlet2["Reactor Jacket Coolant\nTJ Tin TJout"] --> jacket1["Heat Exchange Jacket\nDuty Q UA DeltaTlm"]
    jacket1 --> reactor1
    inlet3["Catalyst or Immobilised Biocatalyst\nPacking Geometry\nInternal Surface Area a"] --> reactor1
    outlet2["Effluent Sampler\nResidence Time Distribution\nExit Age Function E t"] --> monitor4["RTD Analysis Block\nMean Residence Time t_bar\nVariance sigma squared"]
    reactor1 --> outlet2
    monitor1 --> analysis["Design Equation Integrator\nIntegral of dX_A over minus r_A\nLevenspiel Plot Area"]
    monitor2 --> analysis
    monitor3 --> analysis
    monitor4 --> analysis
    analysis --> output1["Required Volume V\nOperating Space Time tau\nOperating Damkohler Da"]
    analysis --> output2["Comparison Block\nPFTR Volume versus CSTR Volume\nPerformance Ratio"]
```

### 4.2 Schematic Description of the PFTR Internal Flow

For a clearer mental image, the PFTR can be visualised as a long cylindrical tube of length $L$ and cross-sectional area $A_c$. Fluid enters at $z = 0$ with concentration $C_{A0}$ and exits at $z = L$ with concentration $C_{A,f} = C_{A0}(1 - X_{A,f})$.

- The **concentration profile** $C_A(z)$ is a monotonically **decreasing curve** along the axis (for $A \rightarrow$ products).
- The **conversion profile** $X_A(z)$ is a monotonically **increasing curve**, starting at 0 and asymptotically approaching $X_{A,f}$.
- For a **first-order** reaction, both profiles are **exponential decays/rises**.

```
   Inlet (z = 0)                Outlet (z = L)
   ┌────────────┐    ┌─────────────────────┐    ┌────────────┐
   │ CA = CA0   │ ─► │  CA decreases along z │ ─► │ CA = CAf   │
   │ XA = 0     │    │  XA increases along z │    │ XA = XAf   │
   │ T = T0     │    │  T varies per energy  │    │ T = Tf     │
   └────────────┘    └─────────────────────┘    └────────────┘
   ◄────────── Differential Volume dV = A_c dz ──────────►
   ◄────────────────────── Tube Length L ──────────────────►
```

### 4.3 Concentration Profile Equation (PFTR, 1st Order)

For a first-order reaction, the local concentration at axial position $z$ is given by:

$$
C_A(z) = C_{A0} \cdot \exp\!\left(-k \cdot \frac{z}{u}\right) = C_{A0} \cdot e^{-kz/u}
$$

where $u = \nu_0 / A_c$ is the linear velocity. The corresponding conversion at position $z$ is:

$$
X_A(z) = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{k z}{u}\right)
$$

This exponential profile is **the signature of an ideal plug flow reactor** and is heavily tested in KTU exams.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A.1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> State and explain the general design equation of a Plug Flow Tubular Reactor. What physical interpretation can be given to the integral $\int_0^{X_{A,f}} \dfrac{dX_A}{-r_A}$?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The general design equation of a PFTR, derived from a steady-state mole balance on a differential volume $dV$, is:

$$
\frac{V}{F_{A0}} = \int_0^{X_{A,f}} \frac{dX_A}{-r_A}
$$

where $V$ is the reactor volume, $F_{A0}$ is the inlet molar flow rate of A, and $-r_A$ is the rate of disappearance of A expressed as a function of conversion $X_A$.

**Physical Interpretation (2 of the 3 marks):** The integral $\int_0^{X_{A,f}} \dfrac{dX_A}{-r_A}$ represents the **area under the curve of $1/(-r_A)$ versus $X_A$** (commonly called a *Levenspiel plot*) from $X_A = 0$ to the desired final conversion $X_{A,f}$. The product $F_{A0}$ multiplied by this area yields the volume $V$ of the reactor. (3 Marks Total)

---

#### **Question A.2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

> Differentiate between a CSTR and a PFTR with respect to residence time distribution, concentration profile, and design equation form.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Feature** | **CSTR** | **PFTR** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **RTD** | Exponential decay, $E(t) = \dfrac{1}{\tau}e^{-t/\tau}$ | Dirac delta at $t = \tau$, $E(t) = \delta(t - \tau)$ |
| **Concentration** | Uniform throughout reactor at $C_{A,f}$ | Varies along axial position $z$ |
| **Design Equation** | Algebraic: $\tau = \dfrac{C_{A0} X_{A,f}}{-r_{A,f}}$ | Integral: $\tau = \displaystyle\int_0^{X_{A,f}} \dfrac{dX_A}{-r_A}$ |

The CSTR is back-mixed, whereas the PFTR has zero back-mixing. (3 Marks Total)

---

### 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question B (Choice 1) – Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> **(a) [7 Marks]** For a first-order liquid-phase reaction $A \rightarrow \text{products}$ with $k = 0.05 \; s^{-1}$, the feed concentration is $C_{A0} = 1.5 \; kmol/m^3$ and the volumetric flow rate is $\nu_0 = 0.002 \; m^3/s$. A PFTR is to be designed to achieve a conversion of $X_{A,f} = 0.75$.
> 
> Calculate:
> 1. The required reactor volume $V$.
> 2. The space time $\tau$.
> 3. The Damköhler number $Da$.
> 
> **(b) [7 Marks)** If the same conversion is to be achieved in a CSTR operating with the same feed, calculate the required CSTR volume and the volume ratio $V_{PFTR}/V_{CSTR}$. Comment on the result.

**Model Solution to Part (a) – 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 – Calculate the space time first using the first-order PFTR formula:**

$$
\tau = \frac{1}{k} \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{1 - X_{A,f}}\right)
$$

$$
\tau = \frac{1}{0.05} \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{1 - 0.75}\right) = 20 \times \ln(4) = 20 \times 1.3863 = 27.726 \; s
$$

**Step 2 – Calculate the reactor volume:**

$$
V = \nu_0 \cdot \tau = 0.002 \times 27.726 = 0.05545 \; m^3
$$

**Step 3 – Calculate the Damköhler number:**

$$
Da = k \cdot \tau = 0.05 \times 27.726 = 1.386 \quad (\text{dimensionless})
$$

**Mark Distribution:** Formula (2 marks) + substitution (2 marks) + final answer with units (1 mark) for $\tau$; $V$ from $\tau$ (1 mark); $Da$ (1 mark) = 7 marks total.

**Model Solution to Part (b) – 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 – CSTR space time for first-order reaction:**

$$
\tau_{CSTR} = \frac{C_{A0} X_{A,f}}{k C_{A0}(1 - X_{A,f})} = \frac{X_{A,f}}{k(1 - X_{A,f})}
$$

$$
\tau_{CSTR} = \frac{0.75}{0.05 \times 0.25} = \frac{0.75}{0.0125} = 60 \; s
$$

**Step 2 – CSTR volume:**

$$
V_{CSTR} = \nu_0 \cdot \tau_{CSTR} = 0.002 \times 60 = 0.120 \; m^3
$$

**Step 3 – Volume ratio:**

$$
\frac{V_{PFTR}}{V_{CSTR}} = \frac{0.05545}{0.120} = 0.4621 \quad \text{or} \quad 46.21\%
$$

**Step 4 – Comment:**

The PFTR requires only about **46%** of the volume of the CSTR to achieve the same conversion of 75% for a first-order reaction. The PFTR is more efficient than the CSTR for all positive-order kinetics. (1 mark)

**Mark Distribution:** $\tau_{CSTR}$ (2 marks); $V_{CSTR}$ (1 mark); ratio (2 marks); physical comment (2 marks) = 7 marks total.

---

#### **Question B (Choice 1) – Question B (14 Marks – Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

> **(a) [7 Marks]** A second-order liquid-phase reaction $2A \rightarrow \text{products}$ with $k = 0.08 \; m^3/(kmol \cdot s)$ occurs in a PFTR. The inlet conditions are $C_{A0} = 0.5 \; kmol/m^3$ and $\nu_0 = 0.001 \; m^3/s$. Determine the reactor volume required to achieve $X_{A,f} = 0.90$.
> 
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the closed-form expression for conversion in a PFTR operating with a first-order reaction in terms of the Damköhler number $Da = k\tau$. Use the result to show how the conversion varies between $Da = 0$ and $Da \to \infty$.

**Model Solution to Part (a) – 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 – Use the second-order PFTR formula:**

$$
V = \frac{\nu_0}{k C_{A0}} \cdot \frac{X_{A,f}}{1 - X_{A,f}}
$$

**Step 2 – Substitute values:**

$$
V = \frac{0.001}{0.08 \times 0.5} \cdot \frac{0.90}{1 - 0.90}
$$

**Step 3 – Compute intermediate results:**

$$
k C_{A0} = 0.08 \times 0.5 = 0.04
$$

$$
\frac{\nu_0}{k C_{A0}} = \frac{0.001}{0.04} = 0.025 \; m^3
$$

$$
\frac{X_{A,f}}{1 - X_{A,f}} = \frac{0.90}{0.10} = 9
$$

**Step 4 – Final volume:**

$$
V = 0.025 \times 9 = 0.225 \; m^3
$$

**Mark Distribution:** Formula (2 marks) + substitution (2 marks) + intermediate (1 mark) + final answer with units (1 mark) + verification of consistency (1 mark) = 7 marks.

**Model Solution to Part (b) – 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 – First-order PFTR result in terms of $Da$:**

For a first-order reaction, $Da = k\tau$, and the conversion is:

$$
X_{A,f} = 1 - e^{-k\tau} = 1 - e^{-Da}
$$

**Step 2 – Limit as $Da \to 0$:**

Using the Taylor expansion $e^{-Da} \approx 1 - Da + \dfrac{Da^2}{2} - \cdots$:

$$
X_{A,f} \approx Da \quad \text{for } Da \ll 1
$$

This corresponds to the **linear region** of small conversions at short residence times.

**Step 3 – Limit as $Da \to \infty$:**

$$
\lim_{Da \to \infty} e^{-Da} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad X_{A,f} \to 1
$$

The conversion asymptotically approaches 100% as the space time becomes very large.

**Step 4 – Behaviour in between:**

- $Da = 1$: $X_{A,f} = 1 - e^{-1} = 1 - 0.3679 = 0.6321$ (i.e., 63.2% conversion, the famous "one time-constant" rule).
- $Da = 2$: $X_{A,f} = 1 - e^{-2} = 0.8647$ (86.5% conversion).
- $Da = 5$: $X_{A,f} = 1 - e^{-5} = 0.9933$ (99.3% conversion).

**Mark Distribution:** Derivation (2 marks); $Da = 0$ limit (1 mark); $Da \to \infty$ limit (1 mark); tabulation/commentary (3 marks) = 7 marks.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in PFTR Numerical Problems (KTU Board Pattern)**
> 1. **Units inconsistency:** Mixing $k$ in $s^{-1}$ with $\tau$ in $min$ or $hr$ is the #1 cause of full-mark loss. **Always convert $k$ and $\tau$ to the same time unit before substituting.**
> 2. **Confusing inlet vs outlet flow rate:** For PFTR of positive-order reactions, the volumetric flow rate $\nu_0$ refers to *inlet conditions*. Using $\nu_{\text{out}}$ for liquid systems of constant density gives the same numerical value, but the equation form changes for variable-density (gas-phase) systems.
> 3. **Forgetting the natural logarithm:** A surprising number of students write $\log_{10}$ instead of $\ln$ in the first-order formula. KTU examiners **strictly deduct 1 mark** for this error.
> 4. **Second-order quadratic sign error:** When solving for $X_{A,f}$ from the second-order expression, students often write the wrong sign in front of the square root. The **correct** root is the one with the **negative** sign: $X_{A,f} = \dfrac{(1 + k C_{A0} \tau) - \sqrt{1 + 2 k C_{A0} \tau}}{k C_{A0} \tau}$.
> 5. **CSTR vs PFTR volume ratio must be less than 1:** If a student calculates $V_{PFTR}/V_{CSTR} > 1$ for a positive-order reaction, it is a clear indicator of a numerical error. Always sanity-check this.
> 6. **Not stating limiting conversion for zero-order:** A zero-order reaction is valid only up to $X_A \le 1$. State this constraint in the answer.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **PFTR design equation:** $V/F_{A0} = \displaystyle\int_0^{X_{A,f}} dX_A/(-r_A)$ – the foundation.
- **Space time:** $\tau = V/\nu_0$ – the central operating parameter.
- **First-order conversion:** $X_{A,f} = 1 - e^{-k\tau}$ – the most-tested closed form.
- **Second-order conversion (PFTR):** $X_{A,f} = k C_{A0}\tau/(1 + k C_{A0}\tau)$ – elegant closed form.
- **Zero-order conversion (PFTR):** $X_{A,f} = k\tau/C_{A0}$ – valid only up to $X_{A,f} \le 1$.
- **Levenspiel plot:** Area under $1/(-r_A)$ vs $X_A$ gives $V/F_{A0}$.
- **PFTR is always more efficient than CSTR for positive-order kinetics** ($V_{PFTR}/V_{CSTR} < 1$).
- **RTD signature of ideal PFTR:** Dirac delta $E(t) = \delta(t - \tau)$ – sharp pulse at $\tau$.
- **Damköhler number** $Da = k\tau$ for first-order; quantifies reaction rate relative to flow rate.
- **Mean residence time $\bar{t} = \tau$** for the ideal PFTR (all elements have the same residence time).
- **Assumptions to explicitly state:** No radial gradients, no axial mixing, steady state, constant density (for liquid systems).
- **Concentration profile along length:** $C_A(z) = C_{A0} e^{-kz/u}$ (first-order, isothermal).
- **Real-world analogues:** Tubular fermenters, packed-bed bioreactors, sterilisation columns, hollow-fibre bioreactors.
- **Compare carefully with Module 2 concepts:** Batch kinetics, CSTR design, semi-batch – PFTR is the *spatial* counterpart of the Batch.
- **Practise drawing the Levenspiel plot** for at least three reaction orders (zero, first, second) – this is a high-frequency 14-mark question in KTU ESE.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Comparison of Ideal Mixed Flow (Batch and CFSTBR) and Plug Flow Tubular of Reactor Length and Residence Time
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT526 - BIOREACTOR DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Comparison of Ideal Mixed Flow (Batch and CFSTBR) and Plug Flow Tubular of Reactor Length and Residence Time

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In Bioreactor Design, the performance of any chemical or biochemical conversion system is governed by two **idealized flow models** that serve as the theoretical extremes of all real reactors. These are:

- **Ideal Mixed Flow (Backmix) Reactors:** This category includes the **Batch Reactor (BR)** and the **Continuous Flow Stirred Tank Bioreactor (CFSTBR)**. In these reactors, the contents are perfectly homogeneous, meaning the composition, temperature, and concentration are uniform throughout the entire reaction volume at any instant of time.
- **Ideal Plug Flow Tubular Reactor (PFTR):** A reactor in which fluid flows in a single direction (piston-like) without any axial mixing, and every fluid element has an identical residence time equal to the **space time (τ)** of the reactor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The comparison of these three ideal reactor types forms the foundation for selecting the appropriate bioreactor configuration for a given biochemical process (e.g., fermentation, enzymatic conversion, cell culture). The comparison is evaluated based on **reactor volume (V)**, **residence time (τ)**, **reactor length (L)**, and **conversion (Xₐ)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine three different ways to brew a large batch of tea for a party:

| Reactor Type | Tea-Making Analogy | Key Characteristic |
|---|---|---|
| **Batch Reactor (BR)** | You boil all the water, add tea leaves, and let it steep in a closed pot for a fixed time. At the end, you draw off the brewed tea. | **Time** is the independent variable; no inflow or outflow during reaction. |
| **CFSTBR** | You have a continuously stirred pot. Cold water enters one end, and brewed tea exits the other end at the same rate. The pot is stirred so vigorously that the tea leaving has the **same concentration** as the tea inside. | **Position-independent concentration**; the exit stream equals the tank composition. |
| **PFTR** | A long, narrow pipe filled with tea leaves. Cold water enters one end and travels as a "plug" — water that entered first comes out first. The tea darkens gradually as it moves along the pipe length. | **Position-dependent concentration**; fluid moves like a rigid piston. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Physical Metric:** The **Space Time (τ)** is defined as the time required to process one reactor volume of feed at the inlet conditions, mathematically given by:
> $$\tau = \frac{V}{v_0} = \frac{V \cdot C_{A0}}{F_{A0}}$$
> where **V** is the reactor volume, **v₀** is the volumetric feed flow rate, **Cₐ₀** is the inlet substrate concentration, and **Fₐ₀** is the molar feed rate of substrate A. The unit of τ is **seconds (s)** or **hours (h)**.

## 1.3 Boundary Conditions for the Three Ideal Reactors

| Parameter | Batch Reactor (BR) | CFSTBR | PFTR |
|---|---|---|---|
| Inlet stream | None (closed) | Continuous, steady | Continuous, steady |
| Outlet stream | Drawn at end of cycle | Continuous, steady | Continuous, steady |
| Composition uniformity | Uniform (spatially) | Uniform (spatially & temporally) | Varies with position |
| Independent variable | Time (t) | None (steady state) | Position (z) or length (L) |
| Operating mode | Cyclic (charge → react → discharge) | Continuous | Continuous |
| Mixing intensity | Perfect (ideal) | Perfect (ideal) | Zero axial mixing |
| Steady state? | No (transient) | Yes | Yes |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** A common confusion among students is treating a Batch Reactor as a steady-state system. Always remember: a batch reactor operates in an **unsteady-state (transient)** mode, where composition changes with **time**, while CFSTBR and PFTR can operate at **steady-state** where composition at any point is constant with time.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why Compare Ideal Reactors?

The three ideal reactor models represent the **theoretical bounds** of reactor performance. In real industrial bioreactors (e.g., airlift reactors, bubble columns, packed-bed bioreactors), the actual behavior lies somewhere between these extremes. By understanding the limits, engineers can:

1. **Predict upper and lower bounds** on conversion for a given volume.
2. **Select the optimal reactor type** for a specific reaction kinetics (e.g., autocatalytic reactions favor CFSTBR; simple nth-order reactions favor PFTR).
3. **Compute the deviation** of real reactors from ideality using **Residence Time Distribution (RTD)** analysis.

## 2.2 Derivation Logic — General Performance Equations

The general **mole balance** for a reactant A in a flowing system is:

$$F_{A0} - F_A + r_A \cdot V = \frac{dN_A}{dt}$$

For **steady-state** conditions (CFSTBR and PFTR), the accumulation term $\frac{dN_A}{dt} = 0$.

For the **Batch Reactor** (no inflow/outflow during reaction), the mole balance reduces to:

$$r_A \cdot V = \frac{dN_A}{dt}$$

## 2.3 Performance Equation for Batch Reactor (BR)

Starting from the general mole balance and expressing in terms of conversion $X_A$:

$$N_A = N_{A0}(1 - X_A)$$

Differentiating with respect to time:

$$\frac{dN_A}{dt} = -N_{A0} \frac{dX_A}{dt}$$

Substituting into the batch mole balance:

$$-r_A \cdot V = -N_{A0} \frac{dX_A}{dt}$$

Since the volume of a liquid-phase batch reactor is constant:

$$t = N_{A0} \int_0^{X_A} \frac{dX_A}{-r_A \cdot V} = C_{A0} \int_0^{X_A} \frac{dX_A}{-r_A}$$

This is the **Batch Reactor Design Equation** — the time required to achieve a conversion $X_A$.

## 2.4 Performance Equation for CFSTBR

For steady-state CSTR/CFSTBR, a mole balance on substrate A yields:

$$F_{A0} - F_A = -r_A \cdot V$$

Expressing in terms of conversion: $F_A = F_{A0}(1 - X_A)$, so $F_{A0} - F_A = F_{A0} X_A$:

$$F_{A0} X_A = -r_A \cdot V$$

$$V = \frac{F_{A0} X_A}{-r_A} = v_0 \cdot C_{A0} \cdot \frac{X_A}{-r_A}$$

Dividing by $v_0$ to express in terms of space time:

$$\tau = \frac{V}{v_0} = \frac{C_{A0} X_A}{-r_A}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This equation is **algebraic** (no integration required) because CFSTBR composition is uniform and equals the **exit composition**. The reaction rate $-r_A$ is evaluated at the **exit conditions** (i.e., at the lowest substrate concentration in the system).

## 2.5 Performance Equation for PFTR

For a plug flow reactor at steady state, a differential mole balance over a slice of volume $dV$ gives:

$$F_A \big|_{V} - F_A \big|_{V+dV} + r_A \, dV = 0$$

Since $F_A = F_{A0}(1 - X_A)$, we have $dF_A = -F_{A0} dX_A$:

$$F_{A0} \, dX_A = -r_A \, dV$$

Integrating over the entire reactor:

$$V = F_{A0} \int_0^{X_A} \frac{dX_A}{-r_A}$$

In terms of space time:

$$\tau = \frac{V}{v_0} = C_{A0} \int_0^{X_A} \frac{dX_A}{-r_A}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Structural Difference:** The PFTR equation has the **same mathematical form** as the Batch Reactor equation! The integral $\int \frac{dX_A}{-r_A}$ appears in both, but the meaning of the independent variable is different:
> - In **Batch**: integration is over **time** (t)
> - In **PFTR**: integration is over **position** (z or V)
> - In **CFSTBR**: the equation is **algebraic** (no integral)

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Reactor Type | Design Equation | First-Order Solution ($-r_A = kC_A$) | Volume Ratio (vs PFTR) | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Batch (BR)** | $t = C_{A0} \displaystyle\int_0^{X_A} \frac{dX_A}{-r_A}$ | $t = \dfrac{1}{k} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{1-X_A}\right)$ | $V_{BR}/V_{PFTR} = 1$ | Transient, time-based |
| **CFSTBR** | $\tau = \dfrac{C_{A0} X_A}{-r_A}$ | $\tau = \dfrac{1}{k}\!\left(\dfrac{X_A}{1-X_A}\right)$ | $V_{CFSTBR}/V_{PFTR} > 1$ | Steady, perfectly mixed |
| **PFTR** | $\tau = C_{A0} \displaystyle\int_0^{X_A} \frac{dX_A}{-r_A}$ | $\tau = \dfrac{1}{k} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{1-X_A}\right)$ | $1$ (reference) | Steady, position-based |

> [!NOTE]
> **For First-Order Reactions:** Notice that $\tau_{BR} = \tau_{PFTR}$ for the same conversion, meaning a single batch reactor and a single PFTR achieve identical conversion for the same feed conditions. However, $\tau_{CFSTBR}$ is always **larger** (more volume needed) for any conversion $X_A > 0$.

## 2.7 Reactor Length Comparison

The reactor length $L$ relates to the space time through the **superficial velocity** $u_s$ (volumetric flow rate per unit cross-sectional area):

$$L = u_s \cdot \tau$$

For a **PFTR**, $L$ has clear physical meaning — it is the actual axial length of the tubular reactor. For a **CFSTBR**, $L$ is less physically meaningful since the reactor is well-mixed; the equivalent length is often expressed in terms of **tank diameter** and **liquid height** (H/D ratio).

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** In a PFTR (e.g., a long tubular bioreactor for continuous beer maturation or immobilized-enzyme packed-bed conversion), the **axial length** is the primary design variable. In a CFSTBR (e.g., a stirred-tank fermenter), the **volume (or H/D ratio)** and **mixing intensity (impeller tip speed)** are the design variables. The choice depends on kinetics, rheology, and mass-transfer requirements.

## 2.8 Why CFSTBR Always Requires More Volume

For **positive-order kinetics** ($-r_A$ increases with $C_A$), the reaction rate is highest where substrate concentration is highest. In a CFSTBR, the entire reactor operates at the **lowest** concentration (exit), so the driving force is minimized. In contrast, a PFTR has a region near the inlet where concentration is high, providing a higher reaction rate and therefore more conversion per unit volume.

Mathematically, for first-order kinetics:

$$\frac{V_{CFSTBR}}{V_{PFTR}} = \frac{X_A / (1-X_A)}{\ln[1/(1-X_A)]} > 1 \quad \text{for all } X_A > 0$$

## 2.9 Comparison Summary Table

| Feature | Batch Reactor | CFSTBR | PFTR |
|---|---|---|---|
| Volume for given $X_A$ (1st order) | $V_{BR} = V_{PFTR}$ | $V_{CFSTBR} > V_{PFTR}$ | Minimum (reference) |
| Best suited for | Small-scale, multi-product, slow reactions | Liquid-phase, autocatalytic reactions | Gas-phase, simple nth-order |
| Operating mode | Unsteady | Steady | Steady |
| Ease of sterilization | Excellent (closed system) | Good (CIP/SIP possible) | Moderate (long tube) |
| Scale-up challenge | Time-based scaling | Mixing and mass transfer | Axial dispersion control |
| Conversion per unit volume | Same as PFTR | Lower | Higher |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1: Batch Reactor Time for First-Order Reaction

**Problem Setup:** A first-order irreversible reaction $A \rightarrow Products$ follows the rate law:

$$-r_A = k C_A$$

In a batch reactor, the substrate concentration relates to conversion by:

$$C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X_A)$$

**Step 1:** Write the batch design equation.

$$t = C_{A0} \int_0^{X_A} \frac{dX_A}{-r_A}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the first-order rate law. Note that $-r_A = k C_A = k C_{A0}(1 - X_A)$.

$$t = C_{A0} \int_0^{X_A} \frac{dX_A}{k C_{A0}(1 - X_A)}$$

**Step 3:** Cancel the common factor $C_{A0}$ from numerator and denominator.

$$t = \frac{1}{k} \int_0^{X_A} \frac{dX_A}{1 - X_A}$$

**Step 4:** Perform the integration. The integral of $\frac{1}{1 - X_A}$ is $-\ln(1 - X_A)$.

$$t = \frac{1}{k} \Big[-\ln(1 - X_A)\Big]_0^{X_A}$$

**Step 5:** Apply the upper and lower limits.

$$t = \frac{1}{k} \left[-\ln(1 - X_A) - (-\ln(1 - 0))\right]$$

**Step 6:** Simplify using $\ln(1) = 0$.

$$t = -\frac{1}{k} \ln(1 - X_A) = \frac{1}{k} \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{1 - X_A}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result for Batch Reactor (1st order):**
> $$t = \frac{1}{k} \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{1 - X_A}\right)$$

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2: CFSTBR Space Time for First-Order Reaction

**Step 1:** Write the CFSTBR design equation.

$$\tau = \frac{C_{A0} X_A}{-r_A}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the first-order rate law evaluated at **exit conditions** $C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X_A)$.

$$\tau = \frac{C_{A0} X_A}{k C_{A0}(1 - X_A)}$$

**Step 3:** Cancel $C_{A0}$ from numerator and denominator.

$$\tau = \frac{X_A}{k(1 - X_A)}$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange to a more standard form.

$$\tau = \frac{1}{k} \cdot \frac{X_A}{1 - X_A}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result for CFSTBR (1st order):**
> $$\tau = \frac{1}{k} \cdot \frac{X_A}{1 - X_A}$$

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3: PFTR Space Time for First-Order Reaction

**Step 1:** Write the PFTR design equation.

$$\tau = C_{A0} \int_0^{X_A} \frac{dX_A}{-r_A}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the first-order rate law.

$$\tau = C_{A0} \int_0^{X_A} \frac{dX_A}{k C_{A0}(1 - X_A)} = \frac{1}{k} \int_0^{X_A} \frac{dX_A}{1 - X_A}$$

**Step 3:** Perform the integration.

$$\tau = \frac{1}{k} \Big[-\ln(1 - X_A)\Big]_0^{X_A}$$

**Step 4:** Apply the limits.

$$\tau = -\frac{1}{k} \ln(1 - X_A) = \frac{1}{k} \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{1 - X_A}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result for PFTR (1st order):**
> $$\tau = \frac{1}{k} \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{1 - X_A}\right)$$

**Observation:** This is **identical** to the batch reactor result, confirming the well-known equivalence: for the **same space time and inlet conditions**, a single batch reactor and a single PFTR deliver the same conversion.

## 3.4 Exhaustive Derivation 4: Volume Ratio $V_{CFSTBR} / V_{PFTR}$ for First-Order Reaction

**Step 1:** Set up the ratio using the derived space times.

$$\frac{V_{CFSTBR}}{V_{PFTR}} = \frac{\tau_{CFSTBR}}{\tau_{PFTR}}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the formulas derived above.

$$\frac{V_{CFSTBR}}{V_{PFTR}} = \frac{\dfrac{1}{k} \cdot \dfrac{X_A}{1 - X_A}}{\dfrac{1}{k} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{1 - X_A}\right)}$$

**Step 3:** Cancel the common factor $1/k$.

$$\frac{V_{CFSTBR}}{V_{PFTR}} = \frac{X_A / (1 - X_A)}{\ln[1/(1 - X_A)]}$$

**Step 4:** Let $y = 1 - X_A$ (so $X_A = 1 - y$), where $0 < y < 1$ for $0 < X_A < 1$.

$$\frac{V_{CFSTBR}}{V_{PFTR}} = \frac{(1 - y)/y}{\ln(1/y)} = \frac{1 - y}{y \cdot \ln(1/y)}$$

**Step 5:** Numerical verification at $X_A = 0.5$:

$$\frac{V_{CFSTBR}}{V_{PFTR}} = \frac{0.5 / 0.5}{\ln(1/0.5)} = \frac{1}{0.693} \approx 1.44$$

**Step 6:** Numerical verification at $X_A = 0.9$:

$$\frac{V_{CFSTBR}}{V_{PFTR}} = \frac{0.9 / 0.1}{\ln(1/0.1)} = \frac{9}{2.303} \approx 3.91$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Implication:** At 50% conversion, a CFSTBR requires **44% more volume** than a PFTR. At 90% conversion, it requires nearly **4× more volume**. This is why PFTRs are strongly preferred for high-conversion simple-order reactions.

## 3.5 Exhaustive Derivation 5: Reactor Length for PFTR

**Step 1:** Recall the relationship between volume, cross-section, and length for a tubular reactor.

$$V = A_c \cdot L$$

where $A_c$ is the cross-sectional area and $L$ is the length.

**Step 2:** Express the volumetric flow rate in terms of superficial velocity.

$$v_0 = u_s \cdot A_c$$

**Step 3:** Combine the space time definition with the geometry.

$$\tau = \frac{V}{v_0} = \frac{A_c \cdot L}{u_s \cdot A_c} = \frac{L}{u_s}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for the length.

$$L = u_s \cdot \tau$$

**Step 5:** For first-order kinetics, substitute the PFTR space time.

$$L = \frac{u_s}{k} \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{1 - X_A}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The reactor length scales **logarithmically** with conversion in a PFTR. Doubling the conversion from 50% to 75% (going from $X_A = 0.5$ to $X_A = 0.75$) does not double the length — it increases it by a factor of $\ln(0.5^{-1})/\ln(0.25^{-1}) = 0.693/1.386 = 0.5$. So at higher conversion, length grows more slowly than linearly with conversion.

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix

For a first-order reaction with rate constant $k = 0.1 \, \text{min}^{-1}$ and $C_{A0} = 1 \, \text{mol/L}$:

| Conversion $X_A$ | $\tau_{BR}$ (min) | $\tau_{PFTR}$ (min) | $\tau_{CFSTBR}$ (min) | $V_{CFSTBR}/V_{PFTR}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0.50 | 6.93 | 6.93 | 10.00 | 1.44 |
| 0.75 | 13.86 | 13.86 | 30.00 | 2.16 |
| 0.90 | 23.03 | 23.03 | 90.00 | 3.91 |
| 0.95 | 29.96 | 29.96 | 190.00 | 6.34 |
| 0.99 | 46.05 | 46.05 | 990.00 | 21.50 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU High-Yield Observation:** As $X_A \to 1$, the CFSTBR volume becomes **infinitely large**, while the PFTR volume grows only logarithmically. This is a direct consequence of the algebraic vs. integral nature of the design equations.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow Diagram — Comparison of Three Ideal Reactors

The following block-level architecture flow maps the conceptual relationship between the three ideal reactor models, their governing equations, and the parameters used for comparison.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ideal Reactors for Bioconversion] --> B[Ideal Mixed Flow]
    A --> C[Ideal Plug Flow Tubular]

    B --> B1[Batch Reactor BR]
    B --> B2[Continuous Flow Stirred Tank Bioreactor CFSTBR]

    C --> C1[Plug Flow Tubular Reactor PFTR]

    B1 --> D1[Design Equation: t equals C A0 integral dX over minus rA]
    B2 --> D2[Design Equation: tau equals C A0 X A over minus rA]
    C1 --> D3[Design Equation: tau equals C A0 integral dX over minus rA]

    D1 --> E1[Independent Variable: TIME t]
    D2 --> E2[Independent Variable: NONE steady state]
    D3 --> D3A[Independent Variable: POSITION z or Length L]

    E1 --> F1[Operating Mode: TRANSIENT]
    E2 --> F2[Operating Mode: STEADY STATE]
    D3A --> F3[Operating Mode: STEADY STATE]

    F1 --> G1[Concentration: Uniform in space varies with time]
    F2 --> G2[Concentration: Uniform in space and time]
    F3 --> G3[Concentration: Varies along length]

    G1 --> H1[Best for Small Scale Multi Product]
    G2 --> H2[Best for Liquid Phase Autocatalytic]
    G3 --> H3[Best for Gas Phase High Conversion]

    H1 --> I1[Key Metric: Cycle Time t per batch]
    H2 --> I2[Key Metric: Space Time tau equals V over v0]
    H3 --> H3A[Key Metric: Space Time and Length L equals us times tau]

    I1 --> J1[First Order Result: t equals one over k times ln one over one minus X A]
    I2 --> J2[First Order Result: tau equals one over k times X A over one minus X A]
    H3A --> J3[First Order Result: L equals us over k times ln one over one minus X A]
```

## 4.2 Schematic Block Diagram — Conversion Behaviour vs Reactor Type

The block diagram below illustrates how substrate concentration profiles differ across the three ideal reactor configurations for the same inlet concentration $C_{A0} = 1$ and the same conversion $X_A = 0.5$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BR_BLOCK[Batch Reactor Profile]
        BR_IN[Time equals 0] --> BR_MID[Mid reaction t equals 6.93 min] --> BR_OUT[t equals 13.86 min]
        BR_IN_LABEL[C A equals 1.00] -.-> BR_IN
        BR_MID_LABEL[C A equals 0.50] -.-> BR_MID
        BR_OUT_LABEL[C A equals 0.10] -.-> BR_OUT
    end

    subgraph CFST_BLOCK[CFSTBR Profile]
        CFST_IN[Inlet Feed] --> CFST_TANK[Well Mixed Tank]
        CFST_TANK --> CFST_OUT[Outlet Stream]
        CFST_IN_LABEL[C A0 equals 1.00] -.-> CFST_IN
        CFST_TANK_LABEL[C A uniform equals 0.50] -.-> CFST_TANK
        CFST_OUT_LABEL[C A exit equals 0.50] -.-> CFST_OUT
    end

    subgraph PFR_BLOCK[PFTR Profile]
        PFR_IN[Inlet z equals 0] --> PFR_MID[Middle z equals L over 2] --> PFR_OUT[Outlet z equals L]
        PFR_IN_LABEL[C A0 equals 1.00] -.-> PFR_IN
        PFR_MID_LABEL[C A decreases gradually] -.-> PFR_MID
        PFR_OUT_LABEL[C A exit equals 0.50] -.-> PFR_OUT
    end
```

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart — Choosing the Right Reactor Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Start Reaction Analysis] --> Q1{Order of reaction?}
    Q1 --> Q1A[Positive order n greater than 0] --> Q2{Conversion target?}
    Q1 --> Q1B[Zero or negative order] --> R1[CFSTBR preferred]

    Q2 --> Q2A[X A less than 0.5] --> R2[Either BR or CFSTBR]
    Q2 --> Q2B[X A greater than 0.5] --> R3[PFTR strongly preferred]
    Q2 --> Q2C[Multiple products or flexible] --> R4[Batch Reactor BR]

    R2 --> END[Final Selection]
    R1 --> END
    R3 --> END
    R4 --> END
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Diagram Interpretation Tip:** The decision flowchart above is a **high-yield summary** that examiners often probe in viva or short-answer questions. Memorize the three branches: high conversion + positive order → PFTR; autocatalytic / zero order → CFSTBR; multi-product / flexible → Batch.

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Linking Volume, Length, and Residence Time

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Volumetric Flow Rate v0] --> ST[Space Time tau equals V over v0]
    V[Reactor Volume V] --> ST
    ST --> L1[Reactor Length L equals us times tau]
    US[Superficial Velocity us] --> L1

    ST --> PERF[Conversion Performance]
    K[Rate Constant k] --> PERF
    XA[Conversion X A] --> PERF
    ORD[Reaction Order n] --> PERF

    PERF --> SEL[Reactor Selection]
    SEL --> SEL1[Batch for transient]
    SEL --> SEL2[CFSTBR for backmix]
    SEL --> SEL3[PFTR for high conversion]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**Q: Define space time and space velocity. How are they related? Why is space time preferred over true residence time for ideal reactor design?**

**Model Answer:**

**Space Time (τ):** It is the time required to process one reactor volume of feed at inlet conditions.

$$\tau = \frac{V}{v_0}$$

Unit: **seconds (s)** or **hours (h)**.

**Space Velocity (SV):** It is the number of reactor volumes of feed that can be processed per unit time at inlet conditions.

$$SV = \frac{v_0}{V} = \frac{1}{\tau}$$

Unit: **s⁻¹** or **h⁻¹**.

**Relationship:** $SV = 1/\tau$ — they are reciprocal of each other.

**Why preferred over true residence time:** The true residence time depends on the local fluid velocity and density changes during reaction, making it difficult to measure. Space time, in contrast, is based on **inlet conditions** and is a fixed, calculable design parameter that depends only on **reactor geometry and feed flow rate** — making it a robust metric for comparing different reactor configurations in KTU-level design problems.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**Q: Compare the residence time distribution (RTD) of an ideal Batch reactor, an ideal CFSTBR, and an ideal Plug Flow Tubular Reactor. State the corresponding E(θ) and F(θ) functions.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Residence Time Distribution (RTD)** describes the distribution of times that fluid elements spend inside a reactor. For the three ideal reactors, the dimensionless time $\theta = t/\tau$ is used.

**For Ideal Batch Reactor (BR):** All fluid elements have the **same** residence time equal to the batch holding time.

$$E(\theta) = \delta(\theta - 1), \quad F(\theta) = \begin{cases} 0 & \theta < 1 \\ 1 & \theta \geq 1 \end{cases}$$

This is a **Dirac delta function** at $\theta = 1$ — all molecules leave together at the end of the cycle.

**For Ideal CFSTBR:** Exponential distribution of residence times.

$$E(\theta) = e^{-\theta}, \quad F(\theta) = 1 - e^{-\theta}$$

This indicates a wide distribution — some molecules leave almost immediately, while others stay for much longer.

**For Ideal PFTR:** All fluid elements have **identical** residence time equal to $\tau$ (no axial mixing, no bypassing).

$$E(\theta) = \delta(\theta - 1), \quad F(\theta) = \begin{cases} 0 & \theta < 1 \\ 1 & \theta \geq 1 \end{cases}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction:** Although BR and PFTR share the same ideal RTD function, the **Batch RTD is in time** (transient), while the **PFTR RTD is in space** (steady state). In both, however, there is **zero spread** of residence times.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a)** Derive the performance equation for an ideal Plug Flow Tubular Reactor (PFTR) operating at steady state for a first-order irreversible liquid-phase reaction $A \rightarrow Products$. Express the result in terms of space time, conversion, and the rate constant. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A first-order liquid-phase reaction $A \rightarrow Products$ with $k = 0.25 \, \text{min}^{-1}$ is to be carried out in (i) a PFTR and (ii) a CFSTBR. The feed flow rate is $v_0 = 50 \, \text{L/min}$ and the desired conversion is 80%. Calculate the volume of each reactor and the reactor length of the PFTR if the superficial velocity is $u_s = 2 \, \text{cm/min}$. Compare the results and comment. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Step 1:** Write the differential mole balance on substrate A for a slice of PFTR volume $dV$ at steady state:

$$F_A \big|_{V} - F_A \big|_{V+dV} + r_A \, dV = 0$$

**Step 2:** Express in differential form:

$$-dF_A + r_A \, dV = 0 \implies dF_A = r_A \, dV$$

**Step 3:** Convert to conversion form using $F_A = F_{A0}(1 - X_A)$:

$$dF_A = -F_{A0} \, dX_A$$

**Step 4:** Substitute:

$$-F_{A0} \, dX_A = r_A \, dV \implies F_{A0} \, dX_A = -r_A \, dV$$

**Step 5:** Apply the first-order rate law $-r_A = k C_A = k C_{A0}(1 - X_A)$:

$$F_{A0} \, dX_A = k C_{A0}(1 - X_A) \, dV$$

**Step 6:** Rearrange to separate variables:

$$\frac{dX_A}{1 - X_A} = \frac{k C_{A0}}{F_{A0}} \, dV$$

**Step 7:** Note that $F_{A0} = v_0 C_{A0}$, so $\frac{k C_{A0}}{F_{A0}} = \frac{k}{v_0}$:

$$\frac{dX_A}{1 - X_A} = \frac{k}{v_0} \, dV$$

**Step 8:** Integrate from inlet ($V = 0$, $X_A = 0$) to outlet ($V = V$, $X_A = X_A$):

$$\int_0^{X_A} \frac{dX_A}{1 - X_A} = \frac{k}{v_0} \int_0^V dV$$

**Step 9:** Evaluate the integrals:

$$-\ln(1 - X_A) = \frac{k V}{v_0}$$

**Step 10:** Recognize $V/v_0 = \tau$ (space time):

$$\boxed{\tau = \frac{1}{k} \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{1 - X_A}\right)}$$

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Given:** $k = 0.25 \, \text{min}^{-1}$, $v_0 = 50 \, \text{L/min}$, $X_A = 0.80$, $u_s = 2 \, \text{cm/min} = 0.02 \, \text{m/min}$.

**For the PFTR (i):**

**Step 1:** Use the PFTR design equation:

$$\tau_{PFTR} = \frac{1}{k} \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{1 - X_A}\right) = \frac{1}{0.25} \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{1 - 0.80}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate the logarithm: $\ln(1/0.20) = \ln(5) = 1.609$.

$$\tau_{PFTR} = \frac{1.609}{0.25} = 6.437 \, \text{min}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the PFTR volume:

$$V_{PFTR} = v_0 \cdot \tau_{PFTR} = 50 \times 6.437 = 321.85 \, \text{L} \approx 322 \, \text{L}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the PFTR length:

$$L_{PFTR} = u_s \cdot \tau_{PFTR} = 0.02 \, \text{m/min} \times 6.437 \, \text{min} = 0.1287 \, \text{m} \approx 12.87 \, \text{cm}$$

**For the CFSTBR (ii):**

**Step 1:** Use the CFSTBR design equation:

$$\tau_{CFSTBR} = \frac{1}{k} \cdot \frac{X_A}{1 - X_A} = \frac{1}{0.25} \cdot \frac{0.80}{0.20}$$

**Step 2:** Simplify:

$$\tau_{CFSTBR} = 4 \times 4 = 16 \, \text{min}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the CFSTBR volume:

$$V_{CFSTBR} = v_0 \cdot \tau_{CFSTBR} = 50 \times 16 = 800 \, \text{L}$$

**Comparison and Comments:**

$$\frac{V_{CFSTBR}}{V_{PFTR}} = \frac{800}{322} \approx 2.49$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comments:** The CFSTBR requires **~2.49 times more volume** than the PFTR to achieve the same 80% conversion. This confirms the general principle that **PFTR is more efficient for positive-order, single-path reactions** at moderate-to-high conversions. However, the CFSTBR offers operational advantages: easier temperature control, better handling of solid catalysts or suspended biomass, and ability to operate in continuous mode with steady-state conditions. The choice ultimately depends on the bioprocess economics, sterilization requirements, and product specifications.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** With the help of a general mole balance, derive the design equation for an ideal Batch Reactor (BR). Show that for a first-order reaction $A \rightarrow Products$, the time required to achieve a conversion of 75% is $\dfrac{\ln 4}{k}$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A fermentation broth containing substrate A at $C_{A0} = 2 \, \text{g/L}$ undergoes first-order biodegradation with $k = 0.08 \, \text{h}^{-1}$ in (i) a Batch Reactor, (ii) a CFSTBR, and (iii) a PFTR. If the volumetric flow rate is $v_0 = 1000 \, \text{L/h}$, calculate the reactor volume needed in each case to achieve 90% conversion. Comment on the results in the context of bioreactor design. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Step 1:** General mole balance for a Batch Reactor (no inflow/outflow):

$$\text{Rate of Accumulation} = \text{Rate of Generation}$$

$$\frac{dN_A}{dt} = r_A \cdot V$$

**Step 2:** Express moles in terms of conversion for constant-volume batch:

$$N_A = N_{A0}(1 - X_A)$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate with respect to time:

$$\frac{dN_A}{dt} = -N_{A0} \frac{dX_A}{dt}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute into the mole balance:

$$-N_{A0} \frac{dX_A}{dt} = r_A \cdot V$$

**Step 5:** Note that $r_A = -k C_A$ for first-order, and $C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X_A)$ for constant volume, so $N_{A0} = C_{A0} \cdot V$:

$$-C_{A0} V \frac{dX_A}{dt} = -k C_{A0}(1 - X_A) \cdot V$$

**Step 6:** Cancel $-C_{A0} V$:

$$\frac{dX_A}{dt} = k(1 - X_A)$$

**Step 7:** Separate variables and integrate from $t = 0, X_A = 0$ to $t, X_A$:

$$\int_0^{X_A} \frac{dX_A}{1 - X_A} = k \int_0^t dt$$

**Step 8:** Evaluate:

$$-\ln(1 - X_A) = k t$$

**Step 9:** Solve for time:

$$t = -\frac{1}{k} \ln(1 - X_A) = \frac{1}{k} \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{1 - X_A}\right)$$

**Step 10:** Substitute $X_A = 0.75$:

$$t = \frac{1}{k} \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{1 - 0.75}\right) = \frac{1}{k} \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{0.25}\right) = \frac{1}{k} \ln(4) = \frac{\ln 4}{k}$$

$$\boxed{t_{BR} = \frac{\ln 4}{k}}$$

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Given:** $C_{A0} = 2 \, \text{g/L}$, $k = 0.08 \, \text{h}^{-1}$, $v_0 = 1000 \, \text{L/h}$, $X_A = 0.90$.

**Note:** Reactor volume for a batch reactor is the **total batch volume per batch**, computed from $V = v_0 \cdot t$ (i.e., the volume processed in time $t$).

**Case (i): Batch Reactor**

**Step 1:** Compute batch time:

$$t_{BR} = \frac{1}{k} \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{1 - 0.90}\right) = \frac{1}{0.08} \ln(10) = \frac{2.303}{0.08} = 28.78 \, \text{h}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the volume processed per batch:

$$V_{BR} = v_0 \cdot t_{BR} = 1000 \times 28.78 = 28{,}780 \, \text{L} \approx 28.78 \, \text{m}^3$$

**Case (ii): CFSTBR**

**Step 1:** Compute space time:

$$\tau_{CFSTBR} = \frac{1}{k} \cdot \frac{X_A}{1 - X_A} = \frac{1}{0.08} \cdot \frac{0.90}{0.10} = 12.5 \times 9 = 112.5 \, \text{h}$$

**Step 2:** Compute reactor volume:

$$V_{CFSTBR} = v_0 \cdot \tau_{CFSTBR} = 1000 \times 112.5 = 112{,}500 \, \text{L} = 112.5 \, \text{m}^3$$

**Case (iii): PFTR**

**Step 1:** Compute space time (same as BR):

$$\tau_{PFTR} = \frac{1}{k} \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{1 - 0.90}\right) = 28.78 \, \text{h}$$

**Step 2:** Compute reactor volume:

$$V_{PFTR} = v_0 \cdot \tau_{PFTR} = 1000 \times 28.78 = 28{,}780 \, \text{L} = 28.78 \, \text{m}^3$$

**Summary and Comments:**

| Reactor | Volume (m³) | Ratio vs PFTR |
|---|---|---|
| Batch (BR) | 28.78 | 1.00 |
| PFTR | 28.78 | 1.00 |
| CFSTBR | 112.50 | 3.91 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units:** Students often use $k$ in min⁻¹ but $v_0$ in L/h — always convert $k$ to be dimensionally consistent with the flow rate units.
> 2. **Confusing Batch volume with CSTR volume:** The batch reactor "volume" is the volume processed per batch (feed volume), not a cycle time-based reactor size. If a CSTR is operated with the same feed, it is filled continuously.
> 3. **Mixing $\tau_{BR}$ and $\tau_{CFSTBR}$:** Both have the same formula form $\frac{X_A}{k(1-X_A)}$ only for CFSTBR — never for batch. The batch formula is logarithmic.
> 4. **Ignoring down-time in Batch:** The total cycle time = reaction time + charging + discharging + cleaning. The actual daily throughput is lower than the theoretical.
> 5. **Volume ≠ Productivity:** A larger reactor doesn't always mean higher productivity if it's operated inefficiently. Productivity = (Conversion × Flow Rate) / Reactor Volume.

**Comments in Bioreactor Design Context:**

- The PFTR is the most **volume-efficient** configuration for this first-order biodegradation, requiring only **~26% of the CFSTBR volume**.
- The Batch Reactor matches PFTR in volume efficiency but operates in **cyclic (unsteady) mode** with downtime — its real productivity is lower.
- For **bioprocesses involving live cells** (e.g., antibiotic fermentation), CFSTBR is often preferred because it provides **uniform substrate and oxygen levels** that prevent substrate inhibition and oxygen starvation, even though it requires more volume.
- For **continuous beer maturation** or **immobilized-enzyme conversion** (e.g., glucose isomerase), PFTR is the **industry standard** due to its compactness and high conversion efficiency.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Comparison of Ideal Reactors**

- ✅ **Three Ideal Reactors:** Batch (BR), CFSTBR (ideal CSTR for bioprocesses), PFTR.
- ✅ **Design Equation Forms:**
   - **Batch:** $t = C_{A0} \displaystyle\int_0^{X_A} \frac{dX_A}{-r_A}$ (transient, time-based)
   - **CFSTBR:** $\tau = \dfrac{C_{A0} X_A}{-r_A}$ (steady, algebraic)
   - **PFTR:** $\tau = C_{A0} \displaystyle\int_0^{X_A} \frac{dX_A}{-r_A}$ (steady, position-based)
- ✅ **First-Order Results (must memorize):**
   - $t_{BR} = \tau_{PFTR} = \dfrac{1}{k} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{1-X_A}\right)$
   - $\tau_{CFSTBR} = \dfrac{1}{k} \cdot \dfrac{X_A}{1-X_A}$
- ✅ **Volume Ratio for 1st Order:** $\dfrac{V_{CFSTBR}}{V_{PFTR}} = \dfrac{X_A / (1-X_A)}{\ln[1/(1-X_A)]} \geq 1$
- ✅ **Reactor Length for PFTR:** $L = u_s \cdot \tau = \dfrac{u_s}{k} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{1-X_A}\right)$
- ✅ **Residence Time Distribution (RTD):**
   - **BR & PFTR:** $E(\theta) = \delta(\theta - 1)$ (Dirac delta — zero spread)
   - **CFSTBR:** $E(\theta) = e^{-\theta}$ (Exponential — wide spread)
- ✅ **Key Selection Rules:**
   - **High conversion + positive order** → PFTR
   - **Autocatalytic / suspended biomass** → CFSTBR
   - **Multi-product / flexible / slow reactions** → Batch
- ✅ **Space Time Formula:** $\tau = V/v_0$ (inlet conditions); Space Velocity $SV = 1/\tau$.
- ✅ **PFTR Equivalence Theorem:** A single PFTR and a single Batch Reactor yield **identical conversion** for the same inlet conditions, $v_0$, and $V$ (or batch time $t$).
- ✅ **Limiting Behaviour:** As $X_A \to 1$, $V_{CFSTBR} \to \infty$ (algebraic singularity), while $V_{PFTR}$ grows only logarithmically.
- ✅ **For Bioreactor Design:** CFSTBR dominates industrial fermentation (e.g., antibiotic, recombinant protein production) due to mass-transfer and sterilization considerations, while PFTR dominates continuous biocatalysis (e.g., immobilized-enzyme columns, beer maturation, vinegar production).

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Strategy Tip:** When solving numerical problems in the exam, always start by writing the **general design equation** for the reactor type, **substitute the rate law**, **integrate (or apply the algebraic form for CFSTBR)**, and then **insert the numerical values with consistent units**. Drawing a quick sketch with $C_A$ profile and clearly labeling $C_{A0}$, $C_{A,exit}$, $V$, $v_0$, and $L$ before calculation earns easy method marks even if the final numerical answer has a minor error.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recycling in PFTRs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT526 - BIOREACTOR DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Recycling in PFTRs # ♻️ Recycling in Plug Flow Tubular Reactors (PFTRs) ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In **Plug Flow Tubular Reactor (PFTR) engineering**, **recycling** refers to the operational strategy in which a fraction of the reactor's **outlet stream** is diverted, mixed with the **fresh feed stream**, and re-introduced into the inlet of the reactor. Mathematically, this is governed by the **recycle ratio ($R$)**, defined as the volumetric flow rate of the returned stream divided by the volumetric flow rate of the **fresh feed**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Levenspiel Framework):** > "A recycle reactor is one in which a portion of the effluent stream is returned to the reactor inlet. By adjusting the recycle ratio $R$, the operating characteristics of a PFTR can be made to vary continuously from pure plug-flow behavior ($R = 0$) to mixed-flow (CSTR-like) behavior ($R \to \infty$)." ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Coffee Refill" Principle Imagine a coffee vending machine that brews a small, very strong shot into a large cup. To match the strength a customer wants, the machine **recycles** a portion of the previously brewed strong concentrate back into the brewing chamber. The fresh grounds are now steeped in a **mixture of new water and old strong coffee**. - If **no coffee is recycled** ($R = 0$): You get pure plug-flow — water meets fresh grounds once, then leaves. This is the classic PFTR. - If **a lot of coffee is recycled** ($R \to \infty$): The concentration inside the chamber becomes almost uniform — the reactor behaves like a CSTR (well-mixed). - **Recycling in a PFTR is therefore a "tuning knob"** that lets the engineer smoothly transition between ideal PFR and ideal CSTR behavior — without changing the reactor's physical geometry. > [!NOTE] > In **bioreactor design**, recycling is used for **cell/biomass recycle** in continuous fermenters (e.g., waste-water treatment, ethanol production) to elevate cell density and volumetric productivity far beyond what a single-pass PFTR can achieve. ### 1.3 Physical Quantities & Standard Metrics | Symbol | Quantity | Typical Unit (SI) | |:------:|:---------|:------------------| | $v_0$ | Fresh feed volumetric flow rate | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | | $R$ | Recycle ratio (dimensionless) | — | | $X$ | Fractional conversion in the reactor | — | | $F_{A0}$ | Molar feed rate of limiting substrate $A$ | $\text{mol/s}$ | | $C_{A0}$ | Inlet substrate concentration | $\text{mol/m}^3$ or $\text{kg/m}^3$ | | $\tau$ | Space time based on combined inlet flow | $\text{s}$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to recognize that **recycling changes the definition of conversion**. The conversion $X$ in a recycle reactor is always based on the **combined inlet** to the reactor, not on the fresh feed alone. # 📐 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Recycle Loop — Step-by-Step Operating Logic The recycle reactor has **three distinct zones** that must be analyzed separately: **Step 1 — Mixing Point (Junction A):** The fresh feed ($v_0$, $C_{A0}$) and the recycle stream ($R \cdot v_0$, $C_{A2}$) merge to form the reactor inlet stream. - Combined volumetric flow: $v_1 = v_0(1 + R)$ - Combined substrate concentration: $C_{A1} = \dfrac{C_{A0} + R \cdot C_{A2}}{1 + R}$ **Step 2 — Reactor Itself (PFTR):** The combined feed enters the PFTR. We define the **conversion based on the combined inlet**: $$X = \frac{C_{A1} - C_{A2}}{C_{A1}}$$ This is the conversion we use in the design equation. Note that this is **NOT** the same as the conversion based on fresh feed alone. **Step 3 — Splitting Point (Junction B):** The reactor outlet ($v_1$, $C_{A2}$) is split into: - The **product stream**: flow rate $v_0$, concentration $C_{A2}$ - The **recycle stream**: flow rate $R \cdot v_0$, concentration $C_{A2}$ > [!NOTE] > Both split streams have **identical composition** ($C_{A2}$) — only their flow rates differ. This is the critical physical assumption that allows us to use a single recycle ratio $R$. ## 2.2 Why Recycle? — The Engineering Rationale - **For inhibition kinetics:** When product $P$ inhibits the reaction, recycling a fraction of the product stream can *decrease* the driving force ($\Delta C_A$), allowing better selectivity in biochemical systems. - **For autocatalytic reactions:** Including unreacted substrate mimics the "seed" needed for autocatalytic processes (e.g., fermentations where product acts as catalyst). - **For temperature control:** In highly exothermic bioreactions, recycle provides a thermal flywheel effect. - **For biomass concentration:** Cell recycle in continuous culture boosts biomass productivity. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Typical Use | |:-:|:--------|:------------|:------------| | 1 | $R = \dfrac{v_R}{v_0}$ | Recycle ratio definition | All recycle problems | | 2 | $C_{A1} = \dfrac{C_{A0} + R \cdot C_{A2}}{1+R}$ | Mixing-point concentration | Inlet of reactor | | 3 | $X = \dfrac{C_{A1} - C_{A2}}{C_{A1}}$ | Conversion (reactor basis) | Design equation entry | | 4 | $C_{A2} = C_{A1}(1-X)$ | Outlet of reactor | All conversions | | 5 | $C_{A1} = \dfrac{C_{A0} + R \cdot C_{A1}(1-X)}{1+R}$ | Self-consistency | Solving for $C_{A1}$ | | 6 | $C_{A1} = \dfrac{C_{A0}}{1 + R \cdot X}$ | Reactor inlet concentration | Final compact form | | 7 | $C_{A2} = \dfrac{C_{A0}(1-X)}{1 + R \cdot X}$ | Reactor outlet concentration | Effluent composition | | 8 | $X_{\text{overall}} = \dfrac{C_{A0} - C_{A2,\text{product}}}{C_{A0}} = \dfrac{R \cdot X}{1 + R \cdot X}$ | Overall conversion (fresh feed basis) | Reporting to plant management | | 9 | $\dfrac{V}{v_0(1+R)} = \displaystyle\int_{C_{A2}}^{C_{A1}} \dfrac{dC_A}{-r_A}$ | PFTR design equation | Sizing the reactor | | 10 | $\tau_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{V}{v_0(1+R)}$ | Effective space time | Bioreactor design | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse $X$ (reactor conversion, based on combined inlet) with $X_{\text{overall}}$ (overall conversion, based on fresh feed). They are related but **not equal** unless $R = 0$. ## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Bioreactor Engineering In **continuous stirred-tank fermenters (CSTR) with cell recycle** (a common industrial configuration for ethanol, monoclonal antibodies, and wastewater treatment), the membrane filtration recycle loop returns concentrated cells but substrate-free permeate — a direct application of recycle-PFTR mathematics. The KTU 2024 module on bioreactor design specifically asks students to compare **single-pass PFTR productivity** with **PFTR-with-recycle productivity**, demonstrating that recycling can elevate volumetric productivity by an order of magnitude for Monod-type kinetics. # 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: The Self-Consistency Relation for $C_{A1}$ We start from the mixing-point relation and substitute the reactor outlet expression. **Step 1 — Write the mixing-point balance:** $$C_{A1} = \frac{C_{A0} + R \cdot C_{A2}}{1 + R}$$ **Step 2 — Express $C_{A2}$ in terms of $C_{A1}$ and $X$:** $$C_{A2} = C_{A1}(1 - X)$$ **Step 3 — Substitute (Step 2) into (Step 1):** $$C_{A1} = \frac{C_{A0} + R \cdot C_{A1}(1 - X)}{1 + R}$$ **Step 4 — Multiply both sides by $(1 + R)$:** $$C_{A1}(1 + R) = C_{A0} + R \cdot C_{A1}(1 - X)$$ **Step 5 — Expand the right-hand side:** $$C_{A1} + R \cdot C_{A1} = C_{A0} + R \cdot C_{A1} - R \cdot C_{A1} \cdot X$$ **Step 6 — Cancel the common term $R \cdot C_{A1}$ on both sides:** $$C_{A1} = C_{A0} - R \cdot C_{A1} \cdot X$$ **Step 7 — Collect $C_{A1}$ terms on the left:** $$C_{A1} + R \cdot C_{A1} \cdot X = C_{A0}$$ **Step 8 — Factor $C_{A1}$:** $$C_{A1}(1 + R \cdot X) = C_{A0}$$ **Step 9 — Final compact result:** $$\boxed{\,C_{A1} = \frac{C_{A0}}{1 + R \cdot X}\,}$$ **Step 10 — Outlet concentration in compact form:** Substituting back into $C_{A2} = C_{A1}(1 - X)$: $$\boxed{\,C_{A2} = \frac{C_{A0}(1 - X)}{1 + R \cdot X}\,}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation: Overall Conversion $X_{\text{overall}}$ By definition, overall conversion is based on **fresh feed only**: $$X_{\text{overall}} = \frac{C_{A0} - C_{A2,\text{product}}}{C_{A0}}$$ **Step 1 — Substitute $C_{A2,\text{product}} = C_{A2}$ (the split streams have identical composition):** $$X_{\text{overall}} = \frac{C_{A0} - C_{A2}}{C_{A0}}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the compact form of $C_{A2}$ from Section 3.1:** $$X_{\text{overall}} = \frac{C_{A0} - \dfrac{C_{A0}(1 - X)}{1 + R \cdot X}}{C_{A0}}$$ **Step 3 — Factor out $C_{A0}$:** $$X_{\text{overall}} = 1 - \frac{1 - X}{1 + R \cdot X}$$ **Step 4 — Common denominator:** $$X_{\text{overall}} = \frac{(1 + R \cdot X) - (1 - X)}{1 + R \cdot X} = \frac{R \cdot X + X}{1 + R \cdot X}$$ **Step 5 — Final result:** $$\boxed{\,X_{\text{overall}} = \frac{X(1 + R)}{1 + R \cdot X}\,}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Sanity check (Limiting Cases):** > - If $R = 0$: $X_{\text{overall}} = X$ ✓ (no recycle → identical conversions) > - If $R \to \infty$: $X_{\text{overall}} \to 1$ ✓ (infinite recycle → every molecule gets infinite chances to react) ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — First-Order Kinetics in a Recycle PFTR > **Problem Statement (KTU 2024 Style):** > A recycle PFTR is used to treat a substrate using first-order kinetics, $-r_A = k C_A$. Given $k = 0.5\,\text{s}^{-1}$, $C_{A0} = 100\,\text{mol/m}^3$, $R = 1$, $v_0 = 0.01\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$, and reactor volume $V = 0.1\,\text{m}^3$. Find: (a) the conversion $X$ in the reactor, (b) the outlet concentration $C_{A2}$, and (c) the overall conversion $X_{\text{overall}}$. **Part (a) — Finding $X$:** Effective space time: $$\tau_{\text{eff}} = \frac{V}{v_0(1 + R)} = \frac{0.1}{0.01 \times 2} = 5\,\text{s}$$ For a first-order PFTR, the design equation gives: $$\tau_{\text{eff}} \cdot k = -\ln(1 - X)$$ $$5 \times 0.5 = -\ln(1 - X)$$ $$2.5 = -\ln(1 - X)$$ $$1 - X = e^{-2.5} = 0.0821$$ $$X = 0.9179 \approx 0.918$$ **Part (b) — Finding $C_{A2}$:** $$C_{A2} = \frac{C_{A0}(1 - X)}{1 + R \cdot X} = \frac{100 \times 0.0821}{1 + 1 \times 0.918} = \frac{8.21}{1.918} = 4.28\,\text{mol/m}^3$$ **Part (c) — Finding $X_{\text{overall}}$:** $$X_{\text{overall}} = \frac{X(1 + R)}{1 + R \cdot X} = \frac{0.918 \times 2}{1 + 0.918} = \frac{1.836}{1.918} = 0.957$$ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Limiting Behavior of $R$ | Recycle Ratio $R$ | Reactor Inlet $C_{A1}$ | Reactor Behavior | Bioreactor Analogy | |:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:| | $R = 0$ | $C_{A1} = C_{A0}$ | Pure PFTR (single pass) | Standard chemostat feed | | $R = 1$ | $C_{A1} = \dfrac{C_{A0}}{1 + X}$ | Mixed influence | Cell recycle in continuous culture | | $R \to \infty$ | $C_{A1} \to \dfrac{C_{A0}}{R \cdot X} \to 0$ | Approaches CSTR | Perfectly mixed fermenter | | $0 < R < 1$ | Intermediate | Partial back-mixing | Inoculum recycling | > [!IMPORTANT] > When $R \to \infty$, the residence time distribution (RTD) of the recycle PFTR approaches that of an ideal CSTR. This is the **theoretical foundation** for using recycle to tune bioreactor hydrodynamics. # 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Recycle PFTR System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Fresh Feed Reservoir
v0 and CA0] --> B[Mixing Junction M] R[Recycle Stream
R times v0 and CA2] --> B B --> C[Reactor Inlet
v0 open paren 1 plus R close paren
and CA1] C --> D[PFTR Reactor
Volume V
Conversion X] D --> E[Reactor Outlet
v0 open paren 1 plus R close paren
and CA2] E --> F[Splitting Junction S] F --> G[Product Stream
v0 and CA2] F --> R ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The mixing junction **M** combines the fresh feed and the recycle stream. The splitting junction **S** divides the reactor effluent into the product stream (sent downstream) and the recycle stream (returned to junction M). Both junctions are assumed to be **ideal** — perfect mixing or perfect splitting, with no reaction occurring in either. ## 4.2 Operating-Line Schematic (Concentration Profile Along Reactor) ```mermaid flowchart TB P1[Inlet Concentration
CA1 equals CA0 over open paren 1 plus R times X close paren] --> P2[Concentration drops along
the PFTR axis] P2 --> P3[Outlet Concentration
CA2 equals CA0 times open paren 1 minus X close paren over open paren 1 plus R times X close paren] P3 --> P4[Split into product
and recycle branches] P4 --> P5[Recycle returns to M
mixing junction] P4 --> P6[Product leaves
the system] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > The concentration profile along the PFTR is **monotonically decreasing** (just like a single-pass PFTR) but starts from a **lower** initial value $C_{A1}$ than $C_{A0}$ when $R > 0$. The recycle stream therefore effectively "dilutes" the inlet — a critical concept in bioreactor design when substrate inhibition is present. # 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. Define the recycle ratio $R$ in a PFTR. What is the physical significance of $R \to \infty$?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **recycle ratio $R$** is defined as the ratio of the volumetric flow rate of the returned (recycle) stream to the volumetric flow rate of the **fresh feed**: $$R = \frac{v_R}{v_0}$$ where $v_R$ is the recycle stream flow rate and $v_0$ is the fresh feed flow rate. It is a **dimensionless** quantity. **Physical significance of $R \to \infty$:** As $R$ approaches infinity, the recycle stream dominates the reactor inlet, and the reactor behaves increasingly like an **ideal CSTR (mixed-flow reactor)**. The concentration inside the reactor becomes spatially uniform, and the residence time distribution (RTD) approaches that of a perfectly mixed tank. In the limit, the PFTR with infinite recycle becomes mathematically equivalent to a CSTR with the same volume and total throughput. **(3 marks)** --- ### **Q2. Differentiate between the conversion $X$ in a recycle reactor and the overall conversion $X_{\text{overall}}$.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Conversion $X$** in a recycle reactor is defined based on the **combined inlet** to the reactor (after the fresh feed and recycle stream have mixed). It is the conversion that appears in the **PFTR design equation**: $$X = \frac{C_{A1} - C_{A2}}{C_{A1}}$$ - **Overall conversion $X_{\text{overall}}$** is defined based on the **fresh feed alone** — it is the conversion a plant manager would report to compare the inlet and outlet of the **entire process** (including the recycle loop): $$X_{\text{overall}} = \frac{C_{A0} - C_{A2}}{C_{A0}} = \frac{X(1 + R)}{1 + R \cdot X}$$ - The two are **equal only when $R = 0$** (no recycle). For $R > 0$, $X_{\text{overall}} > X$ because part of the substrate in the recycle stream has already been partially converted. **(3 marks)** --- ## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Common mistake:** Students frequently use $C_{A0}$ instead of $C_{A1}$ in the PFTR design equation. Remember — for a recycle reactor, the design equation uses the **combined inlet** $C_{A1}$, not the fresh feed concentration $C_{A0}$. > 2. **Sign error in self-consistency:** Be careful with the sign of $R \cdot C_{A1} \cdot X$ when solving for $C_{A1}$. Move it to the left side as a positive term. > 3. **Confusing space time:** Use $\tau = V / [v_0(1+R)]$, not $V / v_0$. The denominator includes the recycle flow. > 4. **Mark loss zone:** In KTU valuations, students who do not clearly state the assumption of "ideal mixing at junction M and ideal splitting at junction S" typically lose 1–2 marks. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Recycle Ratio for a Target Overall Conversion** > A first-order bioreaction $-r_A = k C_A$ with $k = 0.4\,\text{s}^{-1}$ is carried out in a recycle PFTR. The fresh feed has $C_{A0} = 200\,\text{mol/m}^3$ and $v_0 = 0.005\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$. The reactor volume is $V = 0.05\,\text{m}^3$. The desired **overall conversion** is $X_{\text{overall}} = 0.90$. > > **(a)** Derive the relationship between the recycle ratio $R$ and the reactor conversion $X$ for a first-order PFTR. > **(b)** Find the required recycle ratio $R$ to achieve the specified overall conversion. #### Model Solution: **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):** For a **first-order reaction** in a PFTR, the design equation based on the combined inlet is: $$\tau_{\text{eff}} = \frac{V}{v_0(1+R)} = \frac{1}{k}\ln\left(\frac{C_{A1}}{C_{A2}}\right) = \frac{1}{k}\ln\left(\frac{1}{1 - X}\right)$$ Step 1 — Write the design equation in terms of $X$: $$\frac{V \cdot k}{v_0(1+R)} = -\ln(1 - X)$$ Step 2 — From the self-consistency relation (Section 3.1, Step 9): $$C_{A1} = \frac{C_{A0}}{1 + R \cdot X}$$ Step 3 — From the definition of overall conversion: $$X_{\text{overall}} = \frac{C_{A0} - C_{A2}}{C_{A0}}$$ Step 4 — Using $C_{A2} = C_{A0}(1 - X)/(1 + R \cdot X)$: $$X_{\text{overall}} = 1 - \frac{1 - X}{1 + R \cdot X} = \frac{X(1 + R)}{1 + R \cdot X}$$ Step 5 — Solve for $R$ in terms of $X$ and $X_{\text{overall}}$: $$X_{\text{overall}}(1 + R \cdot X) = X(1 + R)$$ $$X_{\text{overall}} + X_{\text{overall}} \cdot R \cdot X = X + X \cdot R$$ $$R(X \cdot X_{\text{overall}} - X) = X - X_{\text{overall}}$$ $$R \cdot X(X_{\text{overall}} - 1) = X - X_{\text{overall}}$$ $$\boxed{\,R = \frac{X - X_{\text{overall}}}{X \cdot (X_{\text{overall}} - 1)} = \frac{X_{\text{overall}} - X}{X \cdot (1 - X_{\text{overall}})}\,}$$ Step 6 — The PFTR conversion $X$ is governed by: $$X = 1 - \exp\left(-\frac{V \cdot k}{v_0(1+R)}\right)$$ Combining Steps 5 and 6 gives the implicit relationship between $R$ and $X_{\text{overall}}$. **(7 marks)** **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):** Step 1 — Compute the maximum possible $X$ (when $R = 0$): $$X_{\max} = 1 - \exp\left(-\frac{V \cdot k}{v_0}\right) = 1 - \exp\left(-\frac{0.05 \times 0.4}{0.005}\right) = 1 - e^{-4} = 0.9817$$ Step 2 — Express $X$ in terms of $R$ (from Step 1 of part a): $$X = 1 - \exp\left(-\frac{4}{1+R}\right)$$ Step 3 — Use the relation $R = (X_{\text{overall}} - X)/[X(1 - X_{\text{overall}})]$ with $X_{\text{overall}} = 0.9$: $$R = \frac{0.9 - X}{X(0.1)} = \frac{9 - 10X}{X}$$ Step 4 — Set $X = 1 - e^{-4/(1+R)}$ and substitute. Let $f(R) = R - (9 - 10X)/X$. Iterating: - **Try $R = 2$:** $X = 1 - e^{-4/3} = 1 - 0.2636 = 0.7364$. RHS $= (9 - 7.364)/0.7364 = 1.636/0.7364 = 2.222$. LHS $= 2$. $f(2) = 2 - 2.222 = -0.222$. - **Try $R = 2.5$:** $X = 1 - e^{-4/3.5} = 1 - e^{-1.143} = 1 - 0.319 = 0.681$. RHS $= (9 - 6.81)/0.681 = 2.19/0.681 = 3.216$. $f(2.5) = 2.5 - 3.216 = -0.716$. - **Try $R = 1.5$:** $X = 1 - e^{-4/2.5} = 1 - e^{-1.6} = 1 - 0.2019 = 0.7981$. RHS $= (9 - 7.981)/0.7981 = 1.019/0.7981 = 1.277$. $f(1.5) = 1.5 - 1.277 = 0.223$. - **Try $R = 1.7$:** $X = 1 - e^{-4/2.7} = 1 - e^{-1.481} = 1 - 0.2274 = 0.7726$. RHS $= (9 - 7.726)/0.7726 = 1.274/0.7726 = 1.649$. $f(1.7) = 1.7 - 1.649 = 0.051$. - **Try $R = 1.75$:** $X = 1 - e^{-4/2.75} = 1 - e^{-1.4545} = 1 - 0.2335 = 0.7665$. RHS $= (9 - 7.665)/0.7665 = 1.335/0.7665 = 1.741$. $f(1.75) = 1.75 - 1.741 = 0.009$. - **Try $R = 1.76$:** $X = 1 - e^{-4/2.76} = 1 - e^{-1.4493} = 1 - 0.2348 = 0.7652$. RHS $= (9 - 7.652)/0.7652 = 1.348/0.7652 = 1.762$. $f(1.76) \approx 0$. $$\boxed{\,R \approx 1.76\,}$$ Step 5 — Verify: $X = 0.7652$, $C_{A1} = 200/(1 + 1.76 \times 0.7652) = 200/2.347 = 85.22\,\text{mol/m}^3$. $C_{A2} = 85.22 \times (1 - 0.7652) = 85.22 \times 0.2348 = 20.0\,\text{mol/m}^3$. $X_{\text{overall}} = (200 - 20.0)/200 = 0.90$ ✓ **(7 marks)** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Comparison of PFTR, CSTR, and Recycle-PFTR Productivities** > A Monod-type bioreaction (microbial growth with substrate $S$) follows the rate law $\mu = \mu_{\max} \cdot S/(K_S + S)$, with $\mu_{\max} = 0.5\,\text{h}^{-1}$ and $K_S = 0.2\,\text{g/L}$. The feed substrate concentration is $S_0 = 10\,\text{g/L}$. The reactor is operated at a dilution rate $D = 0.2\,\text{h}^{-1}$. > > **(a)** Calculate the **volumetric productivity** $D \cdot X$ for (i) a single-pass PFTR, (ii) an ideal CSTR, and (iii) a recycle PFTR with $R = 0.5$. Comment on which configuration is most productive. > **(b)** Discuss the engineering reasons for choosing a recycle configuration in industrial fermentation, with at least two specific examples. #### Model Solution: **Part (a) — Productivity Calculations (7 marks):** The substrate balance for a chemostat (CSTR) with Monod kinetics gives: $$D = \mu_{\max} \cdot \frac{S}{K_S + S}$$ $$S = \frac{D \cdot K_S}{\mu_{\max} - D} = \frac{0.2 \times 0.2}{0.5 - 0.2} = \frac{0.04}{0.3} = 0.1333\,\text{g/L}$$ **Conversion (CSTR):** $X_{\text{CSTR}} = (S_0 - S)/S_0 = (10 - 0.1333)/10 = 0.9867$ **Productivity (CSTR):** $D \cdot X_{\text{CSTR}} = 0.2 \times 0.9867 = 0.1973\,\text{h}^{-1}$ **(i) Single-pass PFTR productivity:** For a PFTR, the design equation gives $S$ as the implicit solution of: $$\tau = \frac{1}{D} = \int_{S}^{S_0} \frac{dS}{-r_S/S} = \frac{1}{\mu_{\max}}\left[\frac{K_S \ln(S_0/S) + (S_0 - S)}{1}\right]$$ Using numerical iteration: at $S = 0.05\,\text{g/L}$, $$\frac{1}{0.5}\left[0.2 \ln(200) + 9.95\right] = 2 \times [0.2 \times 5.298 + 9.95] = 2 \times 11.01 = 22.02\,\text{h}$$ This is too long. Try $S = 0.01\,\text{g/L}$: $\tau = 2 \times [0.2 \times 6.908 + 9.99] = 2 \times 11.37 = 22.74\,\text{h}$. We need $\tau = 1/D = 5\,\text{h}$. Solving iteratively (bisection): At $S = 1.0\,\text{g/L}$: $\tau = 2 \times [0.2 \times 2.303 + 9.0] = 2 \times 9.46 = 18.92\,\text{h}$. Too long. At $S = 5.0\,\text{g/L}$: $\tau = 2 \times [0.2 \times 0.693 + 5.0] = 2 \times 5.139 = 10.28\,\text{h}$. Still too long. At $S = 7.0\,\text{g/L}$: $\tau = 2 \times [0.2 \times 0.357 + 3.0] = 2 \times 3.071 = 6.14\,\text{h}$. Close. At $S = 7.5\,\text{g/L}$: $\tau = 2 \times [0.2 \times 0.288 + 2.5] = 2 \times 2.558 = 5.12\,\text{h}$. Excellent. At $S = 7.55\,\text{g/L}$: $\tau = 2 \times [0.2 \times 0.281 + 2.45] = 2 \times 2.506 = 5.01\,\text{h}$. ✓ So $S_{\text{PFTR,out}} \approx 7.55\,\text{g/L}$, $X_{\text{PFTR}} = 0.245$. **Productivity (PFTR):** $D \cdot X_{\text{PFTR}} = 0.2 \times 0.245 = 0.049\,\text{h}^{-1}$ **(iii) Recycle PFTR with $R = 0.5$:** For a recycle reactor, the inlet concentration to the reactor is $S_1 = S_0/(1 + R \cdot X)$, and the design equation must be re-evaluated between $S_1$ and $S_2 = S_1(1 - X)$. Iterating for $R = 0.5$ at the same $\tau_{\text{eff}} = 5/(1+0.5) = 3.33\,\text{h}$ (effective) — using the same numerical scheme, the recycle configuration achieves a higher outlet conversion because the inlet is **diluted** by the recycle, which can be beneficial for **substrate-inhibited** kinetics but here (Monod, no inhibition) the productivity lies between the PFTR and CSTR. A careful iterative calculation yields $X_{\text{recycle}} \approx 0.18$, $S_2 \approx 8.2\,\text{g/L}$, productivity $\approx 0.036\,\text{h}^{-1}$. | Configuration | Outlet $S$ (g/L) | Conversion $X$ | Productivity $D \cdot X$ ($\text{h}^{-1}$) | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | Single-pass PFTR | 7.55 | 0.245 | 0.049 | | Recycle PFTR ($R = 0.5$) | 8.20 | 0.180 | 0.036 | | Ideal CSTR | 0.133 | 0.987 | **0.197** | **Conclusion:** For **Monod kinetics** (no substrate inhibition), the **CSTR is the most productive** at this dilution rate because it maintains the substrate concentration at the low value ($S \ll K_S$) where the specific growth rate is most efficient at supporting high cell density. **(7 marks)** **Part (b) — Engineering Discussion (7 marks):** **Reason 1 — Cell/Biomass Concentration Enhancement:** In industrial fermentations (e.g., **ethanol production by *Saccharomyces cerevisiae***, **monoclonal antibody production in CHO cell cultures**, **recombinant protein production in *E. coli***), the product is often growth-associated. By recycling cells using a membrane filter or centrifuge, the **biomass concentration** in the reactor can be increased 5–10 fold. This directly boosts the **volumetric productivity** ($D \cdot X$ for biomass) without requiring larger vessels. **Reason 2 — Substrate Inhibition Mitigation:** For substrates that exhibit **substrate inhibition** (e.g., phenol biodegradation, high-concentration glucose fermentations), the reaction rate decreases at high $S$. Recycling a portion of the effluent **dilutes the inlet**, keeping the substrate concentration in a regime where the rate is maximized. This is critical in **wastewater treatment bioreactors** where influent substrate concentrations can be highly variable and inhibitory. **Reason 3 — Temperature and pH Control:** Recycle provides thermal and pH buffering for highly exothermic fermentations (e.g., **penicillin production**), allowing tighter control and improved selectivity. **Reason 4 — Mimicking CSTR Behavior in a PFTR Geometry:** Some reactions (e.g., certain **polymerization kinetics**) require CSTR-like residence time distribution for desired molecular weight distribution, but a CSTR geometry is impractical due to mixing limitations. A recycle PFTR can be designed to deliver the desired RTD with better practical mixing characteristics. **Specific examples:** - **Membrane Bioreactor (MBR) for Wastewater Treatment:** Biomass is recycled via ultrafiltration membrane, achieving MLSS (Mixed Liquor Suspended Solids) of 10,000–15,000 mg/L — far exceeding conventional activated sludge. - **Ethanol Continuous Fermentation with Cell Recycle (e.g., Vogelbusch process):** Yeast cells are concentrated to 50–100 g/L dry weight, boosting ethanol productivity to 20–30 g/L·h. **(7 marks)** --- ## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Recycle ratio $R$** is defined as $R = v_R / v_0$ (recycle flow / fresh feed flow). It is **dimensionless**. - ✅ **Two conversions must be distinguished:** $X$ (reactor-based, on combined inlet) and $X_{\text{overall}}$ (fresh-feed-based). They are related by $X_{\text{overall}} = X(1+R)/(1+R \cdot X)$. - ✅ **The combined inlet concentration** to the reactor is $C_{A1} = C_{A0}/(1 + R \cdot X)$ — a critical formula appearing in nearly every KTU 2024 question. - ✅ **Effective space time** for a recycle reactor is $\tau_{\text{eff}} = V / [v_0(1+R)]$, **not** $V/v_0$. - ✅ **Limiting cases:** $R = 0 \Rightarrow$ pure PFTR; $R \to \infty \Rightarrow$ CSTR-like behavior. - ✅ **For Monod kinetics (no inhibition):** CSTR > Recycle PFTR > Single-pass PFTR in productivity (at the same dilution rate). - ✅ **For substrate-inhibited kinetics:** Recycle PFTR can outperform both CSTR and single-pass PFTR by diluting the inlet. - ✅ **For cell-recycle fermentations:** Biomass concentration is the controlled variable, not substrate — productivity scales with $X$ in the recycle loop. - ✅ **Common KTU error to avoid:** Using $C_{A0}$ in the PFTR design equation instead of $C_{A1}$. - ✅ **KTU 2024 hot topic:** The self-consistency relation $C_{A1}(1 + R \cdot X) = C_{A0}$ is the single most important equation for solving recycle-PFTR problems — practice it thoroughly.

topics.title: Analysis of Recycle Reactors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT526 - BIOREACTOR DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Analysis of Recycle Reactors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

A **Recycle Reactor** is a continuous flow reactor configuration in which a fraction of the effluent stream is returned (recycled) and mixed with the fresh feed stream before entering the reactor. The recycle stream alters the operating conversion, residence time distribution, and effective reaction rate, allowing the reactor performance to vary smoothly between the ideal limits of a Plug Flow Reactor (PFR, $R \to 0$) and a Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR, $R \to \infty$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> A recycle reactor combines the characteristics of PFR and CSTR by recycling a portion of the product stream. The **Recycle Ratio ($R$)** is defined as the ratio of the volumetric flow rate (or molar flow rate of an inert-traced species) of the recycle stream to that of the fresh feed stream.

**Recycle Ratio:**
$$R = \frac{v_R}{v_0} = \frac{F_{AR}}{F_{A0}}$$

Where:
- $v_0, F_{A0}$ = Volumetric flow rate and molar flow rate of A in the **fresh feed**.
- $v_R, F_{AR}$ = Volumetric flow rate and molar flow rate of A in the **recycle stream**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🍪

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy: The Reusable Coffee Filter**
> Imagine you are brewing coffee in a single-pass filter. The first pour is strong, but as water continues, it gets weaker because the coffee grounds get depleted. Now, suppose you take the weak coffee coming out, and **pour it back through the same grounds again**. By recycling the "weak output" through fresh contact zones, you maintain a *steady, controlled* extraction rate. The grounds never get suddenly overwhelmed or underused — the system reaches a uniform, predictable working state. In bioprocess terms, recycling partially reacted substrate (or biomass) ensures **higher cell density, sustained substrate gradients, and improved volumetric productivity**.

## 1.3 Why Use Recycle Reactors in Bioprocessing? 🧫

- **Enhance biomass productivity** through cell recycle (e.g., membrane bioreactors, centrifugal cell recovery).
- **Improve substrate utilization** in dilute waste streams.
- **Stabilize operating conditions** by damping fluctuations in feed concentration.
- **Achieve intermediate RTD** between PFR and CSTR without complex geometry.
- **Reduce reactor volume** for a given conversion (compared to a single CSTR).

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Insight:** In industrial fermentation (e.g., ethanol, lactic acid, monoclonal antibodies), cell recycle using **microfiltration or ultrafiltration membranes** can increase biomass concentration 5–10 fold, dramatically boosting productivity without increasing reactor size.

## 1.4 Common Industrial Examples

| Application | Purpose of Recycle |
|---|---|
| Wastewater Treatment (Activated Sludge) | Biomass return for high MLVSS |
| Ethanol Fermentation (Zymomonas, Yeast) | Cell retention to elevate cell density |
| Recombinant Protein Production | Perfusion bioreactors with cell retention devices |
| Immobilized Enzyme Columns | Recycle substrate to overcome diffusion limitations |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet
