# 🔷 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define bioreactor geometry. List the four standard geometric ratios used in stirred-tank reactor design.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Bioreactor geometry refers to the proportional dimensional design of a fermentation vessel that determines its mixing, mass transfer, and power characteristics.
>
> **The four standard geometric ratios are:**
> 1. **Aspect Ratio (H/T)** — typically 1.0 – 3.0
> 2. **Impeller-to-Tank Ratio (Dᵢ/T)** — typically 1/3 (0.333) for Rushton
> 3. **Baffle Width Ratio (Wᵦ/T)** — T/10 (0.10)
> 4. **Wall Clearance Ratio (C/T)** — T/10 (0.10)
>
> These ratios together define the **internal spatial architecture** of a stirred-tank bioreactor. *(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for ratios)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Why are baffles provided in a stirred-tank bioreactor? What happens if the width of the baffle is increased beyond T/10?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Purpose of baffles:** Baffles prevent the formation of a deep central vortex (which would otherwise form due to the rotating fluid in an unbaffled tank), thereby ensuring effective radial and axial mixing.
>
> **If Wᵦ > T/10:** Beyond the T/10 standard, the flow pattern becomes *constrained* — *stagnant pockets* develop behind the baffles, and the fluid tends to *flow up along the baffle* and *down in the centre*, creating a *secondary circulation* that *reduces* the bulk mixing efficiency. In extreme cases, the impeller is "starved" of liquid and power consumption drops sharply despite a "dead" tank.
>
> **Hence T/10 is the geometric optimum** for baffle width in standard Rushton systems. *(3 marks — 1 for purpose, 2 for over-width effect)*

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **⭐ QUESTION A — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 4]**

**A pilot stirred-tank bioreactor has the following geometric specifications:**
- Tank diameter: **T = 0.80 m**
- Liquid height: **H = 1.60 m**
- Number of Rushton impellers: **n = 2**
- Impeller diameter: **Dᵢ = 0.267 m**
- Baffle width: **Wᵦ = 0.08 m**
- Impeller speed: **N = 4.0 rev/s**
- Working volume fraction: **η = 0.75**
- Liquid density: **ρ = 1000 kg/m³**
- Viscosity: **μ = 1.0 × 10⁻³ Pa·s**

**Answer the following:**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Verify whether the bioreactor conforms to the standard geometric ratios (H/T, Dᵢ/T, Wᵦ/T). Calculate the **total volume**, **working volume**, and the **Reynolds number**. State whether the flow is laminar, transitional, or turbulent.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Calculate the **impeller tip speed**, the **vortex depth that would form if the baffles were removed**, and the **power input** (assuming turbulent regime, Nₚ = 6.0).

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A**

##### **Part (a) — Geometric Verification, Volumes, and Reynolds Number**

**Step 1 — Verify Aspect Ratio (H/T):**
$$\frac{H}{T} = \frac{1.60}{0.80} = 2.0 \quad \checkmark \text{ (Standard STR)}$$

**Step 2 — Verify Dᵢ/T ratio:**
$$\frac{D_i}{T} = \frac{0.267}{0.80} = 0.3338 \approx \frac{1}{3} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 3 — Verify Wᵦ/T ratio:**
$$\frac{W_b}{T} = \frac{0.08}{0.80} = 0.10 \quad \checkmark \text{ (T/10 rule satisfied)}$$

**Step 4 — Total Volume of the cylindrical tank:**
$$V_t = \frac{\pi}{4} \, T^2 \, H$$
$$\begin{aligned}
V_t &= \frac{3.1416}{4} \times (0.80)^2 \times 1.60 \\
&= 0.7854 \times 0.64 \times 1.60 \\
&= 0.8042 \text{ m}^3 \approx 804 \text{ L}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Working Volume:**
$$V_w = \eta \times V_t = 0.75 \times 0.8042 = 0.6032 \text{ m}^3 \approx 603 \text{ L}$$

**Step 6 — Reynolds Number:**
$$Re = \frac{\rho \, N \, D_i^2}{\mu}$$
$$\begin{aligned}
Re &= \frac{1000 \times 4.0 \times (0.267)^2}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}} \\
&= \frac{1000 \times 4.0 \times 0.07129}{0.001} \\
&= \frac{285.16}{0.001} \\
&= 2.8516 \times 10^{5}
\end{aligned}$$

Since **Re = 2.85 × 10⁵ >> 10,000**, the flow is **fully turbulent**. *(Part (a) — 7 marks: 3 for verification, 2 for volumes, 2 for Re & regime)*

---

##### **Part (b) — Tip Speed, Vortex Depth, and Power**

**Step 1 — Impeller Tip Speed:**
$$v_{tip} = \pi \, N \, D_i$$
$$\begin{aligned}
v_{tip} &= \pi \times 4.0 \times 0.267 \\
&= 3.1416 \times 1.068 \\
&= 3.355 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Vortex Depth (Unbaffled Case):**
$$h_v = \frac{(\pi \, N \, D_i)^2}{2g} = \frac{v_{tip}^2}{2g}$$
$$\begin{aligned}
h_v &= \frac{(3.355)^2}{2 \times 9.81} \\
&= \frac{11.256}{19.62} \\
&= 0.5737 \text{ m} \approx 57.4 \text{ cm}
\end{aligned}$$

> **[Comment]:** A 57.4 cm vortex in a 160 cm tall tank is **catastrophic** — it would expose the impellers and cause massive aeration failure. This justifies the use of baffles (Wᵦ = T/10), which suppress h_v to < 1 cm.

**Step 3 — Power Input (Turbulent Regime, Nₚ = 6.0):**
$$P = N_p \, \rho \, N^3 \, D_i^5$$
$$\begin{aligned}
D_i^5 &= (0.267)^5 \\
&= (0.267)^2 \times (0.267)^2 \times 0.267 \\
&= 0.07129 \times 0.07129 \times 0.267 \\
&= 0.001357 \text{ m}^5 \\
N^3 &= (4.0)^3 = 64.0 \text{ rev}^3/\text{s}^3 \\
P &= 6.0 \times 1000 \times 64.0 \times 0.001357 \\
&= 6.0 \times 86.85 \\
&= 521.1 \text{ W} \approx 0.52 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

*(Part (b) — 7 marks: 2 for tip speed, 2 for vortex, 3 for power with full substitution)*

---

### **⭐ QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative to Question A)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 4]**

**A pharmaceutical company wishes to scale up a successful 100 L pilot bioreactor to a 10,000 L production fermenter while preserving geometric similarity. The pilot reactor has the following dimensions:**

- Pilot: **T₁ = 0.40 m**, **H₁ = 0.80 m**, **Dᵢ₁ = 0.133 m**, **N₁ = 5.0 rev/s**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Calculate the **linear scale factor (λ)**, the **production-scale dimensions (T₂, H₂, Dᵢ₂)**, the **number of Rushton impellers** required in the production vessel, and the **impeller speed N₂** if **constant tip speed** is to be maintained.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Calculate the **power requirement** in both vessels (assume fully turbulent, Nₚ = 6.0, ρ = 1000 kg/m³). Comment on the **scale-up dilemma** revealed by the power comparison.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B**

##### **Part (a) — Scale Factor, Production Dimensions, Impeller Count, N₂**

**Step 1 — Linear Scale Factor:**
$$\lambda = \left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)^{1/3} = \left(\frac{10{,}000}{100}\right)^{1/3} = 4.6416$$

**Step 2 — Production-Scale Dimensions:**
$$\begin{aligned}
T_2 &= T_1 \times \lambda = 0.40 \times 4.6416 = 1.857 \text{ m} \\
H_2 &= H_1 \times \lambda = 0.80 \times 4.6416 = 3.713 \text{ m} \\
D_{i2} &= D_{i1} \times \lambda = 0.133 \times 4.6416 = 0.6174 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Number of Impellers:**
$$n = \text{ceil}\!\left(\frac{H_2}{D_{i2}}\right) = \text{ceil}\!\left(\frac{3.713}{0.6174}\right) = \text{ceil}(6.014) = 7 \text{ impellers}$$

(In practice, **6 impellers** with Hᵢ = 0.62 m spacing is used, since 7 is mechanically excessive.)

**Step 4 — Impeller Speed at Constant Tip Speed:**
Constant tip speed means $v_{tip,1} = v_{tip,2}$:
$$\pi \, N_1 \, D_{i1} = \pi \, N_2 \, D_{i2}$$
$$N_2 = N_1 \times \frac{D_{i1}}{D_{i2}} = 5.0 \times \frac{0.133}{0.6174} = 5.0 \times 0.2154 = 1.077 \text{ rev/s}$$

*(Part (a) — 7 marks: 2 for λ, 2 for dimensions, 1 for impeller count, 2 for N₂)*

---

##### **Part (b) — Power Comparison and Scale-Up Dilemma**

**Step 1 — Pilot Power (P₁):**
$$\begin{aligned}
D_{i1}^5 &= (0.133)^5 \\
&= (0.133)^2 \times (0.133)^2 \times 0.133 \\
&= 0.01769 \times 0.01769 \times 0.133 \\
&= 4.163 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^5 \\
N_1^3 &= (5.0)^3 = 125 \text{ rev}^3/\text{s}^3 \\
P_1 &= 6.0 \times 1000 \times 125 \times 4.163 \times 10^{-5} \\
&= 31.22 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Production Power (P₂):**
$$\begin{aligned}
D_{i2}^5 &= (0.6174)^5 \\
&\approx 0.08984 \text{ m}^5 \quad \text{(computed as } 0.6174^2 \times 0.6174^2 \times 0.6174) \\
N_2^3 &= (1.077)^3 = 1.249 \text{ rev}^3/\text{s}^3 \\
P_2 &= 6.0 \times 1000 \times 1.249 \times 0.08984 \\
&= 673.2 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Power Density Comparison:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Pilot: } \frac{P_1}{V_1} &= \frac{31.22}{0.100} = 312.2 \text{ W/m}^3 \\
\text{Production: } \frac{P_2}{V_2} &= \frac{673.2}{10.0} = 67.3 \text{ W/m}^3
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — The Scale-Up Dilemma:**
- **Volume scaled by 100×**, but **power scaled by only ~21.5×**.
- **Power density dropped by 78%** (from 312 to 67 W/m³).
- This means the **large fermenter is *under-mixed* per unit volume** compared to the pilot.
- The kₗa, mixing time, and heat removal will *all deteriorate* at scale — the classic **"scale-up dilemma"**.
- The remedy is to abandon *constant tip speed* and switch to **constant power per unit volume (P/V)** as the scale-up criterion.

*(Part (b) — 7 marks: 2 for P₁, 2 for P₂, 1 for P/V, 2 for dilemma explanation)*

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cause 5–8 mark deductions on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the π in tip speed:** Tip speed is $v_{tip} = \pi N D_i$, **not** $N D_i$. The π factor arises because N is in rev/s, not rad/s. A missing π gives a 57% error in tip speed and the vortex depth becomes 1/π² ≈ 10% of the correct value. **(–3 marks)**
>
> 2. **Mixing up working and total volume in power calculations:** Power density (P/V) uses **working volume V_w**, not total volume V_t. Using V_t understates P/V by ~25% and makes the scale-up analysis invalid. **(–2 marks)**
>
> 3. **Wrong use of T/10:** Students often write Wᵦ = T/10 *as a diameter*, but it is in fact *a linear width*. For T = 2 m, Wᵦ = 0.2 m (linear), not 0.2 m². **(–1 mark)**
>
> 4. **Impeller count error:** Using the formula n = H/Dᵢ **without the ceiling function** can give a non-integer (e.g., 5.7) — always **round up** to the next integer. **(–1 mark)**
>
> 5. **Vortex depth sign convention:** h_v is the *depression below* the static liquid level, not the *rise above* it. The vortex dips DOWN at the centre. **(–1 mark)**
>
> 6. **Neglecting viscosity at small scale:** At Re < 1000, Nₚ is *not* 6.0. Examiners may deduct marks if you blindly use 6.0 for a viscous broth. **(–2 marks)**

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision — the 15 most critical points to memorize:**

- ☐ **Definition:** Bioreactor geometry = the set of proportional design ratios (H/T, Dᵢ/T, Wᵦ/T, C/T) that define a fermenter's internal architecture.
- ☐ **Standard STR aspect ratio:** **H/T = 2.0**.
- ☐ **Standard Rushton impeller ratio:** **Dᵢ/T = 1/3 = 0.333**.
- ☐ **Standard baffle width:** **Wᵦ = T/10 = 0.10 T** (with **4 baffles** at 90°).
- ☐ **Standard wall clearance:** **C = T/10 = 0.10 T**.
- ☐ **Inter-impeller spacing:** **Hᵢ = (1.0 to 1.5) Dᵢ**.
- ☐ **Number of impellers:** $n = \text{ceil}(H/D_i)$ — always **round up**.
- ☐ **Working volume fraction:** **η = 0.70 – 0.80** (antibiotics at low end, yeasts/airlifts at high end).
- ☐ **Cylindrical volume:** $V_t = \dfrac{\pi}{4} T^2 H$ — verify H/T ratio first.
- ☐ **Vortex depth (unbaffled):** $h_v = \dfrac{\pi^2 N^2 D_i^2}{2g} = \dfrac{v_{tip}^2}{2g}$.
- ☐ **Tip speed:** $v_{tip} = \pi \, N \, D_i$ (note the π).
- ☐ **Reynolds number:** $Re = \dfrac{\rho N D_i^2}{\mu}$; > 10⁴ = turbulent.
- ☐ **Power (turbulent):** $P = N_p \, \rho \, N^3 D_i^5$, with **Nₚ = 6.0** for Rushton.
- ☐ **Geometric similarity:** Linear scale factor $\lambda = (V_2/V_1)^{1/3}$ preserves *all* dimensionless ratios.
- ☐ **Scale-up dilemma:** Volume scales as λ³, power as λ⁵/³ (if N constant) — *P/V drops* drastically at large scale, so choose a *non-geometric* criterion (e.g., constant P/V or constant kₗa).

> 🎯 **KTU Final Tip:** Whenever the question says "scale up maintaining geometric similarity", your *first* line in the solution must state: **"λ = (V₂/V₁)¹ᐟ³"** and *all* dimensions scale linearly by λ. Examiners award **1 mark** specifically for stating this principle.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Guidelines for Bioreactor Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT526 - BIOREACTOR DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Guidelines for Bioreactor Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Guidelines for Bioreactor Design** constitute the codified set of engineering principles, design heuristics, empirical correlations, and process constraints that govern the conceptualization, sizing, and specification of a biological reactor. These guidelines provide a structured framework that ensures the designed bioreactor delivers the desired **volumetric productivity (g/L·h)**, maintains **sterility**, achieves adequate **mass transfer (k_La, s⁻¹)**, provides precise **temperature control (°C)**, and operates safely across the **operational window (working volume range)**.

In KTU 2024 scheme parlance, bioreactor design guidelines are best understood as a **multi-objective optimization problem** satisfying the intersection of three domains:

1. **Biological Domain** — Microbial kinetics, substrate inhibition, product formation, shear sensitivity.
2. **Transport Domain** — Momentum, heat, and mass transfer operations.
3. **Mechanical Domain** — Vessel geometry, agitator selection, sparger design, baffling, materials of construction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A bioreactor is NOT just a "tank with stirrers." It is a precisely engineered vessel where **geometry (H/D ratio)**, **power input (P/V)**, **mixing time (t_m)**, **shear rate (γ̇)**, and **k_La** are balanced simultaneously to support a fragile living catalyst.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are designing a **luxury high-rise apartment building** for a very particular tenant — a famous, temperamental musician (the microorganism). 

- The **apartment** must have the **right room dimensions (bioreactor H/D ratio)** so sound (oxygen) propagates evenly.
- The **HVAC system (agitation/aeration)** must be tuned so the musician gets enough fresh air without deafening noise (excess shear).
- The **temperature control (cooling jacket/cooling coils)** must keep the studio at exactly 22 °C, not 21 °C or 23 °C.
- The **plumbing (sparger, feed lines, harvest line)** must be leak-proof — a single drop of contaminated water (foreign microbe) ruins the show.
- The **building (vessel)** must withstand weather (pressure, temperature) and not corrode (stainless steel SS-316L).

If any of these fail, the musician walks out (cells die / product yield collapses). **Bioreactor design guidelines are the architectural rules of this high-rise for microbes.**

---

## 1.3 The Three Universal Design Pillars

> [!NOTE]
> **The Bioreactor Design Triangle (BDT):**
> Every KTU problem on bioreactor design implicitly tests the trade-off between these three vertices:

| Pillar | Engineering Parameter | Biological Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| **Mixing** | Impeller type, speed (N), power (P) | Nutrient homogeneity, pH uniformity |
| **Mass Transfer** | k_La (s⁻¹), gas hold-up (ε_g) | Oxygen availability for aerobic cells |
| **Shear** | γ̇ (s⁻¹), tip speed (v_tip) | Cell morphology, viability, product quality |

The art of bioreactor design is choosing operating conditions that **simultaneously satisfy** all three.

---

## 1.4 Why Are Guidelines Needed?

Unlike chemical reactors, bioreactors must respect **life** — and life is fragile, nonlinear, and often poorly understood at molecular level. Guidelines exist to:

1. **Protect the catalyst** (microorganism/cell) from harmful shear, foam, toxic gradients.
2. **Maintain sterility** — absolute barrier against contamination.
3. **Provide reproducibility** — identical batches at scales 10 L, 100 L, 10,000 L.
4. **Ensure economic viability** — capex, opex, and product titer-based ROI.
5. **Comply with cGMP / FDA / WHO** standards (for pharma applications).
6. **Allow scale-up and scale-down** without loss of performance.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic — "SCALES"** for remembering the 6 design pillars:
> **S** — Sterility
> **C** — Cell/Shear protection
> **A** — Aeration & Agitation adequacy
> **L** — Liquid level and Loading (working volume)
> **E** — Energy dissipation & Economics
> **S** — Safety & Sterilization-in-Place (SIP)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hierarchical Design Workflow

Bioreactor design follows a strict top-down hierarchy. Skipping levels leads to redesign and cost overruns.

### Step 1: Define the Biological Brief
- **Cell type:** bacteria, yeast, filamentous fungi, mammalian cells, plant cells.
- **Kinetics:** μ_max (h⁻¹), K_s (g/L), Y_X/S (g/g), product formation type (Type I, II, III — Gaden classification).
- **Medium composition** and **substrate concentration (g/L)**.
- **Oxygen demand** (OUR, mmol O₂/L·h) and **heat evolution** (Q, kW/m³).

### Step 2: Choose the Reactor Mode
- Batch, Fed-batch, Continuous (CSTR), Perfusion, Airlift, Bubble column, PBR, Membrane.
- Selection based on kinetics, genetics (recombinant instability), and product toxicity.

### Step 3: Establish Volumetric Scale (V_R)
Using the **material balance** approach:

$$V_R = \frac{Q_{prod} \cdot Y_{P/S}}{r_P \cdot t_{cycle} \cdot \eta_{downstream}}$$

Where:
- $V_R$ = working volume (L or m³)
- $Q_{prod}$ = annual product demand (kg/yr)
- $Y_{P/S}$ = inverse of yield — substrate required per kg product (kg/kg)
- $r_P$ = volumetric productivity (g/L·h)
- $t_{cycle}$ = total cycle time including turnaround (h)
- $\eta_{downstream}$ = downstream recovery efficiency (0–1)

### Step 4: Determine Geometric Proportions
Standard **aspect ratio** (H_T/D_T) ranges:

| Reactor Type | H_T/D_T Range | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Standard STR (lab) | 1.0 – 1.5 | Bench/pilot |
| Standard STR (production) | 1.5 – 3.0 | Industrial |
| Tall airlift | 4.0 – 6.0 | Single-cell protein |
| Air-lift/BC (slender) | > 6.0 | Wastewater |

Liquid height is typically **0.7 – 0.8 of total height** to allow headspace for foam disengagement.

### Step 5: Design the Aeration–Agitation System
Select **sparger type** (porous vs. orifice vs. nozzle) and **impeller configuration** (Rushton, pitched-blade, marine, Scaba, elephant ear).

Calculate required **superficial gas velocity (v_s)**:

$$v_s = \frac{Q_g}{A_{cross}} = \frac{4 \cdot Q_g}{\pi \cdot D_T^2}$$

Typical $v_s$ range: **0.01 – 0.05 m/s** for STRs (above 0.05 m/s → flooding regime).

### Step 6: Verify Mass Transfer (k_La)
Use empirical correlations (Van't Riet, 1979):

$$k_L a = K \cdot \left(\frac{P_g}{V_L}\right)^{\alpha} \cdot v_s^{\beta}$$

For coalescing (air-water): K ≈ 0.026, α ≈ 0.4, β ≈ 0.5
For non-coalescing (electrolyte): K ≈ 0.002, α ≈ 0.7, β ≈ 0.2

Required OUR: $OUR = k_L a \cdot (C^* - C_L)$

### Step 7: Heat Transfer Design
Cooling duty Q (kW) balances metabolic heat:

$$Q = \Delta H_{metab} \cdot r_X \cdot V_L$$

Where $\Delta H_{metab} \approx 0.48$ J/mmol O₂ (for glucose oxidation on biomass).

Cooling area:

$$A_{cool} = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}}$$

Typical U values: jacket → 500–1500 W/m²·K, internal coil → 1500–3000 W/m²·K.

### Step 8: Sterilization & Cleaning (SIP/CIP)
- **SIP:** 121 °C × 30 min minimum (F₀ ≥ 15 min for clean-in-place cycles).
- **CIP:** typically 1–2% NaOH at 80 °C, followed by rinse.
- All internal surfaces **electropolished to Ra ≤ 0.4 μm** (for pharma).
- **No dead legs** (L/D ≤ 2 in piping).

### Step 9: Materials of Construction (MOC)
- **SS-316L** is the industry standard (low carbon content avoids sensitization/grain-boundary corrosion).
- Buna-N, EPDM, or silicone for O-rings and gaskets.
- PTFE for aseptic seals.
- Borosilicate glass for lab-scale (< 20 L).

### Step 10: Instrumentation & Control
- **pH, DO, T, Foam, Level, Weight, Off-gas O₂/CO₂** — minimum sensor suite.
- Control loops: cascaded PID for T, DO-stat or μ-stat feeding strategy.
- SCADA / DCS / PAT integration.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in 70%+ of KTU numerical questions on this module.**

| # | Equation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_R = \dfrac{Q_{prod} \cdot Y_{P/S}}{r_P \cdot t_{cycle} \cdot \eta}$ | Reactor working volume | m³ | Sizing |
| 2 | $v_s = \dfrac{4 Q_g}{\pi D_T^2}$ | Superficial gas velocity | m/s | Aeration sizing |
| 3 | $P_g = P_{ungassed} \cdot \left(1 - C \cdot \dfrac{Q_g}{N \cdot V_L}\right)$ | Gassed power draw (Hassan-Whitman) | W | Power calc with gassing |
| 4 | $\dfrac{P}{V_L} = \dfrac{P_o \cdot \rho \cdot N^3 \cdot D_i^5}{V_L}$ | Volumetric power input | W/m³ | Scale-up criterion |
| 5 | $k_L a = K \cdot \left(\dfrac{P_g}{V_L}\right)^{\alpha} v_s^{\beta}$ | Volumetric mass transfer coeff | s⁻¹ | O₂ transfer design |
| 6 | $OUR = k_L a (C^* - C_L)$ | Oxygen uptake rate | mmol/L·h | Aeration adequacy |
| 7 | $N_{Re} = \dfrac{\rho N D_i^2}{\mu}$ | Impeller Reynolds number | – | Flow regime (laminar/turb/transition) |
| 8 | $N_p = \dfrac{P}{\rho N^3 D_i^5}$ | Power number (Po) | – | Impeller selection |
| 9 | $t_m \propto \left(\dfrac{P}{V_L}\right)^{-1/3} \left(\dfrac{D_T}{D_i}\right)^{1/3}$ | Mixing time correlation | s | Mixing scale-up |
| 10 | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$ | Heat transfer rate | W | Cooling coil/jacket design |
| 11 | $\Delta P_{sterile\ filter} = \dfrac{\mu \cdot v \cdot L}{d^2 \cdot \varepsilon^3} \cdot \text{(Kozeny-Carman)}$ | Filter pressure drop | Pa | Air sterilization |
| 12 | $\nabla_{sterile} = k \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{N_0}{N}\right)$ | Sterilization value | min | Steam-in-place design |
| 13 | $D_T = \left(\dfrac{4 V_R}{\pi \cdot (H_T/D_T)}\right)^{1/3}$ | Vessel diameter from volume & aspect ratio | m | Geometric design |
| 14 | $v_{tip} = \pi \cdot N \cdot D_i$ | Impeller tip speed | m/s | Shear-sensitive design |
| 15 | $\tau_{shear} \propto \left(\dfrac{P}{V_L}\right)^{1/2} \mu^{-1/2}$ | Local shear rate (Metzner-Otto) | s⁻¹ | Cell damage estimation |

---

## 2.3 Physical Constants and Standard Benchmarks

> [!NOTE]
> **Bookmark these benchmarks — they are the "default values" examiners use when no data is given.**

- Standard atmospheric pressure: $P_{atm} = \textbf{101.325 kPa}$
- Saturation DO in water at 25 °C, 1 atm: $C^* = \textbf{7.5 mg/L} = 0.234$ mmol/L
- Critical DO for most aerobic microbes: $C_{L,crit} = 0.5 - 2.0$ mg/L
- Water density (process): $\rho = \textbf{1000 kg/m}^3$
- Viscosity (water-like broths): $\mu = \textbf{1 mPa·s}$
- Maximum practical $N_p$ for Rushton: **~5.0** (turbulent)
- For pitched-blade (45°, 4-blade): $N_p \approx \textbf{1.3}$ (turbulent)
- $k_L a$ in production STR: **100 – 1000 h⁻¹** (≈ 0.03 – 0.28 s⁻¹)
- Power input (industrial aerobic): $P/V = 1 - 5$ kW/m³
- Power input (high-cell-density): $P/V = 10 - 25$ kW/m³

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

These guidelines are not academic exercises. They drive:

- **Pharma** (insulin, monoclonal antibodies, vaccines) — perfusion bioreactors up to 2,000 L.
- **Industrial enzymes** (subtilisin, amylases) — 100 m³ STRs.
- **Biofuels** (cellulosic ethanol) — 1,000+ m³ cascades.
- **Single-cell protein (SCP)** — airlift reactors, ICI Pruteen process (1,500 m³, H/D ≈ 6).
- **Wastewater / activated sludge** — bubble columns and airlift reactors.
- **Cell & gene therapy** — single-use rocking-motion bioreactors.

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Snapshot:** The world's largest STR (as of 2024) exceeds 5,000 m³ for SCP and enzyme production; the world's largest mammalian-cell STR (sanofi/Roche) operates at 25,000 L for mAb manufacturing.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivations You Must Know for KTU 2024

### Derivation 1: Required Working Volume from Productivity Target

**Statement:** A recombinant *E. coli* process must produce 50,000 kg/yr of intracellular product. The yield is 0.05 kg product/kg glucose, productivity is 0.3 g/L·h, cycle time is 36 h, and downstream recovery is 80%. Find the required working volume.

**Given:**
- $Q_{prod} = 50{,}000$ kg/yr
- $Y_{P/S} = 0.05$ kg product/kg substrate → substrate needed = $1/0.05 = 20$ kg/kg
- $r_P = 0.3$ g/L·h = 0.0003 kg/L·h
- $t_{cycle} = 36$ h
- $\eta = 0.80$
- Operating days: 330 d/yr → 330 × 24 = 7,920 h/yr

**Step 1 — Substrate required per year:**

$$\text{Substrate}_{yr} = Q_{prod} \times \frac{1}{Y_{P/S}} = 50{,}000 \times 20 = 1{,}000{,}000 \text{ kg/yr}$$

**Step 2 — Required substrate per cycle:**

$$\text{Substrate}_{cycle} = \frac{1{,}000{,}000 \text{ kg/yr}}{7{,}920 / 36 \text{ cycles/yr}} = \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{220} = 4{,}545.5 \text{ kg/cycle}$$

**Step 3 — Required working volume:**

$$V_R = \frac{\text{Substrate}_{cycle} \cdot Y_{P/S}}{r_P \cdot t_{cycle} \cdot \eta}$$

$$V_R = \frac{4{,}545.5 \times 0.05}{0.0003 \times 36 \times 0.80}$$

$$V_R = \frac{227.27}{0.00864} = 26{,}304 \text{ L} \approx 26.3 \text{ m}^3$$

**Engineering decision:** Use 4 reactors of 8 m³ each (with redundancy) OR a single 30 m³ reactor.

---

### Derivation 2: Vessel Diameter and Height from Working Volume

**Given:** Working volume = 26.3 m³; choose H_T/D_T = 2.0 (standard tall STR).

**Step 1 — Total volume (with 80% fill):**

$$V_T = \frac{V_R}{0.80} = \frac{26.3}{0.80} = 32.88 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Relate H_T = 2 D_T to cylinder volume:**

$$V_T = \frac{\pi}{4} D_T^2 \cdot H_T = \frac{\pi}{4} D_T^2 \cdot 2 D_T = \frac{\pi}{2} D_T^3$$

$$32.88 = \frac{\pi}{2} D_T^3$$

$$D_T^3 = \frac{32.88 \times 2}{\pi} = 20.93$$

$$D_T = 2.755 \text{ m}, \quad H_T = 5.51 \text{ m}$$

---

### Derivation 3: Aeration Design and Superficial Gas Velocity

**Given:** Aerobic yeast fermentation, $Q_g = 0.5$ vvm (volume of gas per volume of liquid per minute). $V_R = 26.3$ m³, $D_T = 2.755$ m.

**Step 1 — Volumetric gas flow:**

$$Q_g = 0.5 \times 26.3 = 13.15 \text{ m}^3/\text{min} = 0.2192 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Cross-sectional area:**

$$A_{cross} = \frac{\pi}{4} D_T^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (2.755)^2 = 5.96 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Superficial gas velocity:**

$$v_s = \frac{Q_g}{A_{cross}} = \frac{0.2192}{5.96} = 0.0368 \text{ m/s}$$

This is within the acceptable 0.01–0.05 m/s range. ✓

---

### Derivation 4: Power Requirement and P/V

**Given:** Two Rushton turbines (P_o = 5.5), D_i = D_T/3 = 0.918 m, N = 2.5 rps (150 rpm), broth ρ = 1,050 kg/m³, μ = 0.002 Pa·s.

**Step 1 — Impeller Reynolds number:**

$$N_{Re} = \frac{\rho N D_i^2}{\mu} = \frac{1050 \times 2.5 \times (0.918)^2}{0.002} = 1{,}107{,}000$$

Since $N_{Re} \gg 10^4$, flow is **fully turbulent** → use $P_o = 5.5$.

**Step 2 — Ungassed power:**

$$P_{ungassed} = P_o \cdot \rho \cdot N^3 \cdot D_i^5$$

$$P_{ungassed} = 5.5 \times 1050 \times (2.5)^3 \times (0.918)^5$$

$$P_{ungassed} = 5.5 \times 1050 \times 15.625 \times 0.652$$

$$P_{ungassed} \approx 58{,}805 \text{ W} \approx 58.8 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Gassed power (Hassan-Whitman, C ≈ 0.85 for Rushton):**

$$P_g = P_{ungassed} \cdot \left(1 - 0.85 \cdot \frac{Q_g}{N V_L}\right)$$

$$P_g = 58{,}805 \cdot \left(1 - 0.85 \cdot \frac{0.2192}{2.5 \times 26.3}\right)$$

$$P_g = 58{,}805 \cdot (1 - 0.85 \times 0.00333)$$

$$P_g = 58{,}805 \cdot (1 - 0.00283) = 58{,}639 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4 — Volumetric power input:**

$$\frac{P}{V} = \frac{58{,}639}{26.3} = 2{,}230 \text{ W/m}^3 = 2.23 \text{ kW/m}^3$$

This is in the **typical 1–5 kW/m³ range**. ✓

---

### Derivation 5: k_La Verification (Van't Riet Correlation)

**Given:** Coalescing broth, P/V = 2.23 kW/m³ = 2,230 W/m³, v_s = 0.0368 m/s.

**Step 1 — Apply correlation:**

$$k_L a = 0.026 \times (2230)^{0.4} \times (0.0368)^{0.5}$$

$$k_L a = 0.026 \times 13.42 \times 0.1918$$

$$k_L a = 0.0669 \text{ s}^{-1} = 241 \text{ h}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Oxygen transfer rate (OTR):**

$$OTR = k_L a \cdot (C^* - C_L) = 0.0669 \times (0.234 - 0.05) \text{ mmol/L/s}$$

$$OTR = 0.0669 \times 0.184 = 0.0123 \text{ mmol/L/s} = 44.3 \text{ mmol/L/h}$$

**Step 3 — Compare to OUR of typical yeast (5–10 mmol/L/h):**

OUR (typical) = 8 mmol/L/h < OTR = 44.3 mmol/L/h ✓

**Design is ADEQUATE — oxygen is not limiting.**

---

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use What

| Design Question | If YES → | If NO → |
|---|---|---|
| Are cells shear-sensitive (mAb, plant cells)? | Use marine / elephant ear impeller at low N | Use Rushton for high O₂ demand |
| Is broth highly viscous / non-Newtonian? | Use helical / Intermig impeller | Use Rushton / PBT |
| Is fill fraction < 0.6? | Increase working vol. (underutilized) | Optimize H/D ratio |
| Is $P/V > 5$ kW/m³ needed? | Add 2nd impeller (Rushton + PBT) | Recheck mixing time |
| Is $C^* - C_L$ approaching zero? | Increase v_s or N | Add pure O₂ enrichment |
| Is broth foaming strongly? | Add mechanical foam breaker; chemical antifoam | Recheck sparger placement |
| Is k_La < OUR × 1.5? | **AERATION INADEQUATE** — redesign sparger/impeller | Design acceptable |
| Will batch time exceed 120 h? | Consider continuous / perfusion | Fed-batch is fine |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bioreactor Design Decision Tree (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Process Requirement] --> B{Define cell type and kinetics}
    B -- Aerobic bacteria --> C[STR / CSTR]
    B -- Filamentous fungi --> D[STR with PBT and low N]
    B -- Mammalian cells --> E[Single-use STR / Wave]
    B -- Anaerobic --> F[Anaerobic CSTR / Tower]
    C --> G{Volumetric scale calculation}
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H{Geometric proportions}
    H --> I[Choose H over D ratio]
    I --> J[Aeration and agitation design]
    J --> K[Calculate vs and P by V]
    K --> L{Mass transfer kLa check}
    L -- Adequate --> M[Heat transfer and MOC]
    L -- Inadequate --> N[Redesign: increase v_s or N]
    N --> J
    M --> O[Instrumentation and SIP CIP]
    O --> P[Final Design Package]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Bioreactor System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FeedBank [Feed and Inoculum Bank]
        A1[Media Prep Tank]
        A2[Inoculum Train]
        A3[pH and Sterile Buffers]
    end
    subgraph CoreReactor [Core Bioreactor Vessel]
        B1[Jacketed SS316L Vessel]
        B2[Impeller Assembly with 2 Rushton Turbines]
        B3[Ring Sparger Below Lower Impeller]
        B4[Baffles x 4]
    end
    subgraph Sensors [Instrumentation Suite]
        C1[pH Probe]
        C2[DO Probe Polarographic]
        C3[Temperature RTD]
        C4[Foam Capacitance Probe]
        C5[Load Cell for Mass]
        C6[Off Gas O2 and CO2]
    end
    subgraph Utilities [Utilities and Support]
        D1[Sterile Air Compressor and Filters]
        D2[Cooling Chiller and Heating Skid]
        D3[CIP and SIP Skid]
        D4[Exhaust Condenser]
    end
    subgraph Control [Control and PAT]
        E1[PID Loop Cascade]
        E2[SCADA and DCS]
        E3[Recipe Management]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B1
    D1 --> B3
    D2 --> B1
    B1 --> D4
    B1 --> B2
    B1 --> B4
    C1 --> E1
    C2 --> E1
    C3 --> E1
    C4 --> E1
    C5 --> E1
    C6 --> E1
    D3 --> B1
    E1 --> E2
    E2 --> E3
```

## 4.3 Design Heuristics Flow Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Reactor Sizing] --> Step1[Step 1: V_R from productivity]
    Step1 --> Step2[Step 2: D_T and H_T from aspect ratio]
    Step2 --> Step3[Step 3: Impeller selection and count]
    Step3 --> Step4[Step 4: Aeration Q_g and vs]
    Step4 --> Step5[Step 5: P and P by V]
    Step5 --> Step6[Step 6: kLa and OTR check]
    Step6 --> Step7[Step 7: Heat transfer Q and A_cool]
    Step7 --> Step8[Step 8: Sterilization F0]
    Step8 --> Step9[Step 9: MOC and surface finish]
    Step9 --> Step10[Step 10: Sensor suite and control]
    Step10 --> Final[Validated Design]
```

## 4.4 Scale-Up Method Selection Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Scale-up criterion] --> Q1{Is oxygen limiting?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> R1[Constant P by V]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is mixing time critical?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> R2[Constant tip speed]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Is shear critical?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> R3[Constant N_Re and Np]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Is heat transfer critical?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> R4[Constant U and A by V]
    Q4 -- No --> R5[Constant k_La]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: Define "Bioreactor Design Guidelines" and list four design considerations that distinguish a bioreactor from a chemical reactor.
**[Model Answer]:**

Bioreactor design guidelines are a codified set of engineering principles that govern the sizing, geometry, aeration, agitation, heat transfer, and material selection of a biological reactor, ensuring it provides the optimal microenvironment for living cells or enzymes to convert substrates into desired products with high yield, productivity, and safety.

**Four distinguishing design considerations:**

1. **Sterility** — Aseptic operation is mandatory because living catalysts are vulnerable to contamination; chemical reactors often tolerate impurities.
2. **Cell viability & shear sensitivity** — Microbial cells, mammalian cells, and filamentous fungi have strict upper limits on hydrodynamic shear, requiring careful impeller selection.
3. **Low operating temperature & pressure** — Most bioprocesses run at 25–37 °C and ≤ 2 bar, demanding gentle conditions.
4. **Complex, non-Newtonian broth rheology** — Fermentation broths change viscosity with cell density and product formation; bioreactors must be designed for this variability.

### Question A2: What is the significance of H_T/D_T ratio in bioreactor design? Mention typical values for standard stirred-tank reactors and airlift reactors.
**[Model Answer]:**

The H_T/D_T ratio (aspect ratio) determines the **hydrodynamics, mixing time, and gas–liquid mass transfer** in a reactor.

- A **high H_T/D_T** (> 3) gives longer gas residence time (better O₂ transfer) and lower floor area — typical of **airlift reactors (4–6)** and bubble columns used in SCP and wastewater treatment.
- A **moderate H_T/D_T (1.5–3.0)** provides balanced mixing and is typical of **standard stirred-tank reactors (STRs)** used for recombinant proteins and antibiotics.
- A **low H_T/D_T (< 1.5)** offers better mixing at low viscosity but poor gas utilization — typical of **mammalian cell bioreactors and rocking-wave systems**.

Choosing the ratio is a **trade-off** between mass transfer, mixing, footprint, and capital cost.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** Two alternative questions with internal choice pattern as per KTU 2024 ESE module structure.

---

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **step-by-step guidelines for the design of a stirred-tank bioreactor (STR)** for an aerobic recombinant *E. coli* fermentation. Cover volumetric scale-up, geometric proportions, aeration, agitation, mass transfer, heat transfer, and materials of construction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pilot STR with $D_T = 0.5$ m, $H_T = 1.0$ m, $D_i/D_T = 0.33$, two Rushton turbines, N = 4 rps, $P_o = 5.5$, $Q_g = 0.6$ vvm, broth ρ = 1,020 kg/m³, μ = 0.001 Pa·s. Calculate the volumetric power input $P/V$ (gassed), the superficial gas velocity, and verify that $k_La$ ≥ 200 h⁻¹ using Van't Riet correlation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

1. **Define biological brief** — μ_max, K_s, Y_X/S, OUR, heat evolution, product toxicity.
2. **Reactor mode** — batch/fed-batch for unstable plasmids; fed-batch is the default for *E. coli*.
3. **Volumetric scale** — $V_R = \dfrac{Q_{prod} \cdot Y_{P/S}}{r_P \cdot t_{cycle} \cdot \eta}$.
4. **Geometric proportions** — H_T/D_T = 1.5–2.5 for STR; liquid height = 0.7 H_T; D_i = D_T/3; impeller spacing = D_T; 4 baffles, B/T = 0.1.
5. **Aeration** — choose sparger type (porous for low v_s, orifice for high v_s); v_s = 0.01–0.05 m/s; Q_g = 0.5–1.5 vvm.
6. **Agitation** — 2–4 Rushton for high OTR, or 1 Rushton + 1 PBT for dual function; N from mixing-time criteria; P/V = 1–5 kW/m³.
7. **Mass transfer** — $k_La$ correlation: $k_La = K(P_g/V)^{\alpha} v_s^{\beta}$; verify OTR ≥ 1.5 × OUR.
8. **Heat transfer** — metabolic heat = 0.48 J/mmol O₂; jacket or coil area from $A = Q/(U \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD})$.
9. **Sterilization** — SIP at 121 °C × 30 min; F₀ ≥ 15 min; 0.2 μm sterile air filter.
10. **MOC** — SS-316L, Ra ≤ 0.4 μm, EPDM gaskets, PTFE seals.
11. **Instrumentation** — pH, DO, T, foam, level, weight, off-gas O₂/CO₂.
12. **Validation** — FAT, SAT, IQ, OQ, PQ, media hold tests.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:**
- D_T = 0.5 m, H_T = 1.0 m, D_i = 0.33 × 0.5 = 0.165 m
- N = 4 rps, P_o = 5.5, ρ = 1,020 kg/m³, μ = 0.001 Pa·s
- Q_g = 0.6 vvm, fill = 0.8 → V_L = 0.8 × V_T

**Step 1 — Vessel volume and working volume:**

$$V_T = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.5)^2 \times 1.0 = 0.1963 \text{ m}^3$$

$$V_L = 0.8 \times 0.1963 = 0.1571 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Volumetric gas flow:**

$$Q_g = 0.6 \times 0.1571 = 0.0943 \text{ m}^3/\text{min} = 1.571 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Superficial gas velocity:**

$$v_s = \frac{4 Q_g}{\pi D_T^2} = \frac{4 \times 1.571 \times 10^{-3}}{\pi \times 0.25} = 8.00 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Impeller Reynolds number:**

$$N_{Re} = \frac{1020 \times 4 \times (0.165)^2}{0.001} = 111{,}080 \gg 10{,}000 \rightarrow \text{fully turbulent}$$

**Step 5 — Ungassed power:**

$$P_{ungassed} = 5.5 \times 1020 \times 4^3 \times (0.165)^5$$

$$P_{ungassed} = 5.5 \times 1020 \times 64 \times 1.92 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$P_{ungassed} = 690 \text{ W}$$

**Step 6 — Gassed power (Hassan-Whitman, C = 0.85):**

$$P_g = 690 \times \left(1 - 0.85 \times \frac{1.571 \times 10^{-3}}{4 \times 0.1571}\right)$$

$$P_g = 690 \times (1 - 0.85 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-3}) = 690 \times 0.9979 = 688.5 \text{ W}$$

**Step 7 — Volumetric power input:**

$$\frac{P}{V} = \frac{688.5}{0.1571} = 4{,}383 \text{ W/m}^3 \approx 4.38 \text{ kW/m}^3$$

**Step 8 — k_La (Van't Riet for coalescing):**

$$k_La = 0.026 \times (4383)^{0.4} \times (0.008)^{0.5}$$

$$k_La = 0.026 \times 18.06 \times 0.0894 = 0.0419 \text{ s}^{-1} = 151 \text{ h}^{-1}$$

**Interpretation:** 151 h⁻¹ < 200 h⁻¹ required → **k_La is INADEQUATE**.

**Remedy:** Increase N from 4 to ~4.7 rps, or increase v_s by increasing Q_g to 0.9 vvm. The student should explicitly state the design is inadequate and propose a corrective action.

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the **role of aeration and agitation** in a bioreactor. Derive the **superficial gas velocity** and discuss the relationship between **$k_La$, $P/V$, and $v_s$** for scale-up. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A production STR has $D_T = 3$ m, $H_T = 6$ m, two Rushton + one PBT impeller (D_i = 1 m), N = 1.5 rps, $P_o = 5.5$ (Rushton), gassed $P/V = 1.8$ kW/m³, v_s = 0.03 m/s, OUR = 12 mmol O₂/L·h, $C_L$ setpoint = 2 mg/L = 0.0625 mmol/L, $C^* = 0.234$ mmol/L. Verify the aeration adequacy and find the **margin of safety** in OTR/OUR. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Role of aeration:** Supplies O₂ for aerobic metabolism and removes CO₂ and volatile byproducts. Operates through spargers (porous, orifice, nozzle) at 0.5–1.5 vvm.

**Role of agitation:** Promotes bulk mixing, gas dispersion, heat transfer, and suspension of cells. Provided by Rushton (radial flow, high shear) or PBT (axial flow, low shear).

**Derivation of superficial gas velocity:**

Gas volumetric flow Q_g (m³/s) entering a vessel of cross-section A = π D_T²/4.

$$\boxed{v_s = \frac{Q_g}{A} = \frac{4 Q_g}{\pi D_T^2}}$$

**$k_La$ relationship (Van't Riet 1979):**

$$k_L a = K \cdot \left(\frac{P}{V}\right)^{\alpha} \cdot v_s^{\beta}$$

- Coalescing systems (air-water): K = 0.026, α = 0.4, β = 0.5
- Non-coalescing (electrolytes): K = 0.002, α = 0.7, β = 0.2

**Scale-up implications:** Maintaining constant $P/V$ keeps $k_La$ similar across scales if v_s is also held constant; however, $P/V$ increases with scale-up due to higher $D_i$ values, often requiring multi-impeller shafts.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Compute k_La (coalescing, typical for production broth):**

$$\frac{P}{V} = 1.8 \text{ kW/m}^3 = 1{,}800 \text{ W/m}^3$$

$$k_La = 0.026 \times (1800)^{0.4} \times (0.03)^{0.5}$$

$$k_La = 0.026 \times 12.05 \times 0.1732 = 0.0543 \text{ s}^{-1} = 195 \text{ h}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — OTR:**

$$OTR = k_La \cdot (C^* - C_L) = 0.0543 \times (0.234 - 0.0625) = 0.0543 \times 0.1715$$

$$OTR = 9.31 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mmol/L/s} = 33.5 \text{ mmol/L/h}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with OUR:**

$$\text{Margin} = \frac{OTR}{OUR} = \frac{33.5}{12} = 2.79$$

**Interpretation:** OTR/OUR ≈ 2.8, which exceeds the recommended safety factor of 1.5. ✓ **Design is adequate with healthy safety margin (180% spare capacity)**. This means the system can handle spikes in OUR up to ~33.5 mmol/L/h before becoming oxygen-limited.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose 1–3 Marks Each:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency** — Mixing vvm with m³/s, or s⁻¹ with h⁻¹. Always convert to SI (s, m, kg) before computing. *Loss: 1 mark per occurrence.*
> 2. **Forgetting to Subtract C_L from C^** — OTR = k_La × (C^* − C_L), NOT C^* alone. *Loss: 1 mark.*
> 3. **Using P_o for gassed power directly** — Always apply Hassan-Whitman (or Michel-Miller) correction for gas flow. *Loss: 2 marks.*
> 4. **Wrong number of impellers in P/V** — Multi-impeller: total P is sum, but P/V uses V_L. *Loss: 1 mark.*
> 5. **Mixing fill fraction with working volume** — V_L = 0.7–0.8 V_T, never V_T directly. *Loss: 1 mark.*
> 6. **Forgetting to verify k_La ≥ 1.5 × OUR/ΔC* — k_La alone is meaningless without the OUR check. *Loss: 2 marks.*
> 7. **Omitting H_T/D_T justification** — Always state why the chosen aspect ratio fits the process. *Loss: 1 mark.*

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 4, "Guidelines for Bioreactor Design"**

- [x] **Bioreactor design is multi-objective** — must balance mixing, mass transfer, shear, sterility, and economics simultaneously.
- [x] **The 10-step design workflow**: biological brief → mode selection → volume sizing → geometry → aeration → agitation → mass transfer → heat transfer → sterilization → MOC.
- [x] **Standard aspect ratio (H_T/D_T)**: 1.5–3.0 for STR, 4–6 for airlift, < 1.5 for mammalian cell reactors.
- [x] **Working volume = 70–80% of total vessel volume** — always include headspace for foam disengagement.
- [x] **v_s = 0.01–0.05 m/s** for STRs (above 0.05 m/s → flooding).
- [x] **Q_g = 0.5–1.5 vvm** for typical aerobic fermentations.
- [x] **D_i/D_T ≈ 0.33** for Rushton turbines.
- [x] **P/V = 1–5 kW/m³** for industrial aerobic, 10–25 for high-cell-density.
- [x] **Power number (N_p)**: 5.5 (Rushton, turbulent), 1.3 (PBT 45°), 0.35 (marine propeller).
- [x] **Gassed power < Ungassed power** — apply Hassan-Whitman correction with C ≈ 0.85 for Rushton.
- [x] **Van't Riet correlation** for k_La: K = 0.026, α = 0.4, β = 0.5 (coalescing); K = 0.002, α = 0.7, β = 0.2 (non-coalescing).
- [x] **Design rule:** OTR ≥ 1.5 × OUR (safety margin).
- [x] **Metabolic heat = 0.48 J/mmol O₂** for glucose oxidation.
- [x] **Cooling U-values**: jacket 500–1500 W/m²·K, internal coil 1500–3000 W/m²·K.
- [x] **SS-316L** is the standard MOC; surface finish Ra ≤ 0.4 μm for pharma.
- [x] **No dead legs** (L/D ≤ 2) and **0.2 μm sterile air filter** for sterility assurance.
- [x] **Minimum sensor suite**: pH, DO, T, foam, level, weight, off-gas O₂/CO₂.
- [x] **Scale-up criteria** depend on the rate-limiting step — P/V, k_La, tip speed, or mixing time.
- [x] **SIP** = 121 °C × 30 min (F₀ ≥ 15 min); **CIP** = 1–2% NaOH at 80 °C.
- [x] **cGMP / FDA / WHO** compliance is mandatory for pharmaceutical bioreactors.
- [x] **Mixing time** decreases with P/V (∝ (P/V)^(−1/3)) and increases with D_T/D_i.
- [x] **Shear-sensitive cells** (mAb, plant cells) prefer marine / elephant ear impellers at low N.
- [x] **Single-use bioreactors** dominate mammalian cell culture up to 2,000 L (rocking, SUBs).
- [x] **Airlift and bubble columns** are best for shear-sensitive or large-volume SCP production.
- [x] **Bubble-free aeration** (membrane, silicone tubing) used for animal cell culture.
- [x] **Cost share** in a typical bioreactor: vessel 30%, instrumentation 25%, utilities 20%, automation 15%, validation 10%.
- [x] **The 80%-fill rule**, **1/3 impeller ratio**, **1.5× safety factor on OTR**, and **0.48 J/mmol O₂ heat of reaction** are the four numbers a KTU topper must know cold.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bioreactor Vessels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT526 - BIOREACTOR DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bioreactor Vessels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Bioreactor Vessels — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **bioreactor vessel** (also termed a *fermenter vessel* or *bioreactor body*) is the **primary containment hardware** of a bioprocess system. It is a closed, sterilizable, pressure-rated, jacketed or coiled, geometrically defined container engineered to:
- Provide a **controlled microenvironment** (temperature, pH, dissolved oxygen, shear) for living cells or enzymes.
- Withstand **thermal cycling** associated with steam sterilization (typically **121 °C at 15 psig = 1.034 bar gauge**).
- Resist **process-side corrosion** from broth components, acids, alkalis, and cleaning agents.
- Allow **aseptic addition and removal** of gases, liquids, and solids via defined ports.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Snapshot**
> A bioreactor vessel is the *enclosure* — it is distinct from the *agitator*, *sparger*, *baffles*, and *instrumentation*, which are components mounted **on or in** the vessel. Always describe the vessel as the containment hardware with its **working volume (V_w)** and **total volume (V_t)** clearly distinguished.

The vessel is normally sized such that:

$$V_w \le 0.75 \, V_t \text{ (for stirred-tank reactors)}$$

The unoccupied headspace (≈ 20–25 % of V_t) is required to:
- Accommodate foam and antifoam dynamics.
- Provide disengagement zone for exit gas.
- Allow head-pressure regulation and sensor access.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"Kitchen Vessel" Analogy**
> Imagine a *pressure cooker* (like a typical Indian 5-litre stainless-steel pressure cooker used in households) — but imagine scaling it up to a 50,000-litre industrial version, and putting a *living, breathing culture* of bacteria inside, with a continuous supply of air bubbles rising from the bottom, paddles stirring gently, temperature jackets circulating hot/cold water, and sensors continuously reporting pH and oxygen. The **vessel is the cooker body** — the rest (paddles, gas pipe, temperature jacket) are accessories. Just as the cooker body must:
> - Not leak steam at high pressure,
> - Not rust when filled with hot sour food,
> - Be cleanable from inside,
> - Have a tightly sealing lid with safety valves,
>
> the **bioreactor vessel** is the engineered industrial counterpart of that pressure cooker.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

The following reference values are **KTU exam-favourite constants** — memorise them:

| Metric | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Sterilization temperature | **121 °C** |
| Sterilization pressure | **1 atm gauge ≈ 15 psi ≈ 1.034 bar** |
| Hold time (typical) | **15–20 min** |
| Design pressure (vessel) | **2–3 barg (commonly 2.5 barg)** |
| Design temperature | **150–160 °C** |
| H/D ratio (stirred tank) | **1.0 – 3.0** (commonly 2.0) |
| Working fillage | **0.6 – 0.8** of V_t |
| Material of construction | **SS 304 / SS 316 / SS 316L** |
| Surface finish (Ra) | **≤ 0.4 – 0.8 µm (electropolished)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SS 316L** (the 'L' = low carbon, ≤ 0.03 % C) is the pharmaceutical-grade standard because the low carbon content prevents *sensitisation* (chromium carbide precipitation at grain boundaries) during welding — a critical issue that causes intergranular corrosion.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Bioreactor Vessels

### 2.1.1 By Mechanical Agitation
- **Stirred-Tank Reactor (STR)** — mechanical impeller (Rushton, pitched-blade, marine propeller).
- **Unstirred / Pneumatically agitated** — bubble column, airlift, loop reactor.

### 2.1.2 By Mode of Operation
- **Batch**
- **Fed-batch**
- **Continuous (CSTR / PFR / Chemostat)**

### 2.1.3 By Containment
- **Single-use / Disposable** (e.g., **SUB** – Single-Use Bioreactor) — pre-sterilised polymer bag inside a steel cage.
- **Multi-use / Reusable** — stainless-steel, steam-in-place (SIP).

### 2.1.4 By Geometry
- **Cylindrical with flat/elliptical/dished heads** (meets ASME code).
- **Rectangular** (rare; used in some photobioreactors).
- **Annular / Concentric-tube** (e.g., hollow-fibre, airlift draft-tube).

## 2.2 Vessel Geometry — The H/D Ratio

The **height-to-diameter ratio** is the single most important geometric descriptor of an STR vessel. Its choice depends on:

| Process requirement | Preferred H/D |
|---|---|
| Aerobic high-cell-density culture | **2.0 – 3.0** (taller column → longer gas residence) |
| Anaerobic / low-shear | **1.0 – 1.5** (squat tank) |
| Shear-sensitive mammalian cells | **1.0 – 1.5** (larger cross-section → lower impeller tip speed) |

For multi-impeller systems, each impeller is positioned roughly at a spacing of:

$$\frac{D_i}{3} \text{ to } \frac{D_i}{2}$$

where $D_i$ is the impeller diameter.

## 2.3 Vessel Components — The Anatomy

A typical STR consists of:

1. **Shell** — cylindrical body (corrosion-resistant, electropolished SS 316L).
2. **Heads (top & bottom)** — dished, elliptical, or conical. ASME standard *torispherical* heads are the most common.
3. **Jacket (or coil)** — for heat transfer. Dimple jackets or half-pipe coils are preferred over conventional jackets for high-pressure service.
4. **Baffles** — 4 flat vertical strips, width ≈ T/10, mounted off-wall (≈ 10–20 mm gap) to prevent vortexing and improve mixing.
5. **Impeller shaft & seal** — top-drive (more hygienic) or bottom-drive (lower shaft length).
6. **Sparger** — ring sparger, porous sparger, or jet sparger for gas dispersion.
7. **Ports** — for probes (pH, DO, temperature, level), sampling, harvest, feed, inoculum, vent, steam-in, CIP.
8. **Mechanical seal / Magnetic coupling** — prevents contamination ingress via the agitator shaft.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why baffles are *off-wall*?** A direct wall-mounting creates a stagnant zone behind the baffle where mycelial or biofilm growth can occur. The 10–20 mm offset forces a controlled tangential flow (toroidal vortex breakdown), creating a *Krebs* or *central vortex* that improves gas–liquid mass transfer.

## 2.4 Heat Transfer — The Jacket Design

Heat removal in a bioreactor is critical because microbial metabolism is highly exothermic (e.g., yeast on glucose: ≈ −100 kJ/mol). The dominant heat-transfer equation is:

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$$

where:
- $Q$ = heat duty (W)
- $U$ = overall heat-transfer coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $A$ = heat-transfer area (m²)
- $\Delta T_{LMTD}$ = log-mean temperature difference (K)

For jacketed vessels, typical **U values**:

| Service | U (W/m²·K) |
|---|---|
| Water-to-broth (cleaning jacket) | 1000 – 1500 |
| Steam-to-broth (during SIP) | 1500 – 2500 |
| Glycol-to-broth (cooling) | 300 – 700 |
| Dimple jacket (steam) | 2000 – 3500 |

The **cooling duty during peak metabolism** is given by:

$$Q_{peak} = Y_{Q/O_2} \cdot OUR_{max} \cdot V_L$$

where:
- $Y_{Q/O_2}$ = **heat yield per oxygen consumed** (≈ **460 kJ/mol O₂** for aerobic culture)
- $OUR_{max}$ = maximum oxygen uptake rate (mol O₂/m³·s)
- $V_L$ = liquid working volume (m³)

This equation is one of the **most-frequently asked KTU numericals**.

## 2.5 Sterilization Design — SIP vs Autoclave

For a vessel sterilized *in situ* (SIP), the empirical **Del factor** (or *lethality* concept) is used:

$$\nabla = \int_0^t 10^{(T(t) - T_{ref})/z} \, dt$$

Reference values for *Bacillus stearothermophilus* spores (the SIP bioindicator):

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| $T_{ref}$ | **121.1 °C** |
| $z$ | **10 °C** |
| $F_0$ required | **≥ 15 min** (pharma) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Aseptic processing requires achieving an **F_0 ≥ 15 minutes at the coldest point** in the vessel. The *coldest point* is normally the *bottom dished head* or *thermowell tip* — KTU questions often test this.

## 2.6 Mechanical Design — Pressure Vessel Equations

Since the vessel is a pressure vessel, it is designed as per **ASME BPVC Section VIII Div. 1**. For a thin-walled cylindrical shell:

$$t = \frac{P \cdot R}{S \cdot E - 0.6 P}$$

where:
- $t$ = required wall thickness (mm)
- $P$ = internal design pressure (MPa)
- $R$ = inside radius (mm)
- $S$ = allowable stress for SS 316L at design temperature (MPa)
- $E$ = joint efficiency (1.0 for seamless; 0.85 for spot-examined welded)

For **torispherical heads**:

$$t_h = \frac{P \cdot L \cdot M}{2 \cdot S \cdot E - 0.2 P}$$

where $L$ = crown radius, $M$ = factor depending on $L/r$ ratio (from ASME tables).

## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — these equations recur in KTU PYQs.**

| Equation | Purpose | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $V_w = f \cdot V_t$ | Working volume from fillage fraction $f$ | m³ |
| $H/D$ | Vessel aspect ratio | dimensionless |
| $N_{Re} = \frac{\rho N D_i^2}{\mu}$ | Impeller Reynolds number | dimensionless |
| $N_{Po} = \frac{P}{\rho N^3 D_i^5}$ | Power number (Rushton ≈ 5–6) | dimensionless |
| $P = N_{Po} \cdot \rho \cdot N^3 D_i^5 \cdot V_L$ | Power input by impeller | W |
| $k_L a = K \cdot \left(\frac{P_g}{V_L}\right)^{\alpha} \cdot v_s^{\beta}$ | Van't Riet correlation for $k_L a$ | s⁻¹ |
| $Q = U A \Delta T_{LMTD}$ | Heat transfer rate | W |
| $Q_{peak} = Y_{Q/O_2} \cdot OUR_{max} \cdot V_L$ | Cooling duty | W |
| $\nabla = \int 10^{(T - T_{ref})/z} dt$ | Del-factor lethality | min |
| $t = \frac{P R}{S E - 0.6 P}$ | Cylindrical-shell thickness (ASME) | mm |
| $\sigma = \frac{P D}{2 t}$ | Hoop stress in shell | MPa |
| $\Delta P_{sterile} = \rho_l g H - \rho_g g H$ | Hydrostatic head minus gas buoyancy | Pa |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical restriction notice** — never use the absolute-value pipe $\vert \cdot \vert$ inside the KTU table; use $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ or $\mid \cdot \mid$ to prevent markdown-table breakage.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Vessel application |
|---|---|
| Pharma (vaccines, mAbs) | SS 316L STRs, 1000–25000 L, cGMP, SIP |
| Industrial enzymes | Larger STRs, 50–200 m³ |
| Brewing & beverage | Cylindro-conical fermenters (CCF) |
| Wastewater / biogas | Concrete, glass-fused-to-steel anaerobic digesters |
| Cell & gene therapy | Single-use bioreactors (S.U.B.), 50–2000 L polymer bags |
| Cultivated meat | Large SS STRs with perfusion + cell retention devices |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Power Number → Gassed Power → Volumetric Mass-Transfer Coefficient

This is the **anchor derivation** of bioreactor scale-up. The KTU board expects you to walk through the chain that links *impeller speed* to *oxygen transfer rate*.

### Step 1 — Impeller power (ungassed)

For a Newtonian fluid in a fully baffled tank (no vortex):

$$P = N_{Po} \cdot \rho_l \cdot N^3 \cdot D_i^5$$

**Text-row explanation:** The power number $N_{Po}$ is a *geometric* constant (depends on impeller type, baffling, vessel geometry). For a standard 4-baffle Rushton turbine in a standard tank, $N_{Po} \approx 5.0$ in the turbulent regime ($N_{Re} > 10^4$).

### Step 2 — Aeration reduces power (gassed power)

When gas is sparged, the effective density in the impeller region drops, reducing power. **Van't Riet (1973)** proposed:

$$\frac{P_g}{P_0} = 0.31 \cdot \left[ \frac{(Q_g / N V_L)^{0.67}}{N_{Po}^{0.25}} \right]^{0.27}$$

For practical use, the simpler Michel-Miller correlation is often used:

$$\frac{P_g}{P_0} = 0.497 \cdot \left( \frac{Q_g}{N V_L} \right)^{0.038} \cdot \left( \frac{N^2 D_i^4}{g \cdot W \cdot V_L^{0.67}} \right)^{-0.19}$$

**Text-row explanation:** $Q_g$ is the gas volumetric flow rate; $W$ is the impeller blade width. The drop in power is typically 20–50 % when aerating.

### Step 3 — Volumetric mass-transfer coefficient

Combining impeller power and superficial gas velocity, the **Van't Riet (1979) coalescing** correlation:

$$k_L a = K \cdot \left( \frac{P_g}{V_L} \right)^{0.7} \cdot v_s^{0.2}$$

**Text-row explanation:** This empirical relation is the basis of scale-up: keeping $P_g / V_L$ and $v_s$ constant between scales preserves $k_L a$ — and therefore OTR.

### Worked Numerical — Sample KTU-Style 14-Mark Problem

> **Problem statement:** A 5 m³ stirred-tank bioreactor is operated at H/D = 2, with two Rushton turbines ($D_i = 0.45 \, D$, $W = 0.20 \, D$). The broth has $\rho = 1000 \, kg/m^3$, $\mu = 1 \times 10^{-3} \, Pa \cdot s$, working fillage $f = 0.7$. Aeration rate is 1 vvm. Agitator speed is 180 rpm. Compute (a) $N_{Re}$, (b) $P_0$, (c) $P_g$, (d) $k_L a$ for coalescing broth.

#### Step (a) — Impeller Reynolds number

Tank diameter: $V_t = V_w / f = 5 / 0.7 = 7.143 \, m^3$.

$$D = \left(\frac{4 V_t}{\pi \cdot (H/D)}\right)^{1/3} = \left(\frac{4 \times 7.143}{\pi \times 2}\right)^{1/3}$$

$$D = (4.549)^{1/3} \approx 1.654 \, m$$

$$D_i = 0.45 \times 1.654 = 0.744 \, m$$

$$N = 180 \, rpm = 3.0 \, s^{-1}$$

$$N_{Re} = \frac{\rho N D_i^2}{\mu} = \frac{1000 \times 3.0 \times 0.744^2}{0.001}$$

$$N_{Re} = \frac{1000 \times 3.0 \times 0.554}{0.001} = 1.66 \times 10^6$$

**Conclusion:** $N_{Re} \gg 10^4$ → **fully turbulent regime**. Power number for Rushton: $N_{Po} = 5.0$.

#### Step (b) — Ungassed power

$$P_0 = N_{Po} \cdot \rho \cdot N^3 D_i^5$$

$$P_0 = 5.0 \times 1000 \times 3.0^3 \times 0.744^5$$

$$0.744^5 = 0.228$$

$$P_0 = 5.0 \times 1000 \times 27.0 \times 0.228 = 30{,}780 \, W \approx 30.8 \, kW$$

#### Step (c) — Gassed power

$Q_g = 1 \, vvm \times V_w = 1 \times 5 = 5 \, m^3/min = 0.0833 \, m^3/s$

Using Michel-Miller:

$$\frac{P_g}{P_0} = 0.497 \cdot \left( \frac{Q_g}{N V_L} \right)^{0.038} \cdot \left( \frac{N^2 D_i^4}{g W V_L^{0.67}} \right)^{-0.19}$$

Compute piece by piece:

$$\frac{Q_g}{N V_L} = \frac{0.0833}{3.0 \times 5} = 0.00555$$

$$\left( 0.00555 \right)^{0.038} = 0.866$$

$$W = 0.20 \times 1.654 = 0.331 \, m$$

$$V_L^{0.67} = 5^{0.67} = 3.085$$

$$N^2 D_i^4 = 3.0^2 \times 0.744^4 = 9.0 \times 0.306 = 2.756$$

$$g \cdot W \cdot V_L^{0.67} = 9.81 \times 0.331 \times 3.085 = 10.02$$

$$\frac{2.756}{10.02} = 0.2751$$

$$\left( 0.2751 \right)^{-0.19} = 1.226$$

$$\frac{P_g}{P_0} = 0.497 \times 0.866 \times 1.226 = 0.527$$

$$P_g = 0.527 \times 30{,}780 = 16{,}221 \, W \approx 16.2 \, kW$$

#### Step (d) — Volumetric mass-transfer coefficient

For coalescing broth, $K = 0.026$, exponents as above:

$$k_L a = 0.026 \cdot \left( \frac{P_g}{V_L} \right)^{0.7} \cdot v_s^{0.2}$$

$$\frac{P_g}{V_L} = \frac{16{,}221}{5} = 3244 \, W/m^3$$

$$\left( 3244 \right)^{0.7} = 247.5$$

$v_s$ = superficial gas velocity. Cross-section area of tank:

$$A_{cs} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 1.654^2}{4} = 2.149 \, m^2$$

$$v_s = \frac{Q_g}{A_{cs}} = \frac{0.0833}{2.149} = 0.0388 \, m/s$$

$$\left( 0.0388 \right)^{0.2} = 0.521$$

$$k_L a = 0.026 \times 247.5 \times 0.521 = 3.35 \, s^{-1} = 1210 \, h^{-1}$$

**Conclusion:** $k_L a = 3.35 \times 10^{-3} \, s^{-1}$ — typical range for a well-aerated industrial STR.

## 3.2 Derivation: Cooling-Jacket Area from Peak Metabolism

A yeast culture has $OUR_{max} = 12 \, mmol \, O_2 / L / h$ at peak respiration. Working volume 5 m³. Compute cooling jacket area for ΔT_LMTD = 10 K with U = 600 W/m²·K.

### Step 1 — Convert OUR to SI

$$OUR_{max} = 12 \times 10^{-3} \, mol/L/h = 0.012 \, mol/m^3 \times 3600 = 43.2 \, mol/m^3/h$$

Wait — recheck: 12 mmol = 0.012 mol per L per h.

$$= 0.012 \times 1000 \, L/m^3 \, mol/L/h = 12 \, mol/m^3/h$$

### Step 2 — Peak heat duty

$$Q_{peak} = Y_{Q/O_2} \times OUR_{max} \times V_L$$

$$Q_{peak} = 460 \times 10^3 \, J/mol \times 12 \, mol/m^3/h \times 5 \, m^3$$

$$Q_{peak} = 460{,}000 \times 60 = 2.76 \times 10^7 \, J/h = 7666 \, W$$

### Step 3 — Required jacket area

$$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}} = \frac{7666}{600 \times 10} = 1.28 \, m^2$$

**Conclusion:** A 5 m³ fermenter needs only ~1.3 m² of jacket — usually provided comfortably by a half-pipe coil or a full dimple jacket wrapped around the cylindrical shell.

## 3.3 Derivation: Sterilization Del-Factor for SIP

A vessel is heated from 90 °C to 121 °C over 8 minutes (linear ramp) and held at 121 °C for 20 minutes, then cooled linearly to 90 °C in 12 minutes. Compute total lethality $F_0$.

### Step 1 — Heating leg

$$F_{0,heat} = \int_0^{480} 10^{(T(t) - 121.1)/10} \, dt$$

T(t) = 90 + (121 − 90)(t/480) = 90 + 0.0646 t

$$F_{0,heat} = \int_0^{480} 10^{(90 + 0.0646t - 121.1)/10} \, dt = \int_0^{480} 10^{-3.11 + 0.00646 t} \, dt$$

Let $a = 10^{-3.11}/(0.00646 \cdot \ln 10)$:

$$F_{0,heat} = \frac{10^{-3.11}}{0.00646 \cdot \ln 10} \left( 10^{0.00646 \times 480} - 1 \right)$$

$$10^{-3.11} = 7.76 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$10^{3.10} = 1258.9$$

$$F_{0,heat} = \frac{7.76 \times 10^{-4}}{0.01487} \times (1258.9 - 1) = 0.0522 \times 1257.9 = 65.7 \, min$$

### Step 2 — Hold leg

$$F_{0,hold} = 20 \times 10^{(121 - 121.1)/10} = 20 \times 10^{-0.01} = 20 \times 0.977 = 19.5 \, min$$

### Step 3 — Cooling leg (analogous, symmetric)

$$F_{0,cool} \approx 32.8 \, min \text{ (by symmetry of ramp)}$$

### Step 4 — Total

$$F_0 = 65.7 + 19.5 + 32.8 \approx 118 \, min$$

**Conclusion:** $F_0 = 118 \gg 15$ — well above the aseptic limit; the cycle is more than sufficient.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Vessel Selection

| Criterion | STR (SS) | Airlift | Bubble Column | Single-Use (SUB) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cell-density suitability | All ranges | Low–medium | Low | Medium–high |
| Shear tolerance | Low (Rushton) → High (marine) | High | High | Adjustable |
| Sterilization | SIP | SIP | SIP | Pre-sterilised γ |
| Capital cost | High | Medium | Low | Low (recurring consumable) |
| Scale-up ease | Well-established | Moderate | Easy | Limited (≤ 2 m³) |
| cGMP compliance | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent | Good (validation) |
| Turnaround | Slow (SIP) | Slow | Slow | Fast (no SIP) |
