```mermaid
    K -- No --> M[Use Np vs Re chart]
    L --> N[Apply Aeration Correction]
    M --> N
    N --> O{Aerated System}

    O -- No --> Q[P equals Np rho N cubed Di power 5]
    P --> R[Final Power P in W]
    Q --> R
    R --> S[End: Motor Sizing]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Define the Power Number. State its physical significance in bioreactor design.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Power Number** $N_p$ is a dimensionless group defined as:

$$N_p = \frac{P}{\rho N^3 D_i^5}$$

**Physical significance:**
1. It represents the **ratio of resistance force to inertia force** acting on the impeller.
2. It is a **measure of energy dissipation per impeller stroke**; a higher $N_p$ means more drag.
3. It is **geometry-dependent** — Rushton turbines (~5.5) consume more power than pitched blades (~1.3) at the same $Re$.
4. It allows **scale-up** of agitator design across different fluid/size combinations.

**Examiner's Allocation:** Definition — 1 mark; any three significance points — 2 marks.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Distinguish between gassed and ungassed power consumption in an agitated fermenter.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Ungassed Power (P) | Gassed Power (Pg) |
|--------|-------------------|-------------------|
| Aeration | Absent | Air sparged through sparger |
| Power | Higher | Lower (typically 30–60 % less) |
| Reason | Liquid-only drag on impeller | Gas-liquid mixture has lower effective density |
| Correlation | $P = N_p \rho N^3 D_i^5$ | $P_g / P = f(Na)$ or $f(Q_g / N V)$ |
| Common use | Initial design, mixing, scale-up | Actual fermentation operation |

**Examiner's Allocation:** Two distinguishing features — 2 marks; one supporting sentence — 1 mark.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice: Question A OR Question B)

### **Question A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With the help of Buckingham's π-theorem, derive the **dimensionless groups** governing power consumption in an agitated vessel. Show that $N_p = f(Re, Fr)$. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

1. **List variables:** $P,\ \rho,\ \mu,\ N,\ D_i,\ g$ → 6 variables, 3 dimensions (M, L, T).

2. **Number of π-groups:** $k = 6 - 3 = 3$.

3. **Choose repeating variables:** $\rho,\ N,\ D_i$.

4. **Form π₁ containing P:**

$$\pi_1 = \frac{P}{\rho^a N^b D_i^c}$$

   Setting $[P] = [\rho]^a [N]^b [D_i]^c$:
   - M: $1 = a \Rightarrow a = 1$
   - T: $-3 = -b \Rightarrow b = 3$
   - L: $2 = -3 + 0 + c \Rightarrow c = 5$

   $$\boxed{\pi_1 = N_p = \frac{P}{\rho N^3 D_i^5}}$$

5. **Form π₂ containing μ:**

$$\pi_2 = \frac{\mu}{\rho^a N^b D_i^c}$$

   Solving: $a = 1$, $b = 1$, $c = 2$.

   $$\boxed{\pi_2 = \frac{1}{Re} = \frac{\mu}{\rho N D_i^2}}$$

6. **Form π₃ containing g:**

$$\pi_3 = \frac{g}{\rho^a N^b D_i^c}$$

   Solving: $a = 0$, $b = 2$, $c = 1$.

   $$\boxed{\pi_3 = \frac{1}{Fr} = \frac{g}{N^2 D_i}}$$

7. **Combine:** $N_p = f(1/Re, 1/Fr)$ ⟹ $\boxed{N_p = f(Re, Fr)}$.

**Examiner's Allocation:** Variable list + n=3 — 2 marks; π₁ derivation — 2 marks; π₂ and π₃ derivations — 2 marks; final functional relation — 1 mark.

---

**(b)** An aerated fermenter of diameter $D_T = 2.0$ m is fitted with a single Rushton impeller of diameter $D_i = 0.667$ m (so $D_T / D_i = 3$). The impeller speed is $N = 1.67$ rev/s. The broth density is $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³ and viscosity is $\mu = 0.001$ Pa·s. The aeration rate is $Q_g = 0.005$ m³/s and broth volume is $V = 4.2$ m³. Calculate the **gassed power** using the **Hughmark correlation**. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Calculate Reynolds number (assumed fully turbulent — verify).

$$Re = \frac{\rho N D_i^2}{\mu} = \frac{1000 \times 1.67 \times (0.667)^2}{0.001}$$

$$Re = \frac{1000 \times 1.67 \times 0.4449}{0.001} = 7.43 \times 10^5$$

Since $Re > 10^4$, flow is **fully turbulent**, so $N_p \approx 5.5$ (Rushton, $D_T/D_i = 3$, baffled).

**Step 2:** Calculate ungassed power.

$$P = N_p \rho N^3 D_i^5 = 5.5 \times 1000 \times (1.67)^3 \times (0.667)^5$$

$$P = 5.5 \times 1000 \times 4.657 \times 0.1317$$

$$P = 5.5 \times 613.3 = 3373 \text{ W} \approx 3.37 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the aeration parameter.

$$\frac{Q_g}{N V} = \frac{0.005}{1.67 \times 4.2} = \frac{0.005}{7.014} = 7.13 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 4:** Apply Hughmark correlation.

$$\frac{P_g}{P} = 0.10 \left(\frac{Q_g}{N V}\right)^{-0.25}$$

$$\frac{P_g}{P} = 0.10 \times (7.13 \times 10^{-4})^{-0.25}$$

First compute $(7.13 \times 10^{-4})^{-0.25}$:
$$\ln(7.13 \times 10^{-4}) = -7.245$$
$$-0.25 \times (-7.245) = 1.811$$
$$e^{1.811} = 6.116$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{P_g}{P} = 0.10 \times 6.116 = 0.6116$$

**Step 5:** Calculate gassed power.

$$P_g = 0.6116 \times 3373 = 2062 \text{ W} \approx 2.06 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{P_g \approx 2.06 \text{ kW}}$$

**Examiner's Allocation:** Re check + Np identification — 2 marks; ungassed power — 2 marks; aeration parameter — 1 mark; Hughmark application — 1 mark; final gassed power — 1 mark.

---

### **Question B. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Discuss the **factors affecting power consumption** in an agitated bioreactor. Explain how **scale-up** is performed on the basis of equal power per unit volume. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Factors affecting power consumption:**

1. **Impeller geometry:** $N_p$ depends on impeller type (Rushton 5.5 vs Pitched Blade 1.3 vs Propeller 0.35). Larger blade area → more drag → more power.

2. **Impeller diameter $D_i$:** Power scales as $D_i^{2.5}$ (from $D_i^5$ in numerator and the implicit dependence of $D_T/D_i$ ratio).

3. **Impeller speed N:** $P \propto N^3$ in the turbulent regime.

4. **Fluid properties:** Density $\rho$ (linear) and viscosity $\mu$ (major effect in laminar regime).

5. **Tank geometry:** $D_T / D_i$, number of baffles, liquid height.

6. **Aeration (gassing):** $P_g = 0.4P$ to $0.7P$ under typical fermentation conditions.

7. **Number of impellers:** Multiple impellers roughly multiply power linearly (with some interaction).

**Scale-up based on equal P/V:**

For geometrically similar tanks, equal $P/V$ at both scales requires:

$$\frac{P_1}{V_1} = \frac{P_2}{V_2}$$

Since $P = N_p \rho N^3 D_i^5$ and $V \propto D_T^3$ (geometric similarity implies $D_i \propto D_T$):

$$\frac{N_p \rho N_1^3 D_{i,1}^5}{D_{T,1}^3} = \frac{N_p \rho N_2^3 D_{i,2}^5}{D_{T,2}^3}$$

Since $D_i \propto D_T$, we have $D_i^5 / D_T^3 \propto D_T^2$, so:

$$N_1^3 D_{T,1}^2 = N_2^3 D_{T,2}^2$$

$$\boxed{N_2 = N_1 \left(\frac{D_{T,1}}{D_{T,2}}\right)^{2/3}}$$

**Example:** If the lab tank has $D_{T,1} = 0.2$ m running at $N_1 = 500$ rpm, and we scale up to $D_{T,2} = 2.0$ m:

$$N_2 = 500 \times \left(\frac{0.2}{2.0}\right)^{2/3} = 500 \times (0.1)^{0.667} = 500 \times 0.215 = 107.7 \text{ rpm}$$

**Examiner's Allocation:** List of 5+ factors — 3 marks; equal P/V derivation — 3 marks; numerical example — 1 mark.

---

**(b)** With the help of the **Michel–Miller correlation**, explain how **gassing reduces power consumption** in a stirred-tank bioreactor. Comment on the effect of impeller type on the gassed-to-ungassed power ratio. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**The Michel–Miller Correlation (1962):**

$$\frac{P_g}{P} = 0.5 \left(\frac{Q_g}{N V}\right)^{-0.25}$$

This empirical correlation relates the gassed power to the ungassed power through the aeration parameter $Q_g / (N V)$.

**Physical explanation of gassing effect:**

1. **Cavities form behind impeller blades:** As air bubbles accumulate on the trailing face of the blades, the effective wetted surface of the blade decreases.

2. **Density reduction near the impeller:** The gas–liquid mixture in the impeller zone has a lower bulk density than the pure liquid, reducing drag.

3. **Form drag reduction:** The drag coefficient on the blade changes as gas cavities change the blade's hydrodynamic profile from "fully wetted" to "partially ventilated."

**Effect of impeller type:**

| Impeller Type | $N_p$ | $P_g / P$ (typical at vvm = 1) | Reason |
|---------------|-------|-------------------------------|--------|
| **Rushton** (radial flow) | 5.5 | 0.5 – 0.7 | High drag, large cavities form |
| **Pitched Blade** (axial flow) | 1.3 | 0.7 – 0.85 | Lower drag, partial gas handling |
| **Scaba / Gas-inducing** | varies | 0.9 – 1.0 | Designed specifically for gas dispersion |

**Key observation:** Pitched-blade and marine propellers exhibit a **smaller drop in power upon gassing** than Rushton turbines, which is why high-cell-density aerobic fermentations often use multiple Rushton impellers (which handle gas better in series, even though each individual one sees a larger power drop).

**Numerical illustration:**

If $Q_g / (N V) = 10^{-3}$, then:
$$\frac{P_g}{P} = 0.5 \times (10^{-3})^{-0.25} = 0.5 \times 5.62 = 2.81$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Michel–Miller gives a value > 1 in some ranges. This is **unphysical** for the actual gassed condition; the correlation is only valid for $Q_g / (N V) > 10^{-3}$ where it correctly predicts $P_g < P$. Always sanity-check the result.

**Examiner's Allocation:** Michel–Miller equation — 1 mark; physical mechanism — 2 marks; impeller type comparison — 3 marks; numerical sanity check — 1 mark.

---

## 5.1 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top Reasons Students Lose Marks on This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversion of N:** Mixing rpm and rev/s — KTU examiners deduct 1 mark if N is not converted to SI.
> 2. **Wrong Power Number from chart:** Using $N_p = 1$ for a Rushton turbine is the single most common error. Rushton ≈ 5.5; Pitched Blade ≈ 1.3; Marine Propeller ≈ 0.35.
> 3. **Confusing $D_i$ with $D_T$:** Always read the problem carefully — sometimes $D_T / D_i$ is given, not $D_i$ itself.
> 4. **Not applying the Froude correction** for unbaffled tanks at low impeller clearances.
> 5. **Dropping units in intermediate steps** — show all units; the examiner allocates 0.5 marks per step.
> 6. **Using Michel–Miller outside its valid range** — produces $P_g > P$, which is unphysical.
> 7. **Forgetting to convert volume $V$ to m³** before plugging into $Q_g / (N V)$.

---

## 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Power Number** $N_p = P / (\rho N^3 D_i^5)$ — the heart of agitation design.
- ✅ **Three flow regimes:** Laminar ($Re < 10$), Transition, Fully Turbulent ($Re > 10^4$).
- ✅ **Rushton turbine** ($N_p \approx 5.5$) — radial flow, high shear, ideal for gas dispersion.
- ✅ **Pitched blade turbine** ($N_p \approx 1.3$) — axial flow, lower shear, good for mammalian cells.
- ✅ **Marine propeller** ($N_p \approx 0.35$) — axial flow, low power, low shear.
- ✅ **Reynolds number** $Re = \rho N D_i^2 / \mu$ — determines $N_p$ from the chart.
- ✅ **Froude number** matters only for **unbaffled tanks**; baffled → drop it.
- ✅ **Ungassed power** = $N_p \rho N^3 D_i^5$.
- ✅ **Gassed power** = $\sim 0.4P$ to $\sim 0.7P$ at typical vvm = 1.
- ✅ **Hughmark:** $P_g / P = 0.10\,(Q_g / N V)^{-0.25}$ — most cited in KTU papers.
- ✅ **Van't Riet:** $P_g / P = 0.79\,Na^{-0.19}$ (Rushton) or $0.062\,Na^{-0.19}$ (PBT) where $Na = Q_g / (N D_i^3)$.
- ✅ **Michel–Miller:** $P_g / P = 0.5\,(Q_g / N V)^{-0.25}$ — older, but still in syllabus.
- ✅ **Scale-up (equal $P/V$):** $N_2 = N_1\,(D_{T,1}/D_{T,2})^{2/3}$.
- ✅ **Scale-up (equal tip speed):** $N_2 = N_1\,(D_{T,1}/D_{T,2})$.
- ✅ **Scale-up (equal Re):** $N_2 = N_1\,(D_{T,1}/D_{T,2})^2$ — most often impractical.
- ✅ **80 % of agitation power becomes heat** — this must be removed by the cooling jacket.
- ✅ **Baffles suppress vortexing** and increase $N_p$ (Rushton turbulent, baffled → 5.5; unbaffled → 1.2–2.0).
- ✅ **Gassing reduces power** — Michel–Miller, Hughmark, Van't Riet — pick the one specified by the question.
- ✅ **Geometry ratios to remember:** Standard $D_T / D_i = 3$; $H / D_T = 1$; four baffles of width $D_T / 10$.
- ✅ **Shear-sensitive cells** (e.g., CHO, hybridoma): use **PBT or marine propeller** at high $D_i / D_T$ to keep $P/V$ low (≤ 100 W/m³).

> **End of Module 4 Topic — Power Requirements for Agitation. Best of luck for your KTU 2024 ESE!**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In bioprocess engineering, **agitation** refers to the mechanical induction of fluid motion inside a bioreactor using impellers (Rushton turbines, pitched-blade, marine, etc.) coupled to a motor-driven shaft. The **volumetric mass transfer coefficient ($k_L a$)** is the most important kinetic parameter characterizing gas–liquid oxygen transfer, and it is profoundly influenced by the intensity of agitation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> *Agitation enhances oxygen transfer from the gas phase (typically air/oxygen-enriched air) into the aqueous broth by (i) increasing the gas–liquid interfacial area 'a' through bubble dispersion, and (ii) thinning the liquid-side boundary layer, thereby increasing the liquid-side mass transfer coefficient $k_L$. The combined effect is captured by the lumped parameter $k_L a$.*

The driving force for transfer is the concentration gradient between the saturation dissolved oxygen concentration ($C^*$) at the bubble interface and the actual dissolved oxygen ($C_L$) in the bulk liquid. The steady-state **Oxygen Transfer Rate (OTR)** is given by:

$$\text{OTR} = k_L a \cdot (C^* - C_L)$$

where $k_L$ is in m/s, $a$ is in m⁻¹, $C^*$ and $C_L$ are in mol/m³ (or kg/m³), so OTR has units of mol/(m³·s) — i.e., rate per unit working volume.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Sweetening Iced Tea**
> Imagine you drop a sugar cube into a still glass of iced tea. The cube dissolves extremely slowly because the liquid around it becomes saturated and forms a stagnant boundary layer. Now stir the tea with a spoon — the sugar vanishes rapidly. Why?
> 1. **Stirring physically breaks the sugar crystal** into many tiny particles, exposing far more surface area (this is analogous to **increasing the interfacial area 'a'** by breaking air bubbles into finer dispersions).
> 2. **Stirring sweeps fresh unsaturated liquid** to the surface of the sugar crystals, replacing the saturated layer (this is analogous to **increasing $k_L$** by thinning the liquid-film resistance).
>
> In a bioreactor, the impeller does exactly this to air bubbles: it shatters large bubbles into fine ones and continuously renews the liquid film at the gas–liquid interface, dramatically increasing the rate at which O₂ reaches the cells.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Metrics (Bold Highlighted)

- **Saturation dissolved oxygen concentration in water at 30 °C, 1 atm:** $C^* \approx 7.6 \text{ mg/L} = 7.6 \text{ g/m}^3$ (Henry's law based).
- **Critical dissolved oxygen concentration ($C_{crit}$):** typically **0.1–0.5 × $C^*$** for most industrial microorganisms.
- **Standard $k_L a$ range in industrial bioreactors:** $100 \text{ h}^{-1}$ to $1000 \text{ h}^{-1}$.
- **Power input per unit volume ($P/V$):** typical industrial values of $1 \text{ kW/m}^3$ to $25 \text{ kW/m}^3$.
- **Universal gas constant $R$** = 8.314 J/(mol·K).

## 1.4 Why Agitation Matters in Bioreactor Design

Agitation is a *double-edged sword*:

1. **Positive effect on mass transfer:** Increases $k_L a$, which raises OTR, supporting high cell densities and high productivities.
2. **Negative effect on cells:** Excessive agitation generates high shear stress and turbulent eddy dissipation rates that can damage shear-sensitive cells (mammalian, plant, filamentous fungi, insect cells).

A successful bioreactor design balances these opposing effects — a key KTU exam theme.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanism of Agitation-Enhanced Mass Transfer

Agitation improves oxygen transfer through four coupled physical mechanisms. Each step is critical to KTU's 2024 Scheme "Understand and Apply" level.

### Step 1 — Bubble Breakup (Primary Effect)
The rotating impeller, especially in **fully turbulent flow** (Reynolds number $Re > 10^4$), generates inertial and viscous forces that exceed the bubble's restoring surface tension force (a function of the **Weber number**, $We$). Bubbles break up when:

$$We = \frac{\rho_L \cdot N^2 \cdot D_i^3}{\sigma} > We_{crit}$$

where $\rho_L$ = liquid density (kg/m³), $N$ = impeller speed (rev/s), $D_i$ = impeller diameter (m), and $\sigma$ = surface tension (N/m). For air–water, $We_{crit} \approx 1$–3. Result: mean bubble diameter $d_B$ **decreases** $\Rightarrow$ interfacial area $a = 6\varepsilon_g / d_B$ **increases** ($\varepsilon_g$ = gas holdup).

### Step 2 — Surface Renewal
The turbulent eddies continuously sweep fresh liquid elements to the gas–liquid interface, replacing depleted ones. The **Danckwerts surface renewal theory** quantifies this via a surface renewal rate $s$ (s⁻¹), giving $k_L = \sqrt{D_L \cdot s}$, where $D_L$ is the molecular diffusivity of O₂ in water (≈ 2.1 × 10⁻⁹ m²/s at 25 °C). Agitation increases $s$ through turbulence.

### Step 3 — Increased Gas Holdup ($\varepsilon_g$)
The impeller retains bubbles in the broth longer by creating downward flow (Rushton turbine) or radial dispersion, raising $\varepsilon_g$ from ~5% (unagitated) to 15–25% (well-agitated). Higher $\varepsilon_g$ directly scales interfacial area $a$.

### Step 4 — Bulk Mixing Homogenization
Agitation eliminates concentration and temperature gradients, ensuring $C_L$ measured by a probe is representative of the whole tank. Without this, the KTU-defined "well-mixed" assumption (used in CSTR mass balances) is violated.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU Board-Examiner-Favorite — appear almost every exam cycle.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{OTR} = k_L a (C^* - C_L)$ | Oxygen transfer rate (steady state) | $\text{kg O}_2/(\text{m}^3 \cdot \text{h})$ | — |
| 2 | $\text{OUR} = q_{O_2} \cdot C_X$ | Oxygen uptake rate by cells | $\text{kg O}_2/(\text{m}^3 \cdot \text{h})$ | — |
| 3 | $\text{Steady state: OTR} = \text{OUR}$ | Operating condition | — | — |
| 4 | $P = N_p \cdot \rho_L \cdot N^3 \cdot D_i^5$ | Ungassed power draw (power number correlation) | W | $N_p = 5$–6 (Rushton) |
| 5 | $P_g = c \cdot \left(\dfrac{P^2 \cdot N \cdot D_i^3}{Q_g^{0.56}}\right)^{0.45}$ | Gassed power (Michels–Hartog–van't Riet) | W | $c$ depends on impeller |
| 6 | $k_L a = K \cdot \left(\dfrac{P_g}{V}\right)^{\alpha} \cdot u_s^{\beta}$ | Van't Riet empirical correlation | $\text{h}^{-1}$ | $\alpha \approx 0.4$–0.7, $\beta \approx 0.3$–0.5 |
| 7 | $a = \dfrac{6 \varepsilon_g}{d_B (1 - \varepsilon_g)}$ | Interfacial area (spherical bubble approximation) | $\text{m}^{-1}$ | — |
| 8 | $u_s = \dfrac{Q_g}{A_X}$ | Superficial gas velocity | m/s | 0.001–0.05 |
| 9 | $\dot{\gamma} \approx k \cdot N$ | Mean shear rate approximation | $\text{s}^{-1}$ | 1000–50000 |
| 10 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho_L N D_i^2}{\mu_L}$ | Impeller Reynolds number | — | $>10^4$ turbulent |
| 11 | $C^* = \dfrac{y_{O_2} \cdot P_{tot}}{H_{O_2}}$ | Henry's law saturation DO | mol/m³ | — |
| 12 | $t_m^{0.31} = \dfrac{3.5 \cdot \tanh \left(0.5 N \cdot t_m\right) \cdot \text{??}}{\text{??}}$ | Mixing-time correlation (impeller-type specific) | s | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark-Saver Note:** In $k_L a = K (P_g/V)^{\alpha} u_s^{\beta}$, the constant $K$ and exponents depend on the **broth type** (non-Newtonian, coalescing, antifoam-suppressed) — examiners LOVE to test this.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Scale-up criterion:** Most industrial scale-ups (50 L → 50,000 L) keep $P/V$ **constant**, which conveniently keeps $k_L a$ approximately constant — but this is a *necessary, not sufficient* condition.
2. **Shear-sensitive cultures (vaccines, monoclonal antibodies in CHO cells):** Use **low-shear pitched-blade or marine impellers** at moderate $P/V$ (~0.5–2 kW/m³) and supplement with **oxygen-enriched air** (raising $C^*$) to maintain OTR.
3. **High-cell-density fed-batch (e.g., E. coli > 100 g DCW/L):** Requires very high OTR, achieved by combined high $P/V$ + pure O₂ sparging.
4. **Diagnostic tool:** The **dynamic gassing-out method** (sudden N₂ → air switch) is the standard KTU-lab technique to experimentally measure $k_L a$.

## 2.4 The Counter-Effect: Shear Damage

Shear stress in agitated bioreactors is characterized by the **Kolmogorov eddy length** $\eta = (\nu^3/\varepsilon)^{1/4}$, where $\nu$ = kinematic viscosity, $\varepsilon$ = local energy dissipation rate (W/kg). Particles/cells smaller than $\eta$ experience the full turbulent strain, while larger entities experience mean shear. This is why $P/V$ has a **practical upper limit**.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Oxygen Mass Balance (OUR = OTR at Steady State)

**Starting point (KTU favourite):** In a well-mixed batch bioreactor, the dissolved oxygen balance is:

$$\frac{dC_L}{dt} = k_L a (C^* - C_L) - q_{O_2} \cdot C_X$$

**Step 1 — Set the time derivative to zero** (steady state — the KTU standard assumption in ESE problems):

$$0 = k_L a (C^* - C_L) - q_{O_2} C_X$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the operating $C_L$:**

$$k_L a (C^* - C_L) = q_{O_2} C_X$$

**Step 3 — Express the "headroom" of oxygen transfer available:**

$$(C^* - C_L) = \frac{q_{O_2} C_X}{k_L a}$$

**Step 4 — Introduce the OUR = $q_{O_2} C_X$** form:

$$(C^* - C_L) = \frac{\text{OUR}}{k_L a}$$

**Step 5 — The maximum OUR the system can satisfy (critical condition $C_L = C_{crit}$):**

$$\text{OUR}_{max} = k_L a (C^* - C_{crit})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the cornerstone relation for bioreactor design:** if the demand $q_{O_2} C_X$ exceeds $k_L a (C^* - C_{crit})$, the cells will experience oxygen limitation and metabolism will shift to mixed-acid fermentation / overflow metabolism.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the $k_L a$ Empirical Correlation via Dimensional Analysis

We seek the dependence $k_L a = f(P_g/V, u_s, \rho_L, \mu_L, \sigma, D_L, g)$. By Buckingham Pi, two dimensionless groups emerge that are KTU-board standard:

**Group 1 — Sherwood number (mass transfer):**

$$Sh = \frac{k_L a \cdot D_i^2}{D_L}$$

**Group 2 — Power number (impeller flow):**

$$\text{Po} = \frac{P_g}{\rho_L N^3 D_i^5}$$

**Group 3 — Aeration number:**

$$Na = \frac{Q_g}{N D_i^3}$$

**Combining** in the form $Sh = A \cdot \text{Po}^{\alpha} \cdot Na^{\beta} \cdot (\text{geometry, fluid factors})$, and re-arranging into the well-known dimensional form gives the Van't Riet (1979) correlation:

$$\boxed{k_L a = K \cdot \left(\frac{P_g}{V}\right)^{\alpha} \cdot u_s^{\beta}}$$

### Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style 14-mark problem)

**Problem (KTU University Exam – May 2024 pattern):** A 2 m³ working-volume bioreactor is sparged with air at $Q_g = 0.02 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$. The impeller (Rushton) draws 3.5 kW when gassed. The broth has $C^* = 8 \text{ g/m}^3$, $C_X = 5 \text{ kg/m}^3$ biomass, $q_{O_2} = 0.25 \text{ g O}_2/(\text{g DCW} \cdot \text{h})$. Estimate the steady-state $C_L$ using the correlation $k_L a = 2.0 \times 10^{-3} (P_g/V)^{0.6} u_s^{0.4}$ (units: $k_L a$ in s⁻¹, $P_g/V$ in W/m³, $u_s$ in m/s; conversion factor 3600 to express in h⁻¹).

**Step 1 — Calculate $P_g / V$:**

$$\frac{P_g}{V} = \frac{3500 \text{ W}}{2 \text{ m}^3} = 1750 \text{ W/m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the cross-sectional area** (assuming $H = 2 D_T$ ratio and $V = \pi D_T^2 H/4 = \pi D_T^3/2$):

$$D_T = \left(\frac{2 V}{\pi}\right)^{1/3} = \left(\frac{2 \times 2}{\pi}\right)^{1/3} = \left(1.273\right)^{1/3} = 1.084 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Cross-sectional area:**

$$A_X = \frac{\pi D_T^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (1.084)^2}{4} = 0.923 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Superficial gas velocity:**

$$u_s = \frac{Q_g}{A_X} = \frac{0.02}{0.923} = 0.0217 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into the $k_L a$ correlation:**

$$k_L a = 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \times (1750)^{0.6} \times (0.0217)^{0.4}$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate $(1750)^{0.6}$:**

$$(1750)^{0.6} = e^{0.6 \ln 1750} = e^{0.6 \times 7.466} = e^{4.480} \approx 88.3$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate $(0.0217)^{0.4}$:**

$$(0.0217)^{0.4} = e^{0.4 \ln 0.0217} = e^{0.4 \times (-3.831)} = e^{-1.532} \approx 0.216$$

**Step 8 — Compute $k_L a$:**

$$k_L a = 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 88.3 \times 0.216 = 3.81 \times 10^{-2} \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**Step 9 — Convert to h⁻¹:**

$$k_L a = 3.81 \times 10^{-2} \times 3600 = 137.3 \text{ h}^{-1}$$

**Step 10 — Calculate OUR:**

$$\text{OUR} = q_{O_2} C_X = 0.25 \times 5000 \text{ g/m}^3 \cdot \text{h} \cdot (\text{g O}_2/\text{g DCW} \cdot \text{h}) \cdots$$

> Correction, units alignment: $q_{O_2} = 0.25 \text{ g O}_2/(\text{g DCW} \cdot \text{h})$, $C_X = 5 \text{ g DCW/L} = 5000 \text{ g/m}^3$:

$$\text{OUR} = 0.25 \times 5000 = 1250 \text{ g O}_2/(\text{m}^3 \cdot \text{h}) = 1.25 \text{ kg O}_2/(\text{m}^3 \cdot \text{h})$$

**Step 11 — Solve for $C_L$:**

$$C_L = C^* - \frac{\text{OUR}}{k_L a} = 8 - \frac{1250}{137.3} = 8 - 9.10 = -1.10 \text{ g/m}^3$$

**Step 12 — Interpret the result:**

The negative $C_L$ is **physically impossible** $\Rightarrow$ the demand exceeds supply $\Rightarrow$ the design fails. To fix this, the engineer must:
- Increase $P_g/V$ (larger motor, multiple impellers), **or**
- Use pure O₂ sparging (raises $C^*$ from 8 to ~34 g/m³), **or**
- Reduce $C_X$ growth rate, **or**
- Use a larger vessel.

This kind of feasibility check is a KTU board favorite!

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Impeller Power Number

**Step 1 — Newton's dimensional approach** for a baffled, fully turbulent, Newtonian flow:

$$P \propto \rho_L^{\alpha_1} N^{\alpha_2} D_i^{\alpha_3}$$

**Step 2 — Balance dimensions [W = kg·m²/s³] vs. [kg/m³]·[1/s]$^a$·[m]$^b$:**

$$[\text{M L}^2 \text{T}^{-3}] = [\text{M L}^{-3}]^{\alpha_1} \cdot [\text{T}^{-1}]^{\alpha_2} \cdot [\text{L}]^{\alpha_3}$$

**Step 3 — Match M, L, T:**

- M: $1 = \alpha_1 \Rightarrow \alpha_1 = 1$
- T: $-3 = -\alpha_2 \Rightarrow \alpha_2 = 3$
- L: $2 = -3\alpha_1 + \alpha_3 = -3 + \alpha_3 \Rightarrow \alpha_3 = 5$

**Step 4 — Final form with dimensionless constant $N_p$:**

$$P = N_p \cdot \rho_L \cdot N^3 \cdot D_i^5$$

For a fully-baffled, fully-turbulent Rushton turbine in water–air, $N_p \approx 5.0$–$6.0$ (gassed, $N_p$ drops to ~1.5–3).

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Coupling of Agitation → Mass Transfer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Agitator Motor Input N rpm] --> B[Impeller Rotation]
    B --> C[Turbulent Flow Field Re greater than ten to the four]
    C --> D1[Weber number greater than critical]
    C --> D2[Surface renewal rate s increases]
    C --> D3[Gas holdup epsilon g rises]
    D1 --> E1[Bubble diameter dB decreases]
    D2 --> E2[Liquid side kL increases]
    D3 --> E3[Interfacial area a increases]
    E1 --> F[Volumetric kL a increases]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G[OTR equal to kL a times C star minus CL]
    G --> H{Steady state OUR satisfied?}
    H --> I1[Yes: C_L within healthy range]
    H --> I2[No: oxygen limitation and cell stress]
    A -.-> J[Side effect: Shear stress]
    J --> K{Shear sensitive cells}
    K --> L1[CHO Mammalian]
    K --> L2[Plant and Insect]
    K --> L3[Filamentous fungi]
    L1 --> M[Choose low shear pitched blade]
    L2 --> M
    L3 --> M
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Mass Transfer Train

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    GAS[Gas Phase Air or O2] --> SPARGER[Sparger Ring or Porous Disc]
    SPARGER --> BUB[Bubble Plume Rising]
    BUB --> IMP[Impeller Hydrodynamic Action]
    IMP --> INTER[Gas Liquid Interface]
    INTER --> FILM[Liquid Boundary Film]
    FILM --> BULK[Bulk Liquid]
    BULK --> CELLS[Microbial Cells and Clusters]
    CELLS --> METAB[Metabolic O2 Consumption]
    METAB --> FEED[Feedback: Lower C_L triggers impeller RPM boost]
    FEED --> IMP
```

## 4.3 Regime Map: Flooding → Loading → Complete Dispersion

| Regime | Description | $N$ (relative) | Effect on $k_L a$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Flooding | Bubbles rise straight up through the impeller, no dispersion | $N$ very low | Very low $k_L a$ |
| Loading | Bubbles partially captured by impeller | Moderate-low $N$ | Low $k_L a$ |
| Complete dispersion | All bubbles captured and dispersed; $k_L a$ peaks | $N$ moderate | **Maximum $k_L a$** |
| Submergence | Bubbles recirculate strongly; some coalescence | $N$ high | Slight $k_L a$ drop |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU tip:** The "complete dispersion" condition is the design target for most bioreactors. It is identified empirically at the impeller speed where the impeller just ceases to flood.

```
           |       GAS OUTLET           |
           |                            |
           |============================|---- exhaust filter
           |        HEADSPACE           |
           |============================|---- C* region
           |                            |

           |  o   o   o   o   o   o     |

```
