# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 marks each)

> **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
> *Define the volumetric mass transfer coefficient ($k_L a$). List two physical mechanisms by which agitation increases $k_L a$ in a stirred-tank bioreactor.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** $k_L a$ is the product of the liquid-side mass transfer coefficient $k_L$ (m/s) and the specific gas–liquid interfacial area $a$ (m²/m³ = m⁻¹). It has units of time⁻¹ and is the lumped parameter in $\text{OTR} = k_L a (C^* - C_L)$.
- **Mechanism 1 (0.75 marks):** Impeller-induced turbulence fragments large bubbles into smaller ones, **increasing the specific interfacial area 'a'** (since $a \propto 1/d_B$).
- **Mechanism 2 (0.75 marks):** Turbulent eddies continuously renew the liquid boundary layer at the bubble surface, **increasing $k_L$** per the surface-renewal (Danckwerts) model.

> **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
> *Explain the "flooding" phenomenon in agitated gas–liquid bioreactors. Why is flooding undesirable?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Flooding definition (1.5 marks):** Flooding is the operating regime in which the gas flow rate $Q_g$ is so high (or impeller speed $N$ so low) that the rising bubble plume passes *uninterrupted* through the impeller plane. The impeller cannot capture and disperse the gas.
- **Why undesirable (1.5 marks):** In flooding, the gas–liquid interfacial area 'a' is minimal (large, undispersed bubbles), so $k_L a$ drops sharply, leading to severe oxygen limitation. Mixing is also poor.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Unit trap:** Students frequently write $k_L a$ in s⁻¹ but OTR/OUR in g/(m³·h) — this mismatch costs 2–3 marks. Always convert either to SI (s) or to hours consistently.
> 2. **Steady-state assumption:** When asked for $C_L$ in batch operation, you must explicitly state $dC_L/dt = 0$. Forgetting this loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Power-number mistake:** Using the **ungassed** power number $N_p = 5.0$ for a sparged Rushton tank when the question clearly says "gassed" loses 1 mark; the correct gassed value is ~1.5–3.
> 4. **$C^*$ is the saturation concentration at the bubble interface**, NOT the bulk gas concentration. Many students confuse Henry's law input.
> 5. **Correlation context:** The Van't Riet exponents ($\alpha$, $\beta$) are *broth-dependent*; blindly quoting textbook values without mentioning coalescing/non-coalescing fluids loses 1 mark.

---

### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks (Module 4 — Agitation & Mass Transfer)**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model Paper Pattern)]**

**Q(a) [7 marks]** *Derive the oxygen mass balance for a well-mixed batch bioreactor and obtain the steady-state expression for $C_L$ in terms of $k_L a$, $C^*$, $q_{O_2}$, and $C_X$.*

**Model Solution — Q(a):**

The dissolved oxygen balance over the broth is:

$$\frac{dC_L}{dt} = (\text{rate in via transfer}) - (\text{rate consumed by cells})$$

$$\frac{dC_L}{dt} = k_L a (C^* - C_L) - q_{O_2} C_X \quad \text{...(1)}$$

At **steady state**, $dC_L/dt = 0$:

$$0 = k_L a (C^* - C_L) - q_{O_2} C_X$$

Rearranging:

$$k_L a (C^* - C_L) = q_{O_2} C_X$$

Solving for $C_L$:

$$C^* - C_L = \frac{q_{O_2} C_X}{k_L a}$$

$$\boxed{C_L = C^* - \frac{q_{O_2} C_X}{k_L a}}$$

For the system to be **feasible** (positive $C_L$):

$$q_{O_2} C_X \leq k_L a \cdot C^*$$

**Q(b) [7 marks]** *A 5 m³ working-volume fermenter is fitted with a single Rushton turbine ($D_i = 0.5 D_T$). The impeller speed is 3 rev/s and the gassed power is 1.8 kW. Air is sparged at $Q_g = 0.04 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$. Using the correlation* $k_L a = 2.1 \times 10^{-2} \left(\dfrac{P_g}{V}\right)^{0.55} u_s^{0.4}$ *(with $k_L a$ in s⁻¹, $P_g/V$ in W/m³, $u_s$ in m/s), determine the steady-state $C_L$ for a culture with $C_X = 3 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and $q_{O_2} = 0.30 \text{ g O}_2/(\text{g DCW} \cdot \text{h})$. Take $C^* = 7.5 \text{ g/m}^3$. State clearly whether the design is feasible.*

**Model Solution — Q(b):**

**Step 1 — Power per unit volume:**

$$\frac{P_g}{V} = \frac{1800}{5} = 360 \text{ W/m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Tank diameter** (assuming $H = 2D_T$, $V = \pi D_T^2 H/4 = \pi D_T^3/2$):

$$D_T = \left(\frac{2V}{\pi}\right)^{1/3} = \left(\frac{10}{\pi}\right)^{1/3} = (3.183)^{1/3} \approx 1.474 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Cross-sectional area:**

$$A_X = \frac{\pi D_T^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (1.474)^2}{4} = 1.706 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Superficial gas velocity:**

$$u_s = \frac{Q_g}{A_X} = \frac{0.04}{1.706} = 0.02345 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the correlation:**

$$(360)^{0.55} = e^{0.55 \ln 360} = e^{0.55 \times 5.886} = e^{3.237} = 25.47$$

$$(0.02345)^{0.4} = e^{0.4 \ln 0.02345} = e^{0.4 \times (-3.752)} = e^{-1.501} = 0.2229$$

$$k_L a = 2.1 \times 10^{-2} \times 25.47 \times 0.2229 = 0.1192 \text{ s}^{-1} = 429.4 \text{ h}^{-1}$$

**Step 6 — Calculate OUR:**

$$\text{OUR} = q_{O_2} \cdot C_X = 0.30 \times 3000 = 900 \text{ g O}_2/(\text{m}^3 \cdot \text{h})$$

**Step 7 — Steady-state $C_L$:**

$$C_L = C^* - \frac{\text{OUR}}{k_L a} = 7.5 - \frac{900}{429.4} = 7.5 - 2.10 = 5.40 \text{ g/m}^3$$

**Step 8 — Feasibility check:**

$C_L = 5.4 \text{ g/m}^3 \in (0, C^*) \Rightarrow$ **Design is feasible**. The dissolved oxygen is comfortably above the typical $C_{crit} = 0.5$ g/m³.

---

### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model Paper Pattern)]**

**Q(a) [7 marks]** *With the aid of neat sketches, describe the four flow regimes that occur in a sparged agitated tank as a function of impeller speed. Explain which regime is the design target and why.*

**Model Solution — Q(a):**

As impeller speed $N$ is increased at constant gas flow rate $Q_g$, the following regimes are observed (in order of increasing $N$):

1. **Flooding (1.5 marks):** The bubble plume rises *uninterrupted* through the impeller plane. The impeller is unable to capture and disperse the gas. $k_L a$ is very low. This occurs at very low $N$ (or very high $Q_g$).

2. **Loading (1.5 marks):** Bubbles begin to be partially captured by the impeller. Some dispersion occurs but bubbles still escape upward directly. $k_L a$ is still low but starts to rise.

3. **Complete dispersion (2 marks):** All bubbles rising toward the impeller are captured and broken into a fine, uniform dispersion throughout the tank. This is the **design target**, because $k_L a$ reaches its maximum value and the broth is well-mixed.

4. **Submergence / Recirculation (2 marks):** At very high $N$, bubbles are recirculated strongly through the impeller repeatedly. Some coalescence can occur in the headspace. $k_L a$ plateaus or slightly declines.

**Design target (0.5 mark):** Complete dispersion — it provides maximum $k_L a$ at minimum power consumption.

**Q(b) [7 marks]** *A 10 m³ bioreactor with $D_T = 2 \text{ m}$ has a single Rushton impeller of $D_i = 0.7 \text{ m}$, running at $N = 2.5 \text{ rev/s}$ in un-gassed condition. The broth density is $\rho_L = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$. The power number for the un-gassed case is $N_p = 5.5$. When air is sparged at $Q_g = 0.06 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$, the power number drops to 2.0. Estimate the ratio of gassed to un-gassed power draw.*

**Model Solution — Q(b):**

**Step 1 — Un-gassed power:**

$$P = N_p \cdot \rho_L \cdot N^3 \cdot D_i^5 = 5.5 \times 1000 \times (2.5)^3 \times (0.7)^5$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate $N^3$:**

$$N^3 = (2.5)^3 = 15.625$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate $D_i^5$:**

$$D_i^5 = (0.7)^5 = 0.16807$$

**Step 4 — Compute un-gassed power:**

$$P = 5.5 \times 1000 \times 15.625 \times 0.16807 = 5.5 \times 1000 \times 2.626 = 14,443 \text{ W} \approx 14.44 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5 — Gassed power** (using $N_p^{(g)} = 2.0$):

$$P_g = 2.0 \times 1000 \times 15.625 \times 0.16807 = 2.0 \times 1000 \times 2.626 = 5,251 \text{ W} \approx 5.25 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6 — Power ratio:**

$$\frac{P_g}{P} = \frac{5.25}{14.44} = 0.364$$

**Step 7 — Interpretation:** Aeration reduces power draw by **~64%** — a critical design consideration for motor selection. The motor must be rated to handle the un-gassed condition; otherwise, it stalls when aeration is suddenly removed.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second Revision List — KTU PEPOT526 Module 4**

- ✅ **Master equation:** $\text{OTR} = k_L a (C^* - C_L) = \text{OUR} = q_{O_2} \cdot C_X$ (steady state)
- ✅ **Operating $C_L$:** $C_L = C^* - \dfrac{q_{O_2} C_X}{k_L a}$ — must be positive and greater than $C_{crit}$.
- ✅ **Power correlation:** $P = N_p \rho_L N^3 D_i^5$ (un-gassed); gassed $N_p$ is much smaller (1.5–3 vs 5–6).
- ✅ **$k_L a$ correlation:** $k_L a = K (P_g/V)^{\alpha} u_s^{\beta}$ — Van't Riet, exponents depend on broth rheology and coalescence behavior.
- ✅ **Mixing time ($t_m$)** scales roughly as $N^{-1}$ — higher speed = faster homogenization.
- ✅ **Flooding → loading → complete dispersion → submergence** — design at complete dispersion for max $k_L a$.
- ✅ **Weber number** governs bubble breakup: $We = \rho_L N^2 D_i^3 / \sigma > We_{crit}$.
- ✅ **Shear sensitivity** is a function of Kolmogorov eddy length $\eta = (\nu^3/\varepsilon)^{1/4}$ and cell size.
- ✅ **Scale-up** is typically done at **constant $P/V$**, which preserves $k_L a$ (via the $\alpha$ exponent).
- ✅ **Dynamic gassing-out method** is the experimental standard for measuring $k_L a$.
- ✅ **C\* (saturation DO)** depends on temperature, pressure, and gas composition (Henry's law: $C^* = y_{O_2} P_{tot} / H_{O_2}$).
- ✅ **Antifoam additions**, high cell density, and high ionic strength **suppress** $k_L a$ by promoting bubble coalescence.
- ✅ **Agitation is double-edged:** it is essential for $k_L a$ but must be limited for shear-sensitive cultures.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Oxygen Delivery System - Spargers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT526 - BIOREACTOR DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Oxygen Delivery System - Spargers ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **sparger** is a mechanical device installed at the base of a bioreactor vessel that disperses incoming gas (typically air, pure oxygen, or oxygen-enriched air) into the liquid fermentation broth as a controlled distribution of bubbles. The primary engineering objective of a sparger is to maximize the **interfacial area available for oxygen transfer** from the gas phase to the liquid phase, thereby sustaining the **oxygen uptake rate (OUR)** demanded by aerobic microbial, animal cell, or plant cell cultures. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sparger Function Triad (KTU Board Definition):** > 1. **Gas Dispersion** — Generates bubbles of controlled Sauter mean diameter ($d_{32}$). > 2. **Oxygen Mass Transfer** — Provides a high volumetric mass transfer coefficient ($k_L a$). > 3. **Bulk Mixing (Secondary)** — Induces convective liquid circulation, supporting heat and mass homogeneity. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Aquarium Air Stone Intuition Imagine a fish aquarium. Without an air stone, the bubbles from a tube rise as large, infrequent pockets — most of the oxygen simply escapes to the atmosphere without dissolving. Now place a **porous air stone** at the tube's end. The same volume of air is shattered into thousands of **microscopic bubbles**, dramatically increasing the total surface area exposed to water. Each tiny bubble lingers longer in the water column, dissolving far more oxygen before reaching the surface. > This is precisely what a bioreactor sparger does — it trades a few large bubbles (low surface-area-to-volume ratio) for countless small bubbles (high surface-area-to-volume ratio), maximizing the **driving force** $(C^* - C_L)$ for oxygen dissolution. ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range (Bioreactor) | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Liquid density | $\rho_L$ | **1000** (water) | kg/m³ | | Gas density (air) | $\rho_G$ | **1.2** (at 25°C, 1 atm) | kg/m³ | | Surface tension (water) | $\sigma$ | **0.072** | N/m | | Viscosity (water, 25°C) | $\mu_L$ | **0.0009** | Pa·s | | Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | **9.81** | m/s² | | Oxygen diffusivity in water | $D_O$ | **2.1 × 10⁻⁹** | m²/s | | Henry's constant for O₂ | $H$ | **769.2** atm·L/mol | — | > [!NOTE] > The **Henry's Law constant for O₂** is critical: it determines $C^*$ (the equilibrium dissolved oxygen concentration). At 1 atm air in water, $C^* \approx 8$ mg/L. With pure O₂ sparging, $C^*$ increases to approximately **40 mg/L**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Classification of Spargers (KTU High-Yield) Spargers are classified by their **bubble-generation mechanism** and the **bubble size distribution** they produce: #### 2.1.1 Porous Spargers (Frits / Sintered Discs) - Constructed from sintered bronze, glass, ceramic, or stainless steel. - **Pore sizes:** 5 µm – 100 µm. - Produces **very fine bubbles** ($d_B$ ≈ 1–5 mm after coalescence). - High $k_L a$ efficiency; low gas flow capacity. - Used in **animal cell culture** and **sensitive microbial systems**. - **Disadvantage:** Prone to clogging; high-pressure drop. #### 2.1.2 Orifice Spargers (Perforated Ring / Cross / Spider) - Simple pipe with drilled holes (orifice diameter $d_o$ = 0.5–5 mm). - **Bubbles produced:** 3–15 mm diameter (medium-sized). - Robust, low maintenance, high flow capacity. - **Most common** in industrial stirred-tank bioreactors. #### 2.1.3 Nozzle / Jet Spargers - Gas is forced through a converging nozzle at high velocity. - Produces **large, fast-moving bubbles** ($d_B$ = 10–30 mm). - Used in **very tall reactors** or where gas throughput is extreme. - Acts as a co-axial pump (Sparger + eductor). #### 2.1.4 Combined Sparger-Agitator Systems - Gas discharged directly **under the Rushton impeller** (sparger located at $D_T/4$ from impeller plane). - Maximizes dispersion by impeller shear. - The **"standard configuration"** for high-cell-density fermentations. ### 2.2 The Engineering "Why" — The Three Hierarchical Design Goals 1. **Maximize $k_L a$** because OTR $= k_L a \cdot (C^* - C_L)$ — the oxygen transfer rate is linearly dependent on the volumetric mass transfer coefficient. 2. **Minimize energy input** because sparging cost dominates bioreactor operating expense in aerobic systems. 3. **Avoid shear damage** to shear-sensitive cells (e.g., mammalian cells, plant cells, filamentous fungi with hyphal extensions). ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Equation | Symbols | Engineering Utility | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $C^* = \dfrac{p_{O_2}}{H}$ | $C^* = $ saturation DO; $p_{O_2} = $ partial pressure; $H = $ Henry's constant | Determines **maximum achievable DO** for a sparger gas | | 2 | $\text{OTR} = k_L a \cdot (C^* - C_L)$ | OTR = oxygen transfer rate | **Master equation** of sparger design | | 3 | $d_{32} = \dfrac{\sum n_i d_i^3}{\sum n_i d_i^2}$ | $d_{32} = $ Sauter mean diameter | **Equivalent diameter** representing same volume-to-area ratio as whole population | | 4 | $a = \dfrac{6 \varepsilon_G}{d_{32}(1-\varepsilon_G)}$ | $a = $ specific interfacial area; $\varepsilon_G = $ gas hold-up | **Bubble surface area per unit liquid volume** | | 5 | $\varepsilon_G = \dfrac{V_G}{V_G + V_L}$ | $V_G$ = gas volume in dispersion | **Gas fraction** in aerated broth | | 6 | $\text{We} = \dfrac{\rho_G u_o^2 d_o}{\sigma}$ | We = Weber number; $u_o = $ orifice gas velocity | Predicts **bubble regime** at orifice | | 7 | $P_G = Q_G \cdot \Delta P_{sparger}$ | $P_G = $ sparger power input; $Q_G = $ gas flow rate | Energy dissipation for gas dispersion | | 8 | $Q_G = C_d \cdot A_o \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho_G}}$ | $C_d = $ discharge coefficient ($\approx 0.6$–$0.9$); $A_o = $ orifice area | **Orifice flow equation** sizing sparger holes | | 9 | $k_L a = K \cdot \left(\dfrac{P_G}{V_L}\right)^{\alpha} \cdot v_s^{\beta}$ | Van't Riet correlation; $v_s = $ superficial gas velocity | **Empirical $k_L a$** from operating variables | | 10 | $\text{SSOTR} = k_L a \cdot C^*$ | SSOTR = Standard Oxygen Transfer Rate | Vendor rating (clean water, 25°C, 1 atm air) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **For clean water (Akita-Yoshida correlation):** > $$k_L a \cdot \dfrac{d_I^2}{D_O} = 0.6 \cdot \left(\dfrac{\mu_L}{\rho_L D_O}\right)^{0.5} \cdot \left(\dfrac{\mu_L g}{v_s^3 \rho_L^2}\right)^{0.62} \cdot \left(\dfrac{\mu_L v_s}{\sigma}\right)^{0.3}$$ > This is a frequently tested formula in KTU Module 4 problems. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility In industrial antibiotic production (e.g., penicillin by *Penicillium chrysogenum*), oxygen demand reaches **10–15 mmol O₂/L/h**. Without an efficient sparger, the dissolved oxygen crashes to zero in seconds, halting antibiotic synthesis. Sparger design therefore directly determines **reactor productivity**, **yield coefficient ($Y_{x/o}$), and volumetric productivity** — the three metrics that govern bioprocess economics. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Oxygen Transfer Rate from Two-Film Theory The **Two-Film Theory (Whitman, 1923)** postulates that resistance to gas-liquid mass transfer lies in two stagnant films: a gas film and a liquid film. For sparingly soluble gases like O₂, the **liquid-film resistance dominates**, giving: #### Step 1: Write the local flux across the liquid film $$N_A = k_L (C^* - C_L)$$ where $N_A$ is the molar flux (kmol O₂/m²·s), $k_L$ is the **liquid-side mass transfer coefficient** (m/s), $C^*$ is the saturation concentration, and $C_L$ is the bulk dissolved concentration. #### Step 2: Multiply by the interfacial area per unit volume $$\text{OTR} = k_L \cdot a \cdot (C^* - C_L) = k_L a (C^* - C_L)$$ The product $k_L a$ is the **volumetric mass transfer coefficient** (s⁻¹) — the single most important sparger performance metric. #### Step 3: Develop the dynamic mass balance For a batch bioreactor, the rate of change of dissolved oxygen is: $$\dfrac{dC_L}{dt} = k_L a (C^* - C_L) - \text{OUR}$$ At **pseudo-steady state** ($dC_L/dt = 0$), OTR = OUR. This is the design condition for spargers. #### Step 4: Solve the transient balance (Dynamic Sparger Response Test) Setting OUR = 0 (by switching off aeration and sparging with N₂) and then re-sparging with air, the dynamic gassing-out/gassing-in experiment gives: $$C_L(t) = C^* - (C^* - C_{L,0}) \exp(-k_L a \cdot t)$$ A semi-log plot of $(C^* - C_L)$ vs. $t$ yields $k_L a$ from its slope — the **standard KTU laboratory method** for sparger characterization. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Sauter Mean Diameter for a Polydisperse Bubble Population A sparger produces bubbles of various sizes. We need a single representative diameter for the population that preserves the **surface-to-volume ratio**. #### Step 1: Define the volume-to-surface ratio of a single bubble For a spherical bubble of diameter $d_i$: $$\dfrac{\text{Volume}}{\text{Surface}} = \dfrac{\pi d_i^3 / 6}{\pi d_i^2} = \dfrac{d_i}{6}$$ #### Step 2: Compute the volume-weighted average for $n_i$ bubbles of size $d_i$ $$\left(\dfrac{V}{A}\right)_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{\sum n_i (\pi d_i^3/6)}{\sum n_i (\pi d_i^2)} = \dfrac{\sum n_i d_i^3}{6 \sum n_i d_i^2}$$ #### Step 3: Equate to the equivalent monodisperse diameter Since the equivalent monodisperse bubble has $V/A = d_{32}/6$: $$\dfrac{d_{32}}{6} = \dfrac{\sum n_i d_i^3}{6 \sum n_i d_i^2}$$ $$\boxed{\,d_{32} = \dfrac{\sum n_i d_i^3}{\sum n_i d_i^2}\,}$$ #### Step 4: Example numerical evaluation Suppose a porous sparger produces: $n_1 = 1000$ bubbles of $d_1 = 1$ mm; $n_2 = 500$ of $d_2 = 2$ mm; $n_3 = 100$ of $d_3 = 4$ mm. | $i$ | $n_i$ | $d_i$ (mm) | $n_i d_i^2$ | $n_i d_i^3$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 1000 | 1 | 1000 | 1000 | | 2 | 500 | 2 | 2000 | 4000 | | 3 | 100 | 4 | 1600 | 6400 | | **Σ** | | | **4600** | **11400** | $$d_{32} = \dfrac{11400}{4600} \approx 2.478 \text{ mm}$$ Thus the bubble population is equivalent to a monodisperse swarm of 2.48 mm bubbles for mass transfer purposes. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Specific Interfacial Area ($a$) from Gas Hold-Up #### Step 1: Express bubble surface area per bubble $$A_{\text{bubble}} = \pi d_{32}^2$$ #### Step 2: Express bubble volume per bubble $$V_{\text{bubble}} = \dfrac{\pi d_{32}^3}{6}$$ #### Step 3: Determine number of bubbles per unit dispersion volume If $V_G$ is the total gas volume in dispersion volume $V_D = V_G + V_L$: $$N_{\text{bubbles}} = \dfrac{V_G}{V_{\text{bubble}}} = \dfrac{6 V_G}{\pi d_{32}^3}$$ #### Step 4: Compute total interfacial area per unit dispersion volume $$a_{\text{disp}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{bubbles}} \cdot \pi d_{32}^2}{V_D} = \dfrac{6 V_G}{d_{32} V_D} = \dfrac{6 \varepsilon_G}{d_{32}}$$ Since $V_D \approx V_L$ (gas hold-up is small, $\varepsilon_G \ll 1$): $$\boxed{\,a = \dfrac{6 \varepsilon_G}{d_{32}(1 - \varepsilon_G)}\,}$$ This is the **master link** between bubble size, hold-up, and mass transfer area. --- ### 3.4 Derivation 4: Power Input to the Sparger (Isothermal Expansion Assumption) For an isothermal gas expansion from supply line to vessel: #### Step 1: Apply the steady-flow energy equation $$P_G = Q_G \cdot \Delta P_{\text{sparger}}$$ #### Step 2: Express $\Delta P$ using the orifice flow equation Rearranging Equation 8 from the Formula Sheet: $$\Delta P = \dfrac{\rho_G}{2 C_d^2 A_o^2} \cdot Q_G^2$$ #### Step 3: Substitute $$P_G = \dfrac{\rho_G Q_G^3}{2 C_d^2 A_o^2}$$ #### Step 4: Show $P_G$ scales as the cube of gas flow rate This **cubic dependence** means that doubling the gas flow rate increases sparger power demand by 8× — a critical cost factor in aeration economics. --- ### 3.5 Derivation 5: Volumetric Mass Transfer Coefficient ($k_L a$) — Dimensionless Synthesis The $k_L a$ of a sparger can be derived from surface-renewal theory and bubble hydrodynamics. Beginning with the penetration model: $$k_L = 2 \sqrt{\dfrac{D_O}{\pi \theta_s}}$$ where $\theta_s$ is the **surface exposure time** of a fluid element. For a rising bubble of diameter $d_{32}$ at terminal velocity $u_t$: $$\theta_s = \dfrac{d_{32}}{u_t}$$ Substituting: $$k_L = 2 \sqrt{\dfrac{D_O u_t}{\pi d_{32}}}$$ Multiplying by the interfacial area: $$k_L a = 2 \sqrt{\dfrac{D_O u_t}{\pi d_{32}}} \cdot \dfrac{6 \varepsilon_G}{d_{32}(1-\varepsilon_G)}$$ $$\boxed{\,k_L a = \dfrac{12 \varepsilon_G}{d_{32}^{3/2}(1-\varepsilon_G)} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{D_O u_t}{\pi}}\,}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical interpretation:** $k_L a$ scales as $d_{32}^{-3/2}$ — halving the bubble diameter increases $k_L a$ by approximately **2.83×**. This single insight justifies the design priority of **fine-bubble spargers**. --- ### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Sparger Type vs. Performance | Criterion | Porous Frit | Orifice Ring | Nozzle Jet | Sparger + Impeller | |---|---|---|---|---| | Bubble size $d_{32}$ (mm) | 1–5 | 3–15 | 10–30 | 2–8 | | Typical $k_L a$ (h⁻¹) | 200–600 | 80–250 | 40–120 | 250–800 | | Pressure drop | **High** | **Low** | **Very High** | **Medium** | | Clogging risk | **High** | **Low** | **Very Low** | **Medium** | | Shear damage | **Low** | **Medium** | **High** | **Medium-High** | | Industrial usage | Lab / Animal cells | Standard fermenters | Wastewater | **Standard industrial** | | Cost | High | Low | Medium | High | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Sparger Placement and Bubble Generation Mechanism ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Compressed Air Supply] --> B[Filter 0.2 micron] B --> C[Pressure Regulator] C --> D[Rotameter Flow Meter] D --> E[Sparger Inlet Manifold] E --> F1[Porous Disc Frit] E --> F2[Orifice Ring Sparger] E --> F3[Nozzle Jet Sparger] F1 --> G1[Fine Bubbles d32: 1-5 mm] F2 --> G2[Medium Bubbles d32: 3-15 mm] F3 --> G3[Large Bubbles d32: 10-30 mm] G1 --> H[Liquid Culture Broth] G2 --> H G3 --> H H --> I[Oxygen Dissolution at Interface] I --> J[Cellular Uptake OUR] H --> K[Exhaust Gas CO2 + Stripped O2] K --> L[Condenser Off-Gas Analysis] ``` ### 4.2 Bubble Formation Regimes at an Orifice (Weber Number Criterion) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Gas Velocity at Orifice] --> B{Weber Number We} B -->|We less than 2| C[Discrete Bubbles
Low Frequency Regime] B -->|We between 2 and 4| D[Jet Formation
Coalescing Bubbles] B -->|We greater than 4| E[Gas Jet
Churn-Turbulent Regime] C --> F[Small d32 - High kLa] D --> G[Medium d32] E --> H[Large d32 - Low kLa] ``` ### 4.3 Sparger Functional Architecture Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Inputs direction LR In1[Gas Pressure] In2[Gas Composition] In3[Gas Flow Rate QG] In4[Liquid Viscosity] end subgraph SpargerHardware direction TB HW1[Orifice Geometry] HW2[Pore Size Distribution] HW3[Manifold Layout] HW4[Material of Construction] end subgraph OperatingParameters direction LR Op1[Superficial Gas Velocity VS] Op2[Power Input PG] Op3[Reynolds Number in Broth] end subgraph Outputs direction TB Out1[Sauter Mean Diameter d32] Out2[Gas Hold-up epsilon G] Out3[Volumetric kLa] Out4[OTR = kLa times C star minus CL] end Inputs --> SpargerHardware SpargerHardware --> OperatingParameters OperatingParameters --> Outputs Outputs --> OutResp[Bioreactor Productivity and Cell Growth] classDef inputBox fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,color:#0d47a1 classDef hwBox fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#bf360c classDef opBox fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,color:#3a015c classDef outBox fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#0d3b10 class In1,In2,In3,In4 inputBox class HW1,HW2,HW3,HW4 hwBox class Op1,Op2,Op3 opBox class Out1,Out2,Out3,Out4,OutResp outBox ``` ### 4.4 Typical Industrial Sparger Installation — Cross-Sectional Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Vessel["Cylindrical Bioreactor Vessel"] direction TB Top[Headspace with Off-Gas Outlet] Mid[Liquid Level with Impeller Shaft] Bot[Bottom Dish with Sparger] Top --> Mid --> Bot end subgraph SpargerSystem["Sparger Assembly Below Impeller"] direction TB Ring[Ring Sparger Diameter DT by 4] Mani[Central Inlet Manifold] Orif[8-16 Orifices of 2 mm Diameter] Mani --> Ring --> Orif end SpargerSystem --> Vessel subgraph FlowPath["Gas Flow Path During Operation"] direction LR FA[Filtered Air] --> FB[Regulator and Rotameter] --> FC[Sparger Ring] --> FD[Bubble Plume] --> FE[Impeller Dispersion Zone] end ``` > [!NOTE] > The **ring sparger diameter** is conventionally placed at $0.5 D_T$ to $0.8 D_T$ (where $D_T$ is the tank diameter) and positioned approximately $D_T/4$ below the lowest impeller to ensure uniform radial dispersion without dead zones. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Q1. Define a sparger. List the four major types used in bioprocess industries.** **Model Answer:** A sparger is a device installed at the bottom of a bioreactor that disperses incoming air or oxygen as bubbles into the culture broth to facilitate oxygen transfer. The four major types are: 1. **Porous spargers** (sintered frits) — fine bubbles, low flow 2. **Orifice spargers** (perforated ring, cross) — medium bubbles, robust 3. **Nozzle spargers** (jet) — large bubbles, high flow 4. **Combined sparger-agitator** systems (under-impeller sparging) — industrial standard > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Q2. What is the Sauter mean diameter ($d_{32}$)? Why is it preferred over arithmetic mean diameter in sparger characterization?** **Model Answer:** Sauter mean diameter $d_{32}$ is the diameter of a hypothetical monodisperse bubble swarm that has the **same surface-to-volume ratio** as the actual polydisperse swarm, defined as: $$d_{32} = \dfrac{\sum n_i d_i^3}{\sum n_i d_i^2}$$ It is preferred because the **volumetric mass transfer coefficient $k_L a$** depends on the surface-to-volume ratio, not on the arithmetic size distribution. Hence $d_{32}$ directly characterizes the sparger's mass transfer capability. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **Note (KTU 2024 Pattern):** Each Part B question carries 14 marks with internal choice. Below, two alternative questions (A and B) are provided; the student answers one. --- #### **[KTU University Exam — Model Question A]** *(14 Marks)* **Q A (a)** With a neat diagram, describe the construction and working of an **orifice ring sparger** used in a stirred-tank bioreactor. Discuss the criteria for selecting orifice diameter and placement location. *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** **Construction (2 marks):** An orifice ring sparger consists of a circular pipe (typically stainless steel SS 316L) bent into a ring whose diameter equals **0.5 $D_T$ to 0.8 $D_T$** of the tank. The ring has 8–24 drilled orifices of diameter $d_o$ = 1–5 mm each, distributed uniformly along the circumference. The ring is connected via a single inlet pipe passing through the tank base or side wall. A sintered metal filter upstream removes particulates; a pressure regulator and rotameter control gas flow. **Working (2 marks):** Pressurized air enters the ring, exits through the orifices as discrete bubbles, and rises through the impeller-swept zone where mechanical shear breaks the bubbles into smaller ones. The rising plume induces liquid circulation, creating a toroidal mixing pattern. **Selection criteria (3 marks):** | Criterion | Design Rule | |---|---| | Orifice diameter | $d_o = 1$–$3$ mm for 1–10 m³ vessels; $3$–$5$ mm for $>10$ m³ | | Orifice velocity $u_o$ | 5–30 m/s; must satisfy Weber $We > 2$ for jetting regime | | Orifice count $N_o$ | Sized to keep $u_o$ in the above range at design $Q_G$ | | Submergence depth | At least $0.6 D_T$ below liquid surface | | Distance from impeller | Approximately $D_T/4$ below lowest Rushton impeller | **Q A (b)** Derive the relationship between the **specific interfacial area ($a$)** and the **gas hold-up ($\varepsilon_G$)** in a sparged bioreactor. A sparger produces bubbles of $d_{32} = 3$ mm in a tank where $\varepsilon_G = 0.10$. Calculate the specific interfacial area. *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** **Derivation (4 marks):** As shown in Section 3.3: $$\boxed{\,a = \dfrac{6 \varepsilon_G}{d_{32}(1 - \varepsilon_G)}\,}$$ **Numerical substitution (3 marks):** $$a = \dfrac{6 \times 0.10}{3 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} \times (1 - 0.10)}$$ $$a = \dfrac{0.60}{2.7 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$\boxed{\,a \approx 222.2 \text{ m}^{-1}\,}$$ **Physical interpretation:** A one-liter broth sample contains approximately **0.222 m² of gas-liquid interfacial area** — equivalent to a flat plate 47 cm × 47 cm folded into 1 L of liquid. This enormous area is what makes aerobic fermentation feasible. --- #### **[KTU University Exam — Model Question B]** *(14 Marks)* **Q B (a)** Explain the **two-film theory** of gas-liquid mass transfer. Derive the integrated form of the oxygen mass balance used in the **dynamic gassing-out method** to determine $k_L a$. *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** **Two-film theory (3 marks):** Whitman (1923) postulated that the gas-liquid interface is flanked by two stagnant films: a gas film and a liquid film. Mass transfer is by steady-state molecular diffusion across each film. For sparingly soluble gases like O₂, the gas-film resistance is negligible, and the **liquid-film resistance** dominates. The flux is: $$N_A = k_L (C^* - C_L)$$ where $k_L = D_L / \delta_L$ and $\delta_L$ is the effective film thickness. **Dynamic gassing-out method derivation (4 marks):** Mass balance on dissolved oxygen in a batch reactor (OUR set to zero during re-aeration): $$\dfrac{dC_L}{dt} = k_L a (C^* - C_L)$$ Separating variables: $$\dfrac{dC_L}{C^* - C_L} = k_L a \, dt$$ Integrating from $C_{L,0}$ at $t = 0$ to $C_L$ at $t = t$: $$-\ln(C^* - C_L)\Big\vert_{C_{L,0}}^{C_L} = k_L a \cdot t$$ $$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{C^* - C_{L,0}}{C^* - C_L}\right) = k_L a \cdot t$$ A plot of $\ln(C^* - C_L)$ vs. $t$ yields a straight line with slope $-k_L a$, allowing experimental determination of the sparger performance. **Q B (b)** A 50 m³ stirred-tank bioreactor with $D_T = 3.5$ m uses a porous sparger. The following data are recorded: $Q_G = 0.05$ m³/s, $\Delta P = 30$ kPa, $d_{32} = 2.5$ mm, $\varepsilon_G = 0.08$, $C^* = 8$ mg/L, $C_L = 2$ mg/L. Calculate: *(i)* sparger power input $P_G$, *(ii)* specific interfacial area $a$, and *(iii)* OTR assuming $k_L = 3 \times 10^{-4}$ m/s. *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** **(i) Sparger power input (2 marks):** $$P_G = Q_G \cdot \Delta P = 0.05 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \times 30{,}000 \text{ Pa}$$ $$\boxed{\,P_G = 1500 \text{ W} = 1.5 \text{ kW}\,}$$ **(ii) Specific interfacial area (2 marks):** $$a = \dfrac{6 \times 0.08}{2.5 \times 10^{-3} \times (1 - 0.08)} = \dfrac{0.48}{2.3 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$\boxed{\,a \approx 208.7 \text{ m}^{-1}\,}$$ **(iii) Oxygen Transfer Rate (3 marks):** First, compute $k_L a$: $$k_L a = 3 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/s} \times 208.7 \text{ m}^{-1} = 0.0626 \text{ s}^{-1}$$ Convert to per hour: $k_L a = 0.0626 \times 3600 \approx 225.4$ h⁻¹. Then: $$\text{OTR} = k_L a (C^* - C_L) = 0.0626 \text{ s}^{-1} \times (8 - 2) \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/m}^3$$ $$\boxed{\,\text{OTR} = 3.76 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg O}_2/\text{m}^3/\text{s} \approx 1.35 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{m}^3/\text{h}\,}$$ **Sanity check:** For a 50 m³ vessel, total oxygen supply ≈ 67.6 kg O₂/h, sufficient for a moderately dense industrial fermentation. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Module 4 — Spargers):** > > 1. **Unit mismatch on $d_{32}$ in $a$ formula** — Always convert $d_{32}$ from mm to m before substituting into the $a = 6\varepsilon_G/[d_{32}(1-\varepsilon_G)]$ equation. A 1000× error is the most common mistake. > > 2. **Forgetting the $(1 - \varepsilon_G)$ term** — Many students drop the denominator term, giving an answer 10%–15% high. At low hold-up, this is forgivable; at $\varepsilon_G > 0.15$, it is a full mark loss. > > 3. **Confusing OTR with OUR** — Oxygen Transfer Rate (OTR) is what the sparger delivers; Oxygen Uptake Rate (OUR) is what the cells consume. At steady state, OTR = OUR. Examiners test this distinction explicitly. > > 4. **Wrong Henry's Law setup** — $C^*$ in pure O₂ sparging is $\approx 5\times$ the value in air sparging (because $p_{O_2}$ rises from 0.21 to 1.0 atm). Marks are awarded for **recognizing gas composition** when $C^*$ is given. > > 5. **Sauter mean diameter misuse** — Do not compute the arithmetic mean $\bar{d} = (\sum n_i d_i)/(\sum n_i)$. Examiners specifically test for $d_{32}$ form because of its direct link to mass transfer. > > 6. **Dimensionless groups** — In derivation problems, write units on **both sides** of the equation. If units fail to cancel, marks are deducted even if the numerical answer is correct. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Oxygen Delivery System: Spargers** - [x] **Definition** — Sparger = bottom-mounted gas dispersion device; primary purpose = maximize $k_L a$ for oxygen transfer. - [x] **Four Types** — Porous frit, Orifice ring, Nozzle jet, Combined sparger-impeller. - [x] **Master Equation** — $\text{OTR} = k_L a (C^* - C_L)$, valid at steady state when OTR = OUR. - [x] **Sauter Mean Diameter** — $d_{32} = \sum n_i d_i^3 / \sum n_i d_i^2$; **not arithmetic mean**. - [x] **Specific Interfacial Area** — $a = 6\varepsilon_G / [d_{32}(1-\varepsilon_G)]$. - [x] **Orifice Sizing** — $Q_G = C_d A_o \sqrt{2 \Delta P / \rho_G}$; typical $C_d \approx 0.7$. - [x] **Sparger Power** — $P_G = Q_G \Delta P$; scales as $Q_G^3$ at constant orifice. - [x] **Dynamic Method** — $\ln(C^* - C_L)$ vs. $t$ yields slope $= -k_L a$ (gassing-out experiment). - [x] **Bubble-Size Rule** — $k_L a \propto d_{32}^{-3/2}$; halving $d_{32}$ increases $k_L a$ by $2^{3/2} \approx 2.83$×. - [x] **Standard Ranges** — $C^* = 8$ mg/L in air-sparged water at 25°C; **$C^* \approx 40$ mg/L** in pure O₂. - [x] **Empirical Correlations** — Van't Riet: $k_L a = K(P_G/V_L)^\alpha v_s^\beta$; Akita-Yoshida for clean water. - [x] **Placement Rule** — Ring sparger at $0.5D_T$–$0.8D_T$, located $D_T/4$ below the lowest impeller. - [x] **Process Constraint** — Aerobic demand for industrial antibiotic fermentation: **10–15 mmol O₂/L/h**. - [x] **Cost Driver** — Sparger power + compressor cost typically 20–40% of total bioreactor OPEX. - [x] **Trade-off** — Smaller bubbles = higher $k_L a$ but higher $\Delta P$, more clogging, possible cell shear.

topics.title: Foam Control System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT526 - BIOREACTOR DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Foam Control System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Foam Control System (FCS)** in a bioreactor is an integrated set of **mechanical, chemical, and operational subsystems** designed to **suppress, eliminate, or regulate the formation of gas–liquid interfacial foam** that develops during aerobic microbial, mammalian-cell, and plant-cell cultivations. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, it is a **critical process utility** of a stirred-tank or airlift bioreactor that ensures the broth volume is maintained within the vessel, the off-gas filter remains dry, and mass-transfer efficiency is preserved.

Mathematically, foam can be represented as a **two-phase dispersion** where gas bubbles are separated by thin liquid films (lamellae) stabilized by surface-active compounds such as **proteins, peptides, polysaccharides, and fatty acids** secreted by the culture.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Soap Suds on Your Shakes

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine pouring a carbonated drink into a tall glass. The bubbles rise and form a foamy "head." Now imagine a bioreactor is that glass, but the culture broth contains biological "soap" (proteins and surfactants from cells). Without a foam control system, the foam will keep rising until it overflows the glass, spills onto the floor (contamination), and blocks the straw (off-gas filter). The foam control system is the combination of:
> - **A small spinning blade** above the liquid that physically pops the bubbles (mechanical),
> - **A drop of oil** added to the drink that makes the bubbles unstable (chemical antifoam), and
> - **Adjusting how fast you pour** so fewer bubbles form (operational control).

## 1.3 Why Foam Forms in Bioreactors

Foam is an inevitable consequence of **intense aeration and agitation** in a nutrient-rich broth. The primary drivers are:

| Driver | Mechanism | Typical Bioprocess Example |
|---|---|---|
| **High sparged-gas flowrate** | Bubble–bubble coalescence at the sparger | Fed-batch Pichia pastoris |
| **Surface-active metabolites** | Proteins/peptides lower surface tension | Recombinant *E. coli* |
| **High cell density** | Cell debris and lysed proteins stabilize films | Mammalian cell (CHO) culture |
| **Agitation intensity** | Shear generates small stable bubbles | Streptomyces fermentations |
| **pH and temperature shifts** | Protein denaturation at gas–liquid interface | Bacillus subtilis |

## 1.4 The Cost of *Uncontrolled* Foam

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Students frequently underestimate foam. Foam is not a cosmetic nuisance — it can **shut down a 50,000-litre facility** in 30 minutes. Be ready to list at least **five** engineering problems caused by foam in your exam.

1. **Loss of working volume** as broth escapes into the headspace and exhaust line.
2. **Wetting of the off-gas HEPA filter** leading to filter clogging and **pressure surges** of 0.5–2 bar.
3. **Loss of product** to the foam (cells entrained in the foam head).
4. **Foam carry-over to downstream condenser/heat exchangers** causing fouling.
5. **Foam-induced contamination** of the exhaust line and external piping.
6. **Erratic dissolved-oxygen (DO) readings** because foam interferes with the polarographic probe membrane.
7. **Stagnant dead zones** in the foam head, which act as anaerobic pockets and shift metabolic flux (e.g., to lactate in CHO cultures).

## 1.5 Standard KTU Classification of Foam-Control Strategies

The KTU 2024 syllabus groups foam control into **three primary families**:

1. **Mechanical Foam Control** — physical destruction of bubbles.
2. **Chemical Foam Control** — addition of surface-active antifoam agents.
3. **Operational/Process Foam Control** — modifying operating conditions to minimize foam generation.

The most robust industrial systems use **a combination** — for example, an antifoam probe + chemical antifoam + a foam trap in series.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: Foam as a Colloidal System

A foam in a bioreactor is best modelled as a **Kugelschaum (spherical foam)** at the bottom transitioning into a **Polyederschaum (polyhedral foam)** at the top. The transition occurs at a liquid fraction (φ) of approximately **0.36**, with the bulk foam typically having φ ≈ 0.05–0.15.

The **Plateau border** is the curved channel where three lamellae meet, and the **Plateau–Gibbs suction pressure** (Π) drives drainage:

$$\Pi = \dfrac{2 \sigma}{r_{pb}}$$

where:
- **σ** = surface tension of the broth (N/m)
- **r_{pb}** = radius of curvature of the Plateau border (m)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for bioreactor design:** A higher surface tension stabilizes foam by *increasing* the suction pressure that drains the lamellae — wait, this is actually **the opposite**. Higher σ drives faster drainage (more foam collapse). The key is the **viscosity of the surface film** (Gibbs elasticity, E), which determines stability. This is a classic KTU "trick" question.

## 2.2 Foam Stability and the Gibbs–Marangoni Criterion

A foam is stable when surface-active molecules **resist thinning** of the lamella. The **Gibbs elasticity (E)** quantifies this:

$$E = 2 \cdot \dfrac{d\sigma}{d \ln \Gamma}$$

where **Γ** is the surface excess concentration of surfactant (mol/m²). A positive E means the lamella is self-healing — pure foam stability. Industrial antifoams work by **driving E to a small or negative value** so the lamellae cannot recover from disturbances.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is your **must-memorize** formula bank. Every expression below has been asked in KTU examinations in the last five years or follows directly from the PEPOT526 syllabus outcomes.

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Typical Units | KTU 2024 CO Tag |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Pi = \dfrac{2\sigma}{r_{pb}}$ | Plateau suction pressure | Pa | CO4, K2 |
| 2 | $E = 2 \dfrac{d\sigma}{d\ln\Gamma}$ | Gibbs surface elasticity | N/m | CO4, K3 |
| 3 | $N_{Fr} = \dfrac{N^{2} D}{g}$ | Froude number (foam breaker) | dimensionless | CO4, K3 |
| 4 | $N_{We} = \dfrac{\rho N^{2} D^{3}}{\sigma}$ | Weber number (bubble break-up) | dimensionless | CO4, K3 |
| 5 | $P_{FB} = 2 \pi N M$ | Power draw of foam breaker (M = torque) | W | CO4, K3 |
| 6 | $v_{drain} = \dfrac{\rho g r_{pb}^{2}}{20 \mu}$ | Foam drainage velocity | m/s | CO4, K2 |
| 7 | $\dot{V}_{antifoam} = k \cdot \dot{V}_{gas} \cdot C_{crit}$ | Antifoam dosage rule (volumetric) | m³/h | CO4, K3 |
| 8 | $\sigma_{app} = \sigma_{0} \exp(-k_{c} C_{af})$ | Apparent surface tension with antifoam conc. | N/m | CO4, K3 |
| 9 | $k_{L}a_{eff} = k_{L}a \left(1 - \alpha \phi_{foam}\right)$ | Effective $k_{L}a$ with foam hold-up correction | s⁻¹ | CO4, K4 |
| 10 | $\Delta P_{filter} = \dfrac{\mu_{gas} u L_{filt}}{k_{k}}$ | Filter pressure drop (off-gas) | Pa | CO4, K3 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Greek letter conventions used throughout the KTU 2024 PEPOT526 syllabus:**
> - σ (sigma) — surface tension
> - Γ (Gamma) — surface excess concentration
> - μ (mu) — dynamic viscosity
> - ρ (rho) — density
> - φ (phi) — volume fraction / hold-up
> - η (eta) — efficiency factor (0 to 1)

## 2.4 Mechanical Foam Control — The Engineering Toolbox

### 2.4.1 Foam Breaker Discs
A flat or conical disc mounted on a shaft above the liquid surface, rotated at **N = 600–1500 rpm**. The disc **destroys bubbles by impingement** and centrifugal ejection of liquid back into the broth.

**Operating regime** is selected by the **Froude number** $N_{Fr}$:

$$N_{Fr} = \dfrac{N^{2} D_{d}}{g}$$

For a 0.3 m disc, foam is effectively broken when **$N_{Fr} > 0.3$**, i.e.:

$$N > \sqrt{\dfrac{0.3 \cdot g}{D_{d}}}$$

### 2.4.2 Centrifugal Foam Breaker
High-speed (up to **3000 rpm**) rotor with vanes; foam is sucked in, liquid centrifuged out and returned, gas exhausted. Excellent for **continuous cultures** with persistent foam.

### 2.4.3 Ultrasound & Acoustic Defoamers
A **20 kHz transducer** mounted on the vessel wall. The acoustic pressure wave **destabilizes the gas–liquid interface**, causing **drainage coalescence**. Used in small-scale mammalian cell bioreactors where mechanical shear must be minimized.

### 2.4.4 Foam Traps (External Cyclones)
A side-arm cyclone external to the bioreactor. Foam rises, hits a **deflector plate**, the liquid drains back via gravity, the gas exits. Used when the **headspace is too small** to accommodate an internal disc.

## 2.5 Chemical Foam Control — Antifoam Agents

Antifoams are **immiscible droplets** (typically oils) with a low surface tension that **spreads over the gas–liquid interface** (entering coefficient, E > 0) and displaces the stabilizing protein film.

The **spreading coefficient** is given by:

$$S = \sigma_{foam} - \sigma_{af} - \sigma_{int}$$

where $\sigma_{foam}$, $\sigma_{af}$, and $\sigma_{int}$ are the surface tensions of the foam film, the antifoam, and the antifoam–broth interface, respectively. **For defoaming to occur, S > 0** — this is the **Ross criterion**.

### 2.5.1 KTU Classification of Antifoams

| Class | Example Compound | Typical Dose (ppm v/v) | Application | Drawback |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Silicone oils** | Polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) | 5–100 | Fungal, *S. cerevisiae* | Can build up in downstream |
| **Polyols** | Polypropylene glycol (PPG) | 50–500 | Bacterial, CHO | Reduces $k_{L}a$ significantly |
| **Fatty acids / esters** | Stearic acid, oleic acid, mono-/diglycerides | 50–200 | Yeast, fungal | Interfere with oxygen transfer |
| **Natural oils** | Soybean, sunflower, olive | 100–1000 | Antibiotic fermentations | High dose, lot variability |
| **Solid antifoams** | Silica, talc, micronized wax | 10–200 | Recombinant bacteria | Hard to remove, fouling |
| **Fluorinated** | Perfluoropolyether (PFPE) | 1–20 | Vaccine production | Very expensive |

### 2.5.2 Antifoam Probes and Control Loops
A **conductivity probe** (or capacitance probe) is installed in the headspace, **15–20 cm above the working liquid level**. When foam touches the probe, a sharp conductivity change triggers a **peristaltic pump** that delivers antifoam from a reservoir. The control loop is typically **on/off (bang-bang)** with a dead-band of 2–3 cm to prevent pump cycling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** In modern KTU 2024-aligned DCS systems, the antifoam addition uses a **PI controller** with a setpoint foam height, giving smoother dosing and reducing antifoam consumption by 30–50 %.

## 2.6 Operational/Process Foam Control

These are *preventive* measures applied at the **recipe design** stage:

1. **Reduce sparged-gas flowrate** (but this compromises $O_{2}$ supply — trade-off).
2. **Use a microsparger** with smaller pores → smaller, less stable bubbles that coalesce quickly.
3. **Modify media composition** — replace complex nitrogen sources (e.g., yeast extract) with defined amino acids.
4. **Use a foam-tolerant organism** or use a **low-foaming strain** (e.g., *S. cerevisiae* proteinase A mutants).
5. **Two-stage reactors** — high-foaming growth in CSTR, low-foaming production in airlift.
6. **Optimize pH and temperature** to minimize protein denaturation at the gas–liquid interface.

## 2.7 Real-World Utility

Foam control is non-negotiable in:
- **Recombinant Pichia pastoris** for high-cell-density heterologous protein production (Methanol-induced foam surge).
- **Bacillus thuringiensis** (Bt) bioinsecticide fermentation — extremely foam-prone.
- **CHO monoclonal antibody (mAb)** production — 10,000 L single-use bioreactors with limited headspace.
- **Penicillin** fed-batch fermentation (continuous sucrose feed creates high surface tension gradients).

A poorly designed foam control system can **cost ₹2–5 crore per batch** in lost product in a 50,000 L facility.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Power Required by a Rotating Foam-Breaker Disc

> [!NOTE]
> **Setup:** A flat circular disc of diameter $D_d$ rotates at rotational speed $N$ (rps) on a vertical shaft. Foam of height $h_f$ impinges on the disc. We want to find the **minimum power $P_{FB}$** to break the foam.

**Step 1 — Identify the dominant force on a foam element.**
A foam element of mass $m$ arriving at the disc has kinetic energy that must be dissipated, plus the gravitational potential from the foam height $h_f$ above the disc (if the disc is mounted above the free surface). For a disc mounted just above the liquid surface, the dominant energy is **centrifugal ejection work**.

**Step 2 — Energy required per unit mass.**
Each element of foam must be accelerated from rest to the tangential velocity of the disc tip $v_{tip} = \pi N D_d$. The kinetic energy per unit mass is:

$$e_{kin} = \dfrac{1}{2} v_{tip}^{2} = \dfrac{1}{2} (\pi N D_d)^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Mass flow rate of foam intercepted.**
The disc sweeps through the foam at volumetric flowrate $Q_{foam}$:

$$Q_{foam} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D_{d}^{2} \cdot v_{axial}$$

where $v_{axial}$ is the foam rise velocity. For a typical bioreactor with gas superficial velocity $u_g$ and a foam-to-liquid expansion ratio $E_f$:

$$v_{axial} \approx E_f \cdot u_g$$

Hence:

$$Q_{foam} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D_{d}^{2} \cdot E_f \cdot u_g$$

**Step 4 — Mass flow rate of foam.**
The foam density is $\rho_{foam} = \rho_L (1 - \phi_{gas,foam}) \approx 0.1 \rho_L$ to $0.3 \rho_L$, so:

$$\dot{m}_{foam} = \rho_{foam} \cdot Q_{foam} = \rho_{foam} \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4} D_{d}^{2} \cdot E_f \cdot u_g$$

**Step 5 — Total power required.**
The total power is the product of mass flow rate and energy per unit mass, plus the **gravitational work** to return the liquid to the broth surface (height $h_f$):

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{FB} &= \dot{m}_{foam} \cdot e_{kin} + \dot{m}_{foam} \cdot g \cdot h_{eff} \\
P_{FB} &= \dot{m}_{foam} \left[ \dfrac{1}{2}(\pi N D_{d})^{2} + g \cdot h_{eff} \right] \\
P_{FB} &= \rho_{foam} \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4} D_{d}^{2} \cdot E_f \cdot u_g \left[ \dfrac{\pi^{2} N^{2} D_{d}^{2}}{2} + g \cdot h_{eff} \right]
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Dimensionless form using $N_{Fr}$ and $N_{We}$.**
Divide by the surface tension power scale $\sigma \cdot u_g \cdot D_d$:

$$N_{P,FB} = \dfrac{P_{FB}}{\sigma \cdot u_g \cdot D_d} \approx \dfrac{\pi \rho_{foam} E_f D_{d}}{4 \sigma} \left[ \dfrac{\pi^{2} N^{2} D_{d}^{2}}{2} + g h_{eff} \right]$$

This number must exceed a **critical value of approximately 5–10** for effective foam destruction, depending on broth rheology.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Antifoam Dosage from Surface-Tension Decay

> [!NOTE]
> **Setup:** We know that adding antifoam reduces the broth surface tension exponentially. Given an initial surface tension $\sigma_0$ and a desired surface tension $\sigma_{target}$, find the antifoam concentration $C_{af}$ to add.

**Step 1 — Empirical model.**
From surfactant thermodynamics, the **Szyszkowski-type equation** for a fermentation broth with antifoam is:

$$\sigma_{app} = \sigma_{0} \exp(-k_c \cdot C_{af})$$

where:
- $\sigma_0$ = surface tension of the broth *before* antifoam (typically 0.050–0.072 N/m for protein-rich media)
- $k_c$ = empirical decay constant (m³/kg), depends on antifoam type
- $C_{af}$ = antifoam concentration (kg/m³)
- $\sigma_{app}$ = apparent surface tension with antifoam

**Step 2 — Solve for $C_{af}$.**
Take the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln \sigma_{app} = \ln \sigma_{0} - k_c \cdot C_{af}$$

Rearrange:

$$C_{af} = \dfrac{1}{k_c} \ln \left( \dfrac{\sigma_{0}}{\sigma_{app}} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Numerical example.**
Given $\sigma_0 = 0.070$ N/m, target $\sigma_{app} = 0.040$ N/m, and $k_c = 5.0$ m³/kg:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{af} &= \dfrac{1}{5.0} \ln \left( \dfrac{0.070}{0.040} \right) \\
C_{af} &= \dfrac{1}{5.0} \ln(1.75) \\
C_{af} &= \dfrac{1}{5.0} \cdot 0.5596 \\
C_{af} &\approx 0.112 \text{ kg/m}^{3} = 112 \text{ ppm (w/v)}
\end{aligned}$$

This is consistent with a **typical antifoam dose of 50–200 ppm** in industrial Pichia pastoris cultures.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Foam Height in a Stirred Bioreactor

> [!NOTE]
> **Setup:** Estimate the equilibrium foam height $H_f$ in a stirred-tank bioreactor with gas superficial velocity $u_g$, broth surface tension $\sigma$, and liquid height $H_L$.

**Step 1 — Identify the dominant balance.**
At equilibrium, the **upward gas flux** equals the **downward liquid drainage** from the foam.

**Step 2 — Gas flux upward.**
The volumetric gas flow per unit area is $u_g$, but only a fraction $\beta$ supports the foam (the rest passes through channels):

$$J_{gas,up} = \beta \cdot u_g \quad (\beta \approx 0.7 - 0.9)$$

**Step 3 — Liquid drainage rate.**
From the **foam drainage model** (Stevenson model), the superficial liquid velocity in the foam is:

$$v_{L,down} = \dfrac{\rho_L g d_{b}^{2}}{200 \mu_{eff}}$$

where $d_b$ is the mean bubble diameter and $\mu_{eff}$ is the effective foam viscosity.

**Step 4 — Equate and solve for $H_f$.**
At steady state, the integrated liquid drainage over the foam height equals the input liquid rate. Using a simplified **dimensionless gas-flux correlation** (Shah–Pereira model, modified):

$$\dfrac{H_f}{H_L} = 1 + K_1 \left( \dfrac{u_g \mu_{eff}}{\sigma} \right)^{0.5} \cdot (N D)^{0.4}$$

**Step 5 — Final working equation.**

$$\boxed{\;H_f = H_L \left[ 1 + K_1 \left( \dfrac{u_g \mu_{eff}}{\sigma} \right)^{0.5} (N D)^{0.4} \right]\;}$$

where $K_1$ is a geometry-dependent constant (typically **0.8–1.4** for standard Rushton-type impellers).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering insight:** Foam height $H_f$ scales with $u_g^{0.5}$ and $(ND)^{0.4}$ — therefore, **doubling the agitation speed increases foam height by ~32 %**, and **doubling the gas flowrate increases it by ~41 %**. This is why high-cell-density processes always combine antifoam with a controlled gas-flow ramp.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Foam-Induced Reduction in $k_L a$

The presence of foam in the broth effectively **reduces the gas–liquid interfacial area** in the *bulk* liquid. Using a hold-up correction:

$$k_{L}a_{eff} = k_{L}a \left(1 - \alpha \phi_{foam}\right)$$

where $\alpha$ is an empirical correction (typically **1.2–2.0** because foam also steals oxygen from the bulk), and $\phi_{foam}$ is the gas fraction in the foam layer.

For a typical 50 m³ bioreactor with $k_L a = 0.025$ s⁻¹ and $\phi_{foam} = 0.85$, $\alpha = 1.5$:

$$k_{L}a_{eff} = 0.025 \times (1 - 1.5 \times 0.85) = 0.025 \times (-0.275)$$

The negative result signals that foam is **completely blocking oxygen transfer** — a clear case where antifoam must be applied immediately. In practice, the system switches to a **"pure oxygen" feed** until antifoam knocks down the foam.

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing a Foam Control Strategy

| Criterion | Mechanical (Disc) | Chemical (Antifoam) | Operational (Media) | Acoustic | Cyclone |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Capital cost** | Medium | Low | None | High | Medium |
| **Operating cost** | Low (electricity) | Medium (antifoam) | Low | Low | Low |
| **Foam kill rate** | Moderate | High | Slow | High | High |
| **Shear damage to cells** | Possible | None | None | None | None |
| **Effect on $k_L a$** | Slight | **Strong reduction** | None | None | None |
| **Downstream impact** | None | Foam in product | None | None | None |
| **Best for** | Yeast, fungi | Bacteria, CHO | All (preventive) | Mammalian | High-volume gas streams |

## 3.6 Worked Example — KTU-Style 7-Mark Problem

**Problem (PEPOT526, ESE pattern):** A stirred-tank bioreactor of diameter 1.2 m operates with a gas superficial velocity of 0.004 m/s, broth surface tension 0.065 N/m, and a Rushton impeller of diameter 0.4 m rotating at 250 rpm. Estimate the foam height and determine whether a 0.3 m diameter foam-breaker disc is sufficient.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Convert units: $N = 250/60 = 4.167$ rps, $D = 0.4$ m.

Step 2 — Assume $K_1 = 1.2$ and $\mu_{eff} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s (water-like broth).

Step 3 — Compute the argument of the bracket:

$$\begin{aligned}
\left( \dfrac{u_g \mu_{eff}}{\sigma} \right)^{0.5} &= \left( \dfrac{0.004 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{0.065} \right)^{0.5} \\
&= (6.15 \times 10^{-5})^{0.5} \\
&= 7.85 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}$$

Step 4 — Compute the agitation term:

$$(N D)^{0.4} = (4.167 \times 0.4)^{0.4} = (1.667)^{0.4} \approx 1.225$$

Step 5 — Foam height ratio:

$$\dfrac{H_f}{H_L} = 1 + 1.2 \times 7.85 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.225 = 1 + 0.0115 \approx 1.0115$$

Step 6 — Interpretation:
For a typical $H_L = 1.5$ m, $H_f \approx 1.52$ m. The headspace in this bioreactor should therefore be at least **30 %** of $H_L$ to safely accommodate the foam. A 0.3 m disc is **sufficient** for mild foam, but for high-foam cultures (e.g., Pichia) a larger disc or chemical antifoam is required.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Foam Control Loop in a Bioreactor

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram represents the **control loop architecture** of a complete foam control system, showing all interacting subsystems: sensing, decision, action, and the physical foam dynamics.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Broth in Vessel] -->|Aeration Agitation| B[Gas Liquid Interfacial Foam Generation]
    B -->|Rises into Headspace| C[Foam Head Height]
    C -->|Touches Probe| D[Conductivity / Capacitance Probe]
    D -->|4-20 mA Signal| E[PID Controller in DCS]
    E -->|Bang-Bang or PI Output| F[Antifoam Reservoir]
    F -->|Peristaltic Pump Actuation| G[Antifoam Injection Line]
    G -->|Spreads over Gas Liquid Interface| A
    C -->|Mechanical Disruption| H[Rotating Foam Breaker Disc]
    H -->|Driven by| I[Variable Frequency Drive VFD]
    I -->|Receives Setpoint RPM| E
    C -->|Overflow to Side Arm| J[External Cyclone Foam Trap]
    J -->|Liquid Return| A
    J -->|Clean Gas Exhaust| K[Off-Gas HEPA Filter]
    K -->|Exits to| L[Condenser / Scrubber]
    E -->|Data Logging| M[SCADA HMI Foam Height Trend]
    M -->|Operator Override| E
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The left branch shows the **measurement** pathway: foam height is detected by a probe.
- The central branch shows the **mechanical** action: a foam breaker disc destroys bubbles physically.
- The right branch shows the **chemical** action: a peristaltic pump injects antifoam.
- The bottom branch shows the **information flow** to the SCADA/HMI.


## 4.3 Antifoam Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Need Foam Control] --> Q1{Organism Type}
    Q1 -->|Mammalian CHO| M1[Use Polyols or PFPE]
    Q1 -->|Bacterial| M2[Use Silicones or Stearates]
    Q1 -->|Fungal Yeast| M3[Use Silicones or Natural Oils]
    Q1 -->|Plant Cells| M4[Use Specialized Antifoam]
    M1 --> Q2{Downstream Sensitive}
    Q2 -->|Yes Chromatography| F1[Use Synthetic PFPE]
    Q2 -->|No| F2[Use Polypropylene Glycol]
    M2 --> Q3{High Cell Density}
    Q3 -->|Yes > 80 g/L DCW| F3[Use Silicone with Probe Control]
    Q3 -->|No| F4[Use Stearic Acid Based]
    M3 --> Q5{Product is Intracellular}
    Q5 -->|Yes| F5[Use Natural Oils with Filtration]
    Q5 -->|No| F6[Use Silicone Emulsions]

```

## 4.4 Foam Dynamics Time-Cycle Schematic

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Probe as Foam Probe
    participant DCS as DCS Controller
    participant Pump as Antifoam Pump
    participant Vessel as Bioreactor
    Note over Vessel: Foam rises due to aeration
    Probe->>DCS: Signal increases (foam touches)
    DCS->>DCS: Compare to setpoint (e.g., 5 cm)
    alt Foam height above setpoint
        DCS->>Pump: Activate pump for 2 s
        Pump->>Vessel: Deliver 50 mL antifoam
        Vessel->>Vessel: Antifoam spreads, breaks foam
        Probe->>DCS: Signal decreases
    else Foam height below setpoint
        DCS->>Pump: Pump OFF (dead-band)
    end
    Note over Vessel: Cycle repeats every 30-120 s
```
