# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> [!NOTE]
> These are direct-recall, **K1 / K2 level** questions as per KTU 2024 Bloom's Taxonomy. Each must be answered in **60–80 words**.

### Question 1 (CO4, K1, 3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define foam in the context of a bioreactor. List any three problems caused by excessive foam in an industrial-scale fermenter.**

**Model Answer:**
Foam is a colloidal dispersion of gas bubbles in a thin liquid film, stabilized by surface-active compounds (proteins, peptides, polysaccharides) secreted by the culture. In bioreactors, it forms at the broth surface due to intense aeration and agitation.
**Problems caused:**
1. Blockage of the off-gas HEPA filter leading to overpressure.
2. Loss of culture volume and product by foam entrainment.
3. Erratic dissolved-oxygen (DO) probe readings.
4. Anaerobic pockets in the foam head, shifting metabolism.
5. Contamination of downstream piping.

*(Any three, 1 mark each.)*

### Question 2 (CO4, K2, 3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the Ross criterion for antifoam action? State its significance.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Ross criterion** states that an antifoam droplet will spread over a foam film and cause its rupture only if the **spreading coefficient S > 0**, where:

$$S = \sigma_{foam} - \sigma_{af} - \sigma_{int}$$

Here $\sigma_{foam}$, $\sigma_{af}$, $\sigma_{int}$ are the surface tensions of the foam film, antifoam, and the antifoam–broth interface, respectively.
**Significance:** It provides a quantitative selection rule for choosing antifoams; an antifoam with $S < 0$ will not enter the foam film and will be ineffective. Industrially, $S = 0.005 - 0.020$ N/m is preferred.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> These are **K3 / K4 level** analytical questions, each carrying **7 + 7 = 14 marks**. Two independent choices (A and B) are provided per KTU 2024 ESE pattern.

---

### 🔷 Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Classify the different methods of foam control in bioreactors with suitable examples. Compare their relative advantages and limitations. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Foam control in bioreactors is classified into **three primary families** as per the PEPOT526 syllabus:

**1. Mechanical Foam Control** — physical destruction of bubbles.
- *Rotating foam-breaker disc* — flat or conical disc above the broth, 600–1500 rpm.
- *Centrifugal foam breaker* — high-speed rotor, separates gas from liquid.
- *Foam trap / external cyclone* — side-arm gravity separator.
- *Acoustic defoamer* — 20 kHz ultrasonic transducer.

**2. Chemical Foam Control** — addition of antifoam agents.
- *Silicone oils* (PDMS), *Polyols* (PPG), *Fatty acid esters*, *Natural oils*, *Solid antifoams* (silica, talc).

**3. Operational/Process Control** — preventive process modifications.
- Reduced gas flowrate, microsparger use, defined media, two-stage reactors.

| Method | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanical | No chemical contamination, reusable | Capital cost, possible cell shear |
| Chemical | Highly effective, low capital | Reduces $k_L a$, downstream issues |
| Operational | Permanent solution | Slow, may compromise cell growth |

**Best practice:** Industrial bioreactors use a **hybrid strategy** — antifoam probe + chemical antifoam + a small foam breaker — to balance cost, sterility, and product quality. *(7 Marks: 1 mark for classification, 2 marks for examples, 2 marks for comparison, 2 marks for industrial application.)*

**(b) A stirred-tank bioreactor operates with the following data: broth surface tension 0.062 N/m, gas superficial velocity 0.0035 m/s, effective foam viscosity 1.2 × 10⁻³ Pa·s, liquid height 1.8 m, impeller speed 4.2 rps, impeller diameter 0.45 m, and geometry constant $K_1 = 1.3$. Estimate the foam height and comment on whether the available headspace of 0.7 m is sufficient. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Substitute into the foam-height correlation:**

$$\dfrac{H_f}{H_L} = 1 + K_1 \left( \dfrac{u_g \mu_{eff}}{\sigma} \right)^{0.5} (N D)^{0.4}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the argument of the bracket:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\left( \dfrac{u_g \mu_{eff}}{\sigma} \right)^{0.5} &= \left( \dfrac{0.0035 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-3}}{0.062} \right)^{0.5} \\
&= (6.77 \times 10^{-5})^{0.5} \\
&\approx 8.23 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the agitation term:**

$$(N D)^{0.4} = (4.2 \times 0.45)^{0.4} = (1.89)^{0.4} \approx 1.302$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the ratio:**

$$\dfrac{H_f}{H_L} = 1 + 1.3 \times 8.23 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.302 = 1 + 0.0139 \approx 1.0139$$

**Step 5 — Compute foam height:**

$$H_f = 1.0139 \times 1.8 = 1.825 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6 — Required headspace:**

$$h_{HS,req} = H_f - H_L = 1.825 - 1.8 = 0.025 \text{ m} = 2.5 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 7 — Comment:**
The required headspace of **2.5 cm** is well within the available **0.7 m**. The system is comfortably safe from foam overflow at the current operating conditions, but in the event of a 3× increase in gas flow (e.g., during a feed ramp), $H_f$ would scale to roughly 1.85 m, still within margin. However, if the surface tension drops to 0.040 N/m (e.g., high recombinant protein), $H_f$ may grow beyond 1.9 m, warranting an active foam control system. *(7 Marks: 1 mark formula, 2 marks substitution, 2 marks calculation, 2 marks interpretation.)*

---

### 🔷 Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Describe the construction and working of a conductivity-based foam probe coupled with a PID-controlled antifoam dosing system. Draw a labeled schematic. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction:**
A **conductivity-based foam probe** consists of two parallel stainless-steel electrodes (length 5–15 cm, separation 1–2 cm) housed in a sanitary 25 mm port, mounted on the bioreactor head **15–20 cm above the working liquid level**. The electrodes are connected to a **conductivity transmitter** that outputs a 4–20 mA signal proportional to the foam height.

**Working principle:**
The probe measures the **electrical conductance** between the electrodes:
- In **vapor** (no foam): low conductance, signal ≈ 4 mA.
- When **foam** (liquid lamellae) bridges the electrodes: high conductance, signal jumps to ≈ 12–20 mA.
- The transmitter outputs a **linear 4–20 mA** signal mapped to foam height.

**PID control loop:**
The DCS receives the signal, compares it to a **setpoint** (e.g., 5 cm of foam), and actuates a **peristaltic pump** via a **PID algorithm**:
- *P (proportional)*: pump speed proportional to error.
- *I (integral)*: eliminates steady-state offset due to persistent foam.
- *D (derivative)*: dampens response to sudden surges.

The pump delivers antifoam from a sterile reservoir through a **steam-sterilizable line** into the broth. The **dead-band** of 2–3 cm prevents pump cycling.

**Schematic (described):**
- *Top of vessel*: probe electrodes, connected to transmitter.
- *Side of vessel*: pump, connected to controller.
- *Pump output*: antifoam line terminating in the broth or sparger zone.
- *Controller*: DCS with PID block, setpoint input, trend display on HMI.

*(7 Marks: 2 marks construction, 2 marks working, 2 marks PID explanation, 1 mark schematic.)*

**(b) Derive the expression for the equilibrium foam height in a stirred-tank bioreactor using a dimensionless gas-flux correlation. Given $\sigma_0 = 0.070$ N/m and an antifoam decay constant $k_c = 4.5$ m³/kg, find the antifoam concentration required to reduce the surface tension to 0.040 N/m. Comment on the engineering implications. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Derivation (as in Section 3.3):**

The foam height is derived by equating the upward gas flux to the downward liquid drainage:

$$\begin{aligned}
J_{gas,up} &= J_{liq,down} \\
\beta \cdot u_g &= \dfrac{\rho_L g d_{b}^{2}}{200 \mu_{eff}} \cdot \dfrac{\phi_{foam}}{1 - \phi_{foam}}
\end{aligned}$$

Integrating over the foam column and using a power-law closure for the foam viscosity yields the **Shah–Pereira / Stevenson-type correlation**:

$$\boxed{\;H_f = H_L \left[ 1 + K_1 \left( \dfrac{u_g \mu_{eff}}{\sigma} \right)^{0.5} (N D)^{0.4} \right]\;}$$

**Step 2 — Antifoam dose calculation:**

Using $\sigma_{app} = \sigma_0 \exp(-k_c C_{af})$:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{af} &= \dfrac{1}{k_c} \ln \left( \dfrac{\sigma_0}{\sigma_{app}} \right) \\
C_{af} &= \dfrac{1}{4.5} \ln \left( \dfrac{0.070}{0.040} \right) \\
C_{af} &= \dfrac{1}{4.5} \ln(1.75) \\
C_{af} &= \dfrac{1}{4.5} \times 0.5596 \\
C_{af} &\approx 0.124 \text{ kg/m}^{3} = 124 \text{ ppm (w/v)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Engineering implications:**
- A dose of **124 ppm** is on the **low end** of typical industrial antifoam levels, which is favorable.
- However, **lowering surface tension reduces $k_L a$** by 15–30 %, requiring compensation via **higher agitation or pure-O₂ sparging**.
- The antifoam residue may **foul chromatographic columns** in downstream processing, so an antifoam-removal step (e.g., activated carbon) may be required.
- Overdosing above 200 ppm risks **incomplete sterility barrier** in some silicone-based emulsions.

*(7 Marks: 3 marks derivation, 2 marks calculation, 2 marks implications.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing "spreading" with "entering" coefficient.** Spreading is S > 0; entering is E > 0. The Ross criterion is specifically about *spreading*.
> 2. **Forgetting units** in the $k_c$ calculation — it is **m³/kg**, not m²/kg.
> 3. **Mixing up the foam height ratio** $H_f/H_L$ with the absolute $H_f$ — the exam expects both.
> 4. **Omitting the schematic** in foam-probe questions costs 1–2 marks even if the text is correct.
> 5. **Not stating the assumption** that broth behaves as a Newtonian fluid — high-cell-density cultures are non-Newtonian and require a viscosity correction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-minute revision checklist — must-know bullets for the KTU 2024 ESE.**

- **Foam is a colloid** of gas in liquid, stabilized by surface-active metabolites. *(CO4, K1)*
- **Three families of foam control:** Mechanical, Chemical, Operational. *(CO4, K2)*
- **Foam height** scales as $H_f = H_L \left[1 + K_1 (u_g \mu_{eff}/\sigma)^{0.5} (ND)^{0.4}\right]$. *(CO4, K3)*
- **Power for foam breaker disc** $P_{FB} = \rho_{foam} (\pi D_d^2 / 4) E_f u_g \left[(\pi^2 N^2 D_d^2)/2 + g h_{eff}\right]$. *(CO4, K3)*
- **Antifoam dosage** $C_{af} = (1/k_c) \ln(\sigma_0/\sigma_{app})$. *(CO4, K3)*
- **Ross criterion:** $S = \sigma_{foam} - \sigma_{af} - \sigma_{int} > 0$ for spreading. *(CO4, K2)*
- **Plateau–Gibbs suction** $\Pi = 2\sigma/r_{pb}$ governs foam drainage. *(CO4, K2)*
- **Gibbs elasticity** $E = 2 d\sigma/d\ln\Gamma$ governs foam stability. *(CO4, K3)*
- **Conductivity probe + PID** is the standard antifoam control loop in KTU-aligned bioreactors. *(CO4, K4)*
- **Antifoams reduce $k_L a$** by 15–30 % — always compensate via higher agitation or pure-O₂. *(CO4, K4)*
- **Industry favorites:** PDMS (silicone) for yeast, PPG (polyol) for CHO, natural oils for antibiotics. *(CO4, K2)*
- **Five engineering problems** caused by foam: filter blockage, volume loss, product loss, DO probe errors, anaerobic pockets, downstream fouling. *(CO4, K1)*
- **Industrial scale** of foam control: ₹2–5 crore per batch in a 50,000 L facility. *(CO4, K2)*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Mnemonic — "F-O-A-M":**
> - **F**oam = colloidal gas–liquid dispersion.
> - **O**perational control = preventive (media, gas flow).
> - **A**ntifoam = chemical, surface tension reduction.
> - **M**echanical = disc, cyclone, acoustic.

This mnemonic will help you in **descriptive 7-mark questions** where you must structure your answer in logical order.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factors Affecting Antifoam Requirements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT526 - BIOREACTOR DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Factors Affecting Antifoam Requirements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Antifoam Requirements** in a bioreactor refer to the *quantitative and qualitative demand* of an antifoaming (defoaming) chemical agent needed to suppress, control, or eliminate the excessive accumulation of foam during aerobic microbial cultivation. The *factors affecting antifoam requirements* are the set of biophysical, biochemical, and operational variables that collectively dictate how much antifoam must be added, what chemical class of antifoam should be selected, and when the antifoam should be introduced into the fermentation broth.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (PEPOT526 – Bioreactor Design, Module 4: *Aeration, Agitation, and Foam Control in Bioreactors*), these factors are classified under three master categories:

1. **Biological Factors** — the morphology, physiology, and growth phase of the producing microorganism.
2. **Medium / Biochemical Factors** — the composition of the nutrient broth (carbon source, nitrogen source, surface-active metabolites).
3. **Process / Engineering Factors** — agitation intensity (rpm), aeration rate (vvm), temperature, pH, fermenter configuration, and the sterilization regime.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PEPOT526 – Module 4):** Antifoam dosing is NOT a one-size-fits-all parameter. The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to correlate antifoam requirements with the *intrinsic foaming tendency* of the broth, the *operational hydrodynamic regime*, and the *type of antifoam chemistry* (silicone-based, polyol-based, fatty acid, or natural oils).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain-English)

Imagine you are **whisking milk in a steel bowl** to make whipped cream:

- If the milk is **cold and high in fat** (think *protein-rich fermentation broth*), the foam rises quickly and becomes very stable — just like the foam in a stirred-tank bioreactor growing *Streptomyces*.
- If you **whisk faster (high agitation rpm)**, more air gets incorporated — foam builds rapidly.
- If you add a **drop of cooking oil (antifoam)**, the foam collapses almost instantly because the oil breaks the surface tension of the air–liquid film.
- But if you keep whisking fast and the milk keeps producing foam, **a single drop of oil is not enough** — you need to keep adding antifoam *proportionally* to the rate of foam generation.

> **The takeaway:** the *amount* of oil (antifoam) you need is dictated by (a) what is being whisked (microorganism + medium), (b) how fast and how hard you whisk (agitation + aeration), and (c) the bowl shape and temperature (bioreactor geometry + temperature).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Axiom (Foam Control):** *Antifoam requirement is a function of the rate of foam formation minus the rate of natural foam decay.* When generation > decay, foam accumulates and dosing must increase. This is the foundation of the *Antifoam Mass Balance* used in industrial fermenter control loops.

---

## 1.3 Standard Quantitative Metrics (Used in KTU 2024 Exams)

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Range in Industrial Bioreactors |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Working Volume | $V_L$ | **70–80 %** of total vessel volume |
| Foam-Free Headspace | $H_{head}$ | Typically **20–30 %** of total height |
| Volumetric Air Flow Rate | $Q_g$ | **0.5 – 2.0 vvm** (volume of air / volume of broth / minute) |
| Critical Foaming Concentration | $C_{foam}^{cr}$ | Broth-dependent; empirically determined |
| Antifoam Dosage | $D_{AF}$ | **0.01 – 0.5 % v/v** of working volume |
| Dimensionless Foam Number | $Fo$ | Used to quantify foam stability |
| Critical Micelle Concentration (for soluble antifoams) | $CMC$ | **10⁻⁶ – 10⁻³ mol/L** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — Break-Down

The *antifoam requirement* of a given fermentation system is the result of an interplay between **foam generation kinetics** and **foam collapse kinetics**. The KTU 2024 framework treats this as a control problem where the antifoam dose is the manipulated variable.

### Logic Step 1 — Why Does Foam Form in a Bioreactor?
- Aeration + agitation disperses gas as **bubbles**.
- **Surface-active agents** (proteins, peptides, fatty acids, biosurfactants) secreted by microbes or released from the medium **adsorb at the gas–liquid interface**.
- These adsorbed layers form a **stabilizing film** that prevents liquid drainage from the bubble lamella → bubbles persist and accumulate as **foam**.

### Logic Step 2 — Why Is Excessive Foam a Problem?
- Foam escapes the headspace → **loss of broth** (yield loss).
- Foam wet the exhaust gas filter → **contamination risk**.
- Foam interferes with the **dissolved oxygen (DO) probe** and **pH probe** readings.
- Foam causes **mechanical blockage** of the gas outlet line.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** The three primary *consequences* of poor foam control (broth loss, contamination, sensor failure) are also the three most commonly asked 3-mark and 7-mark answer points in KTU university exams.

### Logic Step 3 — How Does an Antifoam Work?
Antifoams operate by one of two physical mechanisms:
1. **Spreading/Entering Mechanism (Ross-Mile Model):** The antifoam droplet spreads across the air–water interface, replacing the stabilizing surfactant film.
2. **Bridging-Stretching Mechanism:** Hydrophobic solid particles (e.g., silica in silicone antifoams) bridge the thin film and rupture it.

### Logic Step 4 — Factors Affecting Antifoam Requirements
The dose and type of antifoam are governed by the following groups:

#### A. Biological Factors
| Factor | Effect on Antifoam Requirement |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Microbial morphology** | Filamentous bacteria (*Streptomyces*, *Actinomycetes*) and fungi produce **more foam** → higher antifoam dose |
| **Growth phase** | Exponential phase = peak surfactant production = peak foam → maximum dosing |
| **Microbial by-products** | Biosurfactants, exopolysaccharides (EPS) stabilize foam |
| **Cell concentration** | Higher biomass = higher broth viscosity = potentially higher foam stability |

#### B. Medium / Biochemical Factors
| Factor | Effect on Antifoam Requirement |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Protein content** (e.g., soy flour, yeast extract) | Strong foam stabilizers → 2–5× more antifoam needed |
| **Sugar concentration** (high in molasses, glucose) | Increases medium viscosity → increases foam stability |
| **Lipids / Oils** | Act as *natural antifoams* → reduces external antifoam need |
| **pH** | Affects protein charge and surfactant ionization |
| **Salinity / Ionic strength** | Can compress the electrical double layer → thinner film → less stable foam |

#### C. Process / Engineering Factors
| Factor | Effect on Antifoam Requirement |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Agitation speed (N)** | Higher rpm = more bubble breakup = more foam → higher dose |
| **Aeration rate (vvm)** | Higher gas flow = more air input = more foam potential |
| **Impeller type** | Rushton disc turbine (high shear) generates more foam than pitched-blade |
| **Temperature** | Higher T lowers surface tension → easier foam formation |
| **Fermenter type** | Airlift < Stirred Tank (in typical foam tendency ranking) |
| **Sterilization cycle** | Maillard reactions between sugars and amino acids produce **brown polymers** that are potent foam stabilizers |
| **Foam control hardware** | Mechanical foam breakers (centrifugal) reduce chemical antifoam demand by **30–60 %** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Benchmark:** In a typical 50,000 L *Streptomyces* fermentation, the antifoam consumption is **0.05–0.3 % v/v** of the working volume, fed in multiple shots (proportional control) rather than a single bolus dose.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Relationship | Engineering Meaning | Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $Fo = \dfrac{V_{foam}}{Q_g \cdot t}$ | **Dimensionless Foam Number** — ratio of foam volume to air supplied | — |
| 2 | $\dfrac{dh_{foam}}{dt} = k_1 \cdot \dfrac{Q_g}{A_{cs}} - k_2 \cdot C_{AF} \cdot h_{foam}$ | **Foam height dynamic balance** | $\text{m/s}$ |
| 3 | $\sigma_{AF} < \sigma_{broth}$ | **Spreading condition** (Ross-Mile) for antifoam efficacy | $\text{N/m}$ |
| 4 | $S = \dfrac{\sigma_{water} - \sigma_{AF}}{\sigma_{water} + \sigma_{AF} - 2\sigma_{broth}}$ | **Spreading coefficient** (S must be > 0 for active antifoam) | — |
| 5 | $D_{AF} = k \cdot C_{S} \cdot \dfrac{Q_g}{N}$ | **Empirical antifoam dosage** (proportional to surfactant and airflow, inversely to rpm) | $\text{kg}$ or $\text{L}$ |
| 6 | $K_{La} = k \cdot \dfrac{P_g^{0.5} \cdot v_s^{0.5}}{V_L}$ | **Volumetric OTR** — foam alters effective $K_{La}$ | $\text{s}^{-1}$ |
| 7 | $Re_{imp} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot N \cdot D_i^2}{\mu_{app}}$ | **Impeller Reynolds number** — determines regime of foam dispersion | — |
| 8 | $\mu_{app} = \mu_0 \cdot e^{k_3 \cdot C_x}$ | **Apparent broth viscosity** increases with cell concentration | $\text{Pa.s}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall:** When using the spreading coefficient $S$, students often forget that the denominator includes $\sigma_{AF} - \sigma_{broth}$. If $\sigma_{AF} > \sigma_{broth}$, the antifoam does NOT spread and is therefore *ineffective*. Always check the **spreading sign** before assuming a chosen antifoam will work.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The factors described above are the foundation of **PAT (Process Analytical Technology)** in modern pharmaceutical and industrial biotechnology. Foam sensors (capacitance probes) feed a **feedback control loop** to a peristaltic pump that injects antifoam in real time. The *gain* of this loop is set empirically based on the factors above. Without understanding them, scale-up from a 10 L lab fermenter to a 100,000 L production fermenter is impossible.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Foam Height Dynamic Balance

The mass balance on a thin foam layer in a stirred-tank bioreactor can be written as a simple first-order ODE. This is the most commonly asked 7-mark derivation in the KTU ESE.

### Step 1 — Define the control volume
The control volume is the foam layer of height $h_{foam}$ in the headspace, with cross-sectional area $A_{cs}$ (assumed equal to tank cross-section).

### Step 2 — Rate of foam generation
The rate of foam generation is proportional to the volumetric gas flow rate $Q_g$ and inversely proportional to the cross-sectional area:

$$R_{gen} = k_1 \cdot \frac{Q_g}{A_{cs}}$$

where $k_1$ is a proportionality constant that captures the **intrinsic foaming tendency** of the broth (dimensionless).

### Step 3 — Rate of foam collapse (natural drainage)
Without antifoam, foam decays by gravity-driven liquid drainage from the lamella:

$$R_{decay}^{natural} = k_2 \cdot h_{foam}$$

where $k_2$ is a first-order decay constant ($\text{s}^{-1}$).

### Step 4 — Rate of foam collapse due to antifoam
Antifoam accelerates film rupture. The collapse rate is proportional to the antifoam concentration $C_{AF}$ (mass/volume) and the existing foam height:

$$R_{decay}^{AF} = k_3 \cdot C_{AF} \cdot h_{foam}$$

### Step 5 — Combine into mass balance
The net rate of change of foam height is:

$$\frac{dh_{foam}}{dt} = R_{gen} - R_{decay}^{natural} - R_{decay}^{AF}$$

Substituting all terms:

$$\frac{dh_{foam}}{dt} = k_1 \cdot \frac{Q_g}{A_{cs}} - k_2 \cdot h_{foam} - k_3 \cdot C_{AF} \cdot h_{foam}$$

### Step 6 — Group the decay terms

$$\frac{dh_{foam}}{dt} = k_1 \cdot \frac{Q_g}{A_{cs}} - (k_2 + k_3 \cdot C_{AF}) \cdot h_{foam}$$

### Step 7 — Steady-state solution
At steady state, $\dfrac{dh_{foam}}{dt} = 0$, so:

$$k_1 \cdot \frac{Q_g}{A_{cs}} = (k_2 + k_3 \cdot C_{AF}) \cdot h_{foam}^{ss}$$

### Step 8 — Solve for antifoam concentration

$$C_{AF}^{ss} = \frac{1}{k_3} \cdot \left( \frac{k_1 \cdot Q_g}{A_{cs} \cdot h_{foam}^{ss}} - k_2 \right)$$

### Step 9 — Engineering interpretation
This is the **famous antifoam dosage equation** used in KTU problems:

> **The required antifoam concentration is directly proportional to the air flow rate and inversely proportional to the allowable foam height.** If the operator wishes to keep $h_{foam}^{ss}$ small (tight headspace), the antifoam dose must increase *non-linearly* (because of the reciprocal).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Spreading Coefficient (Ross-Mile Equation)

The classical thermodynamic criterion for an antifoam to work is given by the spreading coefficient. Derivation is based on the **Young-Dupré equation** applied to a three-phase system (antifoam droplet, foam film, and air).

### Step 1 — Define the three interfacial tensions
Let:
- $\sigma_{AW}$ = surface tension between antifoam and air (foam film gas side)
- $\sigma_{FA}$ = surface tension between foam film liquid and antifoam droplet
- $\sigma_{FW}$ = surface tension between foam film liquid and air

### Step 2 — Write the Young-Dupré relation at the three-phase contact line
For a stable contact, the horizontal force balance at the contact line is:

$$\sigma_{FW} = \sigma_{FA} + \sigma_{AW} \cdot \cos\theta$$

where $\theta$ is the contact angle of the antifoam droplet on the foam film.

### Step 3 — Define the spreading coefficient
The spreading coefficient $S$ is defined as the *excess surface free energy* released when the antifoam spreads over the foam film:

$$S = \sigma_{FW} - (\sigma_{FA} + \sigma_{AW})$$

### Step 4 — Apply the spreading criterion
- If $S > 0$: the antifoam **spreads** spontaneously over the film → film ruptures → foam collapses. **Antifoam is effective.**
- If $S < 0$: the antifoam does NOT spread → it remains as a lens → **antifoam is ineffective.**
- If $S = 0$: partial wetting → marginal effectiveness.

### Step 5 — Simplified form for water-based broth
For a typical aqueous broth, the Ross-Mile equation reduces to:

$$S = \sigma_{broth} - \sigma_{AF} - \sigma_{AF \text{-}broth}$$

> **Critical Insight (KTU-favorite):** For most silicone-based antifoams, $\sigma_{AF} \approx 0.02$ N/m, while $\sigma_{broth} \approx 0.05$–$0.07$ N/m, giving $S > 0$ → effective.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Table for Antifoam Selection

| Foam Source / Factor | Severity of Foam Problem | Recommended Antifoam Class | Typical Dose (v/v) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Streptomyces / Actinomycetes** (filamentous bacteria) | **Very High** | Silicone-polyether copolymer | **0.1 – 0.3 %** |
| **Saccharomyces** (yeast, baker's) | Low | Polypropylene glycol (PPG) | **0.01 – 0.05 %** |
| **E. coli** (recombinant) | Moderate | Stearyl alcohol / octadecanol | **0.02 – 0.1 %** |
| **Bacillus subtilis** (biosurfactant producer) | High | Silicone emulsion + PPG | **0.1 – 0.2 %** |
| **Penicillium / Aspergillus** (filamentous fungi) | High | Natural oil (soybean / corn oil) | **0.05 – 0.2 %** |
| **CHO / Mammalian cell culture** | Low–Moderate | **Pluronic F-68 (block copolymer)** | **0.05 – 0.5 %** |
| **High-protein / molasses medium** | Very High | Silicone-based antifoam | **0.1 – 0.5 %** |
| **Lipid-rich medium (oil substrate)** | Low | Often no external antifoam needed | **0 %** |
| **High aeration (>1.5 vvm)** | High | Mechanical foam breaker + chemical | **0.05 – 0.2 %** |
| **High temperature (>35 °C)** | Moderate | Heat-stable silicone emulsion | **0.05 – 0.15 %** |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style 14-Mark Problem Style)

**Problem:** A 50,000 L stirred-tank bioreactor is operated at $Q_g = 25,000$ L/min (i.e., 0.5 vvm). The tank diameter is 3.5 m, the allowable foam height is $h_{foam}^{ss} = 0.4$ m, the empirical constants are $k_1 = 0.85$, $k_2 = 0.04 \text{ s}^{-1}$, and $k_3 = 0.12 \text{ L/g.s}$. Calculate the steady-state antifoam concentration required.

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area of the tank:**

$$A_{cs} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (3.5)^2 = 9.62 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Convert units of $Q_g$:**

$$Q_g = 25{,}000 \text{ L/min} = \frac{25{,}000}{60} = 416.67 \text{ L/s}$$

Convert to $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$:

$$Q_g = 0.4167 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the steady-state antifoam equation from §3.1:**

$$C_{AF}^{ss} = \frac{1}{k_3} \cdot \left( \frac{k_1 \cdot Q_g}{A_{cs} \cdot h_{foam}^{ss}} - k_2 \right)$$

**Step 4 — Compute the generation term:**

$$\frac{k_1 \cdot Q_g}{A_{cs} \cdot h_{foam}^{ss}} = \frac{0.85 \cdot 0.4167}{9.62 \cdot 0.4} = \frac{0.3542}{3.848} = 0.0921 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**Step 5 — Subtract natural decay:**

$$0.0921 - 0.04 = 0.0521 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**Step 6 — Divide by $k_3$:**

$$C_{AF}^{ss} = \frac{0.0521}{0.12} = 0.434 \text{ g/L}$$

**Step 7 — Convert to v/v dose (assuming density ≈ 1.0 g/mL for typical antifoam emulsion):**

$$D_{AF} = 0.434 \text{ g/L} \times \frac{1 \text{ mL}}{1 \text{ g}} = 0.434 \text{ mL/L} = 0.0434 \text{ \% v/v}$$

**Step 8 — Total antifoam mass required (in 50,000 L working volume):**

$$m_{AF} = 0.434 \text{ g/L} \cdot 50{,}000 \text{ L} = 21{,}700 \text{ g} \approx 21.7 \text{ kg per dosing cycle}$$

> **Interpretation:** Even at moderate aeration, foam control in a 50 kL fermenter requires ~22 kg of antifoam per cycle. This explains why **proportional dosing** (continuous) is preferred over a single bolus addition.
